topics.title: Basic Ship Parameters Including Existing Ship Stability Condition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Ship Parameters Including Existing Ship Stability Condition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Definition of Basic Ship Parameters

The **principal geometric parameters** of a ship are the standard set of dimensions, form coefficients, and tonnage figures used universally by naval architects, classification societies, charterers, and port-state authorities to characterise the hull form. They constitute the *lingua franca* of ship specification and form the absolute baseline against which any conversion design is benchmarked.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** *"Basic ship parameters including existing ship stability condition"* — this topic sits at the foundation of every conversion study because no modification may proceed without quantifying the *as-is* hull envelope and its intact-stability envelope.

The standard parameter set is grouped into four families:

| Family | Parameters |
|---|---|
| Linear dimensions | LOA, LBP, LWL, B, D, T, Freeboard |
| Form coefficients | $C_B$, $C_M$, $C_P$, $C_{WP}$, $C_V$ |
| Area/Volume quantities | $\nabla$, $A_W$, $A_M$, $S$ (wetted surface) |
| Mass quantities | Displacement $\Delta$, DWT, Lightweight, TPC |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Cardboard Box Boat"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a sealed cardboard box floating in a bathtub. To describe its size you immediately quote **length × breadth × depth**. The box *floats at a certain depth* (its draft). Press one corner down — the box tilts (heels). Push too hard and it floods or capsizes. The amount it resists tilting is its **stability**.
>
> A ship is simply a *very precisely engineered cardboard box* — but instead of a hand pushing it, wind, waves, cargo shifts, and turning forces do. The *geometry* of the box and the *position of weight inside it* together determine whether it comes back upright or slowly rolls over.

The lesson: **ship parameters describe the box; stability tells us how the box behaves when pushed.**

### 1.3 Existing Ship Stability Condition — Formal Definition

> **Existing Ship Stability Condition** refers to the complete quantitative statement of a vessel's intact transverse and longitudinal stability **in its present configuration** — i.e., the metacentric height **GM**, righting-lever curve **GZ(φ)**, trim, freeboard, and compliance with the **IMO Code on Intact Stability (Resolution A.749(18))** as applicable to the vessel type, *prior to* any conversion intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For any conversion project, the existing stability condition is **non-negotiable baseline data**. IMO, classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV, IRS), and the **SOLAS Convention** all require demonstration that the *converted* ship meets or exceeds the intact-stability criteria of the *existing* ship plus any new operational profile.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Metrics

The standard reference metrics used in KTU ship-stability problems are:

- **Sea-water density:** $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \; \text{t/m}^3$ (or **1.025 tonnes per cubic metre**)
- **Fresh-water density:** $\rho_{fw} = 1.000 \; \text{t/m}^3$
- **Standard acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$
- **Standard IMO minimum initial GM:** $GM \geq 0.150 \; \text{m}$ for passenger & cargo ships
- **IMO minimum GZ at 30°:** $GZ_{30°} \geq 0.200 \; \text{m}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Twelve Principal Linear Dimensions

1. **Length Overall (LOA)** — Extreme forward to extreme aft, including any projections (bulbous bow, stern overhang).
2. **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP or LPP)** — Measured between the forward perpendicular (FP, usually through the design waterline at the stem) and the aft perpendicular (AP, usually through the rudder stock line).
3. **Length on Waterline (LWL)** — Length of the design waterline, used for speed/power prediction via Froude number $F_n = V/\sqrt{gL_{WL}}$.
4. **Beam or Breadth (B)** — Maximum breadth over the hull shell, typically amidships.
5. **Moulded Depth (D)** — Vertical distance from the **moulded baseline** (inside-shell line at keel) to the **moulded deck line** at the side.
6. **Draft Forward ($T_F$) and Draft Aft ($T_A$)** — Vertical distance from the waterline to the keel at FP and AP respectively.
7. **Mean Draft ($T_M$)** — Arithmetic mean: $T_M = (T_F + T_A)/2$.
8. **Trim** — Difference $T_A - T_F$ (positive = trimmed by stern).
9. **Freeboard (F)** — Distance from the design waterline to the **uppermost continuous deck** at the side. Governed by the **International Load Line Convention (1966)**.
10. **Air Draft** — Distance from waterline to highest fixed point (important for canal passages — Panama Canal max **57.91 m**).
11. **Camber** — Transverse rise of the deck from side to centerline.
12. **Sheer** — Longitudinal rise of the deck from amidships toward FP and AP.

### 2.2 Form Coefficients — The "Shape Numbers"

A ship is not a rectangular box. The **form coefficients** quantify how much volume of the box is actually filled by hull volume.

- **Block Coefficient $C_B$** — Ratio of displacement volume to length × breadth × draft box. A full-form tanker has $C_B \approx 0.80\text{–}0.85$; a fine warship has $C_B \approx 0.45\text{–}0.55$.
- **Midship Coefficient $C_M$** — Ratio of immersed cross-sectional area amidships to $B \cdot T$. Typically **0.95–0.99**.
- **Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$** — Ratio of displacement volume to midship area × length. $C_P = C_B/C_M$. Indicates longitudinal fullness.
- **Waterplane Area Coefficient $C_{WP}$** — Ratio of waterplane area $A_W$ to $L \cdot B$. Typically **0.70–0.85**.
- **Vertical Prismatic Coefficient $C_{VP}$** — $C_{VP} = C_B / C_{WP}$, indicates vertical distribution.

### 2.3 Tonnage and Displacement

- **Displacement (∇ or Δ):** Mass of water displaced by the hull. $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$. Often given in **tonnes (t)**.
- **TPC (Tonnes per Centimetre Immersion):** Mass required to increase draft by **1 cm** in salt water.
- **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT):** $\text{DWT} = \Delta_{loaded} - \Delta_{lightship}$.
- **Lightweight (LS):** Mass of the empty ship (hull, machinery, outfit).
- **Gross/Net Tonnage (GT/NT):** Regulatory, non-dimensional measure under **IMO 1969 Tonnage Convention**.

### 2.4 Theory of Transverse Stability — Conceptual Steps

The transverse stability of a ship is described by the interplay of three key points:

1. **Center of Gravity (G)** — Point through which the weight W acts vertically downward.
2. **Center of Buoyancy (B)** — Centroid of the submerged volume; point through which the buoyancy force $\Delta g$ acts vertically upward.
3. **Metacenter (M)** — Point of intersection of the vertical through the *new* center of buoyancy $B_1$ with the original vertical centerline. For small angles, M is (approximately) **fixed**.

The sequence of analysis is:

- **Step 1:** Ship upright → W (down) and $\Delta g$ (up) act along the same vertical through G = B. No moment.
- **Step 2:** External heel moment applied → ship rolls to angle $\phi$.
- **Step 3:** Hull form below water changes → $B$ shifts to $B_1$ along a curve called the **curve of buoyancy (B-curve)**.
- **Step 4:** Vertical through $B_1$ meets original centerline at $M$.
- **Step 5:** $G$ remains (assuming rigid body and no free surfaces) → distance $GZ$ is the **righting lever**.
- **Step 6:** **Righting moment** $RM = W \cdot GZ$; if $RM >$ heeling moment → ship returns upright.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities in SI units. Lengths in metres, masses in tonnes, forces in kN, angles in radians (or degrees — convert as required).

#### A. Hull Form Coefficients

| Formula | Description | Boundary / Range |
|---|---|---|
| $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Block coefficient | $0.45 \leq C_B \leq 0.85$ |
| $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | Midship coefficient | $0.92 \leq C_M \leq 0.99$ |
| $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{BP}} = \dfrac{C_B}{C_M}$ | Prismatic coefficient | $0.55 \leq C_P \leq 0.85$ |
| $C_{WP} = \dfrac{A_W}{L_{BP} \cdot B}$ | Waterplane area coefficient | $0.70 \leq C_{WP} \leq 0.85$ |
| $C_{VP} = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_W \cdot T} = \dfrac{C_B}{C_{WP}}$ | Vertical prismatic coefficient | $0.65 \leq C_{VP} \leq 0.95$ |

#### B. Hydrostatic Quantities

| Formula | Description |
|---|---|
| $\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | Displacement (weight) |
| $\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$ | Displaced volume from block coefficient |
| $\text{TPC} = \dfrac{A_W \cdot \rho_{sw}}{100}$ | Mass per cm immersion |
| $\text{MCT}_{1cm} = \dfrac{W \cdot \overline{GM}_L}{100 \cdot L_{BP}}$ | Moment to change trim by 1 cm |

#### C. Transverse Stability (Small Angle)

| Formula | Description |
|---|---|
| $BM_T = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}$ | Transverse BM (metacentre above B) |
| $I_T = \dfrac{L_{BP} \cdot B^3}{12}$ | 2nd moment of waterplane (approx.) |
| $KB = f(\nabla, T, C_B)$ | Height of B above keel — from hydrostatics |
| $KG$ | Height of G above keel — from weight distribution |
| $GM_T = KB + BM_T - KG$ | **Initial transverse metacentric height** |
| $GZ \approx GM_T \cdot \sin\phi$ | Righting lever at small angle $\phi$ |
| $RM = \Delta g \cdot GZ$ | Righting moment |

#### D. Transverse Stability (Large Angle)

| Formula | Description |
|---|---|
| $GZ = KB_1\sin\phi - KG\sin\phi + \text{(horizontal shift of }B_1)$ | General righting lever |
| $\text{Free-surface correction: } GG' = \dfrac{i_{L}}{\nabla}$ | Virtual rise of G due to slack tank |
| $GM_{eff} = GM_{solid} - GG'$ | Effective metacentric height |

#### E. Heeling Moment — Common Cases

| Cause | Heeling Moment |
|---|---|
| Wind heeling (IMO) | $HM = P \cdot A \cdot z$ |
| Turning heeling | $HM = \dfrac{\Delta \cdot v^2}{g \cdot R} \cdot \overline{KG}$ |
| Weight shift $w$ across breadth $b$ | $HM = w \cdot b \cdot \cos\phi$ |
| Crowding of passengers to one side | $HM = m_p \cdot y_{crowd} \cdot \cos\phi$ |

### 2.6 Why This Matters in Ship Conversion

In any **ship conversion** — lengthening, heightening, re-engining, conversion from tanker to bulker (OBO), FPSO conversion, PSV-to-AHTS conversion, FSO-to-FSRU, etc. — every change has a *stability shadow*:

- Adding length raises LBP → potentially alters BM and LCG.
- Adding deckhouses raises KG → reduces GM.
- Installing new cranes, helidecks, or LNG tanks alters weight and CoG.
- Re-engining may change weights both longitudinally and vertically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** *Never convert without first locking down the existing GM, KG, and GZ(φ) curve of the as-is ship. The "existing condition" is the only credible baseline against which delta-stability is computed.*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Initial Metacentric Height $GM = KB + BM - KG$

Consider a ship floating upright at design waterline. Three points lie on the centerline:

- **K** (keel): reference point on the baseline.
- **B**: center of buoyancy; vertical line $\ell_B$ passes through it.
- **G**: center of gravity; vertical line $\ell_G$ passes through it.
- Initially $\ell_B$ and $\ell_G$ coincide → ship is in static equilibrium.

A small external heeling moment $\delta M$ tilts the ship by a small angle $\phi$.

**Step 1 — Identify the shifted buoyancy center $B_1$.**

When the hull heels by $\phi$, the previously symmetric submerged wedge on one side is replaced by an asymmetric wedge on the other. The center of buoyancy moves along the **buoyancy curve (B-curve)** to a new position $B_1$.

**Step 2 — Locate the metacenter $M$.**

By definition, draw a vertical through $B_1$ (the new line of action of the buoyancy force). Extend this vertical upward until it intersects the original vertical centerline $\ell_B$. The intersection point is, by definition, the **metacenter $M$**.

**Step 3 — Apply the small-angle (Wallis) approximation.**

For sufficiently small $\phi$, $M$ is independent of $\phi$ and the B-curve may be replaced by a circular arc of radius $BM$ centered at $M$.

**Step 4 — Relate $BM$ to waterplane inertia.**

The transverse shift of the buoyancy center is caused by the volume of the emerged wedge on the high side minus the immersed wedge on the low side. Performing the integration yields

$$
BM_T = \frac{I_T}{\nabla}
$$

where $I_T$ is the **transverse second moment of area** of the waterplane about the longitudinal centerline plane. For an idealised rectangular waterplane:

$$
I_T = \frac{L_{BP} \cdot B^3}{12}
$$

**Step 5 — Geometric relation along the centerline.**

Measuring heights from the keel $K$:

$$
KB = \text{(known from hydrostatic tables)}
$$

The metacentre lies at $KB + BM$ above the keel. The CoG lies at $KG$ above the keel. The difference is the **metacentric height**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
GM &= KM - KG \\
&= (KB + BM) - KG \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{GM_T = KB + BM_T - KG}
$$

**Physical interpretation:**

- $KB$ depends on hull shape and draft (from hydrostatics).
- $BM$ depends on waterplane inertia and displacement — *large beam and shallow draft* increase $BM$.
- $KG$ is a *weight-distribution* quantity — *high CoG reduces* $GM$.

This is the **single most important stability parameter** in naval architecture.

### 3.2 Derivation: Righting Lever $GZ$ at Small Angles

For a ship inclined at a small angle $\phi$:

**Step 1 — Drop perpendicular from G to the new vertical through $B_1$.**

The horizontal distance from $G$ to the vertical line $\ell_{B_1}$ is, by definition, the righting lever $GZ$.

**Step 2 — Use the geometry of triangle $G M Z$.**

In triangle $GMZ$ (right-angled at $Z$), the hypotenuse is $GM$ and the angle at $M$ equals the heel angle $\phi$ (because $MZ$ is perpendicular to the *new* vertical, while $MG$ lies along the *old* vertical; both verticals differ by exactly $\phi$).

$$
\sin\phi = \frac{GZ}{GM}
$$

$$
\boxed{GZ = GM \cdot \sin\phi}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the righting moment.**

The weight $W = \Delta g$ acts downward at $G$. The buoyancy force $\Delta g$ acts upward along $\ell_{B_1}$. The couple arm is $GZ$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
RM &= W \cdot GZ \\
&= \Delta g \cdot GM \cdot \sin\phi
\end{aligned}
$$

For small angles $\sin\phi \approx \phi$ (radians), therefore

$$
RM \approx \Delta g \cdot GM \cdot \phi
$$

**Validity limit:** The above is reliable only up to about $\phi \leq 10°\text{–}15°$. Beyond this, $M$ migrates, the B-curve becomes non-circular, and the **large-angle $GZ$ formula** must be used.

### 3.3 Derivation: Large-Angle $GZ$ via Wall-Froude-Siemens Method

For angles beyond $\sim 15°$, the righting lever is obtained rigorously from

$$
GZ = KB_1 \sin\phi - KG \sin\phi + \overline{BB_1}\cos\phi
$$

where $KB_1\sin\phi$ is the vertical component and $\overline{BB_1}\cos\phi$ is the horizontal component of the shift in buoyancy centroid.

In practice, naval architects read $KB_1$ and $\overline{BB_1}$ directly from the **Bonjean or hydrostatic curves** at successive angles $\phi$ and tabulate $GZ$. The resulting curve — **Cross Curves of Stability (CCS)** — is then entered with the vessel's displacement to read $GZ(\phi)$.

The IMO stability criteria are evaluated on this *true* $GZ$ curve, not the small-angle approximation.

### 3.4 Derivation: Free-Surface Effect on $GM$

A slack (partially filled) tank containing liquid of density $\rho_L$ and free surface moment of inertia $i$ acts as a *virtual* rise of $G$:

**Step 1 — Identify the shift of liquid centroid.**

When the ship heels, the liquid surface remains horizontal; the liquid sloshes. The centroid of the liquid mass shifts across the tank by a distance equal to half the transverse shift of the tank centerline.

**Step 2 — Apply moment equivalence.**

The shift $w \cdot y_{shift}$ of liquid weight produces a heeling couple. Equating to the virtual rise of $G$:

$$
GG' \cdot \Delta = i \cdot \rho_L \cdot g
$$

Dividing through by $\Delta g$:

$$
\boxed{GG' = \frac{i \cdot \rho_L}{\Delta}}
$$

**Step 3 — Corrected metacentric height.**

$$
GM_{eff} = GM_{solid} - \sum_{slack \; tanks} \frac{i_n \cdot \rho_{L,n}}{\Delta}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Free-surface effect is the **single most common cause of stability failure in conversion projects** because new tanks, slop tanks, or partially-filled service tanks may be added without proper cofferdam or filling arrangements.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — IMO A.749 Intact Stability Criteria

| Criterion # | IMO Requirement | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Area under GZ curve from $0°\text{ to }30°$ $\geq 0.055$ m·rad | Sufficient early-stage righting energy |
| 2 | Area under GZ curve from $0°\text{ to }40°$ $\geq 0.090$ m·rad | Sufficient mid-angle righting energy |
| 3 | Area between $30°\text{ and }40°$ $\geq 0.030$ m·rad | Resistance to sustained beam wind |
| 4 | Righting lever $GZ_{max} \geq 0.200$ m at angle $\leq 30°$ | Avoid late peak |
| 5 | Angle of maximum GZ $\geq 30°$ | Peak occurs at sufficient angle |
| 6 | Initial $GM \geq 0.150$ m | Basic stiffness |
| 7 | Weather criterion (severe wind + rolling) | Synergistic wind-wave check |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

```mermaid
    root1 --> dim1
    root1 --> dim2
    root1 --> dim3
    root1 --> dim4
    root1 --> dim5
    root1 --> dim6
    root1 --> dim7
    root1 --> dim8

    root2 --> fc1
    root2 --> fc2
    root2 --> fc3
    root2 --> fc4

    root3 --> mq1
    root3 --> mq2
    root3 --> mq3
    root3 --> mq4

    root4 --> sb1
    root4 --> sb2
    root4 --> sb3
    root4 --> sb4
    root4 --> sb5
    root4 --> sb6

    fc1 -. "CB = CB formula" .- mq1
    sb4 -. "GM = KB + BM minus KG" .- sb1
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Stability Functional Architecture (Heel Mechanism)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    heelCause["External Heeling Moment\nWind / Turn / Shift / Crowding"]
    heelPhi["Ship Heels by Angle phi"]
    bShift["B shifts along B curve to B1"]
    mLocate["Vertical through B1 meets centerline at M"]
    gzForm["Righting Lever GZ forms"]
    rmOut["Righting Moment RM = W times GZ"]
    compare{"RM greater than HM?"}
    upright["Ship Returns Upright\nSTABLE"]
    capsize["Ship Capsizes\nUNSTABLE"]

    heelCause --> heelPhi
    heelPhi --> bShift
    bShift --> mLocate
    mLocate --> gzForm
    gzForm --> rmOut
    rmOut --> compare
    compare -- Yes --> upright
    compare -- No --> capsize
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Existing Stability Condition in Ship Conversion Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startA["As Is Ship Baseline"]
    inclining["Inclining Test"]
    weightBook["Stability Booklet\nUpdated to Existing Condition"]
    gmExisting["GM of Existing Ship"]
    gzExisting["GZ phi Curve Existing"]
    modList["List of Conversion Modifications"]
    deltaKG["Compute delta KG"]
    deltaGM["Compute delta GM"]
    deltaGZ["Compute new GZ phi"]
    checkIMO["Verify against IMO A.749"]
    approval["Classification + Flag Approval"]
    seaTrial["Sea Trial inclining if required"]

    startA --> inclining
    inclining --> weightBook
    weightBook --> gmExisting
    weightBook --> gzExisting
    modList --> deltaKG
    deltaKG --> deltaGM
    deltaGM --> deltaGZ
    gmExisting --> deltaGZ
    gzExisting --> deltaGZ
    deltaGZ --> checkIMO
    checkIMO -- Pass --> approval
    checkIMO -- Fail --> modList
    approval --> seaTrial
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Concept Map — Stability Triangle Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ptK["K Keel Reference"]
    ptB["B Center of Buoyancy"]
    ptG["G Center of Gravity"]
    ptM["M Metacenter"]
    ptZ["Z Foot of Righting Lever"]
    ptB1["B1 Shifted Buoyancy Center"]

    ptK --> ptB
    ptB --> ptM
    ptM --> ptG
    ptG --> ptZ
    ptZ --> ptB1

    ptK -. "KB above keel" .- ptB
    ptB -. "BM above B" .- ptM
    ptM -. "GM above G" .- ptG
    ptK -. "KG above keel" .- ptG
    ptG -. "GZ horizontal arm" .- ptZ
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The four collinear points along the centerline are **K → B → M → G** (in any order depending on GM sign). The horizontal arm **GZ** closes the righting couple. Note the difference between **GZ** (the righting lever, perpendicular distance) and **GM** (the longitudinal segment on the centerline).

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **Define the following with reference to a ship's hull form: (i) Block Coefficient, (ii) Prismatic Coefficient, (iii) Waterplane Area Coefficient. State one typical range for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Block Coefficient ($C_B$):** Ratio of displacement volume $\nabla$ to the product $L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$.
$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP}\cdot B\cdot T}$$
Typical range: **0.55–0.85** (tankers high, fine vessels low). *(1 mark)*

- **Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$):** Ratio of displacement volume to the product of midship area $A_M$ and length $L_{BP}$. Indicates longitudinal fullness.
$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{BP}} = \frac{C_B}{C_M}$$
Typical range: **0.55–0.85**. *(1 mark)*

- **Waterplane Area Coefficient ($C_{WP}$):** Ratio of waterplane area $A_W$ to $L_{BP} \cdot B$. Typical range: **0.70–0.85**. *(1 mark)*

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **What is the significance of the metacentric height (GM) in ship stability? State the IMO minimum permissible value of initial GM for a general cargo ship.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Significance (2 marks):** $GM$ quantifies the *initial transverse stability* of a floating ship. It is the lever arm about which the righting couple acts at small heel angles. A larger $GM$ produces (a) a larger righting moment $RM = \Delta g \cdot GM \cdot \sin\phi$, (b) a stiffer roll motion with shorter natural roll period $T = 2\pi k / \sqrt{GM}$, and (c) higher resistance to upsetting heeling moments such as beam wind or shift of cargo. Too small $GM$ leads to sluggish, un-stiff behaviour and risk of capsize; too large $GM$ leads to a stiff, uncomfortable ship with high acceleration on deck.

- **IMO minimum:** Initial $GM \geq \mathbf{0.150 \; m}$ for intact transverse stability per **Resolution A.749(18)**, Code on Intact Stability for All Types of Ships Covered by IMO Instruments. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) Discuss the principal geometric parameters and form coefficients used to describe a ship's hull. With neat sketches, explain the difference between Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP), Length on Waterline (LWL), and Length Overall (LOA).** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The principal geometric parameters of a ship fall into three families:

**(i) Linear Dimensions (3 marks):**

- **LOA (Length Overall):** Maximum longitudinal extent from the foremost point of the bow (including bulbous bow if any) to the aftermost point of the stern (including any projections like propeller brackets, stern ramps, or rudder horns).
- **LBP (Length Between Perpendiculars):** Measured between the forward perpendicular (FP — usually the vertical line through the design waterline at the stem) and the aft perpendicular (AP — usually the vertical line through the rudder stock, or the midship point of the rudder). LBP is the **design length** used in hull-form coefficient calculations and in Froude-number-based resistance estimates.
- **LWL (Length on Waterline):** Length of the design waterline measured at the actual operating draft. Since most ships operate at a draft near but not equal to design draft, $L_{WL}$ differs slightly from $L_{BP}$.
- **Beam (B):** Maximum moulded breadth, usually at the midship section.
- **Depth (D):** Vertical distance from the moulded baseline (interior keel line) to the moulded deck line at the side, amidships.
- **Draft (T):** Vertical distance from the keel to the waterline; mean draft $T_M = (T_F + T_A)/2$.
- **Freeboard:** Distance from the load waterline to the uppermost continuous deck at the side.

**(ii) Form Coefficients (3 marks):**

- **Block Coefficient $C_B$:** $C_B = \nabla / (L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T)$. Fullness of the hull as a fraction of its bounding box.
- **Midship Coefficient $C_M$:** $C_M = A_M / (B \cdot T)$. Section fullness amidships.
- **Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$:** $C_P = C_B / C_M$. Longitudinal distribution of volume.
- **Waterplane Coefficient $C_{WP}$:** $C_{WP} = A_W / (L_{BP} \cdot B)$. Fullness of waterplane.
- **Vertical Prismatic $C_{VP}$:** $C_{VP} = C_B / C_{WP}$.

**(iii) Mass & Tonnage (1 mark):**

- Displacement $\Delta = \rho \nabla$, DWT, Lightweight, TPC $= A_W \cdot \rho / 100$.

**Sketch description (no marks, but essential):** A profile view of a ship showing FP, AP, LOA, LBP, LWL overlaid, plus a body plan showing $C_M$ as the shaded midship area inside the bounding rectangle $B \times T$.

---

**(b) Explain the concept of transverse metacentric height. Derive the expression $GM = KB + BM - KG$. State the assumption under which the derivation is valid.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (2 marks):**

Transverse metacentric height $GM$ is the *vertical distance from the ship's center of gravity $G$ to the transverse metacenter $M$*. It is the *initial stiffness parameter* of the ship. The metacenter $M$ is defined as the point of intersection of the vertical line through the *new* center of buoyancy (when the ship is inclined at a small angle $\phi$) with the original vertical centerline of the ship.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Let the ship be floating upright. Apply a small heeling moment so the ship tilts to angle $\phi$. The center of buoyancy shifts from $B$ to $B_1$. The vertical through $B_1$ meets the original vertical through $B$ at the metacenter $M$.

For small $\phi$, $M$ is independent of $\phi$ and $BM$ is constant:

$$
BM_T = \frac{I_T}{\nabla}
$$

where $I_T$ is the second moment of the waterplane area about the centerline.

Measuring vertically from the keel $K$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
KM &= KB + BM \\
KG &= \text{(known from weight distribution)} \\
\therefore \quad GM &= KM - KG \\
&= KB + BM - KG
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assumption (1 mark):**

The derivation is valid for **small angles of heel only (typically $\phi \leq 10°\text{–}15°$)**, where:
- The metacenter $M$ remains effectively stationary,
- The B-curve is approximated by a circular arc,
- $GZ \approx GM \sin\phi$ holds.

For larger angles, $GZ$ must be obtained from cross-curves of stability.

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) With the help of a free-body diagram, describe the components of intact ship stability. Derive the small-angle righting moment expression.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Free-body diagram description (2 marks):**

For a ship heeled to angle $\phi$:

- Weight $W = \Delta g$ acts **downward** through the center of gravity $G$.
- Buoyancy force $\rho g \nabla$ acts **upward** through the (new) center of buoyancy $B_1$, along the vertical line through $B_1$.
- The metacenter $M$ is the intersection of this new vertical with the original vertical through $B$.
- The horizontal distance from $G$ to the line of action of buoyancy is the **righting lever $GZ$**.

**Derivation of righting moment (4 marks):**

The two equal and opposite forces ($\Delta g$ up at $B_1$, $\Delta g$ down at $G$) form a **couple**. The arm of this couple is $GZ$.

$$
RM = W \cdot GZ = \Delta g \cdot GZ
$$

For small angles of heel (Wallis approximation):

$$
\sin\phi = \frac{GZ}{GM}
$$

Hence:

$$
GZ = GM \cdot \sin\phi
$$

And the **righting moment** becomes:

$$
RM = \Delta g \cdot GM \cdot \sin\phi
$$

For small $\phi$, $\sin\phi \approx \phi$ (radians), so:

$$
\boxed{RM \approx \Delta g \cdot GM \cdot \phi}
$$

**Physical interpretation (1 mark):** For a ship to be in stable equilibrium at angle $\phi$, the righting moment $RM$ must exceed the applied heeling moment $HM$. If $RM < HM$ at any angle before the *down-flooding angle*, the ship capsizes.

---

**(b) State and explain any four of the IMO Intact Stability Code (Resolution A.749(18)) criteria for general cargo ships.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

> **Background (1 mark):** *Resolution A.749(18)* is the IMO Code on Intact Stability for All Types of Ships Covered by IMO Instruments. It consolidates the intact stability recommendations previously distributed across numerous resolutions and defines seven mandatory + supplementary criteria. The seven primary criteria are listed below; any four are explained.

### **Criterion 1 — Initial GM**

$$
GM_{initial} \geq 0.150 \; \text{m}
$$

Ensures that the ship is not excessively tender. *(1.5 marks)*

### **Criterion 2 — Area under GZ curve from 0° to 30°**

$$
A_{0\to 30} = \int_{0}^{30°} GZ \, d\phi \geq 0.055 \; \text{m·rad}
$$

This represents the **work done by the righting moment** over the first 30° of heel. Guarantees sufficient *early* righting energy to counter sudden heeling forces such as a gust of beam wind. *(1.5 marks)*

### **Criterion 3 — Area under GZ curve from 0° to 40°**

$$
A_{0\to 40} \geq 0.090 \; \text{m·rad}
$$

Ensures the ship has enough total righting energy over the mid-heel range to recover from larger disturbances. *(1.5 marks)*

### **Criterion 4 — Area between 30° and 40°**

$$
A_{30\to 40} \geq 0.030 \; \text{m·rad}
$$

This intermediate-area check ensures that the ship is not just stable initially but **maintains positive righting capability** through the critical 30°–40° band. A ship with a high early peak and rapid decay would fail this criterion. *(1.5 marks)*

### **Criterion 5 — Maximum GZ and Angle of Maximum GZ**

$$
GZ_{max} \geq 0.200 \; \text{m} \quad \text{at} \quad \phi_{max} \geq 30°
$$

Ensures that the peak righting lever is both sufficiently large *and* occurs at a sufficiently large angle, providing a wide range of stable equilibrium. *(Bonus mark if mentioned)*

### **Criterion 6 — Weather Criterion**

A supplementary check combining beam wind pressure and rolling. The ship must remain stable under simultaneous action of a design beam wind and wave-induced roll. *(Bonus mark if mentioned)*

### **Criterion 7 — Angle of Vanishing Stability**

The angle at which $GZ$ falls to zero must exceed the *down-flooding angle* and the *angle of any opening that cannot be closed weathertight*. *(Bonus mark if mentioned)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the small-angle assumption** when writing $GZ = GM\sin\phi$: Examiners routinely deduct 1–2 marks if the student does not explicitly state that the relation holds only for $\phi \leq 10°\text{–}15°$.
> 2. **Confusing $GM$ with $GZ$:** $GM$ is a length along the *vertical centerline*; $GZ$ is a *perpendicular distance* between $G$ and the buoyancy line of action. They are equal only when $\phi = 90°$.
> 3. **Mixing up LBP, LOA, LWL:** Always state the reference (perpendiculars / waterline / extreme). Failure to do so loses 0.5–1 mark in part (a) of 7-mark questions.
> 4. **Omitting units in formula sheets:** All formulas must be quoted with units, especially in numerical sub-questions.
> 5. **Forgetting IMO numerical thresholds:** The exact numbers (0.055 m·rad, 0.090 m·rad, 0.030 m·rad, 0.200 m, 0.150 m, 30°, 40°) are *expected verbatim*. Approximate values lose marks.
> 6. **Ignoring free-surface effect:** If a question mentions a partially-filled tank, the $GG' = i\rho_L/\Delta$ correction is **mandatory**.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and revise on the morning of the exam.**

- ✅ **Principal dimensions:** LOA, LBP, LWL, B, D, T (forward/aft/mean), freeboard, trim.
- ✅ **Form coefficients:** $C_B$, $C_M$, $C_P = C_B/C_M$, $C_{WP}$, $C_{VP} = C_B/C_{WP}$.
- ✅ **Tonnage:** Displacement $\Delta = \rho \nabla$, DWT, GT/NT (regulatory), TPC $= A_W \rho / 100$.
- ✅ **Stability points:** $G$ (CoG), $B$ (CoB), $K$ (keel), $M$ (metacenter), $B_1$ (shifted CoB).
- ✅ **Small-angle relations:** $BM = I_T / \nabla$, $GM = KB + BM - KG$, $GZ = GM \sin\phi$, $RM = \Delta g \cdot GZ$.
- ✅ **Large-angle:** $GZ$ obtained from cross-curves of stability (Wall-Froude-Siemens).
- ✅ **Free surface:** $GG' = i\rho_L / \Delta$, reduce $GM$ by $GG'$.
- ✅ **IMO A.749(18) criteria:** Six criteria — remember the numbers 0.150 m, 0.055, 0.090, 0.030 (m·rad), 0.200 m, 30°, 40°.
- ✅ **Ship conversion rule:** Always lock the *existing* stability baseline first; recompute after every modification.
- ✅ **Conversion context:** Adding mass aloft (deckhouses, cranes) raises $KG$ → reduces $GM$. Adding breadth or waterplane area increases $BM$ → raises $GM$.
- ✅ **Natural roll period:** $T = 2\pi k/\sqrt{GM}$ — larger $GM$ → shorter, stiffer roll.
- ✅ **Heeling sources:** Wind, turning, weight shift, passenger crowding, free-surface slack tanks.
- ✅ **Stability booklet:** Mandatory on every ship under SOLAS; updated after every modification.
- ✅ **Inclining test:** Standard method to determine lightweight $KG$ — performed in sheltered waters with known weights shifted across the deck.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** When a Part B sub-question asks for a *derivation*, mark allocation is roughly **1 mark for stating the assumption**, **4 marks for the step-by-step algebra**, **1 mark for the boxed final result**, and **1 mark for the physical interpretation**. Structure your answer accordingly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principal Particulars of the Ship
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principal Particulars of the Ship

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Principal Particulars** of a ship constitute the fundamental set of dimensions, capacities, and performance parameters that uniquely define the size, form, capacity, and operational capability of a vessel. These particulars form the foundational design baseline from which all hull form coefficients, hydrostatic calculations, stability assessments, powering estimations, and conversion feasibility analyses are derived.

In the context of **Ship Conversion Technology**, the principal particulars serve as the *baseline dataset* against which any proposed modification (lengthening, beam insertion, deck addition, or superstructure alteration) is measured and validated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Principal Particulars are the FIRST design step in any ship conversion project. Every conversion study begins by establishing the *existing* vessel's principal particulars before proposing dimensional or capacity changes.

### Classification of Principal Particulars

The principal particulars of a ship are broadly grouped into four families:

| Category | Parameters Included | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Dimensional Particulars** | LOA, LBP, LWL, Beam (B), Depth (D), Draft (T) | Defines the geometric envelope |
| **Capacity Particulars** | Displacement (Δ), Deadweight (DWT), Tonnages (GT/NT) | Defines payload & earning capacity |
| **Form Coefficients** | Cb, Cp, Cm, Cwp | Defines hull shape efficiency |
| **Performance Particulars** | Service Speed (Vs), Range, Complement | Defines operational envelope |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a ship's principal particulars as a **human being's medical ID card**:
- **Dimensions** = Height, Arm Length, Leg Length (the physical body)
- **Capacity** = Lung Capacity, Weight-Bearing Limit (what it can carry)
- **Form Coefficients** = Body Shape Index (how "stocky" or "slim" — like BMI)
- **Performance** = Running Speed, Stamina (how it performs)

Just as a doctor cannot prescribe treatment without knowing your vitals, a naval architect **cannot initiate any conversion** without first tabulating the vessel's principal particulars.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Term:** In shipyards worldwide, these particulars are compiled into a document called the *"Particulars Sheet"* or *"Datasheet"* — the single most circulated document between Owner, Class Society, Designer, and Yard.

### List of Standard Principal Particulars (Canonical Set)

1. **Length Overall (LOA)** — `m`
2. **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP / Lpp)** — `m`
3. **Length on Waterline (LWL)** — `m`
4. **Moulded Breadth (B)** — `m`
5. **Moulded Depth (D)** — `m`
6. **Design Draft (T)** — `m`
7. **Displacement (Δ)** — `tonnes (t)`
8. **Deadweight (DWT)** — `tonnes (t)`
9. **Gross Tonnage (GT)** — `unitless (dimensionless)`
10. **Net Tonnage (NT)** — `unitless (dimensionless)`
11. **Block Coefficient (Cb)** — `dimensionless`
12. **Service Speed (Vs)** — `knots`
13. **Range / Endurance** — `nautical miles`
14. **Main Engine Power (MCR)** — `kW`
15. **Complement** — `persons`
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Dimensional Particulars — Definition & Logic

#### Length Overall (LOA)
- **Definition:** The maximum length of the ship measured parallel to the design waterline, from the foremost point of the bow (or bowsprit if fitted) to the aftermost point of the stern (or transom).
- **Significance:** Determines berth length, drydock size, canal transit feasibility (e.g., Panama Canal lock is ~**320 m**), and towage requirements.

#### Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP / Lpp)
- **Definition:** The length measured between two vertical reference lines:
  - **Forward Perpendicular (FP):** Vertical line at the point where the design waterline meets the foreside of the stem.
  - **After Perpendicular (AP):** Vertical line passing through the rudder stock centerline (for conventional ships) or the centerline of the sternpost.
- **Significance:** LBP is the **hydrodynamic reference length** used in resistance, powering, and hull coefficient calculations. It is the *true* working length of the hull.

#### Length on Waterline (LWL)
- **Definition:** Length of the ship's waterline plane measured at the design draft.
- **Logic:** At any given trim, LWL differs from LBP. LWL > LBP when the ship has a raked bow/stern.

#### Beam (Breadth) — B
- **Definition:**
  - **Moulded Breadth (B):** Maximum breadth measured at the midship section, inside the shell plating.
  - **Extreme Breadth (Bext):** Maximum breadth measured over the shell plating (including fenders, etc.).
- **Significance:** Determines port crane outreach, canal/Panamax width restrictions (e.g., **Panamax beam ≤ 32.31 m**), and transverse stability.

#### Depth — D
- **Definition:** The vertical distance from the **moulded baseline** (keel plate inner surface) to the **moulded deck line** at the side, measured at the midship section.
- **Significance:** Governs the double-bottom height, number of decks, freeboard computation, and longitudinal strength (section modulus scales as **D²**).

#### Draft — T
- **Definition:** The vertical distance from the waterline to the keel (moulded baseline) at the midship section.
- **Types:**
  - **Design Draft (Td):** The draft at which the ship is intended to operate at full Deadweight.
  - **Scantling Draft:** The maximum allowable structural draft.
  - **Light Ship Draft:** The draft of the vessel completely unloaded.
- **Significance:** Determines port depth requirements and channel accessibility.

#### Air Draft
- **Definition:** Vertical distance from the waterline to the highest fixed point (mast, funnel, or king post).
- **Significance:** Critical for vessels transiting bridges and archipelagos (e.g., **Bosphorus Bridge = 64 m air draft limit**).

### 2.2 Capacity Particulars

#### Displacement (Δ)
- **Definition:** The weight of water displaced by the ship's submerged volume, equal to the ship's total weight (Archimedes' Principle).
- **Equation:**

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \cdot \nabla \quad \text{(for seawater at T = 4°C)}$$

where **ρ** = water density = **1.025 t/m³** (standard seawater) and **∇** = displaced volume in m³.

- **Note:** **1.025 t/m³** is the KTU-standard density. For freshwater: **ρ = 1.000 t/m³**.

#### Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)
- **Definition:** The total weight a ship can carry when fully loaded — cargo + fuel + fresh water + ballast + stores + crew + passengers.
- **Relationship:**

$$DWT = \Delta_{loaded} - \Delta_{lightship}$$

#### Gross Tonnage (GT) & Net Tonnage (NT)
- **GT:** A dimensionless measure of the total internal volume of a ship (under the tonnage deck, in enclosed spaces). Adopted by **IMO ITC 1969** Convention.
- **NT:** Effective revenue-earning volume (GT minus non-revenue spaces like machinery, crew spaces, ballast tanks).
- **Note:** GT/NOT is **NOT** a measure of weight — it is a measure of *volume*. The old "tons" nomenclature is misleading.

### 2.3 Hull Form Coefficients (Form Ratios)

These dimensionless ratios characterize the "fullness" or "fineness" of the hull form. They are critical in **conversion feasibility studies** because changes in hull form directly affect resistance.

#### Block Coefficient (Cb)
The single most important hull form parameter in conversion studies.

$$C_b = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

- **Range:** 0.35 (very fine, fast container ship) to 0.90 (very full, tanker at loaded draft).

#### Prismatic Coefficient (Cp)
Ratio of displaced volume to the volume of a prism with midship cross-section extending LBP.

$$C_p = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{BP}} = \frac{C_b}{C_m}$$

#### Midship Coefficient (Cm)
Ratio of the actual midship cross-sectional area to the area of its circumscribing rectangle.

$$C_m = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$$

#### Waterplane Area Coefficient (Cwp)
Ratio of the actual waterplane area to the circumscribing rectangle on the waterline.

$$C_{wp} = \frac{A_{WP}}{L_{WL} \cdot B}$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Length Between Perp. | LBP | Design datum | m | 50 – 400 |
| 2 | Length Overall | LOA | ≈ 1.05 LBP (approx.) | m | — |
| 3 | Beam (Moulded) | B | Design datum | m | 8 – 60 |
| 4 | Depth (Moulded) | D | Design datum | m | 4 – 30 |
| 5 | Design Draft | T | Design datum | m | 3 – 22 |
| 6 | Freeboard | FB | D − T | m | — |
| 7 | Displacement | Δ | $\rho \cdot \nabla$ | t | 500 – 600,000 |
| 8 | Volume Displaced | ∇ | $C_b \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$ | m³ | — |
| 9 | Deadweight | DWT | $\Delta_{load} - \Delta_{light}$ | t | — |
| 10 | Block Coefficient | Cb | $\nabla / (L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T)$ | — | 0.35 – 0.90 |
| 11 | Prismatic Coefficient | Cp | $C_b / C_m$ | — | 0.55 – 0.85 |
| 12 | Midship Coefficient | Cm | $A_M / (B \cdot T)$ | — | 0.85 – 0.99 |
| 13 | Waterplane Coeff. | Cwp | $A_{WP} / (L_{WL} \cdot B)$ | — | 0.65 – 0.95 |
| 14 | Wetted Surface Area | S | $\approx 1.7 \cdot L_{BP} \cdot (T + B)$ | m² | — |
| 15 | Service Speed | Vs | Design datum | knots | 10 – 30 |
| 16 | Slenderness Ratio | L/B | — | — | 5 – 8 (tankers), 6 – 9 |
| 17 | Length–Beam Ratio | L/B | — | — | — |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Ship Conversion

In any conversion project — such as **PSV-to-AHT conversion**, **general cargo ship-to-bulk carrier**, **tanker-to-FSO**, or **ship-lengthening by inserting a midship block** — the principal particulars serve three concrete engineering roles:

1. **Baseline Establishment:** Capture existing dimensions and capacities for regulatory and class submissions.
2. **Delta Computation:** Calculate changes (ΔL, ΔB, ΔD, ΔT) and their effect on Cb, displacement, stability, and freeboard.
3. **Feasibility Validation:** Confirm that the modified vessel still meets **IMO**, **Classification Society** (DNV, LR, ABS), and **Port State** requirements.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Block Coefficient & Related Coefficients

We begin with the fundamental definition of displacement volume and progressively decompose the hull volume into product of base length × transverse areas.

**Step 1 — Definition of displaced volume ∇:**

$$\nabla = \text{Volume of submerged hull}$$

**Step 2 — Conceptual decomposition into a rectangular block:**

Imagine the ship as a rectangular box with length $L_{BP}$, breadth $B$, and depth $T$. Its volume is:

$$V_{block} = L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$$

**Step 3 — Definition of Block Coefficient:**

The block coefficient expresses the ratio of actual submerged volume to this bounding rectangular box:

$$C_b = \frac{\nabla}{V_{block}} = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

Rearranging for displacement volume:

$$\nabla = C_b \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$$

**Step 4 — Displacement:**

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla = \rho \cdot C_b \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$$

For seawater:

$$\Delta = 1.025 \cdot C_b \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \quad \text{(in tonnes)}$$

**Step 5 — Midship Coefficient derivation:**

At the midship section (station amidships), the cross-sectional area is $A_M$. The bounding rectangle at midship has area $B \cdot T$. Therefore:

$$C_m = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$$

Solving for $A_M$:

$$A_M = C_m \cdot B \cdot T$$

**Step 6 — Prismatic Coefficient derivation:**

If we extended the midship cross-section as a prism (a uniform cylinder of cross-section $A_M$ and length $L_{BP}$), its volume would be:

$$V_{prism} = A_M \cdot L_{BP}$$

The prismatic coefficient is the ratio of actual displaced volume to this prism:

$$C_p = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{BP}}$$

Substituting $A_M$:

$$C_p = \frac{\nabla}{C_m \cdot B \cdot T \cdot L_{BP}} = \frac{\nabla / (L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T)}{C_m} = \frac{C_b}{C_m}$$

Thus, the master relationship:

$$\boxed{C_b = C_p \cdot C_m}$$

**Step 7 — Waterplane Coefficient derivation:**

At the design waterline, the cross-sectional area is $A_{WP}$. The bounding rectangle on the waterline is $L_{WL} \cdot B$. Therefore:

$$C_{wp} = \frac{A_{WP}}{L_{WL} \cdot B}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

> **Problem:** A bulk carrier has the following particulars:
> - LBP = 150 m
> - B = 23 m
> - T = 9.5 m
> - Cb = 0.78
> - Cm = 0.97
> - ρ = 1.025 t/m³
>
> **Calculate:** (i) Displacement, (ii) Prismatic Coefficient, (iii) Volume displaced.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate volume displaced:**

$$\nabla = C_b \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$$

$$\nabla = 0.78 \cdot 150 \cdot 23 \cdot 9.5$$

$$\nabla = 0.78 \cdot 32{,}775 = 25{,}564.5 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Calculate displacement:**

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \cdot 25{,}564.5 = 26{,}203.6 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Prismatic Coefficient:**

$$C_p = \frac{C_b}{C_m} = \frac{0.78}{0.97} = 0.804$$

**Step 4 — Verify (optional):**

Compute $A_M = C_m \cdot B \cdot T = 0.97 \cdot 23 \cdot 9.5 = 211.945 \text{ m}^2$.

Check $C_p = \nabla / (A_M \cdot L_{BP}) = 25{,}564.5 / (211.945 \cdot 150) = 25{,}564.5 / 31{,}791.75 = 0.804$ ✓

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Conversion on Principal Particulars

This matrix is **examination-critical** for KTU Module 1 conversion problems:

| Conversion Type | Parameter Affected | Parameter Unchanged | New Cb Effect | Stability Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Midship Block Insertion** (lengthening) | LBP ↑, ∇ ↑, Δ ↑, DWT ↑ | B, D, T | Cb ↓ (finer form) | GM typically ↑ (if WL area grows) |
| **Beam Insertion** (widening) | B ↑, ∇ ↑, Δ ↑ | LBP, D | Cb ↑ (fuller form) | GM ↑ significantly |
| **Deck Addition** (heightening) | D ↑, ∇ ↑, Δ ↑, GT ↑ | LBP, B, T | Cb ↑ | Freeboard ↑; Co ↑ (CoG rises) |
| **Bow Bulb Addition** | LBP ↑, ∇ ↑ (slightly) | B, D, T | Minimal Cb change | Reduces resistance; may improve GM |
| **Conversion to Bulker** | Loading pattern changes | LBP, B, D | Cb at loaded ≈ same | Trim & GM must be re-verified |
| **Conversion to FSO/FSU** | T ↓ (permanent ballast), Δ ↑ | LBP, B, D | Cb ↑ | Stability critical — permanent ballast |

### 3.4 Derivation of Freeboard

Freeboard is the vertical distance from the design waterline to the **uppermost continuous deck** at the side:

$$FB = D - T$$

**Physical meaning:** FB represents the *reserve buoyancy volume* — the watertight hull volume above the waterline that provides safety against sinking due to overloading, wave action, or compartment flooding.

**KTU Key Fact:** Freeboard is regulated by the **IMO International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL 1966)**, and the assigned freeboard is computed from the *Load Line Regulations* based on ship length, type, and hull form.

### 3.5 Derivation of Wetted Surface Area (Approximation)

A widely used empirical approximation (used in KTU problems when full lines plan is unavailable):

$$S \approx 1.7 \cdot L_{BP} \cdot (T + B)$$

This is used in the **Holtrop-Mennen** and similar resistance estimation methods. A more refined form uses the actual lines plan integration:

$$S = \int_0^{L_{BP}} P(x) \, dx$$

where $P(x)$ is the girth length at station $x$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hull Form Coefficient Relationships (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Displaced Volume Nabla] --> B[Block Coefficient Cb]
    C[Bounding Box Volume] --> B
    B --> D["Cb = Nabla / (LBP x B x T)"]
    E[Midship Area Am] --> F[Midship Coefficient Cm]
    G[Rectangular Midship B x T] --> F
    F --> H["Cm = Am / (B x T)"]
    I[Prismatic Volume] --> J[Prismatic Coefficient Cp]
    A --> J
    F --> J
    J --> K["Cp = Cb / Cm"]
    L[Waterplane Area Awp] --> M[Waterplane Coefficient Cwp]
    N[Rectangular WL LWL x B] --> M
    M --> O["Cwp = Awp / (LWL x B)"]
    B --> P[Master Identity]
    F --> P
    K --> P
    P --> Q["Cb = Cp x Cm"]
```

### 4.2 Principal Particulars Block Architecture (Conversion Workflow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Start Conversion Project] --> P1[Compile Baseline Principal Particulars]
    P1 --> P2[Dimensional Group]
    P1 --> P3[Capacity Group]
    P1 --> P4[Form Coefficient Group]
    P1 --> P5[Performance Group]
    P2 --> P2a["LOA, LBP, LWL"]
    P2 --> P2b["Beam B, Depth D"]
    P2 --> P2c["Draft T, Freeboard FB"]
    P2 --> P2d["Air Draft AD"]
    P3 --> P3a["Displacement Delta"]
    P3 --> P3b["Deadweight DWT"]
    P3 --> P3c["Gross Tonnage GT"]
    P3 --> P3d["Net Tonnage NT"]
    P4 --> P4a["Block Coeff Cb"]
    P4 --> P4b["Prismatic Coeff Cp"]
    P4 --> P4c["Midship Coeff Cm"]
    P4 --> P4d["Waterplane Coeff Cwp"]
    P5 --> P5a["Service Speed Vs"]
    P5 --> P5b["Range Endurance"]
    P5 --> P5c["Main Engine MCR"]
    P5 --> P5d["Complement"]
    P2a --> Q[Apply Conversion Delta]
    P2b --> Q
    P2c --> Q
    P3a --> Q
    P3b --> Q
    P4a --> Q
    Q --> R[Recompute Hydrostatics]
    R --> S[Stability and Resistance Check]
    S --> T{Compliant?}
    T --> U[Submit to Class Society]
    T --> V[Revise Particulars]
    V --> P1
```

### 4.3 Ship Geometry — Coordinate System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Longitudinal["Longitudinal Direction X axis"]
        FA[Forward Perpendicular FP] --> SHIP[Hull LBP]
        SHIP --> AP[After Perpendicular AP]
    end
    subgraph Transverse["Transverse Direction Y axis"]
        PS[Port Side] --> MID[Midship B max]
        MID --> SB[Starboard Side]
    end
    subgraph Vertical["Vertical Direction Z axis"]
        BL[Baseline Moulded Keel] --> ML[Midship Cross Section]
        ML --> MS[Sheer Line Upper Deck]
        ML --> WL[Design Waterline at T]
    end
```

### 4.4 Schematic Description of Principal Particulars Locations

| Notation | Location | Reference Datum |
|---|---|---|
| LOA | Bow foremost point to stern aftermost point | Horizontal |
| LBP | FP to AP | Horizontal |
| B (Moulded) | Outer shell to outer shell at midship | Transverse |
| D (Moulded) | Baseline to moulded deck at side, midship | Vertical |
| T (Draft) | Baseline to waterline at midship | Vertical |
| FB (Freeboard) | Waterline to deck at side | Vertical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Limitation Note:** A precise scale drawing of the ship's principal dimensions cannot be rendered in text-based Mermaid. The flow diagrams above substitute the geometric relationships that KTU Module 1 emphasizes. In actual KTU examinations, students may be asked to **label the principal dimensions on a given ship profile drawing**, which requires memorizing the locations defined in the table above.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define *Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP)* and explain its significance as the hydrodynamic reference length in ship resistance estimation.

**Model Answer:**
LBP is the length of the hull measured between two vertical reference lines:
- **Forward Perpendicular (FP):** Vertical line through the intersection of the design waterline with the foreside of the stem.
- **After Perpendicular (AP):** Vertical line through the rudder stock centerline (or sternpost centerline).

LBP is chosen as the **hydrodynamic reference length** (rather than LOA) because it represents the actual working length of the underwater hull that generates resistance and produces the wave system. The resistance coefficients, Reynolds number, Froude number, and hull form coefficients (Cb, Cp) are all referenced to LBP — not LOA. Using LOA would give misleading resistance values, since LOA includes overhangs and appendages that do not contribute meaningfully to wave-making resistance.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for FP/AP, 1 mark for significance)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between *Displacement* and *Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)*. Why is GT not a measure of weight?

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | Displacement (Δ) | Deadweight (DWT) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Weight of water displaced by the ship | Total weight of cargo, fuel, water, stores, crew that the ship can carry |
| Symbol | Δ (tonnes) | DWT (tonnes) |
| Includes hull weight | YES | NO |
| Varies with loading | YES | NO (it's a capacity) |
| At lightship | = Lightship weight | = 0 |

**Why GT is not a measure of weight:**
GT (Gross Tonnage) is a **dimensionless volumetric measure** of the total internal capacity of the ship under the tonnage deck and in all enclosed spaces, expressed in units of 2.83 m³ each (per the IMO 1969 Tonnage Convention). It is a regulatory and port-fee measure, not a weight. A ship with high GT may have low DWT (e.g., passenger ship), and vice versa (e.g., tanker).

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks differentiation, 1.5 marks GT explanation)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Similar Pattern]

**Q. (a)** Define and explain the four principal hull form coefficients: Block Coefficient (Cb), Prismatic Coefficient (Cp), Midship Section Coefficient (Cm), and Waterplane Area Coefficient (Cwp). Derive the relationship $\mathbf{C_b = C_p \cdot C_m}$.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Block Coefficient (Cb):** It is the ratio of the displaced volume of the ship ($\nabla$) to the volume of a rectangular block having the same length LBP, breadth B, and draft T:

$$C_b = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

It indicates the "fullness" of the hull. Tankers have Cb ≈ 0.80–0.85; fine ships like container vessels have Cb ≈ 0.55–0.65.

**2. Prismatic Coefficient (Cp):** It is the ratio of the displaced volume ($\nabla$) to the volume of a prism formed by extending the midship cross-section along LBP:

$$C_p = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{BP}}$$

It measures the **distribution of volume** along the length. A low Cp indicates fine ends; a high Cp indicates blunt ends (typical of tankers).

**3. Midship Section Coefficient (Cm):** It is the ratio of the actual area of the midship cross-section ($A_M$) to the area of its circumscribing rectangle (B × T):

$$C_m = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$$

Cm describes the fullness of the midship section only. For most merchant ships, Cm is between 0.85 and 0.99.

**4. Waterplane Area Coefficient (Cwp):** It is the ratio of the actual waterplane area ($A_{WP}$) at the design waterline to the bounding rectangle ($L_{WL} \cdot B$):

$$C_{wp} = \frac{A_{WP}}{L_{WL} \cdot B}$$

It governs the **initial transverse stability** (GMT ∝ Cwp).

**Derivation of $C_b = C_p \cdot C_m$:**

Starting from Cp:

$$C_p = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{BP}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(B \cdot T)$:

$$C_p = \frac{\nabla \cdot B \cdot T}{A_M \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

We can rewrite as:

$$C_p = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T} \cdot \frac{B \cdot T}{A_M} = C_b \cdot \frac{1}{C_m}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{C_b = C_p \cdot C_m}$$

**(b)** A bulk carrier has the following particulars:
- LBP = 180 m, B = 28 m, T = 11 m, Cb = 0.81, Cm = 0.98, seawater density 1.025 t/m³
- **Calculate:** (i) Displacement Δ, (ii) Prismatic Coefficient Cp, (iii) Wetted Surface Area S using $S \approx 1.7 \cdot L_{BP} \cdot (T + B)$

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Displacement:**

$$\nabla = C_b \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T = 0.81 \cdot 180 \cdot 28 \cdot 11$$

$$\nabla = 0.81 \cdot 55{,}440 = 44{,}906.4 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \cdot 44{,}906.4 = 46{,}029.06 \text{ t}$$

**(ii) Prismatic Coefficient:**

$$C_p = \frac{C_b}{C_m} = \frac{0.81}{0.98} = 0.8265$$

**(iii) Wetted Surface Area:**

$$S \approx 1.7 \cdot L_{BP} \cdot (T + B) = 1.7 \cdot 180 \cdot (11 + 28)$$

$$S = 1.7 \cdot 180 \cdot 39 = 1.7 \cdot 7020 = 11{,}934 \text{ m}^2$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Similar Pattern]

**Q. (a)** List and explain the **dimensional principal particulars** of a ship. State the standard density of seawater used in displacement calculations.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The dimensional principal particulars are:

| Particular | Definition | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Length Overall (LOA)** | Maximum length from foremost to aftermost point | 50–400 m |
| **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP)** | Length between FP and AP | Slightly less than LOA |
| **Length on Waterline (LWL)** | Length of hull at design waterline | ≈ LBP if no rake |
| **Moulded Breadth (B)** | Max breadth inside shell at midship | 8–60 m |
| **Moulded Depth (D)** | Vertical from baseline to moulded deck at side, midship | 4–30 m |
| **Design Draft (T)** | Vertical from baseline to waterline at midship | 3–22 m |
| **Freeboard (FB)** | D − T | — |
| **Air Draft (AD)** | Waterline to highest fixed point | Critical for bridges |

**Standard seawater density: $\rho = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$** (at 4°C, salinity 35 ppt).

**(b)** A container ship has: LOA = 250 m, LBP = 240 m, LWL = 245 m, B = 32.2 m, D = 19.5 m, T = 12.0 m, Cb = 0.62. Compute (i) Displacement in tonnes, (ii) Block Coefficient check (suitable for container ship?), (iii) Length-to-Beam ratio LBP/B and comment on its significance.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Displacement:**

$$\nabla = C_b \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T = 0.62 \cdot 240 \cdot 32.2 \cdot 12$$

$$\nabla = 0.62 \cdot 92{,}736 = 57{,}496.32 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\Delta = 1.025 \cdot 57{,}496.32 = 58{,}933.73 \text{ t}$$

**(ii) Cb check:**

$C_b = 0.62$ — this is **typical of container ships** (range 0.55–0.65), which are designed for high speed with fine, slender hulls to reduce wave-making resistance. Therefore, the Cb value is consistent with a container ship design.

**(iii) LBP/B ratio:**

$$\frac{L_{BP}}{B} = \frac{240}{32.2} = 7.45$$

A LBP/B ratio of 7.45 indicates a **slender hull**, typical of fast container vessels (range 6–9). A higher L/B ratio improves speed but may compromise course stability. Container ships are designed with high L/B to maximize TEU capacity per shaft horsepower.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing LBP with LOA in formulas** → All hydrodynamic coefficients (Cb, Cp) and resistance formulas MUST use **LBP**, not LOA. Using LOA yields incorrect answers and will lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting seawater density unit**: GT/NOT is dimensionless; displacement is in **tonnes** (t). Mixing units will cost marks.
> 3. **Not stating the freeboard formula explicitly**: Freeboard = D − T. Even if not asked, mentioning it shows conceptual clarity.
> 4. **Mixing up DWT and Δ**: DWT is *capacity* (cargo + consumables); Δ is *total weight* (DWT + lightweight). Do not interchange them.
> 5. **Numerical errors in $C_p = C_b / C_m$**: Always compute Cp LAST, not as part of intermediate steps. Show the formula explicitly.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — Must Memorize for KTU ESE Module 1**

- ✅ **LBP** is the *hydrodynamic length* — used in ALL resistance and coefficient formulas.
- ✅ **Cb** is the *primary* hull form indicator; $C_b = \nabla / (L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T)$.
- ✅ Master identity: $\mathbf{C_b = C_p \cdot C_m}$ — must be derivable from first principles.
- ✅ **Seawater density = 1.025 t/m³**; freshwater = 1.000 t/m³.
- ✅ **Displacement** = weight of water displaced (Archimedes) = ρ × ∇.
- ✅ **DWT** = loaded displacement − lightship displacement.
- ✅ **Freeboard FB** = D − T; **Air Draft** measured from waterline to highest point.
- ✅ **GT/NOT** are **dimensionless volume measures**, NOT weight.
- ✅ **L/B ratio** typically 5–8 for tankers, 6–9 for container ships — indicates slenderness.
- ✅ In **ship conversion**, principal particulars serve as the *baseline* against which all modifications (lengthening, beam insertion, deck addition) are evaluated.
- ✅ The block coefficient of a **tanker** is high (0.80–0.90); of a **container ship** is low (0.55–0.65).
- ✅ **Cm** is typically 0.85–0.99; **Cwp** typically 0.65–0.95; **Cp** typically 0.55–0.85.
- ✅ Wetted surface approximation: $S \approx 1.7 \cdot L_{BP} \cdot (T + B)$.
- ✅ For ship conversion numerical problems, always show: (a) given data, (b) formula, (c) substitution, (d) result with units.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Arrangement Drawing of the Ship
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** General Arrangement Drawing of the Ship

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — General Arrangement Drawing of the Ship

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Arrangement Drawing (GA Drawing):** A scaled, multi-view orthographic engineering drawing of a vessel that presents the **complete internal subdivision, spatial layout, principal dimensions, structural bulkheads, tanks, machinery spaces, accommodation blocks, cargo zones, and access routes** in a single coordinated document. It is the *primary contractual and conversion-planning document* in any ship conversion, refit, or life-extension project.

In the context of **Ship Conversion Technology**, the GA drawing is not merely a descriptive drawing; it is a **decision-making tool**. It visually communicates *what exists on the ship today* and serves as the **baseline canvas** upon which the shipyard superimposes proposed conversion modifications (new tanks, added decks, engine replacements, structural cut-outs, and subdivision changes).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Ship's Architectural Blueprint"**
> Think of a ship the way you would think of a **multi-storey hospital building**:
> - The **Lines Plan** is like the building's *structural skeleton* (curves, frames, geometry).
> - The **Midship Section** is like a *cross-sectional CT scan* of one floor.
> - The **General Arrangement (GA) Drawing** is like the **floor plan of the entire building** — it shows you which rooms (compartments) exist on every level (deck), where the stairs (escape routes/ladders) are, where the heavy machinery (engine room) is, where the storage tanks (fuel/water tanks) are, and how the people (crew) are accommodated.
>
> When you want to **renovate** the hospital (convert the ship), the architect (shipyard engineer) starts with the floor plan (GA), then *colours over* the parts being changed with red pen, and *adds new rooms* in green pen. The GA drawing is exactly that — except the "colours" become revised GA drawings, and the "rooms" become new compartments, tanks, and systems.

### 1.3 Purpose of the GA Drawing in Ship Conversion

The GA drawing fulfils **four functional roles** simultaneously:

1. **Documentation Role** — Captures the *as-built* condition of every compartment, including its function, frame numbers, and tonnage capacity.
2. **Regulatory Role** — Demonstrates compliance with classification society rules (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, BV) and statutory codes (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line).
3. **Conversion-Planning Role** — Provides the *baseline geometry* onto which all proposed modifications are overlaid.
4. **Operational Role** — Acts as the shipboard reference for crew familiarisation, damage-control plans, and cargo loading.

### 1.4 Standard Identification of Views in a GA Drawing

A complete GA drawing set contains **three coordinated views**:

| View | Plane of Projection | What It Shows |
|------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Profile / Sheer Plan** | Longitudinal vertical plane (centreline) | External hull, sheer line, bulwarks, deck houses, masts, funnels |
| **Deck Plan / Horizontal Plan** | Horizontal plane (each deck level) | Compartments, tanks, machinery, accommodation on every deck |
| **Transverse Sections** | Vertical plane perpendicular to CL | Internal subdivision by bulkheads, double bottoms, superstructures |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** In ship-conversion GA drawings, the **profile view always carries the principal dimension table** (LBP, LWL, B, D, T), the **frame numbering system**, and the **load line markings** — examiners frequently test whether the student can identify which view carries which information.

### 1.5 Standard Scales Used

The **scale** of a GA drawing is dictated by the size of the ship and the level of detail required:

- **Large Tankers / Bulk Carriers (>200 m):** 1:100 or 1:200
- **Mid-Sized General Cargo Ships (100–200 m):** 1:50 or 1:100
- **Small Vessels / Tugs / Fishing Boats (<100 m):** 1:20 or 1:50
- **Machinery and Engine Room Plans:** 1:25 or 1:50
- **Accommodation Plans:** 1:50 or 1:100

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention:** Tank capacities and structural scantlings are *not* read from the GA drawing — they are cross-referenced to the **Capacity Plan** and **Structural Drawing** respectively. The GA drawing is a *layout* document, not a *strength* document.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Content

## 2.1 The Information Hierarchy of a GA Drawing

Every GA drawing obeys a strict **information hierarchy**, in which each piece of data is positioned so that the maximum amount of information can be read at a glance. The hierarchy is:

1. **Principal Dimensions Block** (top-right or bottom-right corner).
2. **Title Block** (bottom-right corner) — ship name, yard, hull number, scale, date, revision number, drawing number.
3. **Revision Table** (top-right above title block) — track every conversion modification.
4. **View Layout** — Profile, Deck Plans, Sections arranged for visual continuity.
5. **Compartment Annotations** — name, frame numbers, deck levels, capacity (where applicable).
6. **Legend & Symbol Key** — tank fill symbols, escape-route arrows, watertight door markers, fire-rated bulkheads.
7. **Notes & Compliance References** — class society, flag state, statutory references.

## 2.2 Compartments Typically Depicted on a GA Drawing

The compartments shown on a GA drawing are classified into **six functional zones**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Functional Zones of a Ship's GA Drawing:**
> 1. **Cargo Zone** — cargo holds, cargo tanks, hatches, tweendecks, cofferdams.
> 2. **Propulsion & Machinery Zone** — engine room, boiler room, auxiliary machinery rooms, shaft alley, thrust block recess.
> 3. **Tank Zone** — fuel oil tanks (FO), diesel oil tanks (DO), lube oil tanks (LO), fresh water tanks (FW), ballast tanks (WB), bilge tanks, sludge tanks.
> 4. **Navigation & Command Zone** — bridge, chart room, gyro room, radar mast, wheelhouse top.
> 5. **Accommodation Zone** — cabins, mess rooms, galley, pantry, hospital, recreational spaces.
> 6. **Service & Safety Zone** — emergency generator room, fire-fighting station, life-boat embarkation deck, paint room, CO₂ room, funnel base.

## 2.3 Conversion-Specific Information Added to a GA Drawing

During a ship conversion, the GA drawing is **revised** to incorporate the new layout. The types of modifications depicted are:

- **New compartments to be added** (e.g., additional cabin deck during lengthening conversion).
- **Existing compartments to be removed or merged** (e.g., obsolete cargo hold becoming a new machinery space).
- **New structural bulkheads** to be inserted (e.g., when a single-space vessel is subdivided for SOLAS compliance).
- **New penetrations and cut-outs** in existing decks and bulkheads.
- **New piping and cable routes** (cross-referenced to piping diagrams).
- **Modifications to the subdivision for damaged-stability compliance**.
- **Updated tank capacities and new sounding/venting arrangements**.

## 2.4 Symbols, Notations and Conventions on GA Drawings

A GA drawing is **densely annotated**. Students must recognise the following standardised conventions:

| Symbol / Notation | Meaning |
|-------------------|---------|
| **WT** | Watertight bulkhead / door |
| **ST** | Slightly weathertight (splash-tight) |
| **FST** | Fire-screen / fire-resisting bulkhead (e.g., A-60) |
| **GASD** | Gas-tight and watertight (used in tankers) |
| **FR ## (e.g., A-60, B-15)** | Fire-rating class per SOLAS Ch. II-2 |
| **Hatched circle with line** | Vertical ladder / escape trunk |
| **Solid arrow with chevrons** | Escape route direction |
| **Cross-hatched area** | Cofferdam |
| **Dotted boundary** | New work / proposed modification (in conversion drawings) |
| **Solid boundary** | Existing as-built structure |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Reference Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarises the **standard formulas, abbreviations, and reference data** that appear on GA drawings and are tested in KTU examinations. There is **no heavy numerical computation** in a GA-drawing question — but you must remember the *ratios*, the *dimensional abbreviations*, and the *relationship between principal dimensions*.

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|----------|--------|----------------------|------|
| Length Between Perpendiculars | **LBP** | Distance between forward and aft perpendiculars | m |
| Length Overall | **LOA** | Extreme forward to extreme aft of hull | m |
| Length at Waterline | **LWL** | Waterline length at design draft | m |
| Breadth (Moulded) | **B** | Maximum inside-frame breadth at midship | m |
| Depth (Moulded) | **D** | Keel to main deck at side, measured at midship | m |
| Design Draft | **T** | Design load-line draft | m |
| Block Coefficient | $C_B$ | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}$ | dimensionless |
| Length-to-Breadth Ratio | $L/B$ | $L/B = \dfrac{L_{BP}}{B}$ | dimensionless |
| Beam-to-Depth Ratio | $B/D$ | $B/D = \dfrac{B}{D}$ | dimensionless |
| Block Coefficient Range (typical) | $C_B$ | 0.55 (fine) — 0.85 (full) | dimensionless |
| Frame Spacing | $s_f$ | Typically **0.6 m** (standard) or 0.5 m / 0.75 m | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** In a ship-conversion problem, the GA drawing's **frame numbering system** is the single most important locating reference. Frames are numbered **aft → forward**, with **Frame 0** at the **aft perpendicular (AP)**. The forward perpendicular is typically **Frame 100** (for a 100-frame ship). All compartments are defined as lying between two frame numbers (e.g., *Engine Room: Frame 35 to Frame 50*).

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of the GA Drawing

The GA drawing's engineering utility extends far beyond a simple plan view:

- **Stability calculations** depend on the GA drawing to identify the **centre of gravity (KG)** of every compartment, tank, and load.
- **Damage-control procedures** depend on the GA drawing to identify **floodable length** limits and **cross-flooding** arrangements.
- **Cost estimation** for a conversion depends on the GA drawing to estimate the **volume of compartments to be cut, modified, or added**.
- **Regulatory audit** by classification societies depends on the GA drawing to verify compliance with **SOLAS subdivision regulations**.
- **Crew training and emergency drills** depend on the GA drawing to communicate **escape routes** and **muster stations**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The GA drawing is the **only single document** that simultaneously serves naval architects, marine engineers, shipyard planners, classification surveyors, flag-state inspectors, and the ship's crew. This is why it is often called the *"Master Document"* of a ship.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Analytical Framework — Reading a GA Drawing

A KTU-style analytical question on GA drawings typically tests three competencies:

1. **Identification** — recognising the views and the information carried by each.
2. **Interpretation** — reading compartment locations using frame numbers and deck levels.
3. **Conversion Logic** — proposing what modifications are possible given the existing layout.

The standard analytical procedure is presented below as a **5-step methodology**:

### Step 1 — Identify the Type of Vessel and Its Functional Zones

Read the title block and the principal dimensions to identify the vessel type. A bulk carrier will have large cargo holds; a tanker will have large cargo tanks with cofferdams; a passenger ship will have extensive accommodation zones.

### Step 2 — Locate the Profile View and Read the Sheer Plan

The profile view (sheer plan) shows the external hull. From it, identify:

- The **design waterline** and the **load line markings** (e.g., *Tropical Fresh, Tropical Salt, Summer Salt, Winter Salt, WNA*).
- The **positions of the forward and aft perpendiculars**.
- The **location of masts, funnels, deck houses, and superstructures**.
- The **position of hatches and the bow/stern doors**, if any.

### Step 3 — Read the Deck Plans in Sequence

Decks are usually shown in the order — **lowest deck first (e.g., double bottom) → tank top → main deck → superstructure decks (A, B, C decks) → navigation bridge**.

For each deck, identify:
- The **compartment names** (e.g., *No. 1 Cargo Hold, Engine Room, FO Storage Tank*).
- The **frame range** (e.g., *Frames 35–50*).
- The **adjacent compartments** and the **type of bulkheads** separating them (WT, ST, FST, GASD).

### Step 4 — Cross-Reference Transverse Sections

The transverse sections confirm the **vertical subdivision** of the ship — i.e., what is above and below the compartment of interest. They are especially important for verifying **double-bottom tanks**, **peak tanks** (fore & aft), and **deep-tank** arrangements.

### Step 5 — Apply Conversion Logic

For a conversion question, the analytical step is to identify:

- **What can be removed** (e.g., obsolete cargo hold).
- **What can be added** (e.g., new accommodation deck).
- **What must be preserved** (e.g., structural integrity of the main hull girder, watertight subdivision, class-approved structural continuity).
- **What regulatory changes are triggered** (e.g., a change of service or a change in subdivision may trigger a full SOLAS re-assessment).

## 3.2 Worked Example — Compartment Identification from a GA Drawing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 1 — Reading Frame Numbers and Locating an Engine Room**
>
> **Question (KTU-style, 7 marks):**
> A general cargo ship has a length between perpendiculars of **120 m** and is built on a **standard frame spacing of 0.6 m**. The engine room is shown on the GA drawing as occupying the frame range *Frames 130 to 156*. Calculate the **length of the engine room** and the **percentage of LBP** it occupies.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the total number of frames on the ship.**

$$
N_{total} = \dfrac{L_{BP}}{s_f} = \dfrac{120 \text{ m}}{0.6 \text{ m}} = 200 \text{ frames}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the length of the engine room in metres.**

The engine room spans from Frame 130 to Frame 156. The number of frame spaces in this range is:

$$
N_{ER} = 156 - 130 = 26 \text{ frame spaces}
$$

Therefore, the length of the engine room is:

$$
L_{ER} = N_{ER} \times s_f = 26 \times 0.6 \text{ m} = 15.6 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the percentage of LBP occupied by the engine room.**

$$
\% L_{ER} = \dfrac{L_{ER}}{L_{BP}} \times 100 = \dfrac{15.6}{120} \times 100 = 13.0\%
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the result with a sanity check.**

A typical general cargo ship's engine room occupies between **10% and 18%** of LBP. The calculated value of **13.0%** falls comfortably within this range, confirming that the GA-drawn layout is dimensionally consistent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Engine Room Length = **15.6 m**, occupying **13.0%** of LBP.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Conversion Scenario Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 2 — Proposing a Conversion Modality from a GA Drawing (14 marks)**
>
> **Question:** A 25-year-old single-deck bulk carrier is to be converted into a **Heavy-Lift / General-Purpose Cargo Ship**. Based on the GA drawing of the existing vessel, propose **three major structural modifications** and justify each in terms of its impact on the existing layout, regulatory compliance, and operational performance.

### Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Modification 1 — Insertion of a Tweendeck in Each Cargo Hold**

*Existing layout:* The cargo holds are open from the **main deck** to the **inner bottom** (or tank top), with a typical clear height of 12–15 m. The single-deck arrangement is restrictive for general cargo that requires stacking in multiple layers.

*Proposed modification:* Insert a **horizontal tweendeck plating** at a clear height of approximately 4–5 m above the tank top in each cargo hold. The tweendeck must be supported by **deck longitudinals, transverses, and pillars (stanchions)**, and the access must be via new **vertical ladders or cargo lifts**.

*Impact on the GA drawing:*
- The single cargo-hold compartment on the main-deck plan will be **subdivided** into two compartments vertically.
- New **access openings** (hatches, doors) must be shown on the deck plans.
- The **fire-rating of the tweendeck bulkheads** must be specified (typically A-60 if used as a fire subdivision boundary).
- The **tonnage and stability calculations** must be re-done because the **vertical centre of gravity (VCG)** of the cargo will change.
