*Regulatory impact:* The conversion to a multi-purpose ship may trigger a **change-of-service re-assessment** under the flag-state regulations. The new tweendecks must satisfy the **Load Line Convention** if they are fitted as partial decks; otherwise, they are treated as platforms and are exempt.

**Modification 2 — Strengthening of the Hatch Coamings and Replacement of Steel Hatch Covers**

*Existing layout:* The original hatch covers are **single-pull pontoon-type** with a scantling suitable only for the original bulk-cargo service. They cannot withstand the **point loads** imposed by heavy-lift cargo (e.g., up to 500 tonnes per unit).

*Proposed modification:* Replace the existing hatch covers with **higher-scantling steel pontoon covers** and increase the **hatch coaming height** by approximately 200–300 mm to provide additional strength and to accommodate the increased **stowage height** of heavy-lift cargo.

*Impact on the GA drawing:*
- The **hatch coaming detail** on the profile view will be **revised**.
- The **position and size of cleats and securing points** must be updated.
- The **scantlings table** (referenced on a separate structural drawing) must be revised, although the GA drawing itself only references the change in the *notes* column.

*Operational impact:* The new covers will permit **point loads** of up to 25 t/m² (typical for heavy-lift service), which is roughly 2.5× the original design load. The **ballast system** may also need to be re-tuned to compensate for the increased weight of the new covers.

**Modification 3 — Installation of a Bow Thruster and Associated Tunnel**

*Existing layout:* The original vessel has a **single-screw, single-rudder** configuration with no bow thruster. Manoeuvrability in port is limited, particularly for general-cargo operations requiring frequent berthing and un-berthing.

*Proposed modification:* Install a **300–500 kW bow thruster** with a tunnel diameter of approximately 1.5–2.0 m, located in the **forward lower part of the bow** (typically between Frames 180 and 195 in a 200-frame ship). The tunnel must be reinforced against hull girder shear and bending stresses.

*Impact on the GA drawing:*
- The **tunnel aperture** must be shown on the profile view and on the relevant transverse section.
- The **thruster room** (housing the motor, starter, and lubrication system) must be added to the deck plan — typically at the **forepeak bulkhead** level.
- The **forward peak tank** will be reduced in capacity to accommodate the tunnel, and the **tank capacity plan** must be revised accordingly.
- New **piping and cable routes** must be drawn (these are typically shown on a separate piping diagram, but their terminations are indicated on the GA drawing).

*Regulatory impact:* The bow thruster must be supplied from the **emergency switchboard** (per SOLAS Ch. II-1) and must be capable of operating from the **bridge** as well as locally. The modification will also require **classification society approval** since it alters the hull structure.

## 3.4 Conversion-Cost Estimation Principle (Analytical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight — Cost Estimation from the GA Drawing**
>
> The approximate cost of a structural conversion is often estimated by the **steel-weight approach**:
>
> $$
> W_{steel} = \rho_{steel} \times V_{cut} + \rho_{steel} \times V_{added}
> $$
>
> where:
> - $W_{steel}$ = total weight of steel involved in the conversion (tonnes)
> - $\rho_{steel}$ = density of shipbuilding steel $\approx 7.85$ t/m³
> - $V_{cut}$ = volume of steel to be cut out
> - $V_{added}$ = volume of new steel to be added
>
> The volume is computed from the **compartment dimensions** read from the GA drawing (length × breadth × thickness of plating). A student can use this formula in a KTU exam to estimate the *steel tonnage* involved in a conversion, and then multiply by an average conversion cost per tonne (e.g., **USD 2,500–4,500 per tonne** in 2024 market conditions) to obtain a *first-order cost estimate*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — A GA Drawing's Information Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the **information architecture** of a complete GA drawing set, showing how the various sub-drawings and information blocks interact with each other. This is a **functional flow**, not a physical drawing, and is designed to be safe to render.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Title Block and Revision Table] --> A2[Principal Dimensions Block]
    A2 --> A3[Profile View - Sheer Plan]
    A2 --> A4[Deck Plans - All Levels]
    A2 --> A5[Transverse Sections - Key Stations]
    A3 --> A6[External Hull Geometry and Load Line]
    A4 --> A7[Compartment Layout by Deck]
    A4 --> A8[Tank and Machinery Layout]
    A4 --> A9[Accommodation and Service Layout]
    A5 --> A10[Vertical Subdivision and Bulkheads]
    A7 --> A11[Legend and Symbol Key]
    A8 --> A11
    A9 --> A11
    A10 --> A11
    A11 --> A12[Conversion Overlay - New and Removed Compartments]
    A12 --> A13[Approved GA Drawing for Yard Use]
    A13 --> A14[Classification Society Approval]
    A13 --> A15[Shipyard Planning and Cost Estimation]
    A13 --> A16[Crew Damage Control and Familiarisation]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Six Functional Zones on the GA Drawing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    zone1[Cargo Zone] --> GA[GA Drawing Master Document]
    zone2[Propulsion Zone] --> GA
    zone3[Tank Zone] --> GA
    zone4[Navigation Zone] --> GA
    zone5[Accommodation Zone] --> GA
    zone6[Service and Safety Zone] --> GA
    GA --> conversion[Conversion Overlay]
    conversion --> reg[Regulatory and Class Approval]
    conversion --> ops[Operational Use Onboard]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Modification Acceptance Logic

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram shows the **decision flow** that a ship-conversion engineer follows when deciding whether a proposed modification shown on a revised GA drawing can be accepted.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Proposed Modification on Revised GA] --> q1{Does it preserve hull girder integrity?}
    q1 -- No --> reject1[Reject - Reinforce first]
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Does it comply with SOLAS subdivision?}
    q2 -- No --> reject2[Reject - Redesign subdivision]
    q2 -- Yes --> q3{Does it maintain damaged stability?}
    q3 -- No --> reject3[Reject - Add ballast or subdivide]
    q3 -- Yes --> q4{Is fire rating of new bulkheads acceptable?}
    q4 -- No --> reject4[Reject - Upgrade fire rating]
    q4 -- Yes --> approve[Approve and Forward to Classification Society]
    approve --> class[Class Society Approval]
    class --> yard[Shipyard Construction]
```

## 4.4 Spatial Hierarchy of Information on a GA Sheet

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    top[Top Right Corner - Principal Dimensions and Revision Table] --> mid1[Middle Band - Profile Sheer Plan]
    mid1 --> mid2[Middle Band - Main Deck Plan]
    mid2 --> mid3[Middle Band - Lower Deck Plans]
    mid3 --> mid4[Lower Middle - Transverse Sections]
    mid4 --> low1[Bottom Right - Title Block]
    mid4 --> low2[Bottom Left - Legend and Notes]
    mid4 --> low3[Bottom Middle - Conversion Notes and Compliance References]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** A KTU question may show a student a *single view* of a GA drawing and ask them to identify *which zone of the ship* it represents. The flowchart above (Section 4.2) gives the **six functional zones** to choose from. The student should identify the *compartment names* visible in the view (e.g., *Engine Room, Funnel Uptake* = Propulsion Zone; *Galley, Mess, Cabins* = Accommodation Zone; *CO₂ Room, Fire Station* = Service/Safety Zone).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Define a General Arrangement Drawing. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

A General Arrangement (GA) drawing is a scaled, multi-view engineering drawing of a vessel that presents in coordinated form the *complete internal subdivision, principal dimensions, structural bulkheads, tank capacities, machinery spaces, accommodation zones, and access routes* of the ship. It is the *primary reference document* in ship conversion work, providing the baseline layout upon which all proposed modifications are overlaid. The GA drawing typically comprises three principal views — the **profile (sheer) plan**, the **deck plans** (one for every deck level), and the **transverse sections** — supplemented by a title block, a principal dimensions table, a revision table, and a legend of symbols.

### Question 2 — List any four standard notations used on a GA drawing and explain their meaning. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

| Notation | Meaning |
|----------|---------|
| **WT** | Watertight — a bulkhead or door designed to prevent passage of water under pressure |
| **ST** | Slightly weathertight (splash-tight) — resists spray and light green water |
| **GASD** | Gas-tight and watertight — used in tankers to prevent escape of flammable vapour |
| **A-60** | Fire-rating class — a bulkhead that prevents passage of flame and smoke for 60 minutes under standard fire test conditions |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **ST (slightly weathertight)** with **WT (watertight)**. **ST is NOT a structural watertight boundary** and must not be used as a subdivision boundary under SOLAS. Marks are deducted when students treat ST bulkheads as watertight.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and provides an *internal choice* between two alternatives. Both alternatives are presented below. Sub-parts are typically of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) Describe with a neat sketch the three principal views of a General Arrangement Drawing of a ship. State clearly the information conveyed by each view. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The three principal views of a GA drawing are:

**(1) Profile (Sheer) Plan** — A vertical longitudinal section taken through the centreline of the ship. It shows:
- The external hull profile (sheer line, camber, knuckle lines).
- The **forward and aft perpendiculars**.
- The **load line markings** and the **design waterline**.
- The **position of masts, funnels, deck houses, and superstructures**.
- The **hatch coamings and deck-edge details**.
- The **positions of bow thruster apertures, stabiliser fins**, etc.

**(2) Deck Plan** — A horizontal plan view at the level of each deck. The ship has multiple decks (e.g., tank top, lower deck, main deck, superstructure decks), and *one deck plan is drawn for each level*. Each deck plan shows:
- The **compartment names** (e.g., No. 1 Cargo Hold, Engine Room, etc.).
- The **frame range** of each compartment.
- The **type of bulkhead** (WT, ST, FST, GASD) separating compartments.
- The **tank capacities** (in m³ or tonnes).
- The **position of machinery, boilers, pumps, generators**.
- The **accommodation layout** (cabins, mess rooms, galley).
- The **escape routes** (ladders, doors) and **muster stations**.

**(3) Transverse Sections** — Vertical cross-sections taken at key frame stations (e.g., at the engine room, at a cargo hold, at the bow, at the stern). They show:
- The **internal subdivision** in the vertical direction.
- The **double-bottom tanks**, **peak tanks**, and **deep tanks**.
- The **deck levels** and the **clear heights** between decks.
- The **structural bulkheads** and the **fire-rating of boundaries**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sketch Requirement:** In the KTU exam, the student should draw a simple schematic showing the three views mutually aligned. The profile view should be on the upper half of the sheet, the main deck plan on the lower-left, and a representative transverse section on the lower-right.

**(b) A bulk carrier has a length between perpendiculars of 144 m and a standard frame spacing of 0.6 m. The engine room extends from Frame 110 to Frame 138. The ship has a moulded breadth of 22 m, a moulded depth of 13.5 m, and a design draft of 8.5 m. Calculate (i) the total number of frames on the ship, (ii) the length of the engine room, and (iii) the block coefficient if the displacement volume is 27,500 m³. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Total number of frames:**

$$
N_{total} = \dfrac{L_{BP}}{s_f} = \dfrac{144 \text{ m}}{0.6 \text{ m}} = 240 \text{ frames}
$$

**(ii) Length of the engine room:**

The engine room spans Frames 110 to 138.

$$
N_{ER} = 138 - 110 = 28 \text{ frame spaces}
$$

$$
L_{ER} = N_{ER} \times s_f = 28 \times 0.6 \text{ m} = 16.8 \text{ m}
$$

**Percentage of LBP:**

$$
\% L_{ER} = \dfrac{16.8}{144} \times 100 = 11.67\%
$$

**(iii) Block Coefficient:**

$$
C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T} = \dfrac{27{,}500}{144 \times 22 \times 8.5}
$$

$$
C_B = \dfrac{27{,}500}{26{,}928} = 1.021
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sanity Check Failure — Why the Answer is Wrong:** A block coefficient of 1.021 is **physically impossible** because $C_B \leq 1$ for any real ship. This indicates that either the displacement is incorrectly given or the principal dimensions are inconsistent. In a KTU exam, if you obtain a $C_B > 1$, **do not present the answer as final** — instead, **comment on the anomaly** and re-check the calculation. The likely correction is that the displacement should be **18,000 m³** (not 27,500 m³), which would give:
>
> $$
> C_B = \dfrac{18{,}000}{26{,}928} = 0.668
> $$
>
> which is a realistic value for a bulk carrier (typical range 0.72–0.82). **The examiner will award full marks if you identify the inconsistency and provide the corrected calculation.**

**Corrected final answer (assuming corrected displacement of 18,000 m³):**

$$
C_B = 0.668
$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) Explain the role of the General Arrangement Drawing in a ship conversion project. Discuss, with examples, the four major categories of information that a conversion GA drawing must communicate. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

In a ship-conversion project, the GA drawing is the *single most important document* because it simultaneously serves as a **baseline reference**, a **regulatory compliance record**, a **conversion-planning canvas**, and an **operational guide**. The four major categories of information it must communicate are:

**(1) Existing As-Built Layout:** The drawing must show the *current* arrangement of every compartment, tank, machinery space, and accommodation area, identified by **frame numbers** and **deck levels**. For example, the drawing will indicate *"No. 3 Cargo Hold: Frames 75–95, Main Deck to Inner Bottom, Volume = 7,500 m³"*.

**(2) Regulatory Compliance Information:** The drawing must indicate the **type of bulkhead** (WT, ST, FST, A-60, B-15, etc.) separating each compartment, the **load line markings**, the **fire-control plan cross-references**, and the **SOLAS subdivision line**. For example, the engine-room forward bulkhead will be marked **A-60**, indicating that it must resist fire for 60 minutes.

**(3) Proposed Conversion Modifications:** The drawing must show the *new* compartments, bulkheads, and penetrations that will be added, typically by **dotted-line boundaries** (for proposed work) and by a **revision note** in the title block. For example, during a lengthening conversion, the drawing will show the **new midbody section** inserted amidships, complete with new bulkheads, new tanks, and revised escape routes.

**(4) Operational and Damage-Control Information:** The drawing must indicate the **escape routes** (with directional arrows), the **muster stations**, the **fire-fighting stations**, the **CO₂ room location**, and the **life-boat embarkation points**. This is the *crew-use* version of the GA drawing and is a statutory requirement under SOLAS.

**(b) A 20-year-old product tanker is being converted to a chemical tanker. Based on the GA drawing of the existing vessel, list and briefly justify three major modifications that would be required. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(Modification 1 — Coating of all cargo tanks with an IMO-approved chemical-resistant coating system.)**

*Justification:* Product tankers typically carry **non-corrosive refined petroleum products** (e.g., gasoil, jet fuel), and their cargo tanks are therefore protected by a **conventional epoxy coating**. Chemical tankers, however, may carry **aggressive chemicals** (acids, solvents, caustics) that require a **specialised chemical-resistant coating** (e.g., phenolic epoxy, zinc silicate, or stainless-steel cladding for the most aggressive products). The GA drawing must be revised to show the **updated tank lining schedule** (referenced in the notes section), and the **cargo-tank capacity table** must be updated to account for the reduced internal volume caused by the thicker coating.

**(Modification 2 — Installation of an Inert Gas System (IGS).)**

*Justification:* SOLAS Regulation II-2/4.5.5 mandates that **all chemical tankers and product tankers of 20,000 DWT and above** must be fitted with an IGS. If the existing vessel was a smaller product tanker exempted from this requirement, the conversion will trigger the **mandatory installation of an IGS**. The GA drawing must show the **IGS machinery space** (typically located adjacent to the engine room), the **inert gas main** running along the deck, the **deck water seal**, the **PV valves** (pressure/vacuum relief valves) on each tank, and the **uptake from the boiler flue gas**.

**(Modification 3 — Re-design of the cargo piping system to include a fully segregated manifold with independent tank lines.)**

*Justification:* Product tankers typically have a **common cargo piping manifold** because they carry only a limited number of mutually compatible products. Chemical tankers must be able to carry **multiple incompatible chemicals simultaneously**, and SOLAS Chapter II-2 / MARPOL Annex II requires that the cargo piping system be **fully segregated** — i.e., **each cargo tank must have its own dedicated cargo line** running to the manifold, with no cross-connections. The GA drawing must be revised to show the **new piping layout** (with each line colour-coded and labelled), the **expanded manifold area** on the main deck, and the **new independent pump rooms** (one forward, one aft) if they are required for the highest-class chemical products (Class I — the most hazardous).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B Answers:**
>
> 1. **Do not describe a modification without justifying it.** Examiners award 4–5 marks for *justification* and only 2–3 marks for *identification*. A student who lists three modifications without explaining *why* they are necessary will lose approximately **6–7 marks** out of 14.
>
> 2. **Always cross-reference the GA drawing.** The phrase *"The GA drawing must be revised to show..."* is a *high-yield phrase* that demonstrates the student understands the relationship between the conversion and the document. Examiners explicitly reward this language.
>
> 3. **Distinguish between *statutory* and *class* requirements.** Some modifications are mandated by **statutory codes** (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line) while others are mandated by **classification society rules** (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV). Examiners deduct marks when students confuse the two.
>
> 4. **Numerical errors in Part B must be flagged.** If you obtain a physically impossible result (like the $C_B > 1$ case above), do not present it as final. **Re-check and state the correction explicitly.** This shows the examiner that you understand the *engineering* of the problem, not just the *arithmetic*.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — General Arrangement Drawing of the Ship**

- The **GA drawing** is the *master layout document* of a ship and the *primary reference* in any ship-conversion project.
- The GA drawing comprises **three coordinated views**: the **profile (sheer) plan**, the **deck plans** (one per deck), and the **transverse sections**.
- The **profile view** carries the **principal dimensions** (LBP, LOA, B, D, T), the **load line markings**, and the **sheer line**.
- The **deck plans** carry the **compartment names**, the **frame ranges**, the **tank capacities**, and the **type of bulkheads** (WT, ST, FST, GASD).
- The **transverse sections** confirm the **vertical subdivision** of the ship.
- The **frame numbering system** runs **aft → forward**, with **Frame 0 at the aft perpendicular (AP)**. Frames are spaced at the **standard frame spacing** (typically **0.6 m**).
- The **six functional zones** of a ship shown on a GA drawing are: **Cargo, Propulsion, Tank, Navigation, Accommodation, Service/Safety**.
- **Conversion modifications** are shown on a *revised* GA drawing with **dotted-line boundaries** for new work and **revision notes** in the title block.
- The **symbols** WT, ST, FST, GASD, and the fire-rating codes (A-60, B-15) are the *high-frequency notations* tested in KTU exams.
- The **block coefficient** is computed as $C_B = \nabla / (L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T)$ and must always lie in the range **0.55 to 0.85** for normal ships — a value outside this range indicates an error.
- The **engine room** of a typical merchant ship occupies **10–18%** of LBP.
- **SOLAS subdivision**, **MARPOL segregation**, and **class society approval** are the three *regulatory pillars* that govern any ship-conversion modification.
- The GA drawing is the only single document that **simultaneously serves** the naval architect, the marine engineer, the shipyard, the classification surveyor, the flag-state inspector, and the crew — making it the *true master document* of the vessel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Examination Tip for KTU 2024 PENST415:** The GA-drawing question in the ESE is almost always a **Part B (14 marks)** question with two sub-parts — one *descriptive* (typically 7 marks, asking the student to explain the views or the role of the GA drawing) and one *analytical/numerical* (typically 7 marks, asking the student to calculate frame-based dimensions, compartment volumes, or block coefficients). Master the **5-step analytical methodology** in Section 3.1 and the **two worked examples** in Sections 3.2 and 3.3, and you will be able to answer any GA-drawing question in the KTU examination with confidence.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixation of the Revised Dimensions Based on the Cargo Capacity and Redesignated Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fixation of the Revised Dimensions Based on the Cargo Capacity and Redesignated Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fixation of the Revised Dimensions** in ship conversion technology is the systematic engineering process of recalculating and determining the modified principal dimensions of a vessel — namely the **Length (L)**, **Breadth (B)**, **Depth (D)**, and **Design Draft (T)** — to meet the **new cargo capacity requirements** and the **redesignated functional role** of the ship after conversion, while ensuring compliance with the relevant Classification Society rules, IMO regulations, and stability-safety criteria.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, this stage belongs to the **preliminary design phase** of a ship conversion project. After a vessel is acquired for a new service (e.g., an oil tanker being converted to a chemical carrier, or a general cargo ship being converted to a container ship), the naval architect must mathematically re-establish the geometric envelope of the hull so that the new mission profile can be economically and safely fulfilled.

The principal dimensions that are typically re-fixed during a conversion are:

| Dimension | Symbol | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Length Overall | $L_{OA}$ | metres (m) |
| Length Between Perpendiculars | $L_{PP}$ | metres (m) |
| Length on Waterline | $L_{WL}$ | metres (m) |
| Moulded Breadth | $B$ | metres (m) |
| Moulded Depth | $D$ | metres (m) |
| Design Draft | $T$ | metres (m) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Remodelled House" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of ship conversion as **remodelling a 3-storey house for a completely new family**. The original house (existing vessel) was built for a family of 4 (e.g., a dry-cargo ship). Now a new family of 8 moves in with a large dog (e.g., a tanker converted to a ro-ro ferry). The architect (naval architect) cannot simply add rooms; they must re-check the **foundation load (displacement)**, **floor area (cargo capacity)**, **ceiling height (freeboard)**, **staircase width (access)**, and even the **plot boundary (harbour/canal limits)**. The "fixation of revised dimensions" is the moment when the architect finalises the *new floor plan dimensions* so that the remodelled house can legally, safely, and comfortably host the new family. Any mistake in the new dimensions causes cracks (structural failure) or unfit-for-purpose rooms (operational failure).

## 1.3 Why "Cargo Capacity" and "Redesignated Functions" Drive the Re-Fixation

- **Cargo Capacity:** The new **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)** requirement is set by the commercial mission. For a converted vessel, the volume of cargo holds, tank capacity, or container slots dictates the internal volume, which in turn drives the **block coefficient ($C_B$)** and the **moulded dimensions**.
- **Redesignated Functions:** A ship repurposed from, say, a bulk carrier to a livestock carrier requires different **ventilation rates**, **drainage**, **deck strength**, and **stability characteristics**. The redesignated function changes the required **service speed ($V$)**, **range**, **crew complement**, and **auxiliary systems**, all of which feed back into the principal dimensions.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** When you answer a 14-mark question, always explicitly state *which* cargo capacity metric (DWT, GT, NT, bale cubic, grain cubic) is driving the dimension-fixation, and *which* function (new service route, new cargo type, new regulatory regime like SOLAS/ MARPOL) is imposing the new constraints.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logical Workflow of Dimension Fixation

The fixation of revised principal dimensions follows a sequential engineering logic, also known as the **"Iteration Spiral"** in preliminary ship design:

1. **Establish the Mission Profile (Redesignated Function).**  
   Identify the new trade route, port restrictions, cargo type, service speed, and regulatory regime (IACS, SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line Convention).

2. **Compute the Required Deadweight (DWT).**  
   Sum up: cargo weight + fuel oil (FO) + diesel oil (DO) + lubricating oil (LO) + fresh water (FW) + crew + stores + ballast.

3. **Estimate the Lightweight (LWT).**  
   For a converted ship, LWT is **partially known** (existing steel weight) but must be adjusted for added/removed steel during conversion. A common empirical anchor is **LWT ≈ (0.18 – 0.22) × Δ** for general cargo ships and **(0.35 – 0.45) × Δ** for tankers.

4. **Compute the Required Displacement (Δ).**  
   $$\Delta = DWT + LWT$$

5. **Select Form Coefficients.**  
   The block coefficient $C_B$ depends on the new cargo type. For example, a slow-speed bulk carrier has a *fat* hull (high $C_B \approx 0.78 - 0.85$), whereas a fast container ship has a *slim* hull (lower $C_B \approx 0.55 - 0.65$).

6. **Solve the Displacement Equation for the Principal Dimensions.**  
   $$\Delta = \rho \cdot g \cdot L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B$$
   where $\rho \cdot g$ is the weight density of sea water ($\approx 1.025 \;\text{t/m}^3$ in salt water).

7. **Apply Geometric and Regulatory Constraints.**  
   - **Length-to-Breadth Ratio** ($L/B$): usually 5 – 7 for merchant ships.  
   - **Breadth-to-Depth Ratio** ($B/D$): typically 1.5 – 2.2.  
   - **Freeboard (Load Line)**: minimum value fixed by the International Load Line Convention (ILLC).  
   - **Panama / Suez Canal limits** if the new route requires transit.

8. **Iterate until all constraints are satisfied** and finalize $L_{PP}, B, D, T$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Board Exams)

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols Used:** $\Delta$ = displacement (tonnes), $DWT$ = deadweight (tonnes), $LWT$ = lightweight (tonnes), $C_B$ = block coefficient, $C_P$ = prismatic coefficient, $C_W$ = waterplane area coefficient, $\nabla$ = volumetric displacement (m³).

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\Delta = DWT + LWT$ | Total weight the ship must float at design draft | tonnes (t) |
| 2 | $\Delta = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | Archimedes' principle linking weight to displaced volume | tonnes |
| 3 | $\nabla = L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B$ | Volume of the underwater hull in terms of principal dimensions | m³ |
| 4 | $C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Hull fullness relative to the bounding box | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{PP}}$ | Longitudinal fullness of the hull (refers to mid-section area $A_M$) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $C_W = \frac{A_W}{L_{PP} \cdot B}$ | Waterplane area coefficient | dimensionless |
| 7 | $A_M = C_B \cdot B \cdot T / C_P$ | Mid-ship cross-sectional area | m² |
| 8 | $L_{PP} = \frac{\Delta}{\rho \cdot g \cdot C_B \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Solved for length when $B$ and $T$ are pre-chosen | m |
| 9 | $B = \frac{\Delta}{\rho \cdot g \cdot C_B \cdot L_{PP} \cdot T}$ | Solved for breadth | m |
| 10 | $T = \frac{\Delta}{\rho \cdot g \cdot C_B \cdot L_{PP} \cdot B}$ | Solved for draft | m |
| 11 | $D = T + \text{Freeboard}$ | Moulded depth equals design draft plus statutory freeboard | m |
| 12 | $TPC = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot A_W}{100}$ | Tonnes per centimetre immersion — used to verify draft sensitivity | t/cm |
| 13 | $LWT \approx k \cdot \Delta$ where $k = 0.18 - 0.45$ | Empirical lightweight estimate (depends on ship type) | tonnes |
| 14 | $\text{SF} = \frac{B \cdot D}{\text{Spacing of transverse bulkheads}}$ | Shearing force on the hull girder check | kN |
| 15 | $BM = \frac{I_T}{\nabla}$ | Metacentric radius for transverse stability | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strictly Avoid the Pipe Character `\|` Inside the Table Above.** The absolute-value / "such-that" notation in the formulas has been replaced with descriptive text, e.g. "where $B$ and $T$ are pre-chosen". The reader should use $\mid$ in handwriting if needed.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Commercial Bidding:** Shipyards quote the cost of a conversion by first fixing dimensions. If dimensions are wrong, the bid is non-competitive or technically unviable.
- **Statutory Compliance:** IMO's Load Line Convention, SOLAS Chapter II-1 (Subdivision & Stability), and MARPOL Annex VI all impose dimensional rules. The fixation step ensures the converted ship is "rule-compliant".
- **Harbour/Canal Access:** Deciding $L_{OA}$ and $B$ against the **Panama Canal New Locks** (max $L_{OA} = 366$ m, $B = 49$ m) or the **Suez Canal** (max $B$ unrestricted but draft $\leq 20.1$ m) often determines whether a converted ship is commercially viable.
- **Hull Steel Cost:** Approximately **30 – 40 %** of a ship's steel cost scales with the principal dimensions. Over-sizing the hull by even 5 % can cost the shipowner several million dollars.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Solving the Displacement Equation for a Revised Length $L_{PP}$

**Given (a typical KTU 14-mark problem):**  
A 25 000 DWT general-cargo ship is to be converted into a 30 000 DWT bulk carrier. The existing breadth $B = 32.2$ m and the design draft (after conversion) is to be $T = 11.5$ m. The redesigned function requires a fuller hull form, so the new block coefficient is chosen as $C_B = 0.82$. The lightweight after conversion is estimated at $LWT = 6\,200$ t. Sea-water density factor $\rho g = 1.025$ t/m³. Find the required **Length Between Perpendiculars** $L_{PP}$.

**Step 1 — Write down the governing equation.**  
The displacement of a ship in salt water is:

$$
\Delta = \rho g \cdot L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B
$$

Here $\Delta$ is the total mass displacement (in tonnes) and $\rho g$ is the **specific weight** of sea water.

**Step 2 — Compute the required displacement from the mission profile.**  
The mission demands a deadweight of $DWT = 30\,000$ t. The lightweight after conversion is given as $LWT = 6\,200$ t. Therefore, the design displacement is:

$$
\Delta = DWT + LWT = 30\,000 + 6\,200 = 36\,200 \;\text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute all known values into the displacement equation.**  
We know $\Delta = 36\,200$ t, $\rho g = 1.025$ t/m³, $B = 32.2$ m, $T = 11.5$ m, and $C_B = 0.82$. Substituting:

$$
36\,200 = 1.025 \cdot L_{PP} \cdot 32.2 \cdot 11.5 \cdot 0.82
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the product of the known numerical factors.**  
First multiply the constant and the geometric terms on the right-hand side (excluding $L_{PP}$):

$$
1.025 \cdot 32.2 \cdot 11.5 \cdot 0.82 = 1.025 \cdot (32.2 \cdot 11.5) \cdot 0.82
$$

Compute the inner product:

$$
32.2 \cdot 11.5 = 370.30
$$

Now multiply by 0.82:

$$
370.30 \cdot 0.82 = 303.646
$$

Now multiply by 1.025:

$$
303.646 \cdot 1.025 = 311.23715
$$

**Step 5 — Solve algebraically for $L_{PP}$.**  
The equation reduces to:

$$
36\,200 = 311.23715 \cdot L_{PP}
$$

Dividing both sides by 311.23715:

$$
L_{PP} = \frac{36\,200}{311.23715} = 116.31 \;\text{m (rounded to two decimals)}
$$

**Step 6 — Sanity-check using standard ratios.**  
The $L/B$ ratio is:

$$
\frac{L_{PP}}{B} = \frac{116.31}{32.2} = 3.61
$$

This is **unusually low** for a bulk carrier (typical $L/B = 5 - 7$). Hence, the chosen $B = 32.2$ m and $T = 11.5$ m are too generous. The designer must either:
- (a) **Reduce B and T** to match the typical ratios (e.g., $B = 25$ m, $T = 9.5$ m), or
- (b) **Increase the deadweight DWT** to use the available hull volume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is exactly why fixation of dimensions is an *iterative* exercise. The first pass gives a feasible but uncompetitive hull; the designer re-iterates until all ratios fall within the **Type-Specific Design Corridor** (defined by classification societies and historical databases such as the *Optimum Ship Design Database*).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: From Revised Mission to Revised Depth $D$

**Given:** The same converted bulk carrier must satisfy the **International Load Line Convention** for a type-B ship with a freeboard of $f = 1.80$ m. The design draft after iteration is $T = 11.50$ m. The sheer allowance is $0.20$ m and the camber allowance is $0.15$ m. Find the revised **Moulded Depth $D$**.

**Step 1 — Recall the freeboard equation.**  
According to the ILLC, the **statutory freeboard** is the difference between the **moulded depth** and the **design draft**, adjusted for sheer, camber, and the load line mark position:

$$
D = T + f + s + c
$$

where $f$ is the tabular freeboard, $s$ is the sheer correction, and $c$ is the camber correction.

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
D = 11.50 + 1.80 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 13.65 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify the $B/D$ ratio.**

$$
\frac{B}{D} = \frac{32.2}{13.65} = 2.36
$$

This is at the upper limit of the typical range (1.5 – 2.2) — confirming that the breadth must be reduced in the next iteration to bring $B/D$ into a healthy corridor (target $\approx 1.85$).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Lightweight (LWT) Re-estimation After Conversion

For a converted vessel, the lightweight is calculated using:

$$
LWT_{new} = LWT_{old} + LWT_{added} - LWT_{removed}
$$

where:
- $LWT_{old}$ = lightweight of the parent ship before conversion (from the existing trim-and-stability booklet).
- $LWT_{added}$ = weight of all new steel, outfit, and machinery added during conversion.
- $LWT_{removed}$ = weight of original structures, fittings, and systems dismantled during conversion.

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Worked Sub-Example:** A parent oil tanker has $LWT_{old} = 11\,500$ t. During conversion to a chemical carrier:  
> • New piping and tanks added: $+ 1\,800$ t.  
> • New accommodation block added: $+ 600$ t.  
> • Original cargo piping removed: $- 1\,100$ t.  
> • Original main deck machinery removed: $- 400$ t.  
> Hence:
> $$
> LWT_{new} = 11\,500 + (1\,800 + 600) - (1\,100 + 400) = 12\,400 \;\text{t}
> $$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Fixation Decisions for Common Conversion Scenarios

| Parent Ship | New (Redesignated) Function | Principal Dimension Most Affected | Typical $C_B$ Shift | Engineering Driver |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Oil Tanker | Bulk Carrier | $L_{PP}$ (shorter) & $D$ (deeper) | $0.80 \rightarrow 0.78$ | New double-hull compliance (MARPOL Annex I) |
| General Cargo | Container Ship | $B$ (wider) & $D$ (deeper) | $0.68 \rightarrow 0.60$ | Container-cell stack height and TEU capacity |
| Ro-Ro Ferry | Livestock Carrier | $D$ (taller) & $B$ (wider) | $0.55 \rightarrow 0.58$ | Ventilation & drainage floor area |
| Naval Frigate | Patrol Vessel | $L_{PP}$ (shorter) & $T$ (deeper) | $0.50 \rightarrow 0.55$ | Reduced speed, increased range |
| LNG Carrier | FSRU (Floating Storage Regasification) | $L_{PP}$ (unchanged) & $D$ (modified topside) | $0.72 \rightarrow 0.72$ | Regasification module weight & stability |
| Passenger Liner | Cruise Ship | $D$ (taller) & $T$ (deeper) | $0.60 \rightarrow 0.65$ | Superstructure height & passenger amenities |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for the Exam:** "**L B D T** — Length, Breadth, Depth, Draft — are the four pillars on which the conversion is rebuilt. Whichever pillar is altered, re-check the other three ratios (L/B, B/D, L/T) before submitting the design."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Workflow Diagram — Fixation of Revised Dimensions

The following Mermaid block maps the engineering workflow from the moment the ship is identified for conversion until the principal dimensions are formally frozen for the **Approval-In-Principle (AIP)** stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Identify Parent Ship and Existing Drawings] --> A1[Define Redesignated Function and Trade Route]
    A1 --> A2[Compute New Required DWT]
    A2 --> A3[Estimate LWT After Conversion]
    A3 --> A4[Compute Design Displacement Delta]
    A4 --> A5[Select Form Coefficient C_B for New Hull]
    A5 --> A6[Apply Displacement Equation to Get First Guess L, B, T]
    A6 --> A7{Check Geometric Ratios L over B, B over D}
    A7 -->|Out of Range| A8[Iterate Adjust B, T, or C_B]
    A8 --> A5
    A7 -->|Within Range| A9[Check Statutory Freeboard ILLC]
    A9 --> A10[Check Canal and Port Limits]
    A10 --> A11[Compute Moulded Depth D]
    A11 --> A12[Finalize L_PP, B, D, T]
    A12 --> A13[Submit to Classification Society for AIP]
    A13 --> A14[Document Fixation Report]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The diamond `A7` is a decision node. If the geometric ratios are not within the typical corridor, the workflow loops back to the "Select Form Coefficient" stage. This captures the **iterative nature** of dimension fixation that examiners love to test.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Inputs and Outputs of the Fixation Stage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputs [Engineering Inputs]
        IN1[New Mission Profile]
        IN2[New DWT Target]
        IN3[Existing Hull Survey Data]
        IN4[Regulatory Constraints]
    end
    subgraph process [Fixation Engine]
        P1[Deadweight Decomposition]
        P2[Lightweight Re-estimation]
        P3[Displacement Computation]
        P4[Form Coefficient Selection]
        P5[Principal Dimension Solver]
        P6[Ratio and Constraint Checker]
    end
    subgraph outputs [Engineering Outputs]
        OUT1[Frozen L_PP]
        OUT2[Frozen B]
        OUT3[Frozen D]
        OUT4[Frozen T]
        OUT5[Frozen C_B]
    end
    IN1 --> P1
    IN2 --> P1
    IN3 --> P2
    IN4 --> P6
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P5
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> OUT1
    P6 --> OUT2
    P6 --> OUT3
    P6 --> OUT4
    P6 --> OUT5
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in the KTU exam:** A common 3-mark question is *"List the inputs and outputs of the dimension-fixation stage."* The block diagram above gives you a one-page cheat-sheet to reconstruct the answer in your own words.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Reminder (KTU 2024 Scheme):**  
> • Part A (Short Answer): 3 marks each — direct definition/derivation.  
> • Part B (Long Answer / ESE Module): 14 marks each — sub-parts of 7 + 7. Internal choice is provided between two questions from the same module.  
> • Bloom's Levels Tested: *Apply (L3)* and *Analyse (L4)* dominate this topic.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`  
**Define the term "fixation of revised dimensions" as applied to a ship-conversion project. Mention the four principal dimensions involved.**

**Model Answer:**  
Fixation of revised dimensions is the preliminary design process in a ship-conversion project in which the principal geometric parameters — **Length Between Perpendiculars ($L_{PP}$)**, **Moulded Breadth ($B$)**, **Moulded Depth ($D$)**, and **Design Draft ($T$)** — are re-calculated to suit the new cargo capacity and the **redesignated functional role** of the vessel. The process relies on the displacement equation, form coefficients, and statutory freeboard rules, and ends with a frozen set of dimensions that the yard uses for steel renewal and outfit planning.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`  
**Why does a change in the "redesignated function" of a ship force a re-fixation of the principal dimensions?**

**Model Answer:**  
A redesignated function changes the **mission profile** of the vessel — for example, switching from a tanker to a bulk carrier alters the cargo density, the tank-versus-hold volume, the regulatory regime (MARPOL Annex I double-hull), and the service speed. Each of these factors changes the required **DWT, lightweight, block coefficient, freeboard, and stability margins**, all of which are mathematically linked to the principal dimensions through the displacement equation. Therefore, the dimensions must be re-fixed to ensure the new ship can safely and economically perform its new role.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**  
> • Students often **confuse the lightweight $LWT$ with the deadweight $DWT$**. Marks are deducted if the units (tonnes) and the direction of the equation ($\Delta = DWT + LWT$) are not shown clearly.  
> • Students forget to **convert the draft to metres** if it is given in feet.  
> • The form coefficient $C_B$ is often confused with $C_P$ (prismatic). Use the right one.  
> • The **freeboard** is the distance from the **moulded deck line** to the **waterline at design draft**, not to the keel.

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**An existing general-cargo vessel of 18 000 DWT is being converted to a 22 000 DWT bulk carrier. After the conversion, the breadth is kept at $B = 28.0$ m and the new design draft is $T = 10.0$ m. The redesigned bulk-carrier hull form requires a block coefficient $C_B = 0.80$. The new lightweight after conversion is estimated as $LWT = 5\,500$ t. Sea-water density factor = 1.025 t/m³.**

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks**  
**Compute the required Length Between Perpendiculars $L_{PP}$ and the resulting $L/B$ ratio. Comment on whether the ratio is acceptable for a bulk carrier.**

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks**  
**If the type-B freeboard (ILLC) for the new ship is $f = 1.65$ m with sheer correction $0.20$ m and camber correction $0.15$ m, determine the revised Moulded Depth $D$ and the resulting $B/D$ ratio. Comment on whether the $B/D$ ratio lies in the typical range for bulk carriers.**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Sub-part (a):**

**Step 1 — Write the governing equation.**

$$
\Delta = \rho g \cdot L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the displacement.**

$$
\Delta = DWT + LWT = 22\,000 + 5\,500 = 27\,500 \;\text{t}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the known values.**

$$
27\,500 = 1.025 \cdot L_{PP} \cdot 28.0 \cdot 10.0 \cdot 0.80
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply the constants.**

$$
1.025 \cdot 28.0 \cdot 10.0 \cdot 0.80 = 1.025 \cdot 224.0 = 229.60
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $L_{PP}$.**

$$
L_{PP} = \frac{27\,500}{229.60} = 119.77 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the $L/B$ ratio.**

$$
\frac{L_{PP}}{B} = \frac{119.77}{28.0} = 4.28
$$

**Step 7 — Comment.** The typical $L/B$ ratio for a bulk carrier lies between **5.0 and 6.5**. The computed value of **4.28 is too low**, indicating that the hull is too "stout" (too short and fat). Either the breadth must be reduced (to about $B = 22$ m) or the deadweight must be increased. **Recommendation: iterate by reducing $B$ to 24 m and re-evaluating.**

---

**Sub-part (b):**

**Step 1 — Use the freeboard equation.**

$$
D = T + f + s + c
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
D = 10.0 + 1.65 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 12.00 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the $B/D$ ratio.**

$$
\frac{B}{D} = \frac{28.0}{12.00} = 2.33
$$

**Step 4 — Comment.** The typical $B/D$ ratio for a bulk carrier lies between **1.6 and 2.2**. The computed value of **2.33 is slightly above the upper limit**, indicating a hull that is too broad and shallow — prone to slamming and insufficient stiffness. **Recommendation: increase the moulded depth $D$ to about 13.5 m (perhaps by raising the freeboard deck) or reduce the breadth.**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**A 15-year-old oil tanker is being converted to a chemical parcel carrier. The parent ship has a lightweight $LWT_{old} = 9\,800$ t. During conversion: 1 200 t of new stainless-steel piping is added; a 400 t new accommodation block is fitted; 600 t of original cargo piping is removed; and 300 t of original deck machinery is dismantled. The new mission requires a DWT of 28 000 t. The conversion-design corridor suggests $B = 26.0$ m, $T = 9.5$ m, and $C_B = 0.75$. Sea-water density factor = 1.025 t/m³.**

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks**  
**Compute the new lightweight after conversion and the design displacement.**

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks**  
**Determine the required Length Between Perpendiculars $L_{PP}$ and the resulting $L/B$ and $L/T$ ratios. State whether the hull-form dimensions are acceptable for a chemical parcel carrier (typical corridors: $L/B = 5.5 - 6.5$, $L/T = 25 - 30$).**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Sub-part (a):**

**Step 1 — Apply the LWT re-estimation formula.**

$$
LWT_{new} = LWT_{old} + LWT_{added} - LWT_{removed}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify added and removed weights.**

$$
LWT_{added} = 1\,200 + 400 = 1\,600 \;\text{t}
$$

$$
LWT_{removed} = 600 + 300 = 900 \;\text{t}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and compute.**

$$
LWT_{new} = 9\,800 + 1\,600 - 900 = 10\,500 \;\text{t}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the design displacement.**

$$
\Delta = DWT + LWT_{new} = 28\,000 + 10\,500 = 38\,500 \;\text{t}
$$

---

**Sub-part (b):**

**Step 1 — Write the displacement equation.**

$$
\Delta = \rho g \cdot L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values.**

$$
38\,500 = 1.025 \cdot L_{PP} \cdot 26.0 \cdot 9.5 \cdot 0.75
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the constants.**

$$
1.025 \cdot 26.0 \cdot 9.5 \cdot 0.75 = 1.025 \cdot 185.25 = 189.88125
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $L_{PP}$.**

$$
L_{PP} = \frac{38\,500}{189.88125} = 202.77 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $L/B$.**

$$
\frac{L_{PP}}{B} = \frac{202.77}{26.0} = 7.80
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $L/T$.**

$$
\frac{L_{PP}}{T} = \frac{202.77}{9.5} = 21.34
$$

**Step 7 — Comment.** The $L/B = 7.80$ is **above the typical corridor of 5.5 – 6.5**, indicating a hull that is too slender — likely to cause excessive sagging moments and slamming. The $L/T = 21.34$ is **below the typical corridor of 25 – 30**, indicating a hull that is too short for its draft. **Recommendation: either reduce $L_{PP}$ (impossible without increasing $B$ and $T$ dramatically) or re-evaluate the DWT target and the design corridor. The 7.5 m of additional length is the cost of over-prescribing the deadweight.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this as your last-minute KTU revision sheet — read it 5 minutes before the exam.**

- The four **principal dimensions** to re-fix are $L_{PP}$, $B$, $D$, and $T$.  
- The **master equation** is $\Delta = \rho g \cdot L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B$.  
- **Displacement** always equals $DWT + LWT$. Memorise this — examiners love to test it.  
- For a conversion, the **lightweight changes** as $LWT_{new} = LWT_{old} + LWT_{added} - LWT_{removed}$.  
- The **block coefficient $C_B$** is selected *after* the new function is fixed (slim for fast ships, fat for slow ships).  
- Always verify the **$L/B$**, **$B/D$**, and **$L/T$** ratios against the typical corridors for the new ship type.  
- **Freeboard** is governed by the **International Load Line Convention (ILLC)**; $D = T + f + s + c$.  
- **Sea-water density factor** is **1.025 t/m³** unless stated otherwise.  
- The fixation is **iterative**: first pass gives a feasible hull, second pass gives an *optimum* hull.  
- **Canal limits** (Panama, Suez) and **port drafts** often override the pure mathematical optimum.  
- The output of the fixation stage is a **formal report** submitted to the classification society for the **Approval-In-Principle (AIP)**.  
- Common conversion scenarios to remember: oil tanker → bulk carrier, general cargo → container ship, ro-ro → livestock carrier, LNG carrier → FSRU.  
- **Mark-losing traps**: confusing $C_B$ with $C_P$, forgetting units, using the wrong freeboard rule, omitting the lightweight adjustment during conversion.  
- **Real-world anchoring:** About 30 – 40 % of a ship's steel cost scales with the principal dimensions — small over-sizing costs millions.  
- **Regulatory anchors** to cite: **SOLAS**, **MARPOL**, **ILLC**, **IACS Unified Requirements**, **IMDG Code** (for chemical parcel carriers).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Arrangement Layout
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** General Arrangement Layout

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 – General Arrangement Layout

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Arrangement (GA) Layout** is the comprehensive two-dimensional plan-view and profile-view drawing of a vessel that depicts the spatial organization, location, and interrelationship of every compartment, machinery space, tank, accommodation, hatch, deckhouse, and major structural element of a ship. It is the master document upon which all detailed design, construction, conversion, and regulatory approval activities are based.

In the context of **Ship Conversion Technology** (KTU PENST415), the GA Layout is the foundational engineering blueprint that:
- Integrates the original *as-built* ship arrangement with the *proposed converted* arrangement.
- Ensures compliance with **SOLAS**, **MARPOL**, **Load Line**, and **Classification Society** rules (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IRS).
- Facilitates **damage stability**, **fire safety**, and **crew safety** in the post-conversion vessel.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight – Module 1 (PENST415):** The General Arrangement forms the opening theme of the conversion technology course because every conversion decision (lengthening, shortening, re-purposing, retrofitting) is first tested against the constraints imposed by the existing GA.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you bought an **old three-story house** and want to convert it into a small hotel. Before changing anything, you would draw a *floor plan* showing where each room, staircase, water tank, electrical panel, and kitchen sits — and how guests would move from one space to another. That floor plan is your "General Arrangement."

A **ship GA** plays exactly the same role — but on a much larger, more critical, and internationally regulated scale:

| Element | House Conversion | Ship Conversion |
|---|---|---|
| Master Plan | Floor Plan | General Arrangement (GA) Drawing |
| Regulatory Body | Municipal Building Code | SOLAS / Class Society |
| Key Concern | Habitable area, circulation | Watertight subdivision, stability |
| Modification | Room re-purposing | Compartmentalization changes |

> The GA is therefore the *single most important document* a ship conversion engineer refers to before picking up a welding torch or cutting torch.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

Key dimensional metrics used in GA Layout analysis:

- **Length Overall (LOA)** — measured in **metres (m)**.
- **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP)** — in **m**.
- **Breadth (B)** — moulded breadth, in **m**.
- **Depth (D)** — moulded depth, in **m**.
- **Design Draft (T)** — in **m**.
- **Block Coefficient (Cₘ)** — dimensionless, typically **0.55 – 0.85**.
- **Freeboard** — distance from waterline to deck edge, in **mm**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Components of a General Arrangement Layout

The GA Layout of any sea-going vessel is decomposed into the following structured elements, arranged from bottom (keel) to top (navigation bridge) and from bow to stern:

### A. Hull Form & Structural Zones
1. **Double Bottom Tanks** — for ballast, fresh water, fuel oil.
2. **Engine Room (ER) Space** — single or twin, depending on ship type.
3. **Cargo Holds / Tanks** — bulk carriers, tankers, container ships.
4. **Forepeak & Afterpeak** — collision/trim tanks.
5. **Superstructure** — accommodation block (deckhouse).

### B. Functional Spaces
1. **Machinery Spaces** — Main Engine, Auxiliary Engines, Boilers, Purifiers.
2. **Cargo Handling Spaces** — Hatches, Cranes, Pumps.
3. **Accommodation Spaces** — Cabins, Galley, Mess, Hospital.
4. **Service Spaces** — Steering Gear, Emergency Generator Room.
5. **Navigation Spaces** — Bridge, Chart Room, Wheelhouse.

### C. Safety & Regulatory Spaces
1. **Watertight Bulkheads** — subdivision limit lines.
2. **Fire Zones** — defined under SOLAS Chapter II-2.
3. **Lifeboat Embarkation Stations**.
4. **CO₂ / Foam Flooding Rooms**.

### D. Conversion-Specific Zones (NEW)
1. **New Module Spaces** — pre-fabricated cabins, process plants.
2. **Retrofit Zones** — additional ballast tanks, scrubber rooms.
3. **Interface Zones** — where original structure meets new structure.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of GA Layout Decisions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** A GA is not designed in a vacuum. Every line on the drawing represents a balance between **operational requirement**, **regulatory compliance**, **structural feasibility**, and **economic viability**.

### Decision Logic Flow:
1. **Define the Mission** → e.g., convert bulk carrier → OBO (Ore-Bulk-Oil).
2. **Identify Regulatory Framework** → SOLAS + Class Rules + Flag State.
3. **Map Existing Spaces** → use original GA, structural drawings.
4. **Allocate New Functions** → tank capacities, machinery footprints.
5. **Validate Stability** → intact & damage stability (SOLAS Ch. II-1).
6. **Iterate & Optimize** → minimize steel renewal, maximize cargo capacity.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Application in GA Layout |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Block Coefficient | $C_b = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}$ | dimensionless | Determines hull fullness, affects GA compartment sizing |
| 2 | Prismatic Coefficient | $C_p = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot L_{BP}}$ | dimensionless | Indicates hull form distribution along length |
| 3 | Midship Area Coefficient | $C_m = \dfrac{A_m}{B \cdot T}$ | dimensionless | Cross-section fullness |
| 4 | Length-to-Breadth Ratio | $L/B$ | dimensionless | Affects GA longitudinal vs. transverse space allocation |
| 5 | Length-to-Depth Ratio | $L/D$ | dimensionless | Hull stiffness; affects deckhouse placement |
| 6 | Depth-to-Draft Ratio | $D/T$ | dimensionless | Determines freeboard & dry-docking clearance |
| 7 | Tank Volume (Rectangular) | $V = L_t \cdot B_t \cdot h_t$ | $m^3$ | Capacity of double-bottom, peak tanks |
| 8 | Tank Volume (Cylindrical) | $V = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot L_t$ | $m^3$ | Capacity of bunker, lube oil tanks |
| 9 | Total Steel Weight (Approx.) | $W_s = K_s \cdot \Delta^{2/3} \cdot L$ | tonnes | Used to estimate conversion steel renewal budget |
| 10 | Damage Stability Index | $GZ_{max}, \text{Range} \geq 20°$ | degrees | SOLAS II-1 damage survival criterion |

Where:
- $\nabla$ = displaced volume
- $A_m$ = midship section area
- $\Delta$ = displacement
- $K_s$ = steel weight coefficient (approx. **0.04 – 0.06** for merchant vessels)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Naval architects use these formulas in tandem with GA Layout drawings to perform *initial feasibility studies* during the bid stage of a ship conversion project. A 10% error in tank capacity calculation can lead to a **$500,000 loss** in cargo revenue over a 25-year ship life.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Block Coefficient and Its Role in GA Volume Allocation

The **Block Coefficient ($C_b$)** is the single most important hull-form parameter because it directly governs how much internal volume is available for compartmentalization in the GA.

### Step 1 — Start with Displaced Volume
By Archimedes' principle, a floating ship displaces a volume of water equal to its weight:
$$\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$

where:
- $\Delta$ = displacement weight (tonnes)
- $\rho_{sw}$ = density of seawater = **1.025 t/m³**
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration = **9.81 m/s²**
- $\nabla$ = displaced volume (m³)

### Step 2 — Express $\nabla$ Geometrically
The displaced volume is the volume of the underwater hull, which can be approximated as:
$$\nabla \approx A_m \cdot L_{BP} \cdot C_p$$

where $A_m$ is the midship sectional area, given by:
$$A_m \approx B \cdot T \cdot C_m$$

### Step 3 — Combine to Derive $C_b$
Substituting $A_m$:
$$\nabla \approx B \cdot T \cdot C_m \cdot L_{BP} \cdot C_p$$

Recognizing that $C_b = C_p \cdot C_m$, we obtain the master relationship:
$$\nabla = C_b \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$$

Therefore, solving for $C_b$:
$$\boxed{C_b = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}}$$

> **Conversion Logic Explanation:** $C_b$ tells the GA designer "how much rectangular box volume is *filled* by the ship." A tanker has high $C_b \approx 0.85$ (full hull, lots of cargo space); a frigate has low $C_b \approx 0.55$ (slim, fast hull).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: GA Volume Budget for a Tanker Conversion

**Problem:** A 25-year-old Aframax tanker (LOA = 245 m, LBP = 232 m, B = 32.2 m, T = 12.2 m, $C_b$ = 0.83) is being converted into a **chemical/product carrier**. Compute the **total internal cargo tank volume** if the cargo block occupies 70% of the displaced volume below the load waterline.

### Step 1 — Compute Displaced Volume
$$\nabla = C_b \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$$
$$\nabla = 0.83 \cdot 232 \cdot 32.2 \cdot 12.2$$

Step-by-step multiplication:
$$0.83 \cdot 232 = 192.56$$
$$192.56 \cdot 32.2 = 6200.43$$
$$6200.43 \cdot 12.2 = 75{,}645.27 \;\; m^3$$

### Step 2 — Allocate Cargo Volume (70%)
$$V_{cargo} = 0.70 \cdot 75{,}645.27 = 52{,}951.69 \;\; m^3$$

### Step 3 — Distribute Across 12 Cargo Tanks (Typical Aframax)
$$V_{per\,tank} = \dfrac{52{,}951.69}{12} \approx 4{,}412.64 \;\; m^3$$

### Step 4 — Determine Tank Footprint on GA
Assume tanks have effective height $h_t$ = **18 m** (from inner bottom to deck):
$$A_{tank} = \dfrac{V_{per\,tank}}{h_t} = \dfrac{4{,}412.64}{18} \approx 245.15 \;\; m^2$$

### Step 5 — Determine Tank Length (assume B = 32.2 m)
$$L_{tank} = \dfrac{A_{tank}}{B} = \dfrac{245.15}{32.2} \approx 7.61 \;\; m$$

**Final GA Allocation:**

$$\boxed{L_{tank} \approx 7.61 \;\; m, \quad V_{tank} \approx 4{,}413 \;\; m^3 \;\; \text{per tank}}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Each 7.6 m tank length also must accommodate a **cofferdam** (typically 1 m) between adjacent cargo tanks per MARPOL Annex II. The GA must therefore extend the actual compartment length to **8.61 m** per tank, reducing the *usable* number of cargo slots by 1–2 tanks.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: GA Comparison – Original vs. Converted Vessel

| Parameter | Original Aframax Tanker | Converted Chemical Carrier | Change | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Number of Cargo Tanks | 6 (slop included) | 12 (with cofferdams) | +6 | Segregation under MARPOL |
| Total Cargo Volume | ~75,000 m³ | ~52,950 m³ | −30% | New coatings, slop, cofferdams |
| Engine Room | 1 × MAN B&W | 1 × MAN B&W (retained) | None | Cost-saving |
| Accommodation | 28 berths | 36 berths | +8 | Additional chemical crew |
| Pump Room | 1 × aft | 2 × segregated | +1 | Independent chemical pumping |
| New Steel Addition | — | ~280 tonnes | +280 t | New bulkheads, pump rooms |
| New Compartments on GA | — | 4 hazardous zones | +4 | SOLAS Ch. II-2 fire zoning |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Ship GA Conversion Flow

The diagram below maps the **decision flow** used by naval architects when planning a ship's General Arrangement during a conversion project.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Conversion Requirement] --> B[Survey Existing Vessel GA]
    B --> C[Identify Regulatory Framework]
    C --> D[SOLAS Compliance Check]
    C --> E[MARPOL Compliance Check]
    C --> F[Class Society Rules]
    D --> G[Draft Proposed GA Layout]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compartmentalization Module]
    G --> I[Machinery Module]
    G --> J[Accommodation Module]
    G --> K[Cargo Handling Module]
    H --> L[Intact Stability Check]
    H --> M[Damage Stability Check]
    I --> N[Power Balance Check]
    J --> O[Habitability Check]
    K --> P[Cargo Flow Check]
    L --> Q[Final GA Drawing]
    M --> Q
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Class Society Approval]
    R --> S[Flag State Endorsement]
    S --> T[Construction Phase]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flowchart shows that the GA is *not* a single drawing but a **multi-domain system** integrating safety, machinery, habitability, cargo logistics, and stability. A failure in **any one branch** forces the engineer back to **Step G (Drafting)** for redesign — this iterative cycle is what makes ship conversion a high-discipline activity.

---

## 4.2 Top-Down GA Spatial Hierarchy (Side Profile View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Navigation Bridge - Top] --> B[Accommodation Deck]
    B --> C[Cargo Deck - Main]
    C --> D[Engine Room Level]
    D --> E[Double Bottom Tanks]
    E --> F[Keel - Bottom]

    A1[Bridge Equipment, Radar, GMDSS] -.-> A
    B1[Crew Cabins, Galley, Mess] -.-> B
    C1[Cargo Tanks, Hatches, Piping] -.-> C
    D1[Main Engine, Generators, Purifiers] -.-> D
    E1[Ballast, Fuel, Fresh Water] -.-> E
    F1[Keel Plate, Stem, Stern Frame] -.-> F
```

---

## 4.3 Transverse Sectional GA — Compartmentalization View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Port Side Hull] --> P1[Port Wing Tank]
    P1 --> P2[Port Slop Tank]
    P2 --> C1[Centerline Bulkhead]
    C1 --> S2[Starboard Slop Tank]
    S2 --> S1[Starboard Wing Tank]
    S1 --> S[Starboard Side Hull]
    P1 -.-> P3[Manifold Piping]
    S1 -.-> S3[Manifold Piping]
    C1 -.-> C2[Cross-over Duct]
    D[Bottom Plating] -.-> P1
    D -.-> S1
```

> This transverse schematic represents a **chemical tanker GA section**, where wing tanks provide ballast, slop tanks collect washings, and the centerline bulkhead separates port and starboard cargo systems — a direct requirement of **MARPOL Annex II**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define General Arrangement (GA) Layout of a ship. List its four primary functional zones.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** The GA Layout is the comprehensive top-view and profile-view engineering drawing depicting the spatial organization of all compartments, tanks, machinery, accommodation, and structural elements of a vessel, integrating regulatory, operational, and safety requirements.
- **Four Functional Zones (1.5 marks):**
  1. **Hull / Structural Zone** (double bottom, peak tanks).
  2. **Machinery Zone** (engine room, auxiliary spaces).
  3. **Cargo Zone** (holds, tanks, pump rooms).
  4. **Accommodation Zone** (deckhouse, bridge).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**State any three regulatory bodies whose rules govern a ship's GA Layout during conversion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea)** – IMO convention governing subdivision, stability, fire safety. **(1 mark)**
2. **MARPOL (Marine Pollution)** – IMO convention governing tank arrangements, segregated ballast, slop tanks. **(1 mark)**
3. **Classification Society** (e.g., Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, IRS) – enforces technical rules for hull, machinery, and electrical systems. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Students commonly lose marks in Part B by:
> 1. **Forgetting to mention regulatory clauses** (e.g., SOLAS Ch. II-1 Reg. 8) when discussing stability. Always cite the *exact regulation*.
> 2. **Confusing "displacement" and "displaced volume."** Remember: $\Delta$ (tonnes) ≠ $\nabla$ (m³).
> 3. **Skipping units** in numerical answers. KTU strictly penalizes missing units (typically −0.5 per omission).
> 4. **Drawing GA without scale or orientation markers** — the drawing must show bow direction and scale ratio.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
*With the aid of a labeled sketch, describe the General Arrangement Layout of a bulk carrier. Highlight the location and purpose of: (i) Forepeak Tank, (ii) Cargo Holds, (iii) Engine Room, (iv) Double Bottom, (v) Superstructure.*

**Model Answer:**

The GA of a **bulk carrier** consists of the following key compartments:

| # | Compartment | Location | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | **Forepeak Tank** | Extreme bow, forward of collision bulkhead | Trim adjustment, fresh water/ballast storage, emergency flooding reserve. |
| (ii) | **Cargo Holds** | Midship region, typically 5–9 holds | Stowage of bulk cargo (grain, ore, coal, cement). Each hold separated by transverse bulkheads. |
| (iii) | **Engine Room** | Aft of last cargo hold, separated by watertight bulkhead | Houses main engine, auxiliary engines, purifiers, generators, electrical switchboards. |
| (iv) | **Double Bottom** | Between keel and tank top, full ship length | Ballast water storage, fuel oil, fresh water; protects against bottom grounding damage. |
| (v) | **Superstructure** | Aft, above main deck | Crew accommodation, galley, mess, navigation bridge, hospital. |

**Key GA Principles for Bulk Carriers:**
- **SOLAS Chapter XII** (additional safety measures for bulk carriers) mandates double-side skin construction for ships > 150 m LOA built after 1999.
- **Cargo hold flooding** alarms mandatory; hold drainage systems.
- **Engine room** must have direct access to the escape trunk per SOLAS II-2 Reg. 13.

**Sketch Description (full marks require a labeled side profile):**
```
[Bow]                                              [Stern]
 ┌──┐  ┌──┐ ┌──┐ ┌──┐ ┌──┐ ┌──┐ ┌──┐ ┌──┐          ┌──┐
 │FP│  │H1│ │H2│ │H3│ │H4│ │H5│ │DB│ │ER│          │SS│
 └──┘  └──┘ └──┘ └──┘ └──┘ └──┘ └──┘ └──┘          └──┘
 ↑     ↑                              ↑              ↑
Forepeak Holds                      Double        Super-
Tanks                             Bottom + ER    structure
```
**Mark Allocation:** Diagram (2) + Forepeak (1) + Cargo Holds (1.5) + Engine Room (1) + Double Bottom (1) + Superstructure (0.5).

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
*A Panamax bulk carrier has the following particulars: LBP = 220 m, B = 32.2 m, T = 12.0 m, $C_b$ = 0.82. Calculate: (i) Displaced volume, (ii) Block coefficient, and (iii) If 65% of displaced volume is allocated to cargo holds, find the cargo hold tonnage assuming a stowage factor of 1.25 m³/tonne.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Displaced Volume (2 marks):**
$$\nabla = C_b \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$$
$$\nabla = 0.82 \cdot 220 \cdot 32.2 \cdot 12.0$$
$$\nabla = 0.82 \cdot 220 = 180.4$$
$$180.4 \cdot 32.2 = 5{,}808.88$$
$$5{,}808.88 \cdot 12.0 = 69{,}706.56 \;\; m^3$$

$$\boxed{\nabla \approx 69{,}707 \;\; m^3}$$

**(ii) Block Coefficient — Already given as $C_b$ = 0.82 (1 mark):**
$$\boxed{C_b = 0.82 \;\; (\text{dimensionless})}$$

**(iii) Cargo Hold Tonnage (4 marks):**
- Cargo volume:
$$V_{cargo} = 0.65 \cdot 69{,}706.56 = 45{,}309.26 \;\; m^3$$

- Cargo tonnage using stowage factor:
$$T_{cargo} = \dfrac{V_{cargo}}{\text{Stowage Factor}} = \dfrac{45{,}309.26}{1.25}$$
$$T_{cargo} = 36{,}247.41 \;\; \text{tonnes}$$

$$\boxed{T_{cargo} \approx 36{,}247 \;\; \text{tonnes (deadweight)}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** A Panamax bulk carrier of this size typically loads ~70,000–75,000 DWT. Our 36,247 t is the *cargo hold capacity only*; total DWT would include fuel, ballast, freshwater, stores.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
*Discuss the major considerations and regulatory constraints governing the General Arrangement Layout during a ship conversion project. Mention at least four specific SOLAS / MARPOL clauses.*

**Model Answer:**

The major GA considerations during ship conversion are:

**1. Regulatory Compliance (3 marks):**
- **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Reg. 8** — Subdivision and damage stability calculations. After conversion, the *as-converted* vessel must still satisfy the probabilistic damage stability index.
- **SOLAS Chapter II-2** — Fire safety: conversion must not degrade existing fire zones. New fire divisions (Class A-60) must be provided between machinery and accommodation.
- **MARPOL Annex I, Reg. 18** — Segregated ballast tanks (SBT) requirements for oil tankers; conversion must preserve or upgrade SBT capacity.
- **MARPOL Annex II** — Noxious liquid substances: chemical tanker conversion requires independent cargo piping and segregated tanks.
- **Load Line Convention** — Freeboard assignment may change with new superstructure or deck equipment.
- **Class Society Rules** — e.g., DNV Rules for Ships (Pt.3 Ch.1) define scantling requirements around new openings.

**2. Stability Considerations (1.5 marks):**
- Intact stability: large intact GM, GZ curve characteristics.
- Damage stability: SOLAS probabilistic index $R \geq 0.7$ (for passenger ships) or $s$-factor criteria (for cargo ships).

**3. Structural Feasibility (1 mark):**
- New bulkheads must align with existing primary structure.
- Avoid cutting main longitudinals unless reinforced.
- Existing stress hot-spots (engine room forward bulkhead) must not be disturbed.

**4. Operational Efficiency (1 mark):**
- Cargo flow path → minimize pipe lengths.
- Crew circulation → emergency escape routes within 30 m of any space.
- Maintainability access to machinery.

**5. Economic Constraints (0.5 marks):**
- Conversion should cost < 60% of new-building cost.
- Steel renewal limited to structural necessity only.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
*A 15,000 DWT general cargo ship is being converted into a coastal passenger-cum-cargo ferry. The original GA has a single superstructure at the stern with 20 crew cabins. The new design requires: 80 passenger berths, a new cafeteria, a children's play area, and a dedicated car deck for 30 vehicles. List the major modifications to the GA, the regulatory clauses invoked, and the stability implications.*

**Model Answer:**

**Major GA Modifications (3 marks):**

| # | Modification | New Space Added | Approx. Area (m²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Extend superstructure forward | 60 additional passenger cabins | 720 |
| 2 | Convert 'tween deck | Passenger lounge + cafeteria | 450 |
| 3 | Lower hold modification | Car deck for 30 vehicles (3 decks) | 1,200 |
| 4 | New deckhouse top | Children's play area + sun deck | 250 |
| 5 | Modify bow ramp | Ro-Ro vehicle access | 60 |
| 6 | New emergency generator room | Above main deck, port side | 40 |

**Regulatory Clauses Invoked (2 marks):**

- **SOLAS Ch. II-1, Reg. 6** — *Passenger ship subdivision* — vessel now classified as a passenger ship; lower damage stability index $R \geq 0.7$ applies.
- **SOLAS Ch. II-2, Reg. 13** — Means of escape for passenger ships; corridors max 40 m long, with 2 escape routes.
- **SOLAS Ch. III** — Lifeboats: capacity ≥ 80 pax + crew; muster stations on car deck.
- **MARPOL Annex IV** — Sewage holding tank must be added.
- **Class Rules** — New steel renewal near forward ramp (high stress zone).

**Stability Implications (2 marks):**

- **Lightweight increase:** New accommodation adds ~400 tonnes of lightweight. Draft at departure increases by ~0.18 m.
- **GM reduction:** Higher superstructure raises the transverse metacenter, but added top weight lowers KM-VCG margin. Likely need to install **additional ballast** or **bilge keels**.
- **Damage stability:** As a passenger ship, the GA must show survivability even with **two adjacent compartments flooded**; this requires careful placement of watertight bulkheads between car deck zones.
- **Freeboard:** Net freeboard may decrease; an **increased bow height** or **bulwark** modification may be required.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 – General Arrangement Layout**

- ✅ **GA = Master Drawing** of the ship showing all compartments, structural, machinery, and accommodation spaces.
- ✅ **Four Functional Zones**: Hull / Machinery / Cargo / Accommodation.
- ✅ **Block Coefficient formula**: $C_b = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}$ — direct impact on cargo volume.
- ✅ **Bulk Carrier GA** has: Forepeak → Cargo Holds → DB → Engine Room → Superstructure.
- ✅ **Tanker GA** has: Bow → Slop → Cargo → Slop → Stern; cofferdams between cargo tanks.
- ✅ **Regulatory Pillars**: SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line, Class Rules.
- ✅ **SOLAS II-1 Reg. 8** governs subdivision; **SOLAS II-2** governs fire safety.
- ✅ **MARPOL Annex I** — segregated ballast; **Annex II** — chemical tank segregation.
- ✅ **Conversion considerations**: stability, structural feasibility, regulatory compliance, economics.
- ✅ **Stowage Factor** = $m^3/\text{tonne}$ — used to convert cargo volume to deadweight tonnage.
- ✅ **Always include units** in KTU numerical answers; missing unit = 0.5 mark penalty per KTU valuation key.
- ✅ **Draw with bow direction, scale, and legend** — incomplete GA drawings lose 2–3 marks.
- ✅ **Iterative design loop**: GA ↔ Stability ↔ Regulatory ↔ Cost — no single optimization is final.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engine Room Layout
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Engine Room Layout

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚓ MODULE 1 — ENGINE ROOM LAYOUT
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PENST415 / Module 1):**
> *"Engine Room Layout — Principles of machinery arrangement, spatial planning, main and auxiliary machinery positioning, pipework, ventilation, cable routing, safety zoning, and conversion-driven reconfiguration strategies."*

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Engine Room Layout (ERL)** is the disciplined, multi-disciplinary engineering exercise of spatially organising all propulsion, electrical generation, auxiliary, support, safety, and control equipment within the machinery spaces of a vessel — such that operational efficiency, maintainability, survivability, habitability, statutory compliance (SOLAS, MARPOL, Class Rules), and life-cycle conversion flexibility are simultaneously achieved within the constrained hull envelope.

In the specific context of **Ship Conversion Technology**, engine room layout is not a one-time design activity but a **continuous reconfiguration problem**, because:

- The vessel is being repurposed (e.g., cargo → product tanker, cargo → FPSO, naval auxiliary → hybrid-electric).
- The original General Arrangement (GA) was designed for a specific service profile, machinery outfit, and deadweight.
- New regulations, fuels (LNG, methanol, ammonia, hydrogen), or propulsion concepts (hybrid, electric, fuel cell) demand structural and spatial re-engineering.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Heart & Lung Ward" of a Ship

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:**
> Think of a ship's engine room as the **intensive care unit (ICU) of a hospital**, but for the vessel. The **main engine** is the **heart** (pumps blood = thrust), the **generators** are the **kidneys** (filter and supply clean power), the **coolers/heat exchangers** are the **lungs** (exchange heat with seawater), and the **control consoles** are the **brain stem** (autonomic regulation). Now imagine a **hospital being renovated while patients are inside, with new organ transplants happening**. *That* is exactly what ship conversion + engine room layout feels like to a marine engineer.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics & Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardised Design Metrics (must be memorised for KTU):**
> - **Specific Power Density:** typically **40–80 kW/m³** of ER volume for modern motor vessels.
> - **Engine Room Casings (ERC) height:** generally **2.1 m to 2.6 m** above platform for headroom under piping.
> - **Tank Top Loading:** engine bearer pads are designed for **0.1–0.3 MPa** bearing pressure on tank top plating.
> - **Mean Effective Pressure (MEP)** of main engine: **1.0–2.0 MPa** (2-stroke slow speed).
> - **SOLAS Reg. II-2** governs fire zoning; **Reg. II-1** governs subdivision & machinery space integrity.
> - **MARPOL Annex VI** governs exhaust and air-pollution zoning of machinery spaces.

### 1.4 Three Classical Engine Room Position Philosophies

| Position | Typical Vessel | Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Aft ER** | Tankers, Bulk Carriers, Container Ships | Clean shaft line, large cargo volume forward, propeller in uniform flow | Long shaft → alignment/torsional vibration issues |
| **Midship ER** | Naval Ships, Ferries, LNG Carriers | Short shaft, central CG, balanced trim | Splits cargo/vehicle deck, complex ducting |
