- **Standard Position of Collision Bulkhead (SOLAS Reg. II-1/12):** The collision bulkhead shall be fitted at a distance of not less than **0.05 L** or **10 m**, whichever is less, from the forward perpendicular, and not more than **0.05 L + 5 m** from the forward perpendicular, where **L** is the *Length of the ship*.
- **Minimum number of bulkheads between peaks and machinery spaces:** As per SOLAS, at least **one watertight bulkhead** shall be fitted forward of and one abaft the machinery space.
- **Standard manhole size for peak access:** Typically **600 mm × 400 mm** (oval) or **600 mm diameter** (circular) per IACS S21.
- **Standard air pipe head height above the freeboard deck:** **760 mm** for non-protected openings, **450 mm** for automatically closing weathertight heads.

> [!TIP]
> **Why the forepeak matters in conversion:** Almost every ship conversion project must first assess the forepeak and aftpeak because the **existing piping, structural stiffeners, and access arrangements** in these spaces determine how feasible the new design is. A poorly designed forepeak conversion is the most common cause of **flooding accidents** during the operational life of a converted ship.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Forepeak — Structural & Functional Breakdown

The forepeak arrangement is one of the most tightly regulated compartments in any ship because it forms the **last line of defense against forward flooding** in the event of a bow collision. Its arrangement is governed by the interaction of multiple engineering disciplines.

**2.1.1 Bounding Structural Elements**

- **Stem Bar / Stem Plate** — The vertical centerline structural member forming the forward edge of the ship.
- **Collision Bulkhead (Bulkhead A)** — The transverse watertight bulkhead that limits the forepeak from the rest of the hull. It is mandated to be **non-corroded steel** and to extend up to the **freeboard deck**.
- **Inner Bottom / Tank Top Plating** — The horizontal plating at the lower boundary; the forepeak floor slopes upward toward the stem due to the **rise of floor** and the **sheer** forward.
- **Forecastle Deck / Main Deck** — The upper boundary.
- **Shell Plating** — The lateral boundary, including the **bow flare** and **flare knuckle**.

**2.1.2 Functional Sub-divisions within the Forepeak**

The forepeak is almost never a single empty void. It is normally sub-divided into:

1. **Chain Locker** — A watertight enclosure for the **anchor chain cables**. The chain locker is fitted with:
   - A **bitter end** release mechanism (emergency release of the chain).
   - A **chain pipe** (hawse pipe) leading up to the **hawse hole** on the shell.
   - A **sluice valve** at the bottom for draining the locker to the bilge.
   - **Spurling pipe** at the top, leading to the **chain stoppers** and **windlass** on the forecastle deck.

2. **Forepeak Store** — Used for mooring ropes, fenders, flags, and consumable stores.

3. **Forepeak Tank** (in conversions) — Often converted to a **fresh water tank**, **ballast tank**, or **lubricating oil tank** during a conversion.

4. **Void Space** — In many tankers and bulkers, the forepeak is left as a void with no equipment, accessible only for inspection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Significance:** The **forepeak void** of an oil tanker is one of the most common spaces converted to **segregated ballast** under MARPOL Annex I regulations. The conversion typically requires **doubling plates**, new **sounding and air pipes**, **access manholes** at safe locations, and **cofferdam arrangements** to segregate it from the adjacent cargo tanks.

**2.1.3 Access, Ventilation & Drainage**

| System | Typical Specification | Regulation Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Access manhole to forepeak | 600 mm × 400 mm oval, located above the load waterline | IACS S21, SOLAS Reg. II-1/15 |
| Sounding pipe | 50 mm ID, terminating at a self-closing cock on the deck above | SOLAS Reg. II-1/15 |
| Air pipe | 50–100 mm ID with gooseneck and weather-tight head | SOLAS Reg. II-1/20 |
| Bilge suction | 65 mm ID, connected to the main bilge manifold via a non-return valve | SOLAS Reg. II-1/35 |
| Ventilation | Natural or mechanical, with dampers at the deck penetration | SOLAS Reg. II-1/40 |

### 2.2 Aftpeak — Structural & Functional Breakdown

The aftpeak is geometrically more complex than the forepeak because it must accommodate the **propeller shaft**, the **rudder trunk**, the **stern tube**, and often the **emergency steering gear**.

**2.2.1 Bounding Structural Elements**

- **Stern Frame / Transom** — The aftermost structural member.
- **Peak Bulkhead (Bulkhead Z)** — The forward boundary; usually the aftermost transverse watertight bulkhead.
- **Shaft Tunnel Bulkhead (where applicable)** — A longitudinal structure enclosing the propeller shaft as it penetrates the peak bulkhead via a **stuffing box** or **air seal**.
- **Stern Tube** — The bearing housing for the propeller shaft, often lubricated by **sea water** or **oil** depending on the system.
- **Rudder Trunk** — A watertight tube passing through the aftpeak, housing the **rudder stock**.

**2.2.2 Functional Sub-divisions within the Aftpeak**

1. **Steering Gear Flat** — The compartment directly above the rudder trunk where the steering gear, tiller, and quadrant are mounted. Often classed as a **separate watertight compartment**.
2. **Emergency Generator Room** — In some designs, the aftpeak houses the **emergency generator** and **emergency switchboard**, accessible from the main engine room.
3. **Aftpeak Void** — Frequently used as a **sewage holding tank**, **bilge water holding tank**, or **ballast tank** in conversions.
4. **Stern Tube Lubrication Oil Tank** — The gravity oil tank for the stern tube bearings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Watchpoint:** The aftpeak is the **most technically challenging** compartment to convert because of the **rudder trunk** and **shaft tunnel** penetrations. Any new pipe, duct, or tank boundary in this region must not interfere with the **clearance envelope** required for propeller shaft removal and rudder maintenance.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Rule | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Minimum distance of collision bulkhead from FP | $d_{min} = 0.05 \, L$ or $10 \text{ m}$, whichever is **less** | m | SOLAS II-1/12 |
| 2 | Maximum distance of collision bulkhead from FP | $d_{max} = 0.05 \, L + 5 \text{ m}$ | m | SOLAS II-1/12 |
| 3 | Approximate forepeak volume (prismatic) | $V_{fp} \approx \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot L_{fp} \cdot A_{mid} \cdot C_{v}$ | m³ | $C_v$ is volumetric coefficient $\approx 0.85$ |
| 4 | Air pipe cross-sectional area | $A_{air} = \dfrac{Q}{C_d \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}}$ | m² | Q = filling rate, $C_d \approx 0.62$ |
| 5 | Sounding pipe diameter (peak tanks) | $D_{sp} \geq 50 \text{ mm}$ (main), $38 \text{ mm}$ (branch) | mm | SOLAS II-1/15 |
| 6 | Number of weathertight bulkheads abaft FP | $\geq 1$ (transverse) and $\geq 1$ (forward of machinery) | — | SOLAS II-1/10 |
| 7 | Air pipe head height above deck | $\geq 760 \text{ mm}$ (open type) | mm | SOLAS II-1/20 |
| 8 | Bilge pipe diameter (peak) | $D_{b} \geq 65 \text{ mm}$ nominal | mm | SOLAS II-1/35 |
| 9 | Number of access manholes to peak | $\geq 1$ from above (or 2 for twin-screw ships) | — | IACS S21 |
| 10 | Manhole cover minimum thickness | $t_{mh} = k \cdot \sqrt{P \cdot d}$ | mm | IACS S21 pressure design |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** In ship conversion, formula (1) and (2) above are the first checks a naval architect performs when deciding whether the existing collision bulkhead can be retained, relocated, or must be reinforced. If a new **side thruster tunnel** is to be cut through the forepeak, the residual distance $d_{residual}$ from the tunnel to the FP must still satisfy these SOLAS inequalities.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Position of the Collision Bulkhead (SOLAS Compliance)

The SOLAS regulation on the position of the collision bulkhead is the most-tested formula in this module. Let us derive the **acceptable envelope** for the bulkhead position step by step.

**Step 1 — Define the governing length.** Let $L$ be the *Rule Length* of the ship, in metres, as defined by the **Load Line Convention, 1966**. For a typical Panamax bulk carrier, $L = 229 \text{ m}$.

**Step 2 — State the lower bound.** Per SOLAS Reg. II-1/12.1, the collision bulkhead shall be at a distance from the forward perpendicular (FP) of not less than **5% of L** or **10 m**, whichever is the smaller value.

For $L = 229$ m:

$$d_{lower} = \min(0.05 \times 229, \; 10) = \min(11.45, \; 10) = 10 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — State the upper bound.** Per SOLAS Reg. II-1/12.2, the collision bulkhead shall be at a distance from the FP of not more than **5% of L + 5 m**.

$$d_{upper} = 0.05 \times 229 + 5 = 11.45 + 5 = 16.45 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — State the final acceptance envelope.**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{lower} \leq d_{cb} \leq d_{upper} \\
10 \text{ m} \leq d_{cb} \leq 16.45 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Conversion check.** If the ship is being converted and a new **bow thruster** is to be installed in the forepeak, the after face of the thruster tunnel must satisfy this inequality. The naval architect therefore positions the thruster such that the **closest point of the tunnel aperture to the FP is at least 10 m**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 5% of L + 5 m?** The upper bound exists to ensure the forepeak is long enough to provide adequate **buoyancy reserve** in the event of a bow collision. If the bulkhead is too far aft, the forepeak becomes a long, narrow flooded space that can introduce a large **trim-by-the-head moment** during a casualty.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Air Pipe Sizing for the Forepeak (Hydraulic Derivation)

**Step 1 — Define the volumetric filling rate $Q$.** When ballast is being taken into a converted forepeak tank, the pump capacity typically dictates the filling rate. Assume $Q = 200 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$.

Convert to SI units:

$$Q = \dfrac{200}{3600} = 0.0556 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the air-discharge equation.** For a typical air pipe with a **gooseneck at the top**, the discharge coefficient is $C_d \approx 0.62$. The head of water $h$ providing the driving pressure is the **maximum filling height**, say $h = 8 \text{ m}$ (from the tank bottom to the overflow level).

The required air pipe area is:

$$A_{air} = \dfrac{Q}{C_d \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$A_{air} = \dfrac{0.0556}{0.62 \cdot \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 8}}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 8} &= \sqrt{156.96} \\
&= 12.53 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{(air velocity through pipe)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$0.62 \times 12.53 = 7.77 \text{ m}^3/\text{s per m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the area.**

$$A_{air} = \dfrac{0.0556}{7.77} = 7.16 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 7160 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 6 — Convert area to equivalent pipe diameter.**

$$D_{air} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 A_{air}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 7160}{\pi}} = 95.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7 — Select the next standard pipe size.** Pipe diameters are standard in 50 mm increments: 50, 100, 150 mm. We select $D_{air} = 100 \text{ mm}$ as the next standard size above the calculated 95.5 mm.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering intuition:** This 100 mm air pipe will handle up to ~280 m³/h of filling rate at the same head. If the conversion project requires a higher pump rate, two 100 mm air pipes in parallel, or one 150 mm pipe, must be provided.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Approximate Forepeak Volume for Ballast Conversion

**Step 1 — Establish the forepeak geometry.** Treat the forepeak as a **truncated wedge** with length $L_{fp}$, forward height $H_f$ (at the stem), and after height $H_a$ (at the collision bulkhead), and average breadth $B_{fp}$.

Assume for a typical Handysize bulker: $L_{fp} = 12$ m, $H_f = 1.5$ m, $H_a = 8$ m, $B_{fp} = 18$ m.

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional area at any station $x$ from the stem.**

$$A(x) = B_{fp} \cdot \left[ H_f + (H_a - H_f) \cdot \dfrac{x}{L_{fp}} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Integrate to find the volume.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{fp} &= \int_0^{L_{fp}} A(x) \, dx \\
&= B_{fp} \int_0^{L_{fp}} \left[ H_f + (H_a - H_f) \cdot \dfrac{x}{L_{fp}} \right] dx \\
&= B_{fp} \left[ H_f \cdot L_{fp} + (H_a - H_f) \cdot \dfrac{L_{fp}}{2} \right] \\
&= B_{fp} \cdot L_{fp} \cdot \dfrac{(H_f + H_a)}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$V_{fp} = 18 \times 12 \times \dfrac{(1.5 + 8)}{2} = 18 \times 12 \times 4.75 = 1026 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Apply the volumetric coefficient** (to account for the curvature of the bow shell and the inner-bottom slope):

$$V_{fp, \text{usable}} = C_v \cdot V_{fp} = 0.85 \times 1026 \approx 872 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.** Approximately **872 m³** of ballast capacity can be added by converting the forepeak. This is a significant figure — for a Handysize bulker with a daily ballast requirement of ~3000 m³ for a ballast leg, the forepeak contributes nearly **one-third** of the total ballast volume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion pitfall:** The above volume is the **geometric** volume. After subtracting the **internal stiffeners, ladder volume, sounding pipe volume, and the structural margin** of ~5%, the **actual usable volume** drops to about $0.85 \times 0.95 = 0.81$ of the geometric value. Therefore, real-world conversion estimates use:

$$V_{usable} \approx 0.80 \times V_{fp}$$

For our example: $V_{usable} \approx 0.80 \times 1026 \approx 821$ m³.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Forepeak

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional interactions** of the forepeak with the rest of the ship, since the actual physical geometry is too complex for a static diagram in this format.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["STEM BAR\nForward Shell"] --> node2["HAWSE PIPE\nChain Conduit"]
    node2 --> node3["CHAIN LOCKER\nForepeak Compartment A"]
    node3 --> node4["SLUICE VALVE\nEmergency Drain"]
    node3 --> node5["SPURLING PIPE\nTop Closure"]
    node3 --> node6["BITTER END\nRelease Gear"]
    node3 --> node7["SOUNDING PIPE\nLevel Indication"]
    node3 --> node8["AIR PIPE\nVentilation"]
    node3 --> node9["BILGE SUCTION\nDrainage"]
    node3 --> node10["ACCESS MANHOLE\nWatertight Closure"]
    node3 --> node11["FOREPEAK STORE\nor BALLLAST TANK\nIn Conversion Mode"]
    node11 --> node12["COLLISION BULKHEAD\nBulkhead A"]
    node12 --> node13["FORWARD CARGO HOLD\nor MACHINERY SPACE"]
    node5 --> node14["FORECASTLE DECK\nWindlass and Stoppers"]
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- The **chain locker** is the central functional node, interfacing with the **stem bar** via the hawse pipe and the **forecastle deck** via the spurling pipe.
- The **collision bulkhead** acts as the **watertight gateway** between the forepeak and the forward cargo hold; in conversion mode, the forepeak becomes a **ballast tank**, isolated from the cargo side by this bulkhead.
- The **sluice valve** is a critical **flooding-prevention** device: it must be **closed at all times at sea** and is the **first item checked** by the chief officer during a forepeak inspection.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Aftpeak

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["STERN FRAME\nAftermost Structural Member"] --> nodeB["RUDDER TRUNK\nWatertight Tube"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["TILLER / QUADRANT\nSteering Gear Flat"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["EMERGENCY STEERING\nIndependent System"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["PROPELLER SHAFT\nStern Tube Passage"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["STERN TUBE BEARING\nWhite Metal Lined"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["LUB OIL TANK\nGravity Feed Reservoir"]
    nodeC --> nodeH["PEAK BULKHEAD\nBulkhead Z"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["AFT ENGINE ROOM\nor PUMP ROOM"]
    nodeC --> nodeJ["SOUNDING PIPE"]
    nodeC --> nodeK["AIR PIPE"]
    nodeC --> nodeL["BILGE SUCTION"]
    nodeC --> nodeM["ACCESS MANHOLE\nAbove WL"]
    nodeC --> nodeN["AFTPEAK VOID\nor SEWAGE TANK\nIn Conversion Mode"]
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- The **aftpeak** is dominated by the **steering gear flat** and the **propeller shaft passage**. Any conversion in this region must respect the **maintenance envelope** of these two systems.
- The **stern tube bearing** requires a continuous flow of **lubricating oil** or **seawater**; the **lub oil gravity tank** is fitted high in the aftpeak so that the oil flows by gravity to the bearing even in a **blackout** condition.
- The **peak bulkhead** is the aftermost transverse watertight bulkhead, separating the aftpeak from the machinery space. In a **double-bottom tanker**, this bulkhead is the **forward boundary of the after peak tank** which forms part of the segregated ballast system.

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Decision Flow for Forepeak Conversion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start: New Conversion Project\nExisting Forepeak Identified"] --> check1{"Is forepeak a\nchain locker only?"}
    check1 -- "Yes" --> conv1["Convert to Chain Locker\n+ Mooring Store\n+ Optional Forepeak Ballast Tank"]
    check1 -- "No" --> check2{"Is ship an\nOil Tanker?"}
    check2 -- "Yes" --> conv2["Convert to Segregated\nBallast Tank\nMARPOL Annex I\nCompliant"]
    check2 -- "No" --> check3{"Is ship a\nPassenger Vessel?"}
    check3 -- "Yes" --> conv3["Convert to Forepeak Store\n+ Emergency Equipment Locker"]
    check3 -- "No" --> conv4["Convert to Fresh Water Tank\nor Lub Oil Tank\nor Void with Sounding"]
    conv1 --> verify["Verification:\nSOLAS Reg. II-1/12, 15, 20\nIACS S21\nClass Approval Required"]
    conv2 --> verify
    conv3 --> verify
    conv4 --> verify
    verify --> done["End: Approved\nConversion Plan"]
```

This decision-flow diagram summarises the **three principal conversion pathways** for a forepeak and the regulatory checks that must be satisfied at the end of every pathway.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the arrangement of a forepeak tank in a bulk carrier."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The forepeak of a bulk carrier is the compartment at the extreme forward end of the ship, bounded forward by the **stem plate** and aft by the **collision bulkhead**. In a typical bulk carrier, the forepeak contains the **chain lockers** (port and starboard) for the **anchor chain cables**, a **forepeak store** for mooring ropes and consumables, and a **sounding pipe** terminating at a self-closing cock on the forecastle deck. The **air pipe** with a gooseneck head is fitted on the forward side of the collision bulkhead, and a **bilge suction** connects to the main bilge manifold. A **watertight access manhole** (600 mm × 400 mm oval) is provided on the collision bulkhead above the load waterline. The chain cables pass through **hawse pipes** in the shell plating and are controlled by the **windlass** and **chain stoppers** on the forecastle deck. **Emergency release of the chain is via the bitter end**, a quick-release shackle fitted inside the chain locker. *(Mark distribution: 1 mark for description, 1 mark for sketch components, 1 mark for regulation reference.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"List the four principal items of equipment housed in the aftpeak of a ship and state the function of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Steering Gear Flat —** Houses the **tiller, quadrant, and steering gear** which transmit the rudder commands from the bridge to the rudder stock. Function: mechanical conversion of bridge orders into rudder movement.
2. **Emergency Steering Gear —** An **independent, manually or electrically operated** steering system located in the aftpeak that takes over if the main steering gear fails. Function: ensures the ship remains steerable in a steering-gear casualty.
3. **Stern Tube Lubricating Oil Tank —** A **gravity-feed reservoir** that supplies lubrication oil to the stern tube bearing. Function: ensures continuous lubrication of the propeller shaft bearing even during a **blackout**.
4. **Stern Frame / Rudder Trunk —** The **watertight structural housing** for the rudder stock. Function: provides a watertight passage for the rudder stock from the steering gear flat to the rudder.

*(1.5 marks for the four items correctly named; 1.5 marks for functions.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

**Choose ONE of the following questions:**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a) Describe in detail the structural arrangement of a forepeak compartment of a ship, with the help of a sketch. List the regulatory requirements for the position of the collision bulkhead. (7 marks)**

**(b) During a ship conversion, a bulk carrier is to be fitted with a bow thruster. Discuss the structural and regulatory implications of cutting a tunnel through the forepeak, including the SOLAS position requirements for the collision bulkhead. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The forepeak of a ship is the compartment located at the extreme forward end, forward of the collision bulkhead. The principal structural elements are:

- **Stem Plate / Bar:** A vertical or inclined plate section forming the forward-most structural member of the hull. It absorbs the impact loads during bow collisions and connects the port and starboard shell plates.
- **Shell Plating:** The outer hull plating which curves upward and inward at the bow (the **bow flare**). The **flare knuckle** is the line where the flare meets the vertical side shell.
- **Collision Bulkhead:** A transverse watertight bulkhead fitted at a distance from the forward perpendicular as required by SOLAS Reg. II-1/12. It extends from the **inner bottom** to the **freeboard deck**.
- **Inner Bottom / Tank Top:** The lower horizontal plating, which is sloped upward toward the stem (the **rise of floor**) to allow drainage and to provide structural continuity.
- **Forecastle Deck / Main Deck:** The upper plating which, on a typical bulker, is the **forecastle deck** raised one deck above the main deck.

**Regulatory requirements for the collision bulkhead (SOLAS II-1/12):**

1. It shall be **watertight up to the freeboard deck**.
2. It shall be located at a distance from the forward perpendicular of not less than **0.05 L** or **10 m**, whichever is less.
3. It shall be located at a distance from the forward perpendicular of not more than **0.05 L + 5 m**.
4. No **doors, manholes, or other openings** shall be provided in the collision bulkhead, except for a **sparingly used watertight door** of the sliding type in ships of 120 m or less in length, fitted above the bulkhead deck.
5. The number of **pipe penetrations** shall be kept to a minimum, and each shall be fitted with a **screw-down valve** operable from above the freeboard deck.

*(1 mark for description, 2 marks for sketch components, 2 marks for SOLAS rules 1–3, 2 marks for SOLAS rules 4–5.)*

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

When a bow thruster is to be installed during a bulk-carrier conversion, the forepeak is the natural location for the **thruster tunnel** because the thruster requires access to the sea on both sides of the hull.

**Structural implications:**

- A **circular or oval tunnel** of typical diameter 2.0–3.0 m is cut through the forepeak shell plating.
- The tunnel must be **reinforced with doubler plates** at the hull penetrations to maintain the local plate strength.
- The **inner bottom of the forepeak** must be modified to clear the thruster's lower half.
- A **ring-seal** or **grommet** is fitted at the hull penetration to prevent stress concentrations.
- The thruster body is supported on **pedestals** welded to the forepeak structure.

**Regulatory implications:**

1. **SOLAS II-1/12 position check:** The bow-thruster tunnel aperture must be positioned such that the **closest point of the tunnel to the forward perpendicular** satisfies the collision-bulkhead distance rule. This means the tunnel's forward face must be at least **10 m (or 0.05 L)** from the FP.

For a ship of $L = 180$ m:
- $d_{min} = \min(0.05 \times 180, 10) = \min(9, 10) = 9$ m.
- $d_{max} = 0.05 \times 180 + 5 = 14$ m.
- Therefore the tunnel must lie in the range 9 m $\leq x_{tunnel} \leq$ 14 m from the FP.

2. **Bilge and drainage:** A new **bilge well** must be provided under the thruster, connected to the forepeak bilge system.

3. **Sounding and air pipes:** The forepeak must be re-fitted with new sounding and air pipes terminating at accessible locations.

4. **Class approval:** The conversion drawings must be **approved by the classification society** (e.g., IRS, LR, DNV) before fabrication.

5. **Stability review:** The added weight of the thruster (typically 5–15 tonnes) and the loss of buoyancy due to the tunnel aperture must be accounted for in the **stability booklet**.

*(2 marks for structural implications, 3 marks for SOLAS position calculation, 1 mark for bilge/sounding, 1 mark for class approval and stability.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) With the aid of a sketch, describe the arrangement of the aftpeak of a ship. (7 marks)**

**(b) A 200 m long product tanker is being converted to a chemical tanker. Discuss the modifications required in the aftpeak region to comply with the new service. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The aftpeak of a ship is the compartment at the extreme aft end, aft of the peak bulkhead. The principal elements are:

- **Stern Frame:** The aftermost transverse structure, made of cast steel or fabricated plate. It supports the **rudder gudgeons** and the **propeller shaft boss** (or A-bracket).
- **Peak Bulkhead (Bulkhead Z):** A transverse watertight bulkhead separating the aftpeak from the machinery space or pump room.
- **Shaft Tunnel Bulkhead:** A longitudinal structure (where applicable) that encloses the propeller shaft as it penetrates the peak bulkhead.
- **Stern Tube:** A bearing housing containing the **white-metal lined bearings** for the propeller shaft. It is sealed at the forward end by a **stuffing box** or **air seal** and at the after end by an **air seal** (oil-lubricated systems).
- **Rudder Trunk:** A watertight tube through which the **rudder stock** passes from the steering gear flat to the rudder.
- **Steering Gear Flat:** The compartment directly above the rudder trunk containing the **tiller, quadrant, and steering gear**.
- **Sounding, Air, and Bilge Pipes:** Standard 50 mm sounding pipe, 50–100 mm air pipe, and 65 mm bilge suction.

**Sketch components to be marked:** Stern frame, peak bulkhead, rudder trunk, steering gear flat, propeller shaft, stern tube, tiller, sounding pipe, air pipe, bilge suction. *(2 marks for textual description, 3 marks for labelled sketch, 2 marks for functional interconnections.)*

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

When a 200 m product tanker is being converted to a chemical tanker, the aftpeak is one of the spaces requiring substantial modification, primarily because the new service requires **segregation** of incompatible cargoes and **dedicated ballast management**.

**Required modifications:**

1. **Cofferdam arrangements:** A **cofferdam** of at least **600 mm** (per IBC Code Ch. 4) shall be provided between the aftpeak and any tank carrying a chemical cargo. This is a double-plate void space that prevents heating or leakage from the chemical tank reaching the rudder trunk.

2. **Independent ballast capability:** The aftpeak may be converted into a **segregated ballast tank** (SBT) with:
   - New **sounding pipe** (50 mm ID).
   - New **air pipe** with gooseneck head.
   - **Dedicated ballast pump suction** (separate from the cargo pump).
   - **Drop test** of the new piping to **1.5 × design pressure** for 30 minutes.

3. **Coating system:** The internal surfaces of the aftpeak tank, if used for ballast, shall be coated with an **epoxy or polyurethane** coating system meeting **PSPC** (Performance Standard for Protective Coatings) requirements of **15-year target service life**.

4. **Stern tube sealing:** Because the chemical tanker may carry cargoes that are **incompatible with oil** (e.g., amines, acids), the **stern tube** must be fitted with a **sealed air-type** or **water-lubricated** system to prevent any oil leakage into the sea.

5. **Access modifications:** The existing access manhole to the aftpeak must be relocated above the **bulkhead deck** to ensure **progressive flooding protection** per SOLAS II-1/9.

6. **Stability and damage stability:** The new tank arrangement must be reflected in the **stability booklet** and the **damage stability calculations** performed per SOLAS II-1/25.

7. **Class and flag approval:** The conversion must be approved by the **classification society** (e.g., IRS, LR) and the **flag state administration** before the ship is permitted to load the first chemical cargo.

*(1 mark for cofferdam, 1 mark for SBT, 1 mark for coating, 1 mark for stern tube, 1 mark for access, 1 mark for stability, 1 mark for class approval.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marks-losing mistakes on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "10 m or 0.05 L, whichever is LESS" rule** — Many students write $d_{min} = 0.05 L$ without considering the 10 m cap. For ships below 200 m, the 10 m cap always applies. **(−2 marks typical)**
> 2. **Confusing the "bulkhead" with the "stem"** — The forepeak is forward of the **collision bulkhead**, not forward of the stem. The stem is the forward shell structure, not a bulkhead. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Drawing the rudder inside the steering gear flat** — The rudder is **below** the steering gear flat, separated by the rudder trunk. Many sketches show the rudder in the wrong compartment. **(−2 marks)**
> 4. **Omitting the "bitter end"** — When describing the chain locker, the **bitter end** is the most-missed safety feature. Examiners specifically test this because it is the **last-resort** release in an emergency. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **Failing to mention MARPOL/IBC compliance** — In conversion questions, simply stating "modify the aftpeak" is insufficient. The examiner expects explicit citation of **MARPOL Annex I** (for tankers) or **IBC Code Chapter 4** (for chemical tankers). **(−2 marks)**

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Forepeak and Aftpeak Arrangements:**
> - The **forepeak** is the compartment **forward of the collision bulkhead**; the **aftpeak** is **aft of the peak bulkhead**.
> - The **collision bulkhead** position is **0.05 L (or 10 m, whichever is less) ≤ d ≤ 0.05 L + 5 m** from the forward perpendicular (SOLAS II-1/12).
> - The **collision bulkhead** has **no doors**, **no manholes**, and **no openings** below the freeboard deck (a sparingly used sliding door is permitted only in ships ≤ 120 m).
> - The **forepeak** typically houses **chain lockers** (with bitter end, spurling pipe, sluice valve), **forepeak store**, and (in conversion) **fresh water/ballast tank**.
> - The **aftpeak** typically houses the **steering gear flat**, **emergency steering gear**, **stern tube lubrication tank**, and **rudder trunk**.
> - **Access manholes** must be **above the load waterline** and of minimum size **600 mm × 400 mm oval** (IACS S21).
> - **Sounding pipe** diameter is **50 mm** (main) and **38 mm** (branch); air pipe head is **760 mm above the deck** for non-weathertight types.
> - **Bilge suctions** in peaks are typically **65 mm ID** with non-return valves to the main bilge manifold.
> - During a **bow thruster conversion**, the thruster tunnel must satisfy the SOLAS collision-bulkhead position rule.
> - During a **chemical tanker conversion**, **cofferdams of ≥ 600 mm** are required between the aftpeak and chemical tanks per **IBC Code Ch. 4**.
> - All peak conversion works require **class society approval** and **flag state endorsement** before re-commissioning.
> - The **bitter end** (chain locker), **emergency steering** (aftpeak), and **cofferdam** (chemical tanker conversion) are the three "signature" answers that examiners reward when mentioned explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anchor and Anchor Handling Arrangements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Anchor and Anchor Handling Arrangements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **anchor** is a heavy, forged or cast steel device permanently fitted at the bow (and sometimes stern) of a vessel, designed to be dropped on the seabed and to grip the bottom by its flukes, thereby holding the ship in position against the combined forces of wind, current, and wave action. The **anchor handling arrangement** is the integrated system of equipment used to stow, deploy (let go), weigh (heave up), and secure the anchor — comprising the **windlass**, **hawse pipes**, **chain lockers**, **chain cables**, **spurling pipes**, **bitter ends**, and associated control mechanisms, all governed by the requirements of classification societies such as **IRS (Indian Register of Shipping)**, **Lloyd's Register**, **ABS**, and **DNV**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PENST415 – Module 1):**
> The anchor and its handling arrangement are the *first line of stationary defense* of any vessel. Classification rules mandate a minimum **Number of anchors = 3** (two bower anchors at the bow + one spare/stern anchor), with **bower chain length ≈ 12.5 shackles** per side, and **spare chain length ≈ 4.5 shackles**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Underwater Hand Brake" of a Ship

Imagine you are standing on a smooth, windswept glass floor (the sea) and you need to stay in one spot without slipping. What would you do? You would throw down a heavy multi-pronged hook that **buries itself into the surface** the moment it hits and grabs — the more the wind blows, the harder it digs in.

That is exactly what an anchor does:
- The **flukes** (the spade-like arms) act like fishhooks — they dig into mud, sand, or clay.
- The **shank** is the long backbone that transmits the pull of the ship to the flukes.
- The **stock** forces the flukes to rotate downward so they catch the seabed.
- The **windlass** is the "winch" that lowers and lifts this heavy hook — like a fishing reel but for multi-tonne equipment.

The **chain cable** is not just a rope; it acts as a **horizontal spring** lying on the seabed, providing the *catenary* that absorbs the tugging force of the ship. Without the chain, a rope alone would snap under shock loads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Holding Metrics (KTU Board Data):**
> - Typical **anchor mass** for a 30,000 DWT bulk carrier: **6,500 kg** (Hall or AC-14 type).
> - **Studded chain** diameter: **52 mm to 70 mm** (U2/U3 grade, proof load ≈ 0.45 × breaking load).
> - **Holding power** ≈ 3 to 5 × the anchor's dry weight in good holding ground (mud/clay).

## 1.3 Role of Anchors in Ship Conversion

During **ship conversion** (the focus course PENST415), the anchor handling arrangement is often re-engineered because:
- The vessel's **new operational profile** (e.g., converting a tanker to an FPSO) demands heavier or different anchor types.
- **Dynamic Positioning (DP)** or **mooring spread** retrofits may require abandoning the traditional windlass.
- **Increased DWT** after conversion increases the **equipment number (EN)**, demanding larger anchors.
- Classification rules re-classify the vessel, triggering fresh **anchor & chain** calculations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Conversion Trigger — Equipment Number (EN):**
> $$\text{EN} = \Delta^{2/3} + 2 \cdot h \cdot B + 0.1 \cdot A$$
> Where $\Delta$ = displacement, $h$ = height of head, $B$ = beam, $A$ = projected windage. Any change in these post-conversion mandates re-anchoring per IRS Pt.3, Ch.5.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Marine Anchors — Taxonomy

Modern anchors fall into two broad categories:

### A. **Stocked Anchors** (older, lower holding power)
| Type | Distinguishing Feature | Holding Power (× Weight) |
|------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Admiralty Pattern** | Stocked, articulated shank, tripping palms | 2 – 3 |
| **Herreshoff** | Stocked, fluke-shank articulated | 2 – 3 |
| **Bruce** | Stockless, claw-type, single fluke | 4 – 6 |

### B. **Stockless Anchors** (modern, high holding power, no stock)
| Type | Distinguishing Feature | Holding Power (× Weight) |
|------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Hall Anchor** | Articulated flukes at the head of the shank | 3 – 4 |
| **AC-14 (US Navy)** | High-efficiency, twin flukes, broad palms | 6 – 8 |
| **Spek Anchor** | Conical flukes, fast penetration | 7 – 10 |
| **D'Hone** | Plough-type, seabed ploughing action | 8 – 12 |
| **Flipper Delta** | Delta-shaped, rotates flat, fast dig-in | 8 – 12 |
| **Pool-TW (Plow-Twin)** | Twin plough blades, ideal for soft mud | 10 – 14 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stockless anchors** are the **KTU board default** for any modern ship. They retract into the **hawse pipe**, leaving a flush deck — critical for **bulwark clearances** and **freesboard** post-conversion.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Working Principle (How an Anchor "Bites")

A KTU-favorite analytical topic — the **dig-in sequence** of a stockless anchor:

1. **Free-Fall:** Anchor is released from the **hawse pipe** and falls under gravity. Speed at seabed ≈ 7 m/s for a 5-tonne anchor dropped from 20 m hawse height.
2. **Rotation:** As the anchor strikes, the **crown** is forced down by the shank's centre of gravity being above the fluke pivot, causing the **flukes** to rotate **forward and downward**.
3. **Penetration:** The flukes plough into the seabed. Penetration depth depends on soil — clay: 1–2 m; sand: 0.5–1.5 m; mud: 2–4 m.
4. **Dig-In Lock:** The moment the load comes on the **shank** (from the chain), the **palm** of the fluke rotates to present its **maximum projected area** to the soil, generating passive earth pressure resistance.
5. **Steady State:** Holding force = Passive soil resistance (function of fluke area, soil cohesion, surcharge depth) + Friction along chain catenary.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

| # | Formula / Concept | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Equipment Number** $\text{EN} = \Delta^{2/3} + 2hB + 0.1A$ | $\Delta$ in t, $h, B$ in m, $A$ in m² | Determines anchor mass & chain size |
| 2 | **Anchor Mass** $W = k \cdot \text{EN}$ (k from classification table) | $W$ in kg | IRS/LR Pt.3 anchor tables |
| 3 | **Chain Breaking Load** $L_b = 0.0206 \cdot d^2 \cdot (44 + 0.08 \cdot \text{GR})$ | $d$ in mm, GR = grade (U1/U2/U3) | Grade-specific strength |
| 4 | **Chain Proof Load** $L_p = 0.45 \cdot L_b$ | | Hydraulic test load |
| 5 | **Holding Power** $F_h = n \cdot W$ (n = 3 to 12 by soil & type) | $F_h$ in kN, $W$ in kN | Design check on seabed |
| 6 | **Catenary Sag** $s = w \cdot L^2 / (8 \cdot H)$ | $w$ = chain wt/m, $L$ = length, $H$ = horizontal pull | Mooring line profile |
| 7 | **Catenary Horizontal Force** $H = w \cdot s^2 / (2 \cdot h)$ | $h$ = vertical catenary height | Static equilibrium |
| 8 | **Wind Force on Ship** $F_w = 0.5 \cdot C_d \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot A$ | $C_d \approx 1.2$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ | Environmental load |
| 9 | **Current Force** $F_c = 0.5 \cdot C_c \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S$ | $S$ = submerged lateral area | Combined environmental load |
| 10 | **Required Holding Capacity** $F_R = 1.5 \times (\text{combined env. load})$ | Factor of Safety = **1.5** (KTU default) | Final anchor selection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions & Critical Constants:**
> - Standard chain **stud diameter = 1.5 × chain diameter (d)**.
> - **Shackle length = 27.5 × d** (IRS rule).
> - **Minimum 3 anchors** on every ocean-going vessel.
> - **Bower chain length** = 12.5 shackles/side; **spare** = 4.5 shackles.
> - Grade **U2** breaking stress = **490 N/mm²**; Grade **U3** = **690 N/mm²**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **ship conversion projects**, the anchor handling arrangement determines:
- **Conversion feasibility** when the bow is restructured.
- **Deck reinforcement** for heavier windlass loads (often retrofitted with **electro-hydraulic** units replacing steam-driven ones).
- **Chain locker redesign** for chain of a different grade.
- **Compliance with new operations** — e.g., FPSO conversion may demand **taut-leg mooring** with **suction pile anchors** instead of traditional drag anchors.
- **Safety during heavy weather** operations in the new role.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Equipment Number and Anchor Mass

The **Equipment Number (EN)** is the dimensionless (well, semi-empirical) parameter specified by classification societies to size anchors and chains uniformly. We derive it step by step.

### Step 1: Starting from First Principles
A vessel experiences three major environmental forces when moored:
1. **Wind force** on the superstructure (proportional to projected area $A$).
2. **Current force** on the underwater hull (proportional to displacement $\Delta$).
3. **Wave-induced surge** on the hull (proportional to displacement × form factor).

Classification societies have empirically bundled these into the equation:

$$
\text{EN} = \Delta^{2/3} + 2 \cdot h \cdot B + 0.1 \cdot A
$$

### Step 2: Deriving Anchor Mass from EN
Once EN is known, anchor mass is read from classification tables. For **IRS Rule Table 5.2**, the relationship is approximately:

$$
W_{\text{anchor}} = k(\text{EN})
$$

Where $k$ is a tabulated factor. For EN in the range **50 – 5000**, the empirical fit is:

$$
W \approx 6.8 \cdot \text{EN}^{0.7} \quad \text{(in kg)}
$$

### Step 3: Deriving Chain Diameter from EN
The chain diameter is selected from classification tables such that:

$$
d = c \cdot \text{EN}^{1/3}
$$

Where $c$ ≈ 1.9 for **U2 grade** and 1.7 for **U3 grade** (smaller diameter for higher grade).

### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A bulk carrier is being converted to a 35,000 DWT vessel. Given:
> - Displacement $\Delta = 41,000$ t
> - Height from summer load waterline to top of accommodation $h = 18$ m
> - Breadth $B = 32$ m
> - Projected windage area $A = 1,100$ m²
>
> Find: **(a)** Equipment Number, **(b)** Required anchor mass, **(c)** Chain diameter (U2 grade).

#### Part (a) — Equipment Number

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EN} &= \Delta^{2/3} + 2 \cdot h \cdot B + 0.1 \cdot A \\[4pt]
&= (41{,}000)^{2/3} + 2 \times 18 \times 32 + 0.1 \times 1{,}100 \\[4pt]
&= (41{,}000)^{0.6667} + 1{,}152 + 110
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing $(41{,}000)^{2/3}$:
- $\ln(41{,}000) = 10.621$
- $0.6667 \times 10.621 = 7.0809$
- $e^{7.0809} = 1{,}188.7$

Therefore:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EN} &= 1{,}188.7 + 1{,}152 + 110 \\[4pt]
&= 2{,}450.7 \quad \text{(rounded to } 2{,}451\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — Anchor Mass

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 6.8 \cdot \text{EN}^{0.7} \\[4pt]
&= 6.8 \times (2{,}451)^{0.7}
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing $(2{,}451)^{0.7}$:
- $\ln(2{,}451) = 7.805$
- $0.7 \times 7.805 = 5.464$
- $e^{5.464} = 235.7$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 6.8 \times 235.7 \\[4pt]
&\approx 1{,}603 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Standard anchor mass to use (next size up): **1,650 kg** (Hall type).

#### Part (c) — Chain Diameter

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= 1.9 \cdot \text{EN}^{1/3} \\[4pt]
&= 1.9 \times (2{,}451)^{1/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing $(2{,}451)^{1/3}$:
- $\ln(2{,}451) = 7.805$
- $7.805 / 3 = 2.602$
- $e^{2.602} = 13.49$

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= 1.9 \times 13.49 \\[4pt]
&\approx 25.6 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Standard size: **26 mm U2 studded chain** (next standard size up).

## 3.2 Derivation of Holding Capacity with Safety Factor

The **environmental force** on the ship is:

$$
F_{\text{env}} = F_w + F_c
$$

Where:
- $F_w = 0.5 \cdot C_d \cdot \rho \cdot V_w^2 \cdot A$
- $F_c = 0.5 \cdot C_c \cdot \rho \cdot V_c^2 \cdot S$

The **required holding capacity** (KTU design rule) is:

$$
F_R = 1.5 \cdot F_{\text{env}}
$$

The **anchor's available holding** is:

$$
F_h = n \cdot W
$$

**Condition for safety:**
$$
F_h \geq F_R \quad \Rightarrow \quad n \cdot W \geq 1.5 \cdot (F_w + F_c)
$$

Solving for the **minimum number of anchors** (typically 2 bower anchors share the load):

$$
n_{\text{each}} \geq \frac{1.5 \cdot (F_w + F_c)}{2 \cdot W}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix for Anchor Selection (Post-Conversion)

| Seabed Type | Best Anchor Type | $n$ (holding/weight) | Notes |
|-------------|------------------|----------------------|-------|
| Soft mud (silt, clay) | **Pool-TW / D'Hone** | 10 – 14 | Deep penetration |
| Sand | **AC-14 / Hall** | 4 – 6 | Moderate depth |
| Coral / rocky | **Bruce / Flipper Delta** | 3 – 5 | Limited penetration |
| Hard clay | **Spek / AC-14** | 5 – 7 | Good grip |
| Mixed | **Flipper Delta** | 6 – 8 | Versatile |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Anchor Handling System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bridge Command Console] --> B[Windlass Control Lever]
    A2[Local Deck Control] --> B
    B --> C[Brake Band Assembly]
    C --> D[Wildcat / Cable Lifter]
    D --> E[Chain Cable]
    E --> F[Hawse Pipe]
    F --> G[Anchor on Seabed]
    D --> H[Spurling Pipe]
    H --> I[Chain Locker]
    I --> J[Bitter End Shackle]
    J --> K[Steel Deck Beam Foundation]
    L[Capstan Engine Motor] --> C
    M[Gypsy Wheel] --> D
    N[Wire Rope Drum] --> D
    subgraph SafetyInterlock
        O[Brake Release Solenoid]
        P[Limit Switch at Hawse]
        Q[Chain Stopper]
    end
    O --> C
    P --> A
    Q --> E
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Anchor Component Nomenclature

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SH[Shank] --> CR[Crown]
    SH --> TH[Throat]
    SH --> AR[Arms]
    AR --> FL[Flukes]
    FL --> PL[Palms]
    SH --> RS[Ring or Shackle Eye]
    RS --> CB[Chain Cable Connection]
    FL -.-> HV[Hover - Lifting Face]
    PL -.-> DG[Digging Edge - Penetration Tip]
    SH --> TR[Trunk - Shaft Body]
    CR --> CB2[Crown Pin and Bush]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Force Transfer Path During Anchoring

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Wind Force on Superstructure] --> ENV[Total Environmental Load]
    A2[Current Force on Hull] --> ENV
    A3[Wave Drift Force] --> ENV
    ENV --> SHIP[Ship Hull Reactions]
    SHIP --> CHN[Chain Catenary Tension]
    CHN --> SHK[Shank Axial Load]
    SHK --> FLK[Fluke Penetration]
    FLK --> SOIL[Passive Soil Resistance]
    SOIL --> HOLD[Holding Force Generated]
    CHN --> CAT[Catenary Sag - Energy Absorption]
    CAT --> HOLDB[Additional Holding via Weight]
    HOLD --> BAL[Force Balance Achieved]
    HOLDB --> BAL
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Windlass Operational Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Mode Selector Lever] --> M1[Heaving Up Mode]
    M --> M2[Lowering Mode]
    M --> M3[Brake Holding Mode]
    M --> M4[Warping Capstan Mode]
    M1 --> W1[Wildcat Engaged, Brake Released]
    M2 --> W2[Wildcat Free, Brake Band Controlled Drag]
    M3 --> W3[Wildcat Locked, Brake Applied]
    M4 --> W4[Capstan Engaged, Wildcat Locked]
    W1 --> OUT1[Anchor Retrieved on Deck]
    W2 --> OUT2[Anchor Lowered to Seabed]
    W3 --> OUT3[Anchor Secured at Hawse]
    W4 --> OUT4[Mooring Line Tensioned]
```

## 4.5 Conversion Project Flow (Anchor Re-engineering Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Conversion Inception] --> P2[New Operational Profile Defined]
    P2 --> P3[New Displacement and Windage Calculated]
    P3 --> P4[Equipment Number Recomputed]
    P4 --> P5{Is EN Larger than Original?}
    P5 -->|Yes| P6[Anchor and Chain Re-Specification]
    P5 -->|No| P7[Reuse Existing with Class Approval]
    P6 --> P8[Windlass Power Audit]
    P8 --> P9[Structural Reinforcement at Hawse Pipe]
    P9 --> P10[Chain Locker Resizing]
    P10 --> P11[Class Survey and Drop Test]
    P11 --> P12[Sea Trial Verification]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: List and briefly explain the four principal components of a stockless anchor with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four principal components are:
1. **Shank** (1 mark) — The main longitudinal shaft connecting the arms to the ring/shackle. It transmits the pull from the chain to the flukes.
2. **Arms with Flukes** (1 mark) — The two arms project from the crown end of the shank, terminating in broad, pointed flukes (palms) that dig into the seabed.
3. **Crown** (0.5 mark) — The junction where the shank meets the arms; houses the crown pin/bush allowing fluke rotation.
4. **Ring or Shackle Eye** (0.5 mark) — The end of the shank fitted with a ring for attaching the chain cable via a detachable link or shackle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stock** is absent in stockless anchors — the head of the shank itself acts as the stabiliser, allowing the flukes to rotate via the crown pin.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the function of the spurling pipe and chain locker in an anchor handling system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Spurling Pipe** (1.5 marks): A vertical or inclined pipe fitted on the forecastle deck, leading down to the chain locker. It guides the chain cable from the windlass into the locker and prevents chain "kinking" or jumping out during paying out.
- **Chain Locker** (1.5 marks): A dedicated watertight compartment below the forecastle, sized to stow the entire length of bower chain (~12.5 shackles/side, total ~25 shackles). It has a **bitter end** securing shackle fixed to a structural pad eye, ensuring the chain cannot be lost overboard.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a schematic diagram, describe the construction and working of a **windlass** used for handling the anchor on board a ship. Discuss its different operational modes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a vessel undergoing conversion to a 42,000 DWT bulk carrier, calculate the **(i) Equipment Number**, **(ii) Anchor mass**, and **(iii) Studded chain diameter (U2 grade)** using the following data:
- Displacement $\Delta$ = 49,000 t
- Head height $h$ = 19 m
- Beam $B$ = 33 m
- Projected windage area $A$ = 1,250 m² **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — Windlass Construction & Working

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
- A windlass is mounted on the **forecastle deck**, with its base secured to a heavily reinforced **deck beam foundation** transferring load to the hull girder.
- Main shaft carries **two wildcats** (one for each side), a **centre capstan**, and a **gypsy wheel** in some designs.
- Power is transmitted via **electric motor + gearbox** (modern) or **steam engine** (older).
- **Band brake** wraps around the brake wheel for controlled lowering.
- A **clutch** couples the motor to the shaft.
- **Chain stoppers** (movable jaws) grip the chain when the windlass is at rest.

**Working & Modes (3.5 marks):**
1. **Heaving Up Mode:** Motor drives wildcat; chain is hauled in; flukes disengage seabed; anchor is pulled up the hawse pipe and seated on the bill board.
2. **Lowering Mode:** Brake band is released, gravity pulls chain through the wildcat; the wildcat rotates freely; the anchor falls to the seabed.
3. **Brake Holding Mode:** Wildcat is locked; brake applied; chain is held stationary, e.g., during workboat lowering.
4. **Warping Mode:** Capstan is engaged separately to tension mooring lines or heave in wires without disturbing the chain.

#### Model Solution to (b) — Equipment Number & Anchor Sizing

**Step 1: Equipment Number** (2 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EN} &= \Delta^{2/3} + 2hB + 0.1A \\
&= (49{,}000)^{2/3} + 2 \times 19 \times 33 + 0.1 \times 1{,}250
\end{aligned}
$$

- $(49{,}000)^{2/3}$: $\ln(49{,}000) = 10.799$; $0.6667 \times 10.799 = 7.199$; $e^{7.199} = 1{,}339.0$
- $2 \times 19 \times 33 = 1{,}254$
- $0.1 \times 1{,}250 = 125$

$$
\text{EN} = 1{,}339 + 1{,}254 + 125 = 2{,}718
$$

**Step 2: Anchor Mass** (2.5 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 6.8 \cdot \text{EN}^{0.7} \\
&= 6.8 \times (2{,}718)^{0.7}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $\ln(2{,}718) = 7.907$; $0.7 \times 7.907 = 5.535$; $e^{5.535} = 253.4$

$$
W = 6.8 \times 253.4 = 1{,}723 \text{ kg}
$$

**Standard next size: 1,800 kg Hall-type stockless anchor.**

**Step 3: Chain Diameter (U2 grade)** (2.5 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= 1.9 \cdot \text{EN}^{1/3} \\
&= 1.9 \times (2{,}718)^{1/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $\ln(2{,}718) = 7.907$; $7.907/3 = 2.636$; $e^{2.636} = 13.96$

$$
d = 1.9 \times 13.96 = 26.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Standard size: 27 mm U2 studded chain.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the constructional features of a **Hall anchor** and a **AC-14 anchor**. Compare their performance in different seabed conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship is anchored in 25 m of water in soft mud. The bower chain length is 12.5 shackles of 27 mm U2 grade chain. Each shackle is 27.5 m long. Calculate **(i) the catenary sag height** and **(ii) the horizontal holding force** if the chain weight in water is 0.12 kN/m and the ship is subjected to a 200 kN environmental pull. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — Anchor Comparison

**Hall Anchor (3 marks):**
- Invented by John Hall in 1950s; **stockless design** with **articulated flukes**.
- Two large flukes at the head of the shank; no stock bar.
- Flukes rotate about the crown pin; palms are broad and flat.
- Holding power: **3 to 4 × weight**.
- Suitable for **sand and stiff clay**; less efficient in soft mud.

**AC-14 Anchor (3 marks):**
- US Navy standard; high efficiency twin-fluke design.
- **Broad palms** with a unique **curved fluke geometry** for rapid penetration.
- Holding power: **6 to 8 × weight**.
- Excellent in **soft mud and silt**; moderate in sand.

**Comparison (1 mark):**
| Feature | Hall | AC-14 |
|---------|------|-------|
| Holding/weight | 3 – 4 | 6 – 8 |
| Soft mud | Poor | Excellent |
| Sand | Good | Moderate |
| Penetration speed | Slow | Fast |
| Weight for same holding | Heavier | Lighter |

#### Model Solution to (b) — Catenary Calculation

**Given:**
- Water depth: 25 m
- Chain length: $L = 12.5 \times 27.5 = 343.75$ m
- Submerged weight: $w = 0.12$ kN/m
- Environmental force (treated as horizontal at the ship end): $H = 200$ kN

**Step 1: Catenary Sag** (3.5 marks)
Using the catenary equation for a uniform chain:
$$
H = \frac{w \cdot s^2}{2 \cdot h}
$$
where $h$ = vertical distance from seabed touch-point to the hawse (≈ water depth ≈ 25 m), $s$ = horizontal catenary sag.

Rearranging:
$$
s = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot H \cdot h}{w}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 200 \times 25}{0.12}}
$$

$$
s = \sqrt{\frac{10{,}000}{0.12}} = \sqrt{83{,}333.3} = 288.7 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Chain Check** (1 mark)
Total chain required along catenary ≈ $\sqrt{s^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{288.7^2 + 25^2} = \sqrt{83{,}344 + 625} = 289.8$ m. The 343.75 m chain is sufficient with margin of ≈ 53.9 m of slack at the bitter end (suitable for catenary "stretch" and slack).

**Step 3: Horizontal Holding Component from Weight** (1.5 marks)
The catenary's own weight provides a residual horizontal restoring force:
$$
H_{\text{weight}} = \frac{w \cdot s^2}{2 \cdot h} = 200 \text{ kN (by definition, as this matches the applied force)}
$$

**Conclusion:** The catenary is in equilibrium under 200 kN pull with 288.7 m of horizontal catenary. The anchor must provide the *remaining* environmental resistance, i.e., $F_R - H = 0$ in this ideal case (since catenary is sized to absorb the entire load). In practice, with a margin, the anchor holds the residual.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Avoid These!**
> 1. **Confusing Equipment Number units** — EN is *dimensionless*, not a mass. Do not write "EN = 2718 tonnes" — that is a **guaranteed 1-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Forgetting the 0.1A term** in the EN formula — examiners specifically test whether students include *all three* components. Omitting $0.1A$ = **2 marks lost**.
> 3. **Mixing up chain grades (U1, U2, U3)** — U2 has a breaking stress of **490 N/mm²**, not 690. Wrong grade constants lead to wrong diameter.
> 4. **Not rounding up to standard sizes** — anchor mass and chain diameter are *standardised*. Always state the *next standard size up* (e.g., 27 mm chain, not 26.5 mm).
> 5. **Skipping the seabed type** in design questions — holding power depends on soil. A question that says "soft mud" demands a **Pool-TW or AC-14**, not a Hall.
> 6. **Diagrams without labels** — KTU examiners reward neat, *labelled* diagrams. An anchor diagram without "shank/fluke/crown/ring" labelled = **partial credit only**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 1: Anchor and Anchor Handling Arrangements):**
> - **Anchor mass formula:** $W = 6.8 \cdot \text{EN}^{0.7}$ (kg).
> - **Chain diameter formula (U2):** $d = 1.9 \cdot \text{EN}^{1/3}$ (mm).
> - **Equipment Number formula:** $\text{EN} = \Delta^{2/3} + 2hB + 0.1A$.
> - **Minimum 3 anchors** on every ocean-going vessel (2 bower + 1 spare/stern).
> - **Bower chain** = 12.5 shackles per side; **spare chain** = 4.5 shackles.
> - **Shackle length** = $27.5 \times d$.
> - **Proof load** = $0.45 \times$ breaking load.
> - **Stockless anchors** (Hall, AC-14, Pool-TW) are the KTU-mandated modern default.
> - **Holding power** depends on anchor type AND seabed (soft mud favours AC-14/Pool-TW; sand favours Hall).
> - **Windlass components:** Wildcat, brake band, capstan, spurling pipe, chain locker, bitter end.
> - **Windlass modes:** Heaving, Lowering, Brake Holding, Warping (capstan).
> - **Conversion trigger:** Any change in $\Delta$, $h$, $B$, or $A$ mandates EN recomputation and possible re-anchoring.
> - **Factor of Safety on holding capacity** = 1.5 (KTU/IRS standard).
> - **Catenary equations** are critical for understanding how chains absorb shock loads on the seabed.
> - **Conversion projects** often require windlass upgrades (e.g., steam → electro-hydraulic) and structural reinforcement at the hawse pipe region.
> - **Catenary sag check:** Computed $L_{\text{chain}}$ must always exceed $\sqrt{s^2 + h^2}$.
> - **Studded chain** has **studs** at every link to prevent link-kinking and to increase chain stiffness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computation of New Displacement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Computation of New Displacement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Displacement (Δ)** of a ship is defined as the **mass of water (in tonnes)** displaced by the submerged volume of the hull at a given draft, in accordance with Archimedes' principle of buoyancy.

In ship hydrostatics, **Displacement** is mathematically expressed as:

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$$

Where:
- $\Delta$ = Displacement (tonnes, **t**)
- $\rho$ = Mass density of water (tonnes per cubic metre, **t/m³**)
- $\nabla$ = Submerged volume of the hull (cubic metres, **m³**)

**Standard Water Densities (KTU Reference Values):**
- **Salt water (sea water):** $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$
- **Fresh water:** $\rho_{fw} = 1.000 \text{ t/m}^3$
- **Standard sea water (I.S.O.):** $\rho_{std} = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$

### 1.2 Computation of New Displacement — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Computation of New Displacement** is the analytical procedure used to determine the changed displacement volume and weight of a vessel **after it undergoes a structural conversion** — such as lengthening, deepening, widening, insertion of a parallel middlebody, or modification of hull form.

When a ship is converted, the **submerged volume** $\nabla$ changes. Consequently, the displacement $\Delta$ changes, which in turn alters the **draft, trim, stability, freeboard, and deadweight capacity** of the vessel. Computing this new displacement is the **first and most fundamental step** in any ship conversion design process.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Bathtub" Intuition

Imagine a **toy boat floating in a bathtub**. The boat pushes aside a quantity of water equal to its own weight — this is its displacement.

Now, suppose you **glue an extra block onto the bottom** of the boat. Two things happen simultaneously:
1. The boat becomes **heavier** (more weight added).
2. The boat must sink **deeper** so that a larger volume of water is displaced to balance the new weight.

**The "new displacement" is simply that new, larger volume of water × density of water.**

In ship conversion, the engineer doesn't just add weight — he/she **reshapes the hull**. So the new displacement computation must account for **both the volume added/removed AND the resulting change in equilibrium draft**.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Mnemonic:** *"Displacement is a marriage between Geometry (Volume) and Physics (Buoyancy) — change one, and the other adjusts automatically."*

### 1.4 Why Computation of New Displacement is Critical in Ship Conversion

| Reason | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Draft Verification** | Ensures the converted ship does not exceed permissible draft limits |
| **Stability Check** | New GM, GZ curves must be recomputed using new displacement |
| **Powering Estimation** | New resistance and EHP require updated displacement |
| **Statutory Compliance** | IMO load line, freeboard, and tonnage rules depend on new displacement |
| **Deadweight Optimization** | Cargo capacity must be re-evaluated after conversion |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Buoyancy Principle

A floating vessel is in **hydrostatic equilibrium** when:

$$W_{ship} = \Delta = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla_{submerged}$$

In marine engineering practice, the gravitational constant $g$ is absorbed into the density unit (tonnes/m³), so the working equation becomes:

$$\Delta \text{ (tonnes)} = \rho \text{ (t/m}^3\text{)} \times \nabla \text{ (m}^3\text{)}$$

### 2.2 The Three Principal Methods of Computing New Displacement

In KTU 2024 Scheme Naval Architecture practice, **three methods** are prescribed for computing new displacement after conversion:

#### **Method 1 — Direct Hydrostatic Method (Simpson's Rules on Offsets)**
The new submerged volume is computed from the modified offset table using **Simpson's 1st Rule** for the longitudinal integration of cross-sectional areas.

#### **Method 2 — Difference / Incremental Method (Most Common in Conversion)**
This is the **most widely used method** in ship conversion projects. It computes the *change* in displacement rather than recomputing from scratch.

$$\Delta_{new} = \Delta_{old} + \Delta_{added} - \Delta_{removed}$$

Where:
- $\Delta_{added}$ = Weight (and equivalent volume) of all **new material added** to the hull
- $\Delta_{removed}$ = Weight (and equivalent volume) of all **material cut away** from the hull

#### **Method 3 — Waterplane Area Integration Method**
The new displacement is computed by integrating the **new waterplane area** $A_W$ from the keel to the new design waterline:

$$\Delta_{new} = \rho \cdot \int_{0}^{T_{new}} A_T(z) \, dz$$

Where $A_T(z)$ is the cross-sectional area at height $z$ above the keel.

### 2.3 Special Case — Insertion of a Parallel Middlebody

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most common KTU exam scenario:** A ship is lengthened by inserting a **parallel middlebody** of length $L_{insert}$ between the existing parallel sections. The new displacement is:

$$\Delta_{new} = \Delta_{old} + \rho \cdot L_{insert} \cdot A_{M}$$

Where:
- $L_{insert}$ = Length of inserted middlebody (m)
- $A_{M}$ = Cross-sectional area of the parallel middlebody at midship (m²)

### 2.4 Special Case — Deepening / Raising of the Deck

When the **draft is increased** by deepening (e.g., raising the deck or lowering the keel):

$$\Delta_{new} = \Delta_{old} + \rho \cdot \Delta V_{added}$$

The additional volume $\Delta V_{added}$ is computed by integrating the added wedge/slab volumes.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | Fundamental displacement equation | tonnes |
| 2 | $\Delta_{new} = \Delta_{old} + \Delta_{add} - \Delta_{rem}$ | Difference method (core conversion formula) | tonnes |
| 3 | $\Delta_{new} = \Delta_{old} + \rho \cdot L_{ins} \cdot A_{M}$ | Parallel middlebody insertion | tonnes |
| 4 | $\Delta_{new} = \rho \cdot \int_{0}^{T} A_T(z)\, dz$ | Waterplane integration | tonnes |
| 5 | $\nabla = \frac{L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B}{}$ (approx.) | Block coefficient approximation | m³ |
| 6 | $\Delta = L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B \cdot \rho$ | Approximate displacement using block coeff. | tonnes |
| 7 | $T_{new} = T_{old} + \frac{W_{add} - W_{rem}}{\rho \cdot A_W}$ | New draft after weight change | metres |
| 8 | $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ | Salt water density (KTU standard) | t/m³ |
| 9 | $\rho_{fw} = 1.000$ | Fresh water density (KTU standard) | t/m³ |
| 10 | $\% \text{ Increase} = \frac{\Delta_{new} - \Delta_{old}}{\Delta_{old}} \times 100$ | Percentage displacement change | % |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The computation of new displacement is the **gateway calculation** in every ship conversion project. It feeds directly into:

1. **Stability Booklet Updating** — Required by SOLAS and classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) post-conversion.
2. **Tonnage Re-measurement** — Affects port dues, registration, and insurance.
3. **Trim & Stability Software Validation** — Used as benchmark input for MAXSUR, NAPA, and AutoHydro.
4. **Conversion Feasibility Studies** — Determines whether the modification yields economically viable cargo capacity uplift.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — New Displacement by Parallel Middlebody Insertion

**Problem Statement:** A ship of original length $L_0 = 120$ m is lengthened by inserting a parallel middlebody of length $L_{ins} = 24$ m. The original displacement is $\Delta_0 = 8,000$ tonnes in salt water. The cross-sectional area at the parallel middlebody is $A_M = 65$ m². Compute the new displacement.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation**

$$\Delta_{new} = \Delta_{old} + \rho \cdot L_{insert} \cdot A_{M}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values**

$$\Delta_{new} = 8000 + (1.025) \cdot (24) \cdot (65)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the volume of the inserted middlebody**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{insert} &= L_{insert} \cdot A_M \\
&= 24 \text{ m} \times 65 \text{ m}^2 \\
&= 1560 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert volume to displacement (mass)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{add} &= \rho \cdot V_{insert} \\
&= 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3 \times 1560 \text{ m}^3 \\
&= 1599 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute final new displacement**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{new} &= \Delta_{old} + \Delta_{add} \\
&= 8000 + 1599 \\
&= 9599 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Compute percentage increase**

$$\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Increase} &= \frac{9599 - 8000}{8000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1599}{8000} \times 100 \\
&= 19.99 \% \approx 20 \%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $\Delta_{new} = 9599$ tonnes (a ~20% increase in displacement).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — New Displacement Using Difference Method (Weight & Volume Reconciliation)

**Problem Statement:** A bulk carrier undergoes conversion: 600 tonnes of steel structure is added amidships, 200 tonnes of existing superstructure is removed, and a 500 m³ cargo hold volume is added below the original waterline. Original displacement = 12,000 tonnes in sea water. Compute new displacement.

**Step 1 — List all mass additions**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{steel} &= +600 \text{ tonnes} \\
W_{hold,structural} &= \rho \cdot V_{hold} = 1.025 \times 500 = 512.5 \text{ tonnes (mass equivalent of added volume)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — List all mass removals**

$$W_{super} = -200 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the difference equation**

$$\Delta_{new} = \Delta_{old} + W_{steel} + W_{hold} - W_{super}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{new} &= 12000 + 600 + 512.5 - 200 \\
&= 12912.5 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to convert the **added volume** (500 m³) into its **mass equivalent** using density. This costs 1–2 marks in KTU valuation.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — New Displacement by Waterplane Area Integration (Simpson's 1st Rule)

**Problem Statement:** After conversion, the cross-sectional areas at 5 stations (0, L/4, L/2, 3L/4, L) from keel to new waterline are:

| Station | 0 | L/4 | L/2 | 3L/4 | L |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A_T$ (m²) | 80 | 110 | 125 | 105 | 70 |

Length of vessel $L = 100$ m. Compute new displacement in salt water using Simpson's 1st Rule.

**Step 1 — Simpson's 1st Rule for volume integration**

$$\nabla = \frac{L}{12} \cdot \left[ A_0 + 4(A_1 + A_3) + 2A_2 + A_4 \right] \cdot (\text{number of ordinates depending setup})$$

**Step 2 — Apply correct Simpson multiplier pattern for 5 ordinates**

$$\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= \frac{L}{12} \cdot \left[ A_0 + 4A_1 + 2A_2 + 4A_3 + A_4 \right] \\
&= \frac{100}{12} \cdot \left[ 80 + 4(110) + 2(125) + 4(105) + 70 \right] \\
&= \frac{100}{12} \cdot \left[ 80 + 440 + 250 + 420 + 70 \right] \\
&= \frac{100}{12} \cdot 1260 \\
&= 10500 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert volume to displacement**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{new} &= \rho \cdot \nabla \\
&= 1.025 \times 10500 \\
&= 10762.5 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Simpson's Multiplier Pattern for 5 ordinates:** 1, 4, 2, 4, 1. Memorize this — KTU frequently tests the **multiplier sequence**, not just the formula.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — New Draft After Conversion (Equilibrium Adjustment)

After the displacement changes, the vessel sinks to a new equilibrium draft. The **change in draft** is approximated by:

$$\Delta T = \frac{\Delta W}{\rho \cdot A_W}$$

Where:
- $\Delta W$ = Net weight change (added − removed) in tonnes
- $A_W$ = Waterplane area at the original waterline (m²)

**Example:** If net weight added = 400 tonnes, $A_W = 1200$ m², $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= \frac{400}{1.025 \times 1200} \\
&= \frac{400}{1230} \\
&= 0.325 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore: $T_{new} = T_{old} + 0.325$ m.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of New Displacement Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Ship Data] --> B[Read L0, B, T0, Delta0]
    B --> C{Identify Conversion Type}
    C -->|Lengthening| D[Parallel Middlebody Insertion]
    C -->|Deepening| E[Deck Raise / Keel Lower]
    C -->|Widening| F[Midship Section Widening]
    C -->|Mixed| G[Combined Modification]
    D --> H[Compute Delta_add = rho x L_ins x A_M]
    E --> I[Compute Delta_add from added wedge volume]
    F --> J[Compute Delta_add from added side volume]
    G --> K[Sum all incremental contributions]
    H --> L[Apply Difference Equation]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Delta_new = Delta_old + Delta_add - Delta_rem]
    M --> N[Compute New Draft using Waterplane Area]
    N --> O[Output Delta_new and T_new]
    O --> P[Validate against Stability Criteria]
    P --> Q[Final Report to Classification Society]
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Parallel Middlebody Insertion (Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Original Stern] --> P2[Original Parallel Body 1]
    P2 --> P3[Inserted Middlebody of Length Lins]
    P3 --> P4[Original Parallel Body 2]
    P4 --> P5[Original Bow]
    P3 -.-> P6[Cross Section AM constant]
```

### 4.3 Logic Flow — Difference Method Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Identify all modifications] --> S2[List all weight additions Wadd]
    S2 --> S3[List all weight removals Wrem]
    S3 --> S4[Compute net weight Delta W]
    S4 --> S5[Compute volume additions Vadd]
    S5 --> S6[Convert volumes to mass using rho]
    S6 --> S7[Compute total Delta add]
    S7 --> S8[Compute total Delta rem]
    S8 --> S9[Apply Delta new = Delta old + Delta add - Delta rem]
    S9 --> S10[Check new draft T new]
    S10 --> S11{Within permissible limits?}
    S11 -->|Yes| S12[Approved Conversion]
    S11 -->|No| S13[Revise Modification]
    S13 --> S1
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Displacement Adjustment Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Initial Condition: Delta0, T0] --> R1[Apply Modifications]
    R1 --> R2[Compute New Geometry]
    R2 --> R3[Compute New Submerged Volume nabla new]
    R3 --> R4[Compute Delta new = rho x nabla new]
    R4 --> R5{Buoyancy equals Weight?}
    R5 -->|No| R6[Adjust T iteratively]
    R6 --> R2
    R5 -->|Yes| R7[Convergence Achieved]
    R7 --> R8[Final Delta new and T new recorded]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define "Computation of New Displacement" in the context of ship conversion. Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**

> Computation of New Displacement refers to the analytical procedure of determining the **changed displacement volume and weight** of a vessel after a structural conversion such as lengthening, deepening, or hull form modification.

> It is necessary because every conversion alters the **submerged hull volume** $\nabla$, which in turn changes the **buoyancy, draft, trim, stability, and statutory freeboard** of the vessel. It is the **first step** in re-validating the converted ship's seaworthiness as per SOLAS, IMO Load Line, and classification society rules. *(3 marks — definition 1, significance 2)*

---

**Q2. State the fundamental equation for displacement and the standard KTU salt water density value.**

**Model Answer:**

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$$

Where:
- $\Delta$ = Displacement (tonnes)
- $\rho$ = Density of water = **1.025 t/m³ (salt water)** or **1.000 t/m³ (fresh water)**
- $\nabla$ = Submerged volume (m³)

This is derived from Archimedes' principle. *(3 marks — equation 1, density values 1, units and principle 1)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, End Semester Examination]**

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A cargo vessel of original length 110 m and displacement 7,500 tonnes (salt water) is lengthened by inserting a parallel middlebody of length 20 m. The cross-sectional area of the parallel middlebody is 60 m². Compute the **new displacement** and the **percentage increase**. State any two engineering implications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the **difference method**, explain with a worked example how new displacement is computed when a ship undergoes simultaneous addition and removal of structural weight. Clearly show the conversion of added volume to mass using $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given data**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_0 &= 110 \text{ m} \\
\Delta_0 &= 7500 \text{ tonnes} \\
L_{ins} &= 20 \text{ m} \\
A_M &= 60 \text{ m}^2 \\
\rho &= 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply parallel middlebody equation**

$$\Delta_{new} = \Delta_0 + \rho \cdot L_{ins} \cdot A_M$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and compute**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{new} &= 7500 + (1.025)(20)(60) \\
&= 7500 + 1230 \\
&= 8730 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage increase**

$$\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Inc} &= \frac{8730 - 7500}{7500} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1230}{7500} \times 100 \\
&= 16.4 \%
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering Implications (any two):**
1. The new draft will increase by approximately $\Delta T = \frac{1230}{1.025 \times A_W}$ — must be verified against permissible limits.
2. Stability booklet must be recomputed; new GM, GZ curves required for classification approval.

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Worked Example:** A vessel (original $\Delta_0 = 10,000$ tonnes) undergoes the following modifications:
- Steel addition: +500 tonnes
- Superstructure removal: −150 tonnes
- Cargo hold volume added below WL: +400 m³

**Step 1 — Convert added volume to mass**

$$\Delta_{add,vol} = \rho \cdot V = 1.025 \times 400 = 410 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 2 — Total additions**

$$\Sigma \Delta_{add} = 500 + 410 = 910 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 3 — Total removals**

$$\Sigma \Delta_{rem} = 150 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 4 — Apply difference equation**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{new} &= \Delta_0 + \Sigma \Delta_{add} - \Sigma \Delta_{rem} \\
&= 10000 + 910 - 150 \\
&= 10760 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the formula for new displacement when a ship is lengthened by insertion of a parallel middlebody. State clearly the assumptions made.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A ship has the following cross-sectional areas at 5 stations (after conversion): $A_0 = 90$ m², $A_1 = 120$ m², $A_2 = 135$ m², $A_3 = 115$ m², $A_4 = 85$ m². Length $L = 120$ m. Compute new displacement using Simpson's 1st Rule in salt water.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Let original ship have length $L_0$, displacement $\Delta_0 = \rho \cdot \nabla_0$.

When a parallel middlebody of length $L_{ins}$ is inserted with constant cross-section $A_M$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\nabla_0 &= \text{Original submerged volume} \\
\nabla_{new} &= \nabla_0 + (L_{ins} \cdot A_M)
\end{aligned}$$

By Archimedes' principle:

$$\Delta_{new} = \rho \cdot \nabla_{new} = \rho \cdot (\nabla_0 + L_{ins} \cdot A_M)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta_{new} = \Delta_0 + \rho \cdot L_{ins} \cdot A_M}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Cross-section $A_M$ is **constant** along the inserted length.
2. Hull is **wall-sided** (no flare) in the inserted region.
3. Waterline does not change significantly (or correction is applied).
4. Salt water density of **1.025 t/m³** is used.

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply Simpson's 1st Rule with multipliers 1, 4, 2, 4, 1**

$$\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= \frac{L}{12} \cdot \left[ A_0 + 4A_1 + 2A_2 + 4A_3 + A_4 \right] \\
&= \frac{120}{12} \cdot \left[ 90 + 4(120) + 2(135) + 4(115) + 85 \right] \\
&= 10 \cdot \left[ 90 + 480 + 270 + 460 + 85 \right] \\
&= 10 \cdot 1385 \\
&= 13850 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}$$
