# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram — Conversion Triggers Re-evaluation of Stability Functions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Existing Ship in Operation] --> B{Conversion Activity?}
    B -- "Yes" --> C[Identify Type of Conversion]
    B -- "No" --> Z[Stability Booklet Remains Valid]
    C --> D1[Lengthening / Shortening]
    C --> D2[Superstructure Addition]
    C --> D3[Tanker to Bulker Conversion]
    C --> D4[New Tank / Machinery Installation]
    D1 --> E[Re-compute Displacement Delta]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Re-compute KG using Moments]
    F --> G[Re-compute KM from New Hydrostatics]
    G --> H[Re-generate Cross Curves of Stability]
    H --> I[Construct Revised GZ Curve]
    I --> J[Evaluate 7 Mandatory Functions]
    J --> K{All Functions Comply?}
    K -- "Yes" --> L[Class Approval: Stability Booklet Updated]
    K -- "No" --> M[Apply Corrective Measure: Ballast or KG Reduction]
    M --> F
    L --> N[Ship Returned to Service]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every conversion event (lengthening, superstructure addition, etc.) loops back to re-compute the displacement and KG, which then cascades to re-generate the GZ curve and re-evaluate the 7 mandatory functions. If any function fails, a corrective action (ballast or removal of high-weight items) forces a re-iteration.

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define "Revised Functions of Stability Criteria" in the context of ship conversion. Why are these functions recalculated after a major conversion?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> The Revised Functions of Stability Criteria are the **re-evaluated mathematical relationships** — namely, Initial GM, areas under the GZ curve, maximum righting lever, angle of vanishing stability, and the weather criterion — used to verify that a converted ship satisfies the **IMO intact stability code (MSC.1/Circ.1627)**. **(1 Mark)**
>
> These functions are recalculated after a major conversion because the conversion alters the vessel's **displacement (Δ), KG, KM, and the cross-curves of stability**, making the original stability booklet obsolete. **(1 Mark)**
>
> A failure to revise these functions may result in a **non-compliant vessel with insufficient GM, reduced range of positive stability, or downflooding risk**, endangering the ship and crew. **(1 Mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**List the seven mandatory intact stability functions that must be satisfied by a converted ship under the IMO criteria.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> 1. Initial GM ≥ 0.15 m. **(0.5 Mark)**
> 2. Area under GZ curve from 0° to 30° ≥ 0.055 m·rad. **(0.5 Mark)**
> 3. Area under GZ curve from 0° to 40° ≥ 0.090 m·rad. **(0.5 Mark)**
> 4. Area under GZ curve from 30° to 40° ≥ 0.030 m·rad. **(0.5 Mark)**
> 5. Maximum GZ ≥ 0.20 m at an angle ≥ 30°. **(0.5 Mark)**
> 6. Angle of vanishing stability θ_v ≥ 60°. **(0.25 Mark)**
> 7. Weather criterion: heeling lever / righting lever ratio ≤ 1.0. **(0.25 Mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

> A bulk carrier of lightship weight 9500 tonnes with original KG = 6.8 m is to be converted into a container ship. The conversion involves:
> - Addition of a 400-tonne deckhouse at z = 16.5 m above the keel.
> - Installation of new cargo gear weighing 120 tonnes at z = 14.0 m.
> - Removal of obsolete generator weighing 80 tonnes from a height of z = 4.5 m.
>
> After conversion, the new draft gives KM = 8.9 m at a displacement of 13,200 tonnes. A free surface effect correction of 0.05 m is applicable due to partially filled fuel oil tanks.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the revised **KG** and the **virtual KG** after conversion, and determine whether the **Initial GM** criterion is satisfied. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** The recomputed GZ values at standard heel angles are: GZ(0°) = 0, GZ(10°) = 0.095 m, GZ(20°) = 0.215 m, GZ(30°) = 0.325 m, GZ(40°) = 0.310 m, GZ(50°) = 0.215 m, GZ(60°) = 0.05 m. Using the Trapezoidal Rule, evaluate the **three area criteria** and the **maximum GZ criterion**, stating compliance. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

**Step 1: Compute new lightship weight.**
$$W_{new} = 9500 + 400 + 120 - 80 = 9940 \text{ t}$$

**Step 2: Apply moments to find revised KG (using 1 mark for setup, 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for result).**
$$W_{new} \cdot KG_{new} = (9500 \times 6.8) + (400 \times 16.5) + (120 \times 14.0) - (80 \times 4.5)$$

$$W_{new} \cdot KG_{new} = 64600 + 6600 + 1680 - 360 = 72520 \text{ t·m}$$

$$KG_{new} = \frac{72520}{9940} = 7.2958 \text{ m} \approx 7.30 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Compute virtual KG with FSE.**
$$KG_{virt} = KG_{new} + \text{FSE} = 7.30 + 0.05 = 7.35 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Compute revised GM.**
$$GM_{revised} = KM - KG_{virt} = 8.9 - 7.35 = 1.55 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Compliance check.**
$$GM_{revised} = 1.55 \text{ m} \geq 0.15 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark \text{ PASSES with large margin}$$

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Step 1: Area from 0° to 30° using Trapezoidal Rule.**
$$A_{0 \to 30} = \frac{0.1745}{2} \left[ 0 + 2(0.095) + 2(0.215) + 0.325 \right]$$

$$A_{0 \to 30} = 0.0873 \times \left[ 0.190 + 0.430 + 0.325 \right]$$

$$A_{0 \to 30} = 0.0873 \times 0.945 = 0.0825 \text{ m·rad}$$

**Step 2: Area from 0° to 40° using Trapezoidal Rule.**
$$A_{0 \to 40} = 0.0873 \times \left[ 0 + 2(0.095 + 0.215 + 0.325) + 0.310 \right]$$

$$A_{0 \to 40} = 0.0873 \times \left[ 2(0.635) + 0.310 \right]$$

$$A_{0 \to 40} = 0.0873 \times \left[ 1.270 + 0.310 \right] = 0.0873 \times 1.580 = 0.1379 \text{ m·rad}$$

**Step 3: Area from 30° to 40° (difference).**
$$A_{30 \to 40} = 0.1379 - 0.0825 = 0.0554 \text{ m·rad}$$

**Step 4: Maximum GZ criterion.**
$$GZ_{max} = 0.325 \text{ m at } \theta = 30°$$

**Step 5: Compliance Summary.**

| Criterion | Computed | Threshold | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A_{0 \to 30}$ | 0.0825 m·rad | $\geq 0.055$ m·rad | ✅ PASS |
| $A_{0 \to 40}$ | 0.1379 m·rad | $\geq 0.090$ m·rad | ✅ PASS |
| $A_{30 \to 40}$ | 0.0554 m·rad | $\geq 0.030$ m·rad | ✅ PASS |
| $GZ_{max}$ | 0.325 m @ 30° | $\geq 0.20$ m @ $\theta \geq 30°$ | ✅ PASS |

**Conclusion:** The converted ship satisfies all area criteria and the maximum GZ criterion.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

> A 25,000 DWT tanker is being converted to a chemical parcel tanker. The hydrostatic data for the new loading condition gives:
> - Displacement $\Delta = 28{,}500$ tonnes
> - $KM = 9.4$ m
> - Computed $KG = 8.1$ m (before FSE)
> - Free surface correction due to segregated ballast tanks: $\text{FSE} = 0.18$ m
> - Wind heeling lever $H = 0.42$ m
> - Righting arm at the second intersection $GZ_{2nd} = 0.48$ m
>
> **(a)** Compute the **revised GM** with FSE and verify compliance with the **Initial GM criterion**. Also, state why the **angle of vanishing stability** and the **downflooding angle** are critical safety functions in chemical tankers. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Evaluate the **weather criterion** as per IMO MSC.1/Circ.1627 and explain what corrective action would be required if $K > 1.0$. List **two corrective measures** commonly adopted in converted chemical tankers. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Step 1: Compute virtual KG.**
$$KG_{virt} = KG + \text{FSE} = 8.1 + 0.18 = 8.28 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Compute revised GM.**
$$GM_{revised} = KM - KG_{virt} = 9.4 - 8.28 = 1.12 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Compliance check.**
$$GM_{revised} = 1.12 \text{ m} \geq 0.15 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark \text{ PASSES (1 mark for value, 1 mark for verdict)}$$

**Step 4: Importance of vanishing angle and downflooding angle (chemical tanker context).**
- **Angle of vanishing stability (θ_v)**: The angle beyond which the ship cannot right itself. For chemical tankers carrying low-flashpoint cargo, a small $\theta_v$ can lead to **capsize with possible spillage and fire hazard**. **(1.5 Marks)**
- **Downflooding angle (θ_f)**: The heel angle at which seawater enters through non-weathertight openings. In chemical tankers, downflooding can cause **cargo contamination, loss of buoyancy, and environmental disaster** due to toxic cargo release. Hence, the class requirement that $\theta_f \geq 40°$ or $\theta_f > \theta_{GZ_{max}}$ is critical. **(1.5 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Step 1: Weather criterion evaluation.**
$$K = \frac{H}{GZ_{2nd}} = \frac{0.42}{0.48} = 0.875$$

**Step 2: Compliance check.**
$$K = 0.875 \leq 1.0 \quad \checkmark \text{ PASSES the weather criterion}$$

**Step 3: Corrective actions if K > 1.0 (Hypothetical).**
> [!NOTE]
> **Two commonly adopted corrective measures in converted chemical tankers:**
>
> 1. **Reduction of KG by relocating heavy machinery or cargo handling gear to lower decks** — decreases the moment of mass, thereby lowering $KG_{virt}$ and increasing GM and GZ values. **(2 Marks)**
>
> 2. **Installation of additional fixed ballast in double-bottom tanks** — increases displacement and lowers the effective KG, raising the GZ curve and improving $GZ_{2nd}$. **(2 Marks)**
>
> **Other accepted measures** (for extra credit): increasing freeboard, fitting stabilising fins, reducing windage area by redesigning superstructure. **(1 Mark)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (as observed in KTU valuation keys):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the FSE correction:** Many students compute $GM = KM - KG$ *without* adding the free surface correction. This is a **2-mark direct loss**. Always use $KG_{virt} = KG + \text{FSE}$.
>
> 2. **Angle in degrees instead of radians:** The Trapezoidal Rule requires the area computation in **m·rad**. If you forget to convert degrees to radians (multiply by $\pi/180$), you will get an answer ~57× too large. **2-mark penalty.**
>
> 3. **Misapplying the moment equation:** When items are *removed* from the ship (negative contribution), many students *add* them by mistake. Removals must be **subtracted** in the moment equation.
>
> 4. **Not showing trapezoidal sub-steps:** The model answer expects each interval's contribution to be shown separately. A single-line "I used trapezoidal rule" gets only **1 mark out of 4**.
>
> 5. **Confusing $GZ_{max}$ with GM:** $GZ_{max}$ is the **maximum value of the GZ curve** (typically at 25°–45°), whereas **GM is the *initial slope* of the GZ curve** (at θ = 0). They are *different* quantities.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Revised Functions of Stability Criteria**
>
> - **Why revise?** Any major conversion alters $\Delta$, $KG$, $KM$, and the cross-curves — invalidating the original stability booklet. **(KTU Favourite)**
> - **7 Mandatory Functions (must satisfy all):**
>   - $GM \geq 0.15$ m
>   - $A_{0 \to 30°} \geq 0.055$ m·rad
>   - $A_{0 \to 40°} \geq 0.090$ m·rad
>   - $A_{30° \to 40°} \geq 0.030$ m·rad
>   - $GZ_{max} \geq 0.20$ m at $\theta \geq 30°$
>   - $\theta_v \geq 60°$
>   - Weather Criterion $K \leq 1.0$
> - **Governing Code:** IMO **MSC.1/Circ.1627** (currently the 2020 revised intact stability framework).
> - **Core formula:** $GZ(\theta) = KN(\theta) - KG \cdot \sin\theta$.
> - **KG recalculation:** $W_{new} \cdot KG_{new} = \sum (W_i \cdot z_i)$ — additions increase, removals decrease.
> - **Virtual KG:** Always use $KG_{virt} = KG + \text{FSE}$ for partial-fill tanks.
> - **Trapezoidal Rule:** Use radian angles; show all sub-step trapezoidal intervals.
> - **Corrective measures** if any function fails: fixed ballast, KG reduction, freeboard increase, reduced windage.
> - **Classification requirement:** Re-approval of stability booklet by class (IRS / DNV / LR / BV) is mandatory before re-entering service post-conversion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cross Curves of Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cross Curves of Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Cross Curves of Stability

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross Curves of Stability** (also known as **Curves of Statical Stability** or **KN Curves**) are graphical representations plotted during a ship's inclining experiment or calculated analytically, which depict the relationship between the **righting lever (KN)** on the ordinate and the **angle of heel (θ)** on the abscissa, for the ship floating at its **design waterline**. They are used to determine the **static stability** of a vessel at any angle of heel for any assumed vertical position of the **center of gravity (G)**.

In the context of **Ship Conversion Technology (PENST415)**, these curves become critical because any major conversion (e.g., lengthening, addition of superstructures, machinery upgrade, or change in cargo systems) shifts the position of G, and the cross curves allow naval architects to quickly read off the **GZ values** at any heel angle for the *new* KG.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Leaning Tower of Books

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Analogy:** Imagine you have a tall stack of books (the ship) standing on a table. The stack has its weight concentrated at a certain height (G). Now imagine tilting the stack by varying angles. The further up the center of gravity is located, the harder (or easier) it is to topple the stack.
>
> - A stack of **lightweight, thin books** (low KG) — stable up to large tilt angles.
> - A stack of **heavy, thick encyclopedias** placed at the top** (high KG) — topples very quickly.
>
> The **Cross Curves of Stability** are essentially a "look-up table" that tells you, for a *given stack height* (KG), how much **resistance force arm** (GZ) you have at a *given tilt angle* (θ). They "cross" each other because what is stable at one angle for a low G may become unstable at the same angle for a high G.

### 1.3 Physical Metrics and Conventions

The following standard metrics are used throughout KTU naval architecture papers:

- **KG** — Height of the center of gravity above the keel (in **metres** or **feet**). Standard notation: **KG** with units of **m** or **ft**.
- **KN** — The lever of the righting moment arm measured from the keel intersection with the centerline, equivalent to the perpendicular distance from K to the **buoyancy vector (B) at heel θ**.
- **GZ** — The righting lever (true righting arm) measured from the actual center of gravity G to the line of action of buoyancy at angle θ.
- **θ** — Angle of heel, typically expressed in **degrees**, ranging from **0° to 90°** (or beyond for special cases).
- **GM** — The **metacentric height** (in **m** or **ft**), the value at θ = 0° (tangent to the cross curve at the origin).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The relationship between KN, KG, and GZ at any angle θ is the **single most important formula** in Module 2. Master the equation **GZ = KN − KG·sin(θ)** thoroughly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Cross Curves are Needed

The **GZ curve** (the curve of righting levers vs angle of heel) is the *direct* indicator of a ship's stability. However:

- The **GZ curve changes whenever the position of G (i.e., KG) changes.**
- During a **ship conversion**, weights are added, removed, or relocated — changing KG substantially.
- **Re-computing** the entire GZ curve for every hypothetical KG value would be a monumental task.
- **Solution:** Compute a single **KN curve** (which is *independent* of KG) once, then for *any* KG, simply read off KN at the desired θ and apply:

$$GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

This produces a **family of cross curves** — one GZ curve for each assumed KG, all derived from the *same* master KN curve.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Construction

The cross curve construction relies on the **geometric decomposition** of the moment of buoyancy:

- The total righting moment of the buoyancy force (acting at the **center of buoyancy B_θ** at heel θ) about the **center of gravity G** can be split.
- The line of action of the buoyancy force passes through the new center of buoyancy **B_θ** and the new metacenter **M_θ**.
- The perpendicular distance from the **keel point K** to this line of action is the lever **KN**.
- The perpendicular distance from **G** to the same line of action is the lever **GZ**.

By geometry (since K and G are vertically separated by a distance KG):

$$GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

### 2.3 Shape Characteristics of Cross Curves

| Feature | Behaviour | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **At θ = 0°** | All GZ values = 0, regardless of KG | Stability *arm* is zero at upright, but moment is meaningful |
| **At small angles** (θ ≤ ~10°) | Curves nearly **coincide**; slope gives GM | Use **GM formula**: GZ ≈ GM·sin(θ) |
| **At intermediate angles** (10° – 30°) | Curves **diverge** based on KG | Maximum divergence — curves "cross" here |
| **At large angles** (θ > 30°) | Curves tend to **re-converge** somewhat for low KG | Important for **range of positive stability** |
| **Maximum GZ** | Reached at different θ for different KG | Critical for **dynamic stability** analysis |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every equation you will need for cross-curve problems in the KTU ESE. Memorize the symbols and units.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | Righting lever from KN and KG | m (or ft) |
| 2 | $KN = GZ + KG \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | Rearranged: KN from GZ | m (or ft) |
| 3 | $KG = \dfrac{KN - GZ}{\sin(\theta)}$ | KG from KN and GZ at any angle | m (or ft) |
| 4 | $GZ = GM \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | Small-angle approximation (θ ≤ ~10°) | m (or ft) |
| 5 | $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{V}$ | Transverse metacentric radius | m (or ft) |
| 6 | $GM = KM - KG$ | Metacentric height | m (or ft) |
| 7 | $KM = KB + BM$ | Height of metacenter above keel | m (or ft) |
| 8 | $M_{R} = \Delta \cdot GZ$ | Righting moment = Displacement × GZ | tonne·m (or kip·ft) |
| 9 | $\text{Range of Stability} = \theta_{2} - \theta_{1}$ | Angle at which GZ = 0 on either side of equilibrium | degrees (°) |
| 10 | $A_{\text{deck immersion}} = \Delta \cdot GZ_{\max}$ | Approximate criterion (also $\geq 0.055$ to $0.07$ m·rad) | m·rad |

> **Symbol Glossary:**
> - $I_T$ = **Second moment of waterplane area** about the centerline ($m^4$)
> - $V$ = **Volume of displacement** of the ship ($m^3$)
> - $\Delta$ = **Displacement** (mass) of the ship (tonnes)
> - $B$ = **Center of buoyancy** (at upright waterline)
> - $M$ = **Metacenter**
> - $K$ = **Keel point** (reference)
> - $G$ = **Center of gravity**
> - $\theta_1$ = **Angle of vanishing stability** (down-flooding or GZ = 0)
> - $\theta_2$ = **Angle of deck immersion** (when deck edge submerges)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ship Conversion:** Determines whether adding new superstructure, machinery, or cargo gear will degrade stability — *the single most cited KTU application*.
- **Damage Stability:** Quickly evaluates GZ at any angle for a flooded compartment where KG has changed.
- **Loading Computer Inputs:** Modern loading computers use cross-curve data as a *lookup table* to compute GZ in real time.
- **Statutory Compliance:** IMO and classification societies (DNV, LR, ABS) require curves for **stability booklet** generation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Master Equation: $GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin(\theta)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Geometry:** Consider the ship heeled to an angle θ about its longitudinal centerline. The buoyancy force, equal in magnitude to the displacement Δ, now acts vertically upward through the **new center of buoyancy** $B_\theta$ and along the line that intersects the original centerline at the **new metacenter** $M_\theta$.

**Step 1 — Establish the reference frame.**
Drop a vertical line down from G in the heeled condition; this vertical line is offset horizontally from the original centerline because the ship has rotated.

**Step 2 — Identify the perpendicular distance from the keel point K to the buoyancy line of action.**

The line of action of the buoyancy force is vertical (since buoyancy always acts vertically upward). From the original **keel-centerline intersection point K**, the horizontal distance to the heeled buoyancy line is exactly the lever **KN** (read from the cross curve).

**Step 3 — Identify the perpendicular distance from G to the buoyancy line of action.**

The point G has moved horizontally (in the body-fixed frame, the deck and everything on it tilts). The horizontal shift of G from the original centerline is:

$$\text{Horizontal shift of G} = KG \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

This is because in the right triangle formed by K, G, and the heeled position of G, the **opposite side** to angle θ is exactly $KG \cdot \sin(\theta)$.

**Step 4 — Subtract the two perpendicular distances.**

The lever GZ is the perpendicular from G to the buoyancy line. Since G is shifted *toward* the buoyancy line (in the direction of heel) compared to K, the lever from G is **less** than the lever from K by the amount of G's shift:

$$
\begin{aligned}
GZ &= KN - (\text{horizontal shift of G}) \\
GZ &= KN - KG \cdot \sin(\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **This is the foundational cross-curve equation used in every KTU problem.**

### 3.2 Derivation: Finding KG from Cross-Curve Data

**Given:** KN and GZ at a specific angle θ, find KG.

**Step 1 — Start from the master equation:**

$$GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate KG:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
KG \cdot \sin(\theta) &= KN - GZ \\
KG &= \frac{KN - GZ}{\sin(\theta)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **KTU Problem Type:** "A ship is heeled to 20° and reads KN = 0.45 m, GZ = 0.20 m. Find KG."
> **Solution:** $KG = (0.45 - 0.20) / \sin(20°) = 0.25 / 0.342 = 0.731 \text{ m}$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Curve Data Interpretation

The following worked analytical example shows the *complete* workflow as required for a KTU Part B 14-mark question.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example:** A naval architect is evaluating a ship *after* a conversion. From the model tests on the new hull form, a KN cross-curve is available. The displacement Δ = 12,000 tonnes, and the new KG = 7.2 m. Construct the GZ curve from the following KN data and determine the **righting moment** at 30° heel.

| θ (°) | KN (m) | $\sin(\theta)$ | $KG \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | $GZ = KN - KG\sin(\theta)$ (m) | $M_R = \Delta \cdot GZ$ (tonne·m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 | 0.000 | 0.000 | 0.000 | 0 |
| 10 | 0.18 | 0.174 | 1.253 | −1.073 | negative (unstable) |
| 15 | 0.42 | 0.259 | 1.865 | −1.445 | negative (unstable) |
| 20 | 0.65 | 0.342 | 2.462 | −1.812 | negative (unstable) |
| 30 | 0.95 | 0.500 | 3.600 | −2.650 | negative (unstable) |
| 45 | 1.20 | 0.707 | 5.090 | −3.890 | negative (unstable) |
| 60 | 1.30 | 0.866 | 6.235 | −4.935 | negative (unstable) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** When **GZ comes out negative**, the ship is *unstable* at that angle for the assumed KG. The above dataset with KG = 7.2 m would render the ship **unstable for all positive heel angles** — a clear sign the conversion is unsafe. In practice, KG must be lower for stability.

**Re-evaluation with realistic KG = 4.5 m:**

| θ (°) | KN (m) | $\sin(\theta)$ | $KG \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | $GZ = KN - KG\sin(\theta)$ (m) | $M_R = \Delta \cdot GZ$ (tonne·m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 | 0.000 | 0.000 | 0.000 | 0 |
| 10 | 0.18 | 0.174 | 0.783 | −0.603 | negative |
| 15 | 0.42 | 0.259 | 1.166 | −0.746 | negative |
| 20 | 0.65 | 0.342 | 1.539 | −0.889 | negative |
| 30 | 0.95 | 0.500 | 2.250 | −1.300 | negative |
| 45 | 1.20 | 0.707 | 3.182 | −1.982 | negative |
| 60 | 1.30 | 0.866 | 3.897 | −2.597 | negative |

> The issue: **the ship is severely unstable** because KN values are far smaller than KG·sin(θ) at every angle. This is a *pedagogical* example showing the importance of low KG.

**Realistic case with proper KN values (KN > KG·sin(θ)):**

For a stable ship at 30° heel with KG = 4.5 m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
GZ &= KN - KG \cdot \sin(30°) \\
GZ &= KN - 4.5 \times 0.500 \\
GZ &= KN - 2.25
\end{aligned}
$$

If KN at 30° = **2.50 m** (typical for a stable vessel):

$$
\begin{aligned}
GZ &= 2.50 - 2.25 = 0.25 \text{ m} \\
M_R &= \Delta \cdot GZ = 12{,}000 \times 0.25 = 3{,}000 \text{ tonne·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **This is the type of calculation a KTU examiner expects to see step-by-step in a 14-mark question.**

### 3.4 Analytical Construction of the GZ Curve from KN Curve

**Step 1 — Tabulate KN and θ from the cross curve.**
For each heel angle θ (0°, 10°, 20°, ..., 90°), record the KN value from the master curve.

**Step 2 — Compute KG·sin(θ) for each θ.**
Use the known KG of the *converted* ship.

**Step 3 — Subtract: GZ = KN − KG·sin(θ).**
This is the ordinate of the new GZ curve.

**Step 4 — Plot GZ vs θ.**
The resulting curve is the GZ curve for the converted ship.

**Step 5 — Read off critical values:**
- **Maximum GZ** ($GZ_{max}$)
- **Angle of maximum GZ** ($\theta_{max}$)
- **Angle of vanishing stability** ($\theta_v$) where GZ returns to zero
- **Range of positive stability** = $\theta_v - 0°$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cross Curve Generation

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **data flow and decision logic** used in cross-curve-based stability assessment for a ship-conversion project. This is the block-level functional representation required by the KTU protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Displacement Delta] --> B[Load KN Cross Curve Data]
    C[New KG from Conversion] --> B
    B --> D[Tabulate KN and theta for angles 0 to 90]
    D --> E[Compute sin theta for each angle]
    E --> F[Compute KG times sin theta]
    F --> G[Apply GZ equals KN minus KG sin theta]
    G --> H{Is GZ positive}
    H -- Yes --> I[Plot GZ on ordinate axis]
    H -- No --> J[Flag Instability at this angle]
    I --> K[Read Maximum GZ and Angle of Max GZ]
    K --> L[Compute Righting Moment M sub R equals Delta GZ]
    J --> M[Report unsafe heel range to designer]
    L --> N[Generate Stability Booklet Entry]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Compare with IMO Stability Criteria]
    O --> P{Compliant}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Approve Conversion Design]
    P -- No --> R[Recommend Ballast or KG Reduction]
```

### 4.2 Cross-Curve Family Layout (Schematic)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **shape behaviour of a family of GZ curves** for different KG values, all derived from a single KN curve.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Family[Cross Curve Family for Different KG]
        K1[KG Low 4 m] --> G1[High GZ curve large positive area]
        K2[KG Medium 6 m] --> G2[Moderate GZ curve reduced area]
        K3[KG High 8 m] --> G3[Low GZ curve minimal area]
        K4[KG Critical 9 m] --> G4[Narrow GZ curve near zero]
    end
    G1 --> M[Common intersection near theta equals 0]
    G2 --> M
    G3 --> M
    G4 --> M
    G1 --> N[Curves diverge as theta increases]
    G2 --> N
    G3 --> N
    G4 --> N
    N --> O[Curves re-converge at high theta for low KG only]
```

### 4.3 The Geometry Behind the Master Equation

The following Mermaid schematic shows the **physical relationship** between K, G, B, and the line of action of buoyancy, which underpins $GZ = KN - KG \sin(\theta)$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Point K Keel Centerline at Upright] --> P1[Ship Heels by angle theta]
    P1 --> P2[New Center of Buoyancy B theta]
    P2 --> P3[Line of Action of Buoyancy is Vertical through B theta]
    P3 --> P4[Perpendicular from K to Buoyancy Line equals KN]
    P1 --> P5[Center of Gravity G shifts horizontally by KG sin theta]
    P5 --> P6[Perpendicular from G to Buoyancy Line equals GZ]
    P4 --> P7[GZ equals KN minus KG sin theta]
    P6 --> P7
```

### 4.4 Decision Logic for Stability Compliance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start Stability Check] --> S1[Obtain KN Cross Curve]
    S1 --> S2[Determine New KG post conversion]
    S2 --> S3[Construct GZ Curve using GZ equals KN minus KG sin theta]
    S3 --> S4[Find Maximum GZ]
    S3 --> S5[Find Range of Positive Stability]
    S3 --> S6[Find Angle of Maximum GZ]
    S4 --> S7{GZ max greater than 0.20 m typical IMO}
    S5 --> S8{Range greater than 60 degrees}
    S6 --> S9{Angle of max GZ between 25 and 40 degrees}
    S7 -- Yes --> S10[Pass Criterion 1]
    S7 -- No --> S11[Fail Criterion 1]
    S8 -- Yes --> S12[Pass Criterion 2]
    S8 -- No --> S13[Fail Criterion 2]
    S9 -- Yes --> S14[Pass Criterion 3]
    S9 -- No --> S15[Fail Criterion 3]
    S10 --> S16[Final Stability Status]
    S11 --> S16
    S12 --> S16
    S13 --> S16
    S14 --> S16
    S15 --> S16
    S16 --> S17{All Criteria Passed}
    S17 -- Yes --> S18[Conversion Approved]
    S17 -- No --> S19[Revise Conversion Design]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**

*Define Cross Curves of Stability. Why are they called "cross" curves?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Cross Curves of Stability are graphical plots showing the relationship between the **righting lever KN** (ordinate) and the **angle of heel θ** (abscissa) for a ship at its design waterline. They are *independent* of the position of the center of gravity G and are used to compute GZ for any assumed KG using **GZ = KN − KG·sin(θ)**.

They are called "**cross**" curves because, when GZ curves are plotted for **different vertical positions of G** from the same KN curve, the resulting family of curves **crosses** (intersects) each other at higher angles of heel. The cross-over point typically occurs near the angle of maximum GZ. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for independence of KG, 1 for naming rationale)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]**

*State the relationship between KN, KG, and GZ at any angle of heel θ. What is the significance of this relationship in ship conversion projects?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin(\theta)
$$

where GZ = righting lever, KN = lever from keel to line of action of buoyancy, KG = height of center of gravity above keel, θ = angle of heel.

**Significance in ship conversions:**

- During a conversion, the position of G may change due to added/removed weights.
- The KN curve is computed once (from model test or analytical methods) and is *unchanged* by shifts in G.
- For any *new* KG, the new GZ curve can be derived rapidly using the above relation.
- This avoids repeating expensive model tests for every hypothetical loading condition. **(3 marks — 1 for equation, 1 for each application point = 2; 1 for significance = 3)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]*

**A ship of displacement 18,000 tonnes is being evaluated after a major conversion that added a heavy deckhouse. The KG of the converted ship is 6.8 m. The KN cross curve values at various angles are tabulated below:**

| θ (degrees) | KN (m) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 |
| 10 | 0.32 |
| 20 | 0.95 |
| 30 | 1.60 |
| 40 | 2.10 |
| 50 | 2.40 |
| 60 | 2.55 |
| 70 | 2.55 |
| 80 | 2.45 |
| 90 | 2.20 |

**(a)** Construct the GZ curve using the relation $GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin(\theta)$ and tabulate values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** From the GZ curve, determine the **maximum GZ**, the **angle at which it occurs**, and the **righting moment at maximum GZ**. State whether the ship satisfies the IMO criterion of $GZ_{max} \geq 0.20$ m at an angle $\geq 30°$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Tabulation (7 marks):**

| θ (°) | KN (m) | $\sin(\theta)$ | $KG \cdot \sin(\theta) = 6.8 \sin\theta$ (m) | $GZ = KN - 6.8\sin\theta$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 | 0.0000 | 0.000 | **0.000** |
| 10 | 0.32 | 0.1736 | 1.180 | **−0.860** |
| 20 | 0.95 | 0.3420 | 2.326 | **−1.376** |
| 30 | 1.60 | 0.5000 | 3.400 | **−1.800** |
| 40 | 2.10 | 0.6428 | 4.371 | **−2.271** |
| 50 | 2.40 | 0.7660 | 5.209 | **−2.809** |
| 60 | 2.55 | 0.8660 | 5.889 | **−3.339** |
| 70 | 2.55 | 0.9397 | 6.390 | **−3.840** |
| 80 | 2.45 | 0.9848 | 6.697 | **−4.247** |
| 90 | 2.20 | 1.0000 | 6.800 | **−4.600** |

**Comment on the result:** The GZ values are *all negative*, indicating the ship is **unstable at all positive heel angles** for KG = 6.8 m. The conversion, as proposed, is **unsafe**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often forget to compute $\sin(\theta)$ at every angle and may incorrectly *plot* KN directly. The examiner awards marks only for *correct GZ values* via the subtraction. Also, do not omit the **0° row** — even though it gives GZ = 0, it is required for proper tabulation.

**Part (b) — Analysis (7 marks):**

Since the GZ curve is entirely negative, the ship has **no positive righting lever** for any heel angle under the assumed KG = 6.8 m.

- **Maximum GZ:** Numerically the largest *negative* value is at 90° with GZ = −4.60 m, but this represents *maximum capsize tendency*, not stability.
- **Angle of vanishing stability:** Does not exist (GZ is negative at all angles); the ship has **no range of positive stability**.
- **Righting moment at maximum GZ:** $M_R = \Delta \cdot GZ = 18{,}000 \times (-4.60) = -82{,}800$ tonne·m (negative; *capsizing* moment).
- **IMO Criterion check:** The ship **fails** decisively. $GZ_{max} = -4.60$ m ≪ 0.20 m.

**Conclusion:** The conversion must be redesigned — either by **reducing KG** (lighter superstructure materials, relocating heavy equipment lower), or by **adding ballast**, or by **enlarging the waterplane area** to increase KN values. **(7 marks — 2 for finding max, 2 for IMO compliance verdict, 3 for design conclusion)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]*

**(a)** A ship is inclined to 25° heel. The KN value read from the cross curve at this angle is **1.85 m**, and the actual righting lever **GZ measured is 0.35 m**. Calculate the **KG of the ship**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** After a conversion, the ship's new KG becomes 5.2 m. If the same KN curve is used and the ship is heeled to 40°, calculate the **new GZ** and the **righting moment** given a displacement of **15,000 tonnes**. Comment on whether the ship remains stable at 40° heel. **(7 marks)**

---

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Finding KG (7 marks):**

Given: θ = 25°, KN = 1.85 m, GZ = 0.35 m.

**Step 1 — Apply the master equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GZ &= KN - KG \cdot \sin(\theta) \\
0.35 &= 1.85 - KG \cdot \sin(25°) \\
0.35 &= 1.85 - KG \cdot 0.4226
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate KG:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
KG \cdot 0.4226 &= 1.85 - 0.35 \\
KG \cdot 0.4226 &= 1.50 \\
KG &= \frac{1.50}{0.4226} \\
KG &= 3.55 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** $KG = 3.55$ m. **(7 marks — 2 for correct setup, 2 for algebraic manipulation, 2 for final numerical value, 1 for unit)**

**Part (b) — New GZ and Righting Moment (7 marks):**

Given: New KG = 5.2 m, θ = 40°, KN at 40° = (assume read from curve) = 2.10 m, Δ = 15,000 tonnes.

**Step 1 — Compute GZ at 40° with new KG:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GZ &= KN - KG \cdot \sin(\theta) \\
GZ &= 2.10 - 5.2 \times \sin(40°) \\
GZ &= 2.10 - 5.2 \times 0.6428 \\
GZ &= 2.10 - 3.343 \\
GZ &= -1.243 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Righting moment:**

$$
M_R = \Delta \cdot GZ = 15{,}000 \times (-1.243) = -18{,}645 \text{ tonne·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Stability comment:**

Since GZ = **−1.243 m** (negative) and the righting moment is also negative, the ship is **unstable at 40° heel** under the new loading condition. The conversion has raised G sufficiently to push the ship past its stability limit at this angle.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students make the mistake of computing KG·sin(θ) in degrees and producing a numerically tiny value (e.g., 5.2 × 40° = 208 — totally wrong). Always verify that your calculator is in **radian mode** OR use a precomputed $\sin(\theta)$ table. The examiner will **deduct 2 marks** for a sign error in $M_R$.

**Conclusion:** The conversion has compromised stability at 40° heel. The naval architect must consider **ballasting or KG reduction** before approving the design. **(7 marks — 3 for GZ, 2 for M_R, 2 for stability verdict and design recommendation)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cross Curves of Stability**

- ✅ **Definition:** A KN curve (ordinate) vs angle of heel θ (abscissa), independent of G.
- ✅ **Master Equation (MEMORIZE):** $GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin(\theta)$
- ✅ **Why "cross":** GZ curves for different KGs intersect each other at higher angles.
- ✅ **KN is a function** of hull form and waterline only; *not* of loading.
- ✅ **At θ = 0°:** All curves pass through the origin; GZ = 0 at upright.
- ✅ **Small angle limit** (θ ≤ ~10°): $GZ \approx GM \cdot \sin(\theta)$; KN ≈ GM.
- ✅ **Maximum GZ** is the peak of the GZ curve; **angle of vanishing stability** is where it returns to zero.
- ✅ **Range of stability** = $\theta_v - 0°$; must be ≥ 60° (typical IMO criterion).
- ✅ **Righting moment:** $M_R = \Delta \cdot GZ$ — must be positive for stability.
- ✅ **Conversion Impact:** Adding weight high up increases KG → reduces GZ at all angles.
- ✅ **Negative GZ → ship capsizes** (or rather, will not return upright).
- ✅ **Units consistency:** KN, GZ, KG all in metres (or all in feet); $\theta$ in degrees with $\sin(\theta)$ dimensionless.
- ✅ **Examiner red flag:** Forgetting the **0° row** in the GZ tabulation table (cost: −1 mark).
- ✅ **Examiner red flag:** Mixing up KN and GZ (KN is from keel, GZ is from center of gravity).
- ✅ **Examiner red flag:** Not stating units in the final answer (e.g., "GZ = 0.25" without "m").

> **Final Note:** Cross curves are the **navigator's compass for the naval architect** — once you understand the master equation, every other concept (stability at large angles, range of stability, dynamic stability) is built on it. Master $GZ = KN - KG\sin(\theta)$ and the rest will follow logically.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statical Stability Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Statical Stability Curves # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of naval architecture and ship conversion, **Statical Stability Curves** are graphical representations of a vessel's **righting lever (GZ)** plotted as a function of the **angle of heel (φ)** at a **fixed displacement** and **fixed centre of gravity (KG)**. They are a fundamental output of the **statical stability analysis** of a ship, and they characterise the *quasi-static* restoring capability of the vessel at any angle of inclination, without any reference to the energy or dynamics of the heeling process. The principal curves falling under this topic are: 1. **Cross Curves of Stability (CCS)** — also called *Lever Curves of Stability* or *Pupp's Curves*. These are *universal* curves giving the lever $KN$ (or $KN \cos\phi$ in the simplified form) for various angles of heel at a range of assumed volumes of displacement. 2. **The Actual GZ Curve (Righting Lever Curve)** — derived from the Cross Curves for a *particular* displacement and a *particular* position of $G$, used by the naval architect to assess the vessel's stability at large angles. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** *Statical Stability Curves* form a core deliverable of Module 2. Students must be able to (a) explain the construction of Cross Curves of Stability, (b) derive the actual GZ curve for a given loading condition, and (c) extract the standard stability criteria from the curve. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ship in a Valley" Imagine a small ship floating in a long, smooth, U-shaped valley. The **shape of the valley floor** is the **stability curve**: - The **depth of the valley** at any horizontal position tells you how much *energy* would be needed to roll the ship there (this is *dynamical* stability). - The **steepness of the valley wall** at any point tells you the *restoring force* (or righting moment) pushing the ship back to upright (this is *statical* stability). The **righting lever (GZ)** is essentially that "steepness" — a measure of how strongly the ship's weight and buoyancy cooperate to right the vessel at a given heel angle. The **Statical Stability Curve is a plot of this steepness versus heel angle**, and reading it tells you instantly whether the ship will right itself, what angle it can tolerate, and when it will capsize. ## 1.3 Why These Curves Matter in Ship Conversion During ship conversion (lengthening, re-engining, deckhouse addition, etc.), the **lightweight distribution, KG and displacement** all change. The naval architect *cannot* rely on small-angle formulas (e.g. $GM$) alone; he must construct a new GZ curve to demonstrate to the Classification Society and the regulatory authority (IMO) that the converted ship still meets the **Intact Stability Code (IS Code 2008)** requirements. > [!NOTE] > **Key Constant Reference:** The standard metric used is the **righting lever (GZ)** measured in **metres (m)**. The standard reference angle of heel for plotting is from **0° to 90°** (or beyond, until angle of vanishing stability $\phi_v$). --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — From Geometry to the Righting Lever When a ship heels through a small angle $\phi$ about its longitudinal centreline, two forces act: - **Weight (W)** acting downwards through the centre of gravity **G**. - **Buoyancy (B)** acting upwards through the new centre of buoyancy **B'**, which has shifted because the underwater volume has changed shape. The **righting moment** is the product of the buoyancy force and the horizontal distance between the lines of action of $W$ and $B$. This horizontal distance is the **righting lever, GZ**, and is the central quantity of statical stability. The general, exact geometric expression is: $$GZ = KN - KG \sin\phi$$ where $K$ is a fixed point on the centreline (typically taken at the level of the ship's half-moulded draft, or at the keel), $N$ is the point where the vertical line through $B'$ intersects the centreline, and $KN$ is read from the **Cross Curve of Stability** at the heel angle $\phi$ for the vessel's displacement volume. > [!IMPORTANT] > The cross curve gives the lever $KN$ for a *generic* centre of buoyancy located at the *base point K*. To obtain the actual righting lever for a ship with centre of gravity at height $KG$ above $K$, we must subtract $KG \sin\phi$. This is the bridge between *cross curves* and the *GZ curve*. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Construction Logic The construction of the GZ curve from the cross curves follows a rigid sequence: 1. **Step 1 — Generate the ship's body plan** with all offsets, including any new hull form from the conversion. 2. **Step 2 — Compute Bonjean curves** (areas of transverse sections as a function of draft) for a series of waterlines, e.g. at every $10°$ of heel from $0°$ to $90°$ (and often $90°$ to $120°$ for range of stability assessment). 3. **Step 3 — Integrate the Bonjean areas** to obtain the volume of displacement and the **transverse and longitudinal positions of the centre of buoyancy (B and LCB)** at each heel angle. 4. **Step 4 — Calculate the lever $KN$** as the transverse horizontal offset of the buoyancy centroid from the centreline plane, for each assumed displacement volume. Plot these levers against $\phi$ for each displacement → these are the **Cross Curves of Stability**. 5. **Step 5 — For a given loading condition** (known displacement volume $\nabla$ and known $KG$), enter the cross curve family at the appropriate $\nabla$ and read off $KN(\phi)$. 6. **Step 6 — Compute** $GZ(\phi) = KN(\phi) - KG \sin\phi$ at each heel angle. 7. **Step 7 — Plot $GZ$ versus $\phi$** — this is the **Statical Stability Curve** (the actual GZ curve) for the loading condition in question. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Formula / Definition** | **Units** | |------------|--------------|---------------------------|-----------| | $GZ$ | Righting lever (statical stability measure) | $GZ = KN - KG \sin\phi$ | m | | $KN$ | Lever from Cross Curve of Stability | Read from cross curve at angle $\phi$ and displacement $\nabla$ | m | | $KG$ | Height of centre of gravity above baseline $K$ | Computed from weight distribution | m | | $\phi$ | Angle of heel | Independent variable | deg or rad | | $GM$ | Metacentric height (small angle) | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | m | | $BM$ | Metacentric radius | $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}$ | m | | $I_T$ | Second moment of waterplane area about centreline | $\int y^2 \, dA$ | m$^{4}$ | | $\nabla$ | Volume of displacement (Archimedes) | $\nabla = \dfrac{W}{\rho g}$ | m$^{3}$ | | $\phi_{max}$ | Angle of maximum GZ | Where $\dfrac{dGZ}{d\phi} = 0$ | deg | | $\phi_v$ | Angle of vanishing stability | Where $GZ = 0$ again after maximum | deg | | Range of stability | Total heel range over which $GZ > 0$ | $\phi_v - 0$ | deg | | Dynamical stability | Area under the GZ curve | $A = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\phi_v} GZ(\phi) \, d\phi$ | m·rad | > [!IMPORTANT] > In a markdown table, never use a literal vertical pipe `|` inside a formula cell. Always use the LaTeX commands `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value, set notation, or "such that" symbols. For example, write $GZ \vert_{\phi=30°}$ instead of $GZ \vert \phi=30° \vert$. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Classification Society Approval:** Every conversion must be backed by a GZ curve to satisfy **SOLAS** and **IMO IS Code** criteria (e.g. minimum GZ ≥ 0.20 m at 30°, area under curve from 0–30° ≥ 0.055 m·rad, area from 0–40° ≥ 0.090 m·rad, etc.). - **Operational Guidance:** Masters use the GZ curve to determine safe wind-heeling moments and to understand the vessel's behaviour under partially loaded conditions. - **Damage Stability:** The GZ curve is also generated for each damage case to demonstrate residual stability. - **Conversion Optimisation:** Comparing pre- and post-conversion GZ curves shows the naval architect whether the conversion is stability-compliant, or whether compensations (e.g. added ballast, removed topside weight) are required. --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Righting Lever Equation **Given:** A ship of weight $W$ and displacement volume $\nabla$ (so $W = \rho g \nabla$) heels through an angle $\phi$ about a longitudinal axis. The centre of gravity $G$ lies on the centreline at a height $KG$ above the keel reference point $K$. The new centre of buoyancy $B'$ lies at a horizontal transverse offset $KN$ from the centreline, where $N$ is the foot of the perpendicular from $B'$ to the centreline. **To prove:** $GZ = KN - KG \sin\phi$. **Proof (Geometric Construction):** 1. Draw the centreline of the ship (a vertical line through $K$). The ship has heeled by $\phi$, so what was vertical in the upright condition is now inclined at $\phi$ to the new vertical. 2. The line of action of weight $W$ remains *vertical* in the world frame, passing through $G$. The line of action of buoyancy $B$ remains *vertical* in the world frame, passing through the new centre of buoyancy $B'$. 3. Drop a perpendicular from $B'$ to the centreline. The foot is $N$, and the length is $KN$ (this is the value that the cross curve provides). 4. From $G$, drop a perpendicular to the line of action of $B$. The foot is $Z$, and the length is $GZ$. By definition, $GZ$ is the *horizontal* (i.e. perpendicular to the buoyancy force) distance between the two force lines of action. 5. The righting moment is then $M_R = W \times GZ$. Equivalently, it can be computed as $M_R = W \times KN - W \times (\text{horizontal offset of } G \text{ from centreline})$. 6. The horizontal offset of $G$ from the centreline is $KG \sin\phi$ (because $G$ is at a height $KG$ on the centreline, which itself is tilted by $\phi$). 7. Therefore: $$GZ = KN - KG \sin\phi$$ $$\blacksquare$$ > [!NOTE] > The derivation is purely geometric and is valid for **all angles of heel**, not just small angles. This is what makes the cross-curve method the universal tool for statical stability assessment at large angles. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Small-Angle Approximation For small heel angles (typically $\phi \le 10°$ to $15°$): 1. The cross curve of stability, when expanded in a Taylor series about $\phi = 0$, is dominated by a linear term in $\sin\phi$: $$KN \approx BM_D \sin\phi$$ where $BM_D$ is the *transverse* metacentric radius computed about the inclined waterline at the displaced volume. 2. Substituting into the fundamental equation: $$GZ = BM_D \sin\phi - KG \sin\phi$$ $$GZ = (BM_D - KG) \sin\phi$$ 3. Define the metacentric height as $GM = KB + BM - KG$, and note that for a ship near its design waterline, the metacentric radius at the inclined waterline is well approximated by the value at the upright waterline. Then: $$GZ = GM \sin\phi$$ 4. Differentiating, the righting moment becomes: $$M_R = W \cdot GM \sin\phi \approx W \cdot GM \cdot \phi \quad \text{(for small } \phi \text{ in radians)}$$ This is the **classical small-angle formula** used in initial stability assessment, but it is *not* valid for the large-angle analysis required by the IS Code. ## 3.3 Construction of the Cross Curve of Stability from Bonjean Curves **Procedure (Numerical Integration):** For a series of heel angles $\phi_i$ (commonly $i = 0°, 10°, 20°, \dots, 90°$): 1. **Step 1 — Heel the ship's body plan by $\phi_i$** about the centreline of the upright waterline plane. 2. **Step 2 — Find the new waterline** (the waterline that encloses a volume equal to $\nabla$ for each assumed displacement). 3. **Step 3 — Use Bonjean curves** to obtain the area of each transverse section at the new waterline. The Bonjean curve for a section is a plot of section area vs. draft in the upright condition; when the ship heels, the draft at each section changes, and the new area is read from the Bonjean curve. 4. **Step 4 — Numerically integrate** the section areas along the ship's length (Simpson's rule or trapezoidal rule) to obtain the displaced volume $\nabla$ and the longitudinal position of the buoyancy centre $LCB$: $$\nabla = \int_{L} A(x, \phi_i) \, dx$$ 5. **Step 5 — Compute the transverse moment** of the buoyancy force about the centreplane (the upright midplane of the ship). The first moment gives the position of the centre of buoyancy in the heeled condition; the relevant lever is: $$KN = \dfrac{1}{\nabla} \int_{L} y_c(x, \phi_i) \cdot A(x, \phi_i) \, dx$$ where $y_c$ is the transverse offset of each section's centroid from the midplane in the heeled condition. 6. **Step 6 — Tabulate $KN$ vs. $\phi_i$** for several assumed displacement volumes, producing a family of curves. These are the **Cross Curves of Stability**. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Deriving the GZ Curve A small ship has the following data: - Displacement volume $\nabla = 5000 \text{ m}^3$ - $KG = 5.50 \text{ m}$ - The following values of $KN$ are read from the cross curve at $\nabla = 5000 \text{ m}^3$: | $\phi$ (deg) | $KN$ (m) | $\sin\phi$ | $KG \sin\phi$ (m) | $GZ$ (m) | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0 | 0.000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.000 | | 10 | 0.948 | 0.1736 | 0.9548 | −0.007 | | 20 | 1.860 | 0.3420 | 1.8810 | −0.021 | | 30 | 2.680 | 0.5000 | 2.7500 | −0.070 | | 40 | 3.310 | 0.6428 | 3.5354 | −0.225 | | 50 | 3.660 | 0.7660 | 4.2130 | −0.553 | | 60 | 3.650 | 0.8660 | 4.7630 | −1.113 | > [!WARNING] > **The negative values are NOT realistic for a stable ship.** They indicate an error in either the read-off $KN$ or in the loading condition. In a properly designed ship, $GZ$ should be *positive* up to the angle of vanishing stability $\phi_v$. The example above is used purely to demonstrate the *arithmetic* of the subtraction $GZ = KN - KG \sin\phi$. **Corrected realistic data** (typical values for a stable ship): | $\phi$ (deg) | $KN$ (m) | $\sin\phi$ | $KG \sin\phi$ (m) | $GZ$ (m) | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0 | 0.000 | 0.0000 | 0.000 | 0.000 | | 10 | 0.950 | 0.1736 | 0.955 | −0.005 ≈ 0 | | 20 | 1.900 | 0.3420 | 1.881 | 0.019 | | 30 | 2.850 | 0.5000 | 2.750 | 0.100 | | 40 | 3.700 | 0.6428 | 3.535 | 0.165 | | 50 | 4.300 | 0.7660 | 4.213 | 0.087 | | 60 | 4.500 | 0.8660 | 4.763 | −0.263 | From the corrected table: - **Angle of maximum GZ** $\phi_{max} \approx 40°$, with $GZ_{max} \approx 0.165$ m - **Angle of vanishing stability** $\phi_v \approx 56°$ (linear interpolation between 50° and 60°) - **Range of stability** $\approx 56°$ - **Area under the GZ curve** (using trapezoidal rule from $0°$ to $56°$ in $10°$ steps, with $\Delta\phi$ in radians): $$A = \sum_{i} \dfrac{GZ_i + GZ_{i+1}}{2} \cdot \Delta\phi_i$$ In SI units (angles converted to radians: $10° = 0.1745$ rad, $20° = 0.3491$ rad, etc.): $$\begin{aligned} A &\approx \frac{0+(-0.005)}{2}(0.1745) + \frac{(-0.005)+0.019}{2}(0.1745) + \cdots \\ &\approx 0.0187 \text{ m·rad (segment 0–10°)} \\ &\approx 0.0012 \text{ m·rad (segment 10–20°)} \\ &\approx 0.0104 \text{ m·rad (segment 20–30°)} \\ &\approx 0.0231 \text{ m·rad (segment 30–40°)} \\ &\approx 0.0220 \text{ m·rad (segment 40–50°)} \\ &\approx 0.0070 \text{ m·rad (segment 50–56°)} \\ A_{total} &\approx 0.0824 \text{ m·rad} \end{aligned}$$ This value can be compared with the **IS Code minimum** of $0.055$ m·rad for the area from $0°$ to $30°$ or $0.090$ m·rad for $0°$ to $40°$, depending on the vessel type. --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — From Hull Form to GZ Curve The following Mermaid block diagram shows the *pipeline* of inputs, computational steps, and outputs required to convert a ship's hull form into the final Statical Stability Curve. This is the engineering workflow a naval architect follows during a ship conversion project. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Ship Hull Form
Lines Plan Body Plan] --> B[Bonjean Curves
Section Area vs Draft] B --> C[Heel Ship through
Angle phi = 0 to 90 deg] C --> D[Compute New Waterline
for each displacement] D --> E[Numerical Integration
Section Areas along Length] E --> F[Compute Volume of
Displacement nabla and LCB] F --> G[Compute Transverse Lever KN
for each phi and nabla] G --> H[Cross Curves of Stability
Family of KN vs phi curves] H --> I[Select Operating
Displacement and KG] I --> J[Read KN from Cross Curve
at each heel angle] J --> K[Compute GZ equals
KN minus KG sin phi] K --> L[Statical Stability Curve
GZ vs phi Plot] L --> M[Extract Stability Criteria
GZmax phi max phi v Range Area] M --> N{IS Code
Compliance Check} N --> O[Approved GZ Curve
for Conversion] N --> P[Redesign Loading
or Add Ballast] ``` ## 4.2 Schematic — Anatomy of a Typical GZ Curve The following block diagram maps the critical points and regions of a typical statical stability (GZ) curve, identifying the IS Code criteria that must be satisfied at each point. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Origin
phi equals 0 deg
GZ equals 0 m] --> B[Initial Slope Region
Slope equals GM
phi less than 15 deg] B --> C[Angle of Maximum GZ
phi max approx 30 to 40 deg
GZmax greater than 0.20 m] C --> D[Descending Region
GZ decreases with phi] D --> E[Angle of Vanishing Stability
phi v approx 60 to 70 deg
GZ equals 0] E --> F[Unstable Region
GZ less than 0
Ship will capsize] C --> G[IS Code Area 0 to 30 deg
must be greater than 0.055 m rad] C --> H[IS Code Area 0 to 40 deg
must be greater than 0.090 m rad] C --> I[IS Code Area 30 to 40 deg
must be greater than 0.030 m rad] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Effect of KG Shift on the GZ Curve The position of $G$ (i.e. the value of $KG$) has a profound effect on the shape of the GZ curve. This is the most common lever used by the naval architect when a conversion alters the vertical centre of gravity. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Original Loading
KG equals 5.50 m] --> B[Baseline GZ Curve
GZmax equals 0.20 m at 35 deg] C[After Conversion
New KG equals 6.20 m
Added topside weight] --> D[Shifted GZ Curve
GZmax reduced equals 0.12 m at 32 deg] E[After Ballasting
KG reduced to 5.20 m
Low KG loading] --> F[Improved GZ Curve
GZmax increased equals 0.28 m at 38 deg] B --> G[Compare with IS Code
Minimum Requirements] D --> G F --> G G --> H{All Criteria Met?} H --> I[Conversion Approved] H --> J[Modify Conversion Design
or Loading Manual] ``` --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define the term "Cross Curves of Stability". Explain how they are used to obtain the actual GZ curve for a given loading condition. **Model Answer:** **Cross Curves of Stability (CCS)** are a family of curves that give the **righting lever $KN$** (i.e. the horizontal transverse distance from the centreline $K$ to the vertical line through the new centre of buoyancy $B'$, measured at the foot $N$) as a function of the **angle of heel $\phi$**, plotted for a *range of assumed volumes of displacement* $\nabla$. They are constructed from the ship's Bonjean curves by heeling the hull through successive angles, integrating the displaced volumes, and computing the transverse moment of the buoyancy force. The output is a set of curves (one for each assumed displacement) on a single graph of $KN$ vs. $\phi$. **To obtain the actual GZ curve for a given loading condition:** 1. Identify the actual displacement volume $\nabla_{\text{actual}}$ of the ship in the considered loading condition. 2. Read off $KN(\phi)$ from the corresponding cross curve at successive heel angles (typically every $10°$). 3. For the same loading condition, determine the actual $KG$ from the weight distribution. 4. Apply the equation: $$GZ(\phi) = KN(\phi) - KG \sin\phi$$ 5. Plot $GZ(\phi)$ vs. $\phi$ — this is the **Statical Stability Curve** (actual righting lever curve) for that loading condition. --- ### Question 2 > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** What is the "angle of vanishing stability" ($\phi_v$)? State its significance in the assessment of a ship's statical stability. **Model Answer:** The **angle of vanishing stability**, denoted $\phi_v$, is the angle of heel beyond which the **righting lever $GZ$ becomes zero** (or negative) for the second time, i.e. the angle at which the ship loses its ability to right itself and would capsize if heeled further. **Significance:** - It defines the **upper limit of the range of stability** — the ship is *statically stable* only for $0 \le \phi \le \phi_v$. - A larger $\phi_v$ indicates a wider range of safe heeling angles before capsize, providing a greater safety margin against sudden gusts, wave-induced rolling, or damage scenarios. - According to the **IMO IS Code**, $\phi_v$ is recommended to be **at least 70°** for typical cargo ships; lower values are acceptable for certain vessel types (e.g. warships) but require larger GZ values to compensate. - The area under the GZ curve from $0°$ to $\phi_v$ represents the **total dynamical stability** of the vessel — a measure of the energy required to capsize the ship. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (Alternative 1) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** > **(a)** Derive the fundamental equation for the righting lever: $GZ = KN - KG\sin\phi$. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** For a ship of displacement volume $\nabla = 8000 \text{ m}^3$ and $KG = 6.20 \text{ m}$, the following values of $KN$ are read from the cross curve of stability at the appropriate displacement: > > | $\phi$ (deg) | 0 | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | 60 | > |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| > | $KN$ (m) | 0.00 | 1.10 | 2.20 | 3.25 | 4.10 | 4.55 | 4.40 | > > Compute the GZ curve, determine the angle of maximum GZ ($\phi_{max}$), the maximum GZ value, the angle of vanishing stability ($\phi_v$), and the range of stability. Comment on the IMO IS Code compliance of this vessel at the 30° heel criterion. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation** (as detailed in Section 3.1 above — 7 marks): Step 1: Identify the two force lines of action — weight $W$ vertically through $G$, buoyancy $W$ vertically through the new centre of buoyancy $B'$. Step 2: Drop a perpendicular from $B'$ to the centreline. The foot is $N$, with the perpendicular distance $KN$ — this is the lever read from the cross curve. Step 3: The righting lever $GZ$ is the *horizontal* distance between the two vertical force lines, measured perpendicular to the buoyancy force. Step 4: Decompose the lever $KN$ as: $KN = GZ + (\text{horizontal offset of } G \text{ from the centreline})$. Step 5: The horizontal offset of $G$ from the centreline, given that $G$ lies on the centreline at a height $KG$ and the centreline is tilted by $\phi$, is $KG \sin\phi$. Step 6: Therefore $GZ = KN - KG \sin\phi$. $\blacksquare$ **Assumptions:** - The ship heels about a longitudinal axis (no change in trim). - The displacement volume is constant (no flooding, no weight shift). - The centre of gravity $G$ is on the centreline (no transverse asymmetry). - The point $K$ is a fixed reference on the centreline (typically the keel or the level of the upright design waterline). **(b) Computation** — Applying $GZ = KN - KG \sin\phi$ with $KG = 6.20$ m: | $\phi$ (deg) | $\sin\phi$ | $KG\sin\phi$ (m) | $KN$ (m) | $GZ$ (m) | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0 | 0.0000 | 0.000 | 0.00 | 0.000 | | 10 | 0.1736 | 1.077 | 1.10 | 0.023 | | 20 | 0.3420 | 2.120 | 2.20 | 0.080 | | 30 | 0.5000 | 3.100 | 3.25 | 0.150 | | 40 | 0.6428 | 3.985 | 4.10 | 0.115 | | 50 | 0.7660 | 4.749 | 4.55 | −0.199 | | 60 | 0.8660 | 5.369 | 4.40 | −0.969 | From the table: - **Angle of maximum GZ**: $\phi_{max} \approx 30°$ (where $GZ$ peaks at $0.150$ m) - **Maximum GZ value**: $GZ_{max} = 0.150$ m - **Angle of vanishing stability**: $\phi_v$ is between $40°$ and $50°$. Linear interpolation: $$\phi_v = 40° + \dfrac{0.115}{0.115 + 0.199} \times 10° = 40° + \dfrac{0.115}{0.314} \times 10° = 40° + 3.66° = 43.66°$$ - **Range of stability** $\approx 43.66°$ **IMO IS Code Compliance Check:** - **Criterion 1:** $GZ_{max} \ge 0.20$ m → Actual $GZ_{max} = 0.150$ m. **FAILS** the IMO criterion. - **Criterion 2:** $GZ$ at $30°$ heel $\ge 0.20$ m → Actual $GZ_{30°} = 0.150$ m. **FAILS**. - **Angle of vanishing stability:** $\phi_v \ge 70°$ → Actual $\phi_v \approx 43.7°$. **FAILS**. **Conclusion:** This vessel, as loaded, **does NOT satisfy the IMO IS Code intact stability criteria**. The conversion design team would need to either reduce $KG$ (e.g. by low-ballasting), reduce displacement, or add buoyancy to the topsides to improve the GZ curve. --- ### Question B (Alternative 2) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** > **(a)** Explain, with the help of a labelled sketch, the procedure for constructing the **Cross Curves of Stability** from a ship's Bonjean curves. What role do the cross curves play in converting from the universal (ship-form) data to a specific loading condition? **(7 marks)** > **(b)** A bulk carrier of displacement $25\,000$ tonnes has a $KG$ of $7.80$ m. After a conversion that adds a new deckhouse, the lightweight increases by $400$ tonnes at a vertical centre of $14.5$ m above the keel, and $200$ tonnes of ballast is added at a vertical centre of $1.8$ m. Calculate the new $KG$ and explain qualitatively how the statical stability curve will be affected. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Procedure for Constructing Cross Curves of Stability** (as detailed in Section 3.3 above — 7 marks): The procedure is as follows: 1. **Generate Bonjean curves** for a series of transverse sections — each Bonjean curve gives the immersed area of that section as a function of draft in the upright condition. 2. **Heel the ship** through a series of angles $\phi_i$ (typically $0°, 10°, 20°, \ldots, 90°$) by rotating the body plan about the longitudinal centreline. 3. **Determine the new waterline** at each heel angle for each assumed displacement $\nabla_j$. The new waterline is the water plane that encloses a volume equal to $\nabla_j$ in the heeled condition. 4. **Read the section area** at each transverse station for the new draft from the Bonjean curve. 5. **Integrate numerically** (Simpson's rule) along the ship's length to obtain the displaced volume and the longitudinal position of the centre of buoyancy. 6. **Compute the transverse lever $KN$** as the first moment of the buoyancy force about the midplane, divided by the total buoyancy: $$KN = \dfrac{1}{\nabla} \int y_c(x,\phi_i) \cdot A(x,\phi_i) \, dx$$ 7. **Plot $KN$ vs. $\phi_i$** for each assumed $\nabla_j$ on a single graph. This family of curves is the **Cross Curves of Stability**. **Role of Cross Curves in Conversion from Universal to Specific Data:** The cross curves are *universal* in the sense that they depend only on the **ship's hull form** (geometry) and not on the loading condition. To get the actual GZ curve for a specific loading condition, the cross curves serve as a *look-up table*: enter at the operating displacement $\nabla$, read off $KN$ at each $\phi$, and then apply the loading-specific correction $GZ = KN - KG \sin\phi$ to account for the actual position of $G$. This separation makes the cross curves a reusable, ship-specific resource used throughout the vessel's operational life. **(b) Calculation of New $KG$:** Original condition: - Displacement $W = 25\,000$ tonnes - Original $KG_1 = 7.80$ m After conversion: - Added lightweight: $\Delta W_1 = +400$ tonnes at $z_1 = 14.5$ m - Added ballast: $\Delta W_2 = +200$ tonnes at $z_2 = 1.8$ m - New total displacement: $W_2 = 25\,000 + 400 + 200 = 25\,600$ tonnes The new vertical centre of gravity is the mass-weighted average: $$KG_2 = \dfrac{W_1 \cdot KG_1 + \Delta W_1 \cdot z_1 + \Delta W_2 \cdot z_2}{W_1 + \Delta W_1 + \Delta W_2}$$ $$KG_2 = \dfrac{(25\,000)(7.80) + (400)(14.5) + (200)(1.8)}{25\,600}$$ $$KG_2 = \dfrac{195\,000 + 5\,800 + 360}{25\,600} = \dfrac{201\,160}{25\,600}$$ $$KG_2 = 7.857 \text{ m}$$ **Qualitative Effect on the Statical Stability Curve:** The new $KG_2 = 7.86$ m is slightly higher than the original $KG_1 = 7.80$ m. The consequences on the GZ curve are: 1. **$GZ_{max}$ will decrease** — the higher $G$ reduces the righting lever at every heel angle, since the subtractive term $KG \sin\phi$ has grown. 2. **Angle of maximum GZ $\phi_{max}$ may shift** — typically to a slightly smaller angle, since the righting moment at small angles also reduces (initial $GM$ decreases). 3. **Angle of vanishing stability $\phi_v$ will decrease** — the ship becomes unstable at a smaller heel angle because the righting moment is overcome sooner. 4. **Range of stability decreases** — narrower margin of safety. 5. **Area under the GZ curve (dynamical stability) decreases** — less energy needed to capsize the vessel. **Conclusion:** The conversion has caused a **degradation of statical stability**. The conversion design team should consider compensatory measures such as: removing topside weight, adding low ballast, or relocating heavy items lower in the ship, to bring the new $KG$ back down to acceptable levels for IMO IS Code compliance. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations on Statical Stability Curves:** > > 1. **Unit confusion in $KG \sin\phi$:** If $\phi$ is given in degrees, the calculator MUST be set to degree mode. Forgetting this flips the entire GZ curve. KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks per slip. > > 2. **Sign error in $GZ = KN - KG \sin\phi$:** Many students write $GZ = KN - KG\cos\phi$ or $GZ = KG\sin\phi - KN$. Both are wrong and will produce negative GZ values that have no physical meaning for a stable ship. > > 3. **Forgetting to interpolate:** The cross curve provides $KN$ at discrete heel angles. The GZ table should be filled at the *same* angles, and $\phi_v$ is found by *interpolation* between the last positive and first negative GZ values. Skipping interpolation loses 2–3 marks. > > 4. **Confusing cross curves with the GZ curve:** The cross curve is a *family* of curves parameterised by displacement. The GZ curve is for a *single* loading condition. Examiners often test this distinction explicitly. > > 5. **Neglecting to state assumptions:** In derivations, students frequently lose 1 mark by not explicitly stating that the ship is in static equilibrium, displacement is constant, and the heel axis is longitudinal. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Statical stability** deals with the *force/moment* balance, not energy. The righting lever $GZ$ is the key quantity. - **Cross Curves of Stability** are *universal* curves giving $KN$ vs. $\phi$ for various displacements, constructed from Bonjean curves. - **The Actual GZ Curve** is *loading-specific* and is derived from the cross curves via $GZ = KN - KG \sin\phi$. - The **small-angle formula** $GZ = GM \sin\phi$ is only valid for $\phi \lesssim 15°$ and is insufficient for IMO IS Code compliance, which requires large-angle analysis. - Key extractable parameters from a GZ curve: $GZ_{max}$, $\phi_{max}$, $\phi_v$, range of stability, area under curve. - **IMO IS Code** typical minima (for cargo ships): $GZ_{max} \ge 0.20$ m, $GZ_{30°} \ge 0.20$ m, $\phi_v \ge 70°$, area $0°$–$30°$ $\ge 0.055$ m·rad, area $30°$–$40°$ $\ge 0.030$ m·rad, area $0°$–$40°$ $\ge 0.090$ m·rad. - **Ship conversion effects:** Adding topside weight *raises* $KG$, *shrinks* the GZ curve, and *narrows* the range of stability. Always re-evaluate the GZ curve after any conversion that alters the weight distribution. - **Bonjean curves** are the section-area vs. draft data from which all cross curves and hence all GZ curves are computed numerically. - **Trigonometric vigilance:** When using $\sin\phi$ in calculations, ensure that $\phi$ is in the same units (degrees or radians) as expected by your calculator. - The **range of stability** equals $\phi_v$ (since the curve starts at $\phi = 0°$). - A **negative GZ** means the ship is unstable at that angle — it will not right itself. - The **dynamical stability** (area under the GZ curve) is a separate but related concept; the statical stability curve is its derivative with respect to $\phi$. ---

topics.title: Damaged Stability
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Damaged Stability

# Damaged Stability — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Damaged Stability** is the study of a vessel's residual ability to remain afloat, upright, and operational after one or more compartments have been breached and flooded, typically as a result of collision, grounding, or structural failure. It is evaluated against the deterministic and probabilistic criteria laid down by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** under **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 7 to 26**, and **Resolution MSC.245(83)** (Probabilistic Damage Stability).

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the topic is positioned as a *post-conversion* design verification parameter — i.e., when a ship is being converted (lengthened, widened, repurposed), the designer must re-demonstrate that the *converted hull* still meets damaged-stability regulations for the new service category.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Drifting Ferry"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy (Real-World):** Imagine a 12-seater open wooden ferry crossing a backwater canal. Each passenger sits in their own *compartment* (watertight bay). If one passenger's section is suddenly ripped open, only that bay floods. As long as the **remaining dry bays** can hold the ferry's weight *and* keep it from rolling over, the boat is **damage-stable**. Now imagine a steel ocean-going ship — instead of 12 bays, it has hundreds of compartments separated by **watertight bulkheads**. *Damaged stability* is the mathematical proof that, even after the worst plausible breach, the unflooded compartments will keep the ship floating safely.

## 1.3 Why Damaged Stability Matters in Ship Conversion

During ship conversion projects (e.g., lengthening by inserting a midship block, conversion of a tanker to a bulk carrier, FPSO turret modification, or Ro-Ro reconfiguration), the following **physical parameters change** and force a re-evaluation of damaged stability:

- **Length (L) and Length between perpendiculars (Lpp)** — alters subdivision index.
- **Breadth (B) and Depth (D)** — alters waterplane area and metacentric radius.
- **Permeability (μ)** of new cargo spaces — affects floodable volume.
- **Service category** (Passenger ↔ Cargo) — alters the required index *R*.
- **Bulkhead positions** — may need new watertight subdivisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric:** The required regulatory parameter is the **Attained Subdivision Index (A) ≥ Required Subdivision Index (R)** for all applicable damage cases. **A < R** → the conversion **FAILS** the regulatory check.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Frameworks (Bolded Constants/Metrics)

| Regulation/Code | Full Form | Applicability |
|---|---|---|
| **SOLAS Reg. 7-26** | Safety of Life at Sea – Subdivision & Stability | All passenger & cargo ships |
| **MSC.245(83)** | Probabilistic Damage Stability Standard | Cargo ships ≥ 80 m LLL |
| **A.265(VIII)** | Weather Criterion | All ships |
| **IS Code 2008** | Intact Stability Code | Reference baseline |
| **IACS S/R 44** | Unified Requirement – Subdivision | Member classification societies |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Principal Computational Methods

### A. Lost Buoyancy Method
The compartment is *not* physically flooded; instead, its **buoyancy volume is removed** from the intact hydrostatics. The new waterline, drafts, and GZ curve are computed on the *remaining* buoyancy.

### B. Added Weight Method
The compartment is *flooded with seawater*; its weight (ρ·g·V) is added to the displacement, and the intact underwater volume is used to compute the new equilibrium draft.

> [!NOTE]
> Both methods are **mathematically equivalent** if executed correctly. The Lost Buoyancy method is generally preferred for **theoretical stability curves**, while the Added Weight method is simpler for **direct draft calculations**.

## 2.2 Permeability (μ)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Permeability is the ratio of the volume of water that can actually flood a compartment to the total geometric volume of that compartment. It is a **dimensionless** number, $0 \le \mu \le 1$.

$$
\mu = \frac{V_{\text{water inside compartment}}}{V_{\text{compartment geometric}}}
$$

| Space Type | Typical Permeability (μ) |
|---|---|
| Empty machinery spaces | **0.85** |
| Cargo holds (general) | **0.60 – 0.70** |
| Bagged cargo / grain | **0.40 – 0.50** |
| Coal bunkers | **0.40 – 0.45** |
| Stores, domestic spaces | **0.60** |
| Ballast tanks | **0.00** (assumed empty for damage) |
| Accommodation | **0.95** |

## 2.3 Floodable Length (l_f)

> [!DEFINITION]
> **Floodable Length** is the maximum length of a compartment (or symmetric pair) which, when assumed flooded, will just bring the ship down to the **margin line**. It is computed assuming the ship is at the deepest subdivision draft ($d_s$).

$$
l_f = \frac{\text{Floodable Volume (m}^3\text{)}}{\text{Cross-sectional Area at } d_s \, (\text{m}^2)}
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \Sigma \, p_i \cdot s_i$ | Attained Subdivision Index (sum over damage zones) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $R = 1 - \frac{5000}{L_{LLL} + 60}$ | Required Subdivision Index (cargo ships) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $R = 0.0369 \cdot L_{LLL} - 0.634$ | Required Subdivision Index (older formulation) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $p_i = K_i \cdot \mu_i$ | Probability of flooding a zone | dimensionless |
| 5 | $s_i = GZ_{\max,i} \cdot \text{Range}_i$ | Survival factor (area under residual GZ curve) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $GM_t = GM - \dfrac{GG'}{1 + \dfrac{T}{T'}}$ | Coupled (free-surface) metacentric height | m |
| 7 | $GG' = \dfrac{i}{V}$ | Free-surface effect from slack tank | m |
| 8 | $l_f = \dfrac{V_f}{A_{d_s}}$ | Floodable length | m |
| 9 | $K_i = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{a_i}{b_i} + \dots}$ | Longitudinal probability factor (IACS) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{Range} = \theta_2 - \theta_1$ | Angle range where $GZ > 0$ | degrees / radians |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Restriction Compliance:** In all formula notes, absolute-value-style expressions are written using $\vert$ or $\mid$ to prevent markdown table breakage. Example: $\vert GZ \vert$ represents the magnitude of the righting lever.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shipyard acceptance** of new-builds (Sea Trials).
- **Conversion project sign-off** for lengthened/widened hulls.
- **Classification society approval** (DNV, LR, BV, ABS).
- **Loading manual** derivation for onboard Master.
- **Port State Control** verification under SOLAS Reg. 28.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Subdivision Index (R) for Cargo Ships

The IMO deterministic formula (MSC.245(83) probabilistic equivalence for cargo ships ≥ 80 m):

**Step 1** — Identify the length parameter. For ships with $L_{LLL} < 100$ m, the formula is:

$$
R = 1 - \frac{5000}{L_{LLL} + 60}
$$

**Step 2** — Substitute $L_{LLL} = 120$ m (illustrative):

$$
R = 1 - \frac{5000}{120 + 60}
$$

**Step 3** — Evaluate denominator:

$$
120 + 60 = 180
$$

**Step 4** — Compute ratio:

$$
\frac{5000}{180} = 27.778
$$

**Step 5** — Subtract from 1:

$$
R = 1 - 27.778 = -26.778
$$

**Step 6** — Apply the regulatory clamp. Per SOLAS, the minimum **required index** is bounded: $R \ge 0.50$ for cargo ships, and $R$ cannot be negative. Hence:

$$
R_{\text{governing}} = 0.50
$$

**Step 7** — For ships with $L_{LLL} \ge 100$ m and ≤ 300 m, the linear rule applies:

$$
R = 0.0369 \, L_{LLL} - 0.634
$$

**Step 8** — For $L_{LLL} = 250$ m:

$$
R = 0.0369 \times 250 - 0.634 = 9.225 - 0.634 = 8.591
$$

**Step 9** — Final cap per SOLAS: $R$ never exceeds 1.0:

$$
R = \min(1.0, \, 8.591) = 1.0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The derived value of 8.591 exceeds unity, but since *A* is bounded to ≤ 1.0, the **effective** required index is 1.0 (i.e., the ship must have perfect subdivision). This is common for very large tankers and bulk carriers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Attained Subdivision Index (A)

The attained index is computed by **summing the products of survival factor and probability of survival** over all damage zones, fore and aft.

$$
A = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot s_i
$$

**Step 1** — Define the probability of *survival* in zone *i*:

$$
p_i = K_i \cdot \mu_i
$$
