where $K_i$ is the longitudinal probability factor derived from the longitudinal damage extent ratio $J_i = a_i / b_i$.

**Step 2** — For a transverse single-compartment damage:

$$
K_i = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + J_i^2}
$$

**Step 3** — Compute the survival factor $s_i$ from the residual GZ curve. The GZ curve is generated by the *lost buoyancy* method:

$$
s_i = \frac{\text{Area under } GZ(\theta) \text{ curve from } 0 \text{ to } \theta_2}{\text{Reference area } (0.055 \text{ m·rad})}
$$

**Step 4** — Compute the reference area (SOLAS minimum):

$$
\text{Ref} = 0.055 + 0.001 \, (30 - \theta_2) \quad \text{[for } 20° \le \theta_2 \le 30°\text{]}
$$

**Step 5** — Combine:

$$
A = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i \cdot \mu_i \cdot s_i
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Free-Surface Effect (GG')

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When a partially filled tank is breached during damage, its free surface reduces the effective metacentric height. This is critical in **conversion projects** where ballast tanks are added or modified.

**Step 1** — Moment of inertia of free water surface:

$$
i = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} \quad \text{(rectangular surface)}
$$

**Step 2** — Transverse reduction in GM:

$$
GG' = \frac{\rho_{\text{water}} \cdot i}{\rho_{\text{seawater}} \cdot V_{\text{displacement}}}
$$

**Step 3** — For seawater ≈ fresh water correction, take $\rho_w / \rho_{sw} \approx 0.985$:

$$
GG' = 0.985 \cdot \frac{i}{V}
$$

**Step 4** — New effective GM:

$$
GM_{\text{eff}} = GM - GG'
$$

**Numerical Example:** Displacement $V = 12{,}500 \text{ m}^3$, slack tank $L = 18$ m, $B = 12$ m.

$$
i = \frac{18 \times 12^3}{12} = \frac{18 \times 1728}{12} = 2592 \text{ m}^4
$$

$$
GG' = 0.985 \times \frac{2592}{12{,}500} = 0.2042 \text{ m}
$$

$$
GM_{\text{eff}} = GM - 0.2042
$$

If $GM = 1.05$ m, then $GM_{\text{eff}} = 0.846$ m.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Margin Line and Permissible Draft

**Step 1** — The **margin line** is a line drawn at least **76 mm (3 inches)** below the top of the bulkhead deck at the side.

**Step 2** — Maximum permissible draft after damage is governed by:

$$
d_{\text{max,damage}} = D - 0.076 - h_{\text{transom}}
$$

**Step 3** — The deepest subdivision draft $d_s$ is normally the **load line draft**.

**Step 4** — Any damage case that submerges the margin line is **non-compliant**.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Examiner Pattern: Students frequently forget the **76 mm** margin and lose 2–3 marks. Always cite the SOLAS Reg. 13 value in full.

---

## 3.5 Conversion Project — Worked Sub-Problem

> **Problem Context:** A 130 m general cargo ship is being lengthened by inserting a 12 m parallel midship block. The new $L_{LLL} = 142$ m. Existing GM = 1.10 m, $V = 13{,}200$ m³. Determine:
> 1. New required subdivision index.
> 2. Whether the existing A = 0.62 remains valid.

**Step 1** — Compute R for $L_{LLL} = 142$ m:

$$
R = 0.0369 \times 142 - 0.634 = 5.240 - 0.634 = 4.606
$$

**Step 2** — Apply regulatory cap:

$$
R = \min(1.0, 4.606) = 1.0
$$

**Step 3** — Compare with existing A:

$$
A = 0.62 \quad \text{vs} \quad R = 1.0
$$

**Step 4** — Conclusion: $A < R$ → **Conversion fails compliance**. Designer must:
- Add intermediate bulkheads (subdivide further), or
- Increase GM by relocating low-density cargo spaces, or
- Re-distribute $s_i$ values by optimizing bulkhead positions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even though the *ship got longer* (intuitively more buoyancy), the *required index* scaled up faster because $R$ approaches 1.0 for large ships. **Longer ≠ safer** in damage stability terms.

---

## 3.6 Cross-Flooding & Equalization (Conceptual Derivations)

### 3.6.1 Cross-Flooding Opening
A fitted opening (with a remote-operated valve) that allows *symmetric* flooding on the opposite side of a compartment.

> [!DEFINITION]
> **Cross-flooding** is used to **reduce the heeling moment** after damage. The opening size is computed as:

$$
A_{\text{opening}} = \frac{M_{\text{heel}}}{g \cdot \rho_w \cdot t^2 / 2}
$$

where $M_{\text{heel}}$ is the heeling moment to be neutralized, and $t$ is the time allowed (typically 1–3 minutes for SOLAS).

### 3.6.2 Equalization (SOLAS Reg. 25)
If a watertight compartment contains piping that allows progressive flooding at equal pressure, the time to flood is:

$$
t = \frac{V_{\text{compartment}}}{C_d \cdot A_{\text{pipe}} \sqrt{2g \cdot h}}
$$

**Step 1** — Head $h$ = distance from the load waterline to the centroid of the submerged opening.

**Step 2** — Discharge coefficient $C_d \approx 0.62$ for sharp-edged orifices.

**Step 3** — Plug in numbers: $V = 800$ m³, $A = 0.06$ m², $h = 4$ m.

$$
t = \frac{800}{0.62 \times 0.06 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 4}}
$$

$$
t = \frac{800}{0.0372 \times 8.86} = \frac{800}{0.3297} = 2426 \text{ s} \approx 40.4 \text{ min}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> SOLAS allows up to 60 min for equalization under Reg. 25.7; this opening would be acceptable.

---

## 3.7 Critical Damage Cases (the KTU "Big Five")

| # | Case | Common Conversion Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Side damage amidships, single compartment | Standard worst-case for tankers |
| 2 | Bow damage (typically 0.05 L) | Bulbous bow modification |
| 3 | Stern damage (typically 0.05 L) | Stern ramp / Ro-Ro conversion |
| 4 | Engine room flooding | Generator repowering conversion |
| 5 | Ro-Ro deck flooding | Vehicle deck subdivision |

For each, the **maximum GZ** must occur at an angle $\theta_{\max} \ge 15°$ but not exceeding **45°**, and the area under the GZ curve from 0° to 40° must be **≥ 0.090 m·rad**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Damage-Stability Evaluation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Damage Stability Check]
    A1[Define LLL, B, D, ds]
    A2[Compute Required Index R]
    A3[Set Damage Cases: 5 standard zones]
    A4[Generate Residual GZ Curve per Case]
    A5[Compute Survival Factor si]
    A6[Compute Probability Factor pi = Ki times mu i]
    A7[Sum Attained Index A = sum pi si]
    A8{A greater than or equal to R?}
    A9[Result: COMPLIANT - Issue Stability Booklet]
    A10[Result: NON-COMPLIANT - Revise Subdivision]
    A11[Action: Add Bulkheads or Modify Permeability]
    A12[Re-run GZ and Re-evaluate]
    A13[Final Approval by Class]

    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8
    A8 -- YES --> A9
    A8 -- NO --> A10
    A10 --> A11
    A11 --> A12
    A12 --> A4
    A9 --> A13
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Cross-Section of a Damaged Compartment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[INTACT HULL CROSS-SECTION]
        nodeP[PORT SIDE]
        nodeS[STARBOARD SIDE]
        nodeBL[BULKHEAD DECK]

        nodeML[MARGIN LINE 76 mm below deck]
        nodeG[G - Center of Gravity]
        nodeB[B - Center of Buoyancy]
        nodeM[M - Metacenter]
        nodeGM[GM vector]
    end

    subgraph S2[POST-DAMAGE HULL CROSS-SECTION]
        nodeDAM[DAMAGED COMPARTMENT FLOODED]
        nodeNEW_WL[NEW WATERLINE rises]
        nodeB_NEW[NEW B shifted to port]
        nodeHEEL[Heel angle phi]
    end

    nodeP --- nodeS
    nodeS --- nodeBL
    nodeBL --- nodeWL
    nodeWL --- nodeML
    nodeG --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeM
    nodeM --- nodeGM

    nodeDAM --- nodeNEW_WL
    nodeB_NEW --- nodeHEEL
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Conversion Impact on Subdivision Index

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Existing Ship Hydrostatics]
    C1[Conversion: Lengthen by 12 m]
    C2[New Length LLL new = LLL old plus 12]
    C3[Re-classify Service: Cargo to Multi-Purpose]
    C4[Required R INCREASES toward 1.0]
    C5[Permeability mu of new holds CHANGES]
    C6[Attained A RECOMPUTED]
    C7{A greater than or equal to R?}

    C9[Need additional bulkhead]
    C10[Re-test and Approve]

    C0 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C3 --> C5
    C4 --> C7
    C5 --> C6
    C6 --> C7
    C7 -- YES --> C8
    C7 -- NO --> C9
    C9 --> C10
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — Probabilistic vs Deterministic Assessment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Choose Method]
    P1[Deterministic: A265 older ships]
    P2[Probabilistic: MSC245 all new cargo ships]
    P3[Compute s_i for each zone]
    P4[Compute p_i using IACS K factor curves]
    P5[Sum products]
    P6[Output A]
    P7[Compare with R]

    P0 --> P1
    P0 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P5
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7
```

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `nodeP`, `C7`, `P4`) to avoid conflicts with reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, or `graph`.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the term "Attained Subdivision Index" (A) and state its regulatory significance under SOLAS.**

**Model Answer:**
The *Attained Subdivision Index (A)* is the **sum of products of probability of survival and survival factor** for all damage zones fore and aft of a ship, evaluated under SOLAS Ch. II-1 regulations. It is a **dimensionless** number, $0 \le A \le 1$. Regulatory significance: a ship is considered **compliant** only if $A \ge R$, where $R$ is the *Required Subdivision Index* derived from the ship's length and service category. If $A < R$, the vessel fails the damage-stability check and cannot be registered for service.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the significance of the "Margin Line" in damaged stability assessment?**

**Model Answer:**
The *Margin Line* is a **reference line drawn on the ship's profile, at least 76 mm (3 inches) below the top of the bulkhead deck at the side** (SOLAS Reg. 13). It represents the **maximum permissible waterline** after a damage case. Any damage scenario that causes the residual waterline to reach or submerge this line is considered **non-compliant**. The margin line provides a *safety reserve* against progressive flooding, wave action, and uncertainty in permeability assumptions.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — July 2024]`

**A bulk carrier of length $L_{LLL} = 150$ m is being lengthened by inserting a 10 m parallel midship block. After conversion:**
- New displacement: $V = 19{,}500$ m³
- Original GM = 1.20 m
- Slack ballast tank dimensions: $L = 20$ m, $B = 14$ m

**(a)** Compute the new required subdivision index $R$ and the effective GM after accounting for free-surface effect. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Briefly explain the 'Lost Buoyancy' method of generating a residual GZ curve after damage, with a neat sketch of the procedure. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)

**Step 1** — Compute $R$ for $L_{LLL} = 150 + 10 = 160$ m:

$$
R = 0.0369 \times 160 - 0.634 = 5.904 - 0.634 = 5.270
$$

**Step 2** — Apply the regulatory cap $R \le 1.0$:

$$
R_{\text{effective}} = \min(1.0, 5.270) = 1.0
$$

**Step 3** — Compute moment of inertia of free water surface:

$$
i = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{20 \times 14^3}{12} = \frac{20 \times 2744}{12} = 4573.33 \text{ m}^4
$$

**Step 4** — Compute $GG'$:

$$
GG' = 0.985 \times \frac{4573.33}{19{,}500} = 0.985 \times 0.2345 = 0.2310 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5** — Effective GM:

$$
GM_{\text{eff}} = 1.20 - 0.231 = 0.969 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6** — Conclusion: GM is still positive but reduced by ~19%. Damage stability must be re-evaluated.

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)

**Lost Buoyancy Method — Procedure:**

1. Treat the damaged compartment as **buoyancy-removal** (not flooding). The waterline is redrawn assuming the flooded volume cannot contribute to support.
2. The intact hydrostatic data (KN curves, waterplane area, displacement) is used, **excluding** the geometric volume of the damaged compartment.
3. Heeling moments are computed about the **centerline** based on asymmetric flooding (e.g., side damage, off-center).
4. The residual GZ curve is plotted from $\theta = 0°$ to $\theta = \theta_2$ (vanishing-stability angle).
5. Survival factor $s_i$ = area under residual GZ curve / reference area.
6. Probability $p_i$ = $K_i \cdot \mu_i$ — combined with $s_i$ to yield A.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Q-A)**: Students often apply $R = 0.0369 L - 0.634$ even when $L < 100$ m, which is wrong. The 5000-formula applies for **shorter** ships. Always state the applicable range.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A passenger ship has $L_{LLL} = 110$ m. The forward machinery space (μ = 0.85) is symmetric about the centerline. Cross-flooding is fitted to a side compartment (μ = 0.60) at the same vertical level. Compute the required index R, and discuss the role of cross-flooding in damage stability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cargo ship experiences engine-room flooding. Explain with derivation how the residual righting lever $GZ_R$ is calculated using the added weight method, and show that the maximum permissible damage draft must satisfy the margin-line criterion. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)

**Step 1** — For passenger ships, the formula for R differs from cargo. SOLAS Reg. 6 / 7 uses:

$$
R = 0.0369 \times 110 - 0.634 = 4.059 - 0.634 = 3.425
$$

(Clamped to $R = 1.0$ by SOLAS).

**Step 2** — Therefore $R_{\text{effective}} = 1.0$.

**Step 3** — Role of cross-flooding:
- Reduces **asymmetric flooding** (heeling moment).
- Distributes floodwater symmetrically between port and starboard.
- Permits the ship to remain at a *smaller* final heel angle.
- Increases the survival factor $s_i$ by extending the range of positive GZ.
- Improves the attained index $A$ without changing the required index $R$.

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)

**Step 1** — Added weight: $W_{\text{added}} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \mu \cdot V_{\text{ER}}$

**Step 2** — New equilibrium draft $d_{\text{new}}$ is found by solving:

$$
\Delta_{\text{new}} = \Delta_{\text{intact}} + W_{\text{added}}
$$

**Step 3** — Using intact hydrostatics (constant underwater volume shape), the new mean draft:

$$
d_{\text{new}} = d_{\text{intact}} + \frac{W_{\text{added}}}{\rho_{sw} \cdot A_W}
$$

where $A_W$ is the intact waterplane area.

**Step 4** — Heeling moment (if off-center):

$$
M_{\text{heel}} = W_{\text{added}} \cdot y
$$

where $y$ is the transverse distance from centerline to the centroid of the ER.

**Step 5** — Heel angle $\phi$:

$$
\tan \phi = \frac{M_{\text{heel}}}{\Delta_{\text{new}} \cdot GM_{\text{eff}}}
$$

**Step 6** — Margin line check:

$$
d_{\text{new, max}} \le D - 0.076
$$

If violated, the damage case is non-compliant.

**Step 7** — Conclusion: The added weight method gives a direct route to draft and heel, and the margin-line check is the gating SOLAS criterion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q-B):** Many students write the added weight equation without stating the *waterplane-area assumption* (intact A_W is reused). If not stated, full 7 marks are not awarded. Also, omitting the **0.076 m margin** causes 2-mark deductions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print-friendly high-density summary of Damaged Stability for KTU 2024:**

- **Damaged stability** = residual ability to float safely after flooding.
- **Two methods**: Lost Buoyancy (curve generation) vs. Added Weight (draft calculation).
- **Permeability (μ)** = volume of water / geometric volume of compartment.
- **Floodable length ($l_f$)** = max length of compartment that can flood without submerging margin line.
- **Margin line** = 76 mm (3 in) below top of bulkhead deck.
- **Attained Index $A = \sum p_i s_i$** — must exceed **Required Index $R$**.
- **R formula (cargo, $L_{LLL} \ge 100$ m):** $R = 0.0369 \cdot L_{LLL} - 0.634$ (capped at 1.0).
- **R formula (cargo, $L_{LLL} < 100$ m):** $R = 1 - \dfrac{5000}{L_{LLL} + 60}$ (min 0.50).
- **Survival factor $s_i$** = area under residual GZ / reference area (0.055 m·rad for $\theta_2 \ge 30°$).
- **Probability factor $p_i$** = $K_i \cdot \mu_i$, where $K_i$ is from IACS S/R 44 curves.
- **Free-surface effect:** $GG' = \rho_w i / (\rho_{sw} V)$.
- **Cross-flooding** = symmetric flooding to reduce heel moment.
- **Conversion triggers** (re-evaluation): length change, breadth change, service change, bulkhead repositioning, permeability change.
- **Critical GZ criteria:** $GZ_{\max} \ge 0.20$ m at $\theta \ge 20°$, area under curve $\ge 0.090$ m·rad up to 40°, range $\ge 20°$.
- **Three regulatory pillars:** SOLAS Reg. 7–26, MSC.245(83), IS Code 2008.
- **Permeability reference values**: empty machinery = 0.85, cargo = 0.60, stores = 0.60, accommodations = 0.95.
- **Common conversion fail modes**: too long a block inserted (raises R), wrong μ assumed, slack tanks not credited.
- **Examiner trap**: omitting units in numerical answers, mixing static GM with effective GM.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Seakeeping and Maneuvering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Seakeeping and Maneuvering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Seakeeping — Formal Definition

**Seakeeping** is the nautical science and engineering discipline that studies the behaviour of a ship in a seaway. It quantifies the ship's **motions, accelerations, structural loads, and operability** when subjected to wind-generated ocean waves. Formally, seakeeping analyses determine the response of a vessel in terms of its **six rigid-body degrees of freedom (6-DOF)** in regular and irregular waves.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A ship is said to possess "good seakeeping" if it can accomplish its mission (cargo transport, fishing, patrolling) with **acceptable motions, low risk of slamming, deck wetness, and crew/equipment operability** in the design sea state.

## 1.2 Maneuvering — Formal Definition

**Maneuvering** is the hydrodynamic study of the controlled motion of a ship under its own propulsion and steering system, in calm water, to change course or speed. It covers **course keeping, course changing, turning, and stopping** performance. In ship conversion projects, altered hull form, displacement, appendages (rudder, bilge keels, fins), or superstructure may drastically change both seakeeping and maneuvering characteristics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Context:** When a ship is converted (e.g., a bulk carrier to an FPSO, a tanker to an FSO, or a cargo ship to a livestock carrier), its **displacement, GM, metacentric height, hull form, and added appendages change**, making the original seakeeping and maneuvering data invalid. New analyses are mandatory under Class and IMO rules.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine driving a tall SUV versus a low sports car on a bumpy, undulating road:
- The **SUV rolls and pitches** uncomfortably (poor seakeeping analogue).
- The **sports car** stays planted and turns crisply (good seakeeping + good maneuvering).

For a ship, the "bumpy road" is the **ocean wave spectrum**, and the "car" is a multi-thousand-tonne floating steel structure forced to heave, roll, pitch, surge, sway and yaw simultaneously. The conversion engineer's job is to predict, control, and mitigate these responses.

## 1.4 The Six Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF) of Ship Motion

| Translational | Rotational |
|---|---|
| Surge (along X) | Roll (about X) |
| Sway (along Y) | Pitch (about Y) |
| Heave (along Z) | Yaw (about Z) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Axes (KTU Convention):** X = forward (bow to stern), Y = starboard (port to starboard), Z = upward (keel to deck). Positive rotation follows the right-hand rule.

## 1.5 Key Performance Metrics (Bold = Exam Favourites)

- **Significant Wave Height $H_s$** (m) — average height of the highest 1/3 of waves.
- **Zero-Crossing Period $T_z$** (s) — mean period of wave up-crossings.
- **Peak Spectral Period $T_p$** (s) — period of maximum energy in the wave spectrum.
- **Encounter Frequency $\omega_e$** (rad/s) — frequency at which the ship *meets* the waves.
- **Response Amplitude Operator (RAO)** — ship motion amplitude per unit wave amplitude.
- **Motion Sickness Incidence (MSI)** — % of crew incapacitated per hour.
- **Tactical Diameter $D_T$** (m) — diameter of the turning circle.
- **Advance $A$** (m) — distance travelled along original course during 90° turn.
- **Transfer $T_r$** (m) — sideways displacement at 90° heading change.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sea State Description

A realistic ocean surface is a **superposition of infinite regular wave components** of different frequencies, amplitudes, and random phases. This is described by a **wave energy spectrum** $S(\omega)$ with units of $\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}/\text{rad}$.

### 2.1.1 Pierson–Moskowitz (PM) Spectrum (Fully Developed Sea)

$$S_{PM}(\omega) = \frac{A}{\omega^5} \exp\!\left(-\frac{B}{\omega^4}\right)$$

- $A = 8.1 \times 10^{-3} \, g^2$
- $B = 0.74 \left(\dfrac{g}{U_{19.5}}\right)^4$
- $U_{19.5}$ = wind speed at 19.5 m elevation.
- $g = 9.81 \;\text{m/s}^2$ — **gravitational acceleration constant**.

### 2.1.2 JONSWAP Spectrum (Fetch-Limited Sea)

The JONSWAP spectrum refines PM by adding a **peak enhancement factor** $\gamma$ (typically 1.0 to 7.0, with mean 3.3):

$$S_{J}(\omega) = S_{PM}(\omega) \cdot \gamma^{\exp\left[-\frac{(\omega-\omega_p)^2}{2\sigma^2 \omega_p^2}\right]}$$

## 2.2 Encounter Frequency — The "Relative Motion" Concept

When a ship moves with forward speed $V$ into a wave of frequency $\omega$ and heading angle $\mu$ (angle between ship heading and wave direction), the **relative frequency** at which the ship meets wave crests is:

$$\omega_e = \omega - \dfrac{\omega^2 V \cos\mu}{g}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters:** The ship's motion is excited by $\omega_e$, **NOT** $\omega$. At high forward speed with following seas ($\mu = 0^\circ$), $\omega_e$ can become very small → **long-period resonant heave/pitch motions** — extremely dangerous for converted vessels with large superstructures.

## 2.3 Ship Roll Motion (Simplified Single-DOF)

A roll equation in regular beam waves is treated as a **damped, forced oscillator**:

$$(I_{xx} + A_{44})\ddot{\phi} + B_{44}\dot{\phi} + C_{44}\,\phi = F_4 \cos(\omega_e t - \epsilon)$$

- $I_{xx}$ = mass moment of inertia about the longitudinal axis.
- $A_{44}$ = added moment of inertia (hydrodynamic).
- $B_{44}$ = damping coefficient.
- $C_{44} = \Delta \cdot \overline{GM}$ — **restoring moment coefficient** (huge in KTU exams).
- $F_4$ = wave exciting moment amplitude.
- $\epsilon$ = phase lag.

### Natural Roll Period (The "KTU Favourite" Formula)

$$T_\phi = \dfrac{2\pi \, k}{\sqrt{g \cdot \overline{GM}}}$$

Or, in the more common form using beam $B$:

$$T_\phi \approx \dfrac{C \, B}{\sqrt{\overline{GM}}} \quad \text{with } C \approx 0.80 \text{ (typical warship/merchant)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Conversion Pitfall:** Adding deckhouses, kingposts, or cargo increases $\overline{KG}$ → reduces $\overline{GM}$ → **increases $T_\phi$** → larger roll amplitudes and slower roll damping.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Exam Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| Significant wave height | $H_s \approx 4\sqrt{m_0}$ | m | High |
| Spectral moments | $m_n = \int_0^\infty \omega^n S(\omega)\,d\omega$ | $\text{m}^2 \text{s}^{n-1}$ | Medium |
| Encounter frequency | $\omega_e = \omega - \dfrac{\omega^2 V \cos\mu}{g}$ | rad/s | **Very High** |
| Natural roll period | $T_\phi = 2\pi k / \sqrt{g \cdot \overline{GM}}$ | s | **Very High** |
| Roll RAO | $RAO_\phi = \dfrac{\phi_a}{\zeta_a}$ (function of $\omega_e$) | rad/m | High |
| Critical damping ratio | $\zeta = B_{44}/(2\sqrt{C_{44}(I_{44}+A_{44})})$ | – | High |
| Tactical diameter (IMO) | $D_T \le 5 L_{pp}$ | m | **Very High** |
| Advance (IMO) | $A \le 4.5 L_{pp}$ | m | **Very High** |
| IMO Stopping distance | $\le 15 L_{pp}$ from 90° turn | m | Medium |
| IMO Initial turning radius | $R_0 \approx 0.5 L_{pp}$ to $L_{pp}$ | m | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> In every table, the unit of $\overline{GM}$ is metres (m); $L_{pp}$ is the length between perpendiculars.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **FPSO / FSO conversion:** Loss of forward speed → no $\omega_e$ shift → different motion behaviour in survival storms.
- **Adding side hulls (SWATH conversion):** Drastically alters $A_{44}, B_{44}, C_{44}$ — totally new RAO.
- **Fitting stabilizers:** Adds an active control moment $\Delta M = K_p \phi + K_d \dot{\phi}$, modifying the roll equation.
- **Increased freeboard** (e.g., adding sponsons) increases added heave resistance, reducing heave RAO peaks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Encounter Frequency $\omega_e$

### Step 1: Set Up the Wave-Crest Geometry

Consider a ship moving with velocity $V$ along a heading that makes angle $\mu$ with the wave propagation direction. The wave crests travel with **phase velocity**:

$$c = \dfrac{\omega}{k} = \dfrac{g}{\omega} \quad \text{(deep-water dispersion)}$$

### Step 2: Relative Velocity Along the Wave Direction

The component of the ship's velocity along the wave propagation direction is $V \cos\mu$. The **relative speed of wave crests passing the ship** is:

$$V_{rel} = c - V \cos\mu = \dfrac{g}{\omega} - V \cos\mu$$

### Step 3: Apply the Definition of Encounter Frequency

By definition, the encounter frequency equals the deep-water wave number $k = \omega^2/g$ times $V_{rel}$:

$$\omega_e = k V_{rel} = \dfrac{\omega^2}{g}\left(\dfrac{g}{\omega} - V\cos\mu\right)$$

### Step 4: Simplify

$$\omega_e = \omega - \dfrac{\omega^2 V \cos\mu}{g} \qquad \blacksquare$$

### Step 5: Limiting Cases (Examination Insight)

- **Head seas ($\mu = 180^\circ$):** $\cos\mu = -1 \Rightarrow \omega_e = \omega + \dfrac{\omega^2 V}{g}$ — *frequency increases* (shorter encounter period).
- **Following seas ($\mu = 0^\circ$):** $\cos\mu = +1 \Rightarrow \omega_e = \omega - \dfrac{\omega^2 V}{g}$ — *frequency decreases* (longer encounter period, dangerous resonance).
- **Beam seas ($\mu = 90^\circ$):** $\cos\mu = 0 \Rightarrow \omega_e = \omega$ — ship sees waves at true frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the ship is in following seas with $\omega_e \to 0$, then the period of encounter $T_e = 2\pi/\omega_e \to \infty$ — the ship rides on a single wave for a very long time, and **broaching** can occur.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Natural Roll Period $T_\phi$

### Step 1: Restoring Moment on a Heeled Ship

When a ship heels by a small angle $\phi$, the centre of buoyancy $B$ shifts outwards by an amount proportional to the **transverse metacentric radius** $\overline{BM}$. The righting arm is:

$$\overline{GZ} = \overline{GM} \sin\phi \approx \overline{GM}\,\phi \quad \text{(small angles)}$$

The righting moment is then:

$$M_{restoring} = W \cdot \overline{GZ} \approx W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \phi$$

where $W = \Delta g$ is the weight.

### Step 2: Equation of Motion (Free, Undamped Roll)

Moment of inertia about the roll axis is $I_{xx}$. Newton's second law for rotation:

$$I_{xx}\,\ddot{\phi} = -W \cdot \overline{GM}\,\phi$$

### Step 3: Rearrange into Standard SHM Form

$$\ddot{\phi} + \dfrac{W \cdot \overline{GM}}{I_{xx}}\,\phi = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_n^2 = \dfrac{W \cdot \overline{GM}}{I_{xx}} = \dfrac{\Delta g \cdot \overline{GM}}{I_{xx}}$$

### Step 4: Period of Natural Roll

$$T_\phi = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{xx}}{\Delta g \cdot \overline{GM}}}$$

### Step 5: Express Using Radius of Gyration $k$ where $I_{xx} = \Delta k_{xx}^2$

$$T_\phi = \dfrac{2\pi \, k_{xx}}{\sqrt{g \cdot \overline{GM}}} \qquad \blacksquare$$

### Step 6: Empirical Approximation (Very Common in KTU)

For typical merchant ships, $k_{xx} \approx 0.40\,B$ (where $B$ = beam):

$$T_\phi \approx \dfrac{2\pi (0.40\,B)}{\sqrt{g \cdot \overline{GM}}} \approx 0.80 \dfrac{B}{\sqrt{\overline{GM}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The empirical coefficient varies: $0.78$ (cargo), $0.80$ (passenger), $0.82$ (warship), with $B$ in metres, $\overline{GM}$ in metres, gives $T_\phi$ in seconds.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Motion vs. Sea State vs. Operational Limit

| Motion | Dominant Exciting Force | Critical Sea Direction | Operational Limit | IMO/Class Indicator |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Heave | Buoyancy variation | Head / Following | $\ddot{z} \le 0.2 g$ | Crew effectiveness |
| Roll | Wave moment + wind | Beam (90°) | $\phi_{1/3} \le 30^\circ$ | Cargo shift, capsize |
| Pitch | Heave asymmetry | Head (180°) | $\theta \le 4^\circ$–$6^\circ$ | Slamming, deck wetness |
| Yaw | Asymmetric forces | Quartering | Drift angle ≤ several deg | Course keeping |
| Surge | Added resistance | Head | $R_{AW}$ limit | Speed loss |
| Sway | Lateral drift | Beam | Drift angle | Track-keeping |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For ship conversion projects, this matrix forms the basis of a **Seakeeping Assessment Report** required by classification societies such as DNV, ABS, BV, and LR for any **major conversion or lengthening**.

---

## 3.4 IMO Maneuvering Standards — Derivation of Numerical Criteria

The IMO Resolution **MSC.137(76)** sets the following trial conditions for ships above 100 m LOA:

- **Tactical Diameter ($D_T$):** $D_T \le 5 L_{pp}$
- **Advance ($A$):** $A \le 4.5 L_{pp}$
- **Transfer ($T_r$):** $T_r \le 2.0 L_{pp}$ (some administrations)
- **Stopping Distance:** from approach speed with hard rudder → $\le 15 L_{pp}$ from the 90° heading point
- **Initial Turning Radius:** $R_0$ shall be **between $0.5 L_{pp}$ and $1.0 L_{pp}$**

### Step-by-Step Worked Procedure to Check IMO Compliance

1. From turning-trial data, plot the **trajectory of the ship's CG** in body-fixed coordinates $(X, Y)$.
2. Identify the **final steady turning radius** $R = V / r_{ss}$ where $r_{ss}$ is the steady yaw rate.
3. Read off $D_T = 2R$, $A$ = X-distance at $\psi = 90^\circ$, $T_r$ = Y-distance at $\psi = 90^\circ$.
4. Normalise by $L_{pp}$ and compare with IMO limits.

> [!WARNING]
> **Conversion Impact:** Adding a bulbous bow or mid-body extension changes $L_{pp}$ and the moment of inertia $I_{zz}$ — converted ship may **fail** IMO trial tests. Re-trials and possibly **rudder area increase** may be required.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Encounter Frequency Calculation

**Given:** Ship speed $V = 15$ knots, wave height $H = 4$ m, peak period $T_p = 9$ s, heading relative to waves $\mu = 60^\circ$ (waves on the bow).

**Step 1: Convert speed**  
$V = 15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716 \;\text{m/s}$

**Step 2: Wave frequency**  
$\omega = 2\pi / T_p = 2\pi / 9 = 0.698 \;\text{rad/s}$

**Step 3: Compute encounter frequency**

$$\omega_e = \omega - \dfrac{\omega^2 V \cos\mu}{g}$$

$$\omega_e = 0.698 - \dfrac{(0.698)^2 \times 7.716 \times \cos 60^\circ}{9.81}$$

$$\omega_e = 0.698 - \dfrac{0.487 \times 7.716 \times 0.5}{9.81} = 0.698 - \dfrac{1.880}{9.81}$$

$$\omega_e = 0.698 - 0.1916 = 0.506 \;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 4: Encounter period**

$$T_e = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_e} = \dfrac{6.283}{0.506} \approx 12.4 \;\text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ship meets waves every **12.4 s** (longer than the true 9 s period), so each motion cycle is gentler but longer. This is the basis of the **synchronisation / resonance** check against the ship's natural roll period $T_\phi$.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — Natural Roll Period

**Given:** Beam $B = 30$ m, $\overline{GM} = 1.5$ m, cargo ship empirical constant $C = 0.80$.

**Step 1: Apply empirical formula**

$$T_\phi = \dfrac{0.80 \times 30}{\sqrt{1.5}} = \dfrac{24}{1.225} \approx 19.6 \;\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Resonance check** — If the encounter period $T_e$ in the worst sea state lies between $0.7 T_\phi$ and $1.3 T_\phi$ (i.e., **$13.7$ to $25.5$ s**), then the ship is in a **resonance danger zone** and the seakeeping performance is poor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Engineering Action:** If the converted vessel exhibits $T_\phi$ in the same band as typical $T_e$ values of the operating area, install **bilge keels, anti-roll tanks, fin stabilisers, or reduce KG by lightweight superstructure design**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ship's Six Degrees of Freedom — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Ship at Rest in Waves] --> B[External Excitations]
    B --> B1[Wave Forces & Moments]
    B --> B2[Wind Forces & Moments]
    B --> B3[Current Forces]
    B1 --> C[Hydrodynamic Response System]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D1[Surge - X translation]
    C --> D2[Sway - Y translation]
    C --> D3[Heave - Z translation]
    C --> D4[Roll - Rotation about X]
    C --> D5[Pitch - Rotation about Y]
    C --> D6[Yaw - Rotation about Z]
    D1 --> E[Seakeeping Outputs]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    E --> F1[Motion Amplitudes]
    E --> F2[RMS Accelerations]
    E --> F3[Slamming Probability]
    E --> F4[Crew Operability Index]
    E --> F5[Structural Fatigue Loads]
```

## 4.2 Seakeeping Analysis Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input: Ship Lines Plan and Loading Condition] --> B[Compute Mass and Inertia Matrix]
    A --> C[Hydrodynamic Code: Strip Theory or 3D Panel Method]
    D[Input: Wave Spectrum PM or JONSWAP] --> E[Spectral Discretization into N Regular Wave Components]
    E --> F[Compute RAO for each frequency omega and heading mu]
    B --> F
    C --> F
    F --> G[Apply Encounter Frequency Correction omega_e]
    G --> H[Compute Motion Spectra Sxx_omega]
    H --> I[Integrate to get RMS and significant values]
    I --> J{Operational Limits Satisfied?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Pass Seakeeping Criteria]
    J -->|No| L[Iterate: Add Fins, Change GM, Adjust Hull Form]
    L --> A
```

## 4.3 Maneuvering / Turning Circle Elements

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Ship starts Turn at Origin] --> B[Initial Phase: Drift Angle builds]
    B --> C[Yaw Rate increases r_dot]
    C --> D[Drift reaches maximum r_max]
    D --> E[Steady Turn Phase: constant radius R]
    E --> F[Measure at heading change of 90 degrees]
    F --> G[Read Advance A along original course]
    F --> H[Read Transfer Tr perpendicular to original course]
    E --> I[Measure at heading change of 180 degrees]
    I --> J[Tactical Diameter DT]
    E --> K[Compute Steady Radius R equals V divided by r_ss]
    J --> L[Compare DT with IMO limit of 5 L_pp]
    G --> M[Compare A with IMO limit of 4.5 L_pp]
```

## 4.4 Conversion Impact Map on Seakeeping & Maneuvering

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Ship Conversion Activity] --> B1[Lengthening by Mid-body Insertion]
    A --> B2[Adding Bulbous Bow or Stern]
    A --> B3[Superstructure Enlargement]
    A --> B4[Installation of New Appendages]
    A --> B5[Change of Hull Form to SWATH or Catamaran]
    B1 --> C1[L_pp increases: New IMO limits]
    B1 --> C2[Slenderness changes: New natural periods]
    B2 --> C3[Added resistance in head seas]
    B3 --> C4[Sail area increases: Wind heeling moment up]
    B3 --> C5[KG increases: GM drops: T_phi rises]
    B4 --> C6[Bilge keels, fins, rudders change maneuvering]
    B5 --> C7[Catamaran: New 6-DOF RAOs drastically reduced]
    C1 --> D[Reassess IMO Maneuvering Compliance]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    C7 --> D
    D --> E{Compliance Achieved?}

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define seakeeping. List the six degrees of freedom of a ship moving in a seaway.**

**Model Answer:**
Seakeeping is the ability of a ship to perform its intended mission in a seaway with acceptable motions, structural loads, and crew operability. The six degrees of freedom are:
1. Surge (translation along X)
2. Sway (translation along Y)
3. Heave (translation along Z)
4. Roll (rotation about X)
5. Pitch (rotation about Y)
6. Yaw (rotation about Z)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a Response Amplitude Operator (RAO)? Why is it essential in seakeeping analysis?**

**Model Answer:**
An RAO is the **ratio of the steady-state amplitude of a ship motion (e.g., roll, heave) to the amplitude of the incident regular wave**, expressed as a function of frequency $\omega$ and heading angle $\mu$. It is essential because, combined with the wave spectrum $S(\omega)$, it allows computation of the **motion spectrum** $S_{xx}(\omega) = \vert RAO(\omega)\vert^2 S(\omega)$, from which RMS, significant, and expected-maximum motions are derived for realistic irregular seas.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Module 2 Internal Choice Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **encounter frequency** $\omega_e$ of a ship moving with velocity $V$ through waves of frequency $\omega$ at a relative heading angle $\mu$. Discuss the significance of the result for **head, beam, and following seas**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A converted vessel has beam $B = 28$ m, $\overline{GM} = 1.8$ m, and the empirical roll constant is $C = 0.80$. The ship operates at 14 knots in waves of $T_p = 10$ s and heading angle $\mu = 60^\circ$. Determine the natural roll period and the encounter period, and state whether resonance is a risk. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to A(a):**

**Step 1:** State the geometry. Let $V$ be ship speed, $c = g/\omega$ the wave phase speed, and $\mu$ the angle between ship heading and wave direction.

**Step 2:** Compute the component of ship velocity along the wave direction: $V \cos\mu$.

**Step 3:** The relative velocity of wave crests with respect to the ship is $V_{rel} = c - V\cos\mu = g/\omega - V\cos\mu$.

**Step 4:** The encounter frequency is the wavenumber $k = \omega^2/g$ times $V_{rel}$:

$$\omega_e = k V_{rel} = \dfrac{\omega^2}{g}\left(\dfrac{g}{\omega} - V\cos\mu\right) = \omega - \dfrac{\omega^2 V \cos\mu}{g} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 5 — Head seas ($\mu = 180^\circ$):** $\cos\mu = -1 \Rightarrow \omega_e = \omega + \omega^2 V/g$. The encounter frequency is **larger** than the wave frequency. The ship meets waves rapidly. Dangerous for **slamming** in head seas.

**Step 6 — Beam seas ($\mu = 90^\circ$):** $\cos\mu = 0 \Rightarrow \omega_e = \omega$. No effect of forward speed. **Maximum rolling** often occurs here due to large wave exciting moment.

**Step 7 — Following seas ($\mu = 0^\circ$):** $\cos\mu = +1 \Rightarrow \omega_e = \omega - \omega^2 V/g$. As $V$ increases, $\omega_e$ drops. If $V \to g/\omega$ then $\omega_e \to 0$, leading to **very long-period encounters** — risk of **broaching** and **course instability**.

---

**Model Solution to A(b):**

**Step 1: Convert ship speed to SI units.**

$$V = 14 \times 0.5144 = 7.20 \;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2: Natural roll period using empirical formula.**

$$T_\phi = \dfrac{C \cdot B}{\sqrt{\overline{GM}}} = \dfrac{0.80 \times 28}{\sqrt{1.8}} = \dfrac{22.4}{1.342} \approx 16.7 \;\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Wave frequency.**

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{T_p} = \dfrac{2\pi}{10} = 0.628 \;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 4: Encounter frequency** with $\cos 60^\circ = 0.5$:

$$\omega_e = 0.628 - \dfrac{(0.628)^2 \times 7.20 \times 0.5}{9.81}$$

$$\omega_e = 0.628 - \dfrac{0.394 \times 3.6}{9.81} = 0.628 - 0.1445 = 0.484 \;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 5: Encounter period.**

$$T_e = \dfrac{2\pi}{0.484} = \dfrac{6.283}{0.484} \approx 13.0 \;\text{s}$$

**Step 6: Resonance check** — danger band $0.7 T_\phi$ to $1.3 T_\phi$:

$$0.7 T_\phi = 11.7 \;\text{s}, \quad 1.3 T_\phi = 21.7 \;\text{s}$$

Since $T_e = 13.0$ s falls **within** the band $[11.7, 21.7]$ s → **Resonance is a real risk**.

**Conclusion:** The converted vessel is at risk of resonant roll in this sea state. Recommended actions: install **bilge keels**, **flap-type stabilisers**, or **re-distribute cargo to lower $\overline{KG}$** and increase $\overline{GM}$.

---

### Question B (Module 2 Internal Choice Option 2)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **elements of a turning circle** (advance, transfer, tactical diameter, steady turning radius) and state the **IMO maneuverability criteria** for ships of length $\ge 100$ m. (7 marks)

**(b)** A ship of $L_{pp} = 200$ m is converted by lengthening the mid-body by 10 m, increasing $L_{pp}$ to 210 m. In the post-conversion turning trial at design speed, the steady turning radius was found to be 480 m, the advance 950 m, and the transfer 425 m. **Verify IMO compliance** for tactical diameter and advance. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to B(a):**

**Step 1: Define turning-circle geometry.**
- **Steady turning radius $R$:** Final constant radius of CG trajectory.
- **Advance $A$:** Distance along the original course line, from the point where the rudder is put over to the point where the ship has turned $90^\circ$.
- **Transfer $T_r$:** Lateral distance from the original course line at the moment the heading has changed by $90^\circ$.
- **Tactical diameter $D_T$:** Distance travelled perpendicular to the original course line at the $180^\circ$ heading change point, **equal to $2R$** in the steady turn.
- **Drift angle $\beta$:** Angle between the centreline and the velocity vector of the CG.

**Step 2: State IMO Resolution MSC.137(76) criteria for $L_{pp} \ge 100$ m:**
- $D_T \le 5 L_{pp}$
- $A \le 4.5 L_{pp}$
- Stopping distance (with hard-over rudder) $\le 15 L_{pp}$ from the $90^\circ$ point
- Initial turning radius $R_0$ between $0.5 L_{pp}$ and $1.0 L_{pp}$

**Step 3: Engineering importance.**
- Compliance ensures the ship can avoid collisions, navigate channels, and berth safely.
- Non-compliance is a major **Class and Flag State** deficiency that may prohibit trading.

---

**Model Solution to B(b):**

**Step 1: Identify new length.** After conversion, $L_{pp} = 210$ m.

**Step 2: IMO limits for the converted ship.**

- Tactical diameter limit: $D_{T,\text{limit}} = 5 \times 210 = 1050$ m
- Advance limit: $A_{\text{limit}} = 4.5 \times 210 = 945$ m

**Step 3: Compute actual tactical diameter from steady turning radius.**

$$D_T = 2R = 2 \times 480 = 960 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 4: Compare for tactical diameter.**

$$D_T = 960 \;\text{m} \le D_{T,\text{limit}} = 1050 \;\text{m} \quad \checkmark \; \text{PASS}$$

**Step 5: Compare advance.**

$$A = 950 \;\text{m} \;\;\text{vs.}\;\; A_{\text{limit}} = 945 \;\text{m}$$

$$A = 950 \;\text{m} > A_{\text{limit}} = 945 \;\text{m} \quad \times \; \textbf{FAIL}$$

**Step 6: Conclusion.**
The converted ship **FAILS** the IMO advance criterion by 5 m. Required corrective actions:
1. Increase **rudder area** by 10–15 % (e.g., fit a larger or high-lift rudder).
2. Increase **propeller thrust** at low speeds (e.g., fit a high-skew or controllable-pitch propeller).
3. Trim the ship to a **slight draught** condition to improve manoeuvrability at trial.
4. Submit a re-trial to the Class and Flag State surveyor for final approval.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to update $L_{pp}$** after conversion — students use the original 200 m limits, which is a 5-mark deduction.
> 2. **Mixing $D_T$ with $2R$** — in the steady-turn phase they are equal, but early transient turn is **not** used for the IMO check.
> 3. **Stating the IMO limit incorrectly** as $4 L_{pp}$ for advance — the correct figure is $4.5 L_{pp}$.
> 4. **Skipping units** when plugging in $\overline{GM}$, $B$, and $T_p$ — partial credit loss of 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Seakeeping** quantifies a ship's response in 6-DOF: surge, sway, heave, roll, pitch, yaw.
- **Maneuvering** quantifies turning and course-keeping in calm water.
- **Significant wave height $H_s \approx 4\sqrt{m_0}$** and **zero-crossing period $T_z = 2\pi\sqrt{m_0/m_2}$** are primary sea-state descriptors.
- **Encounter frequency** $\omega_e = \omega - \omega^2 V \cos\mu / g$ governs **all** forced motions; following seas may produce **dangerously long encounter periods**.
- **Natural roll period** $T_\phi \approx 0.80 B / \sqrt{\overline{GM}}$ — **KG rise** during conversion increases $T_\phi$ and worsens seakeeping.
- **RAO** is the bridge between wave spectrum and motion spectrum: $S_{xx}(\omega) = \vert RAO(\omega)\vert^2 S(\omega)$.
- **Strip theory** is the standard 2D seakeeping method; **3D panel methods** are used for unusual hull forms (SWATH, multi-hull conversions).
- **IMO turning-circle limits:** $D_T \le 5 L_{pp}$, $A \le 4.5 L_{pp}$, $R_0$ between $0.5 L_{pp}$ and $1.0 L_{pp}$.
- **Conversion Impact Rule of Thumb:** Mid-body lengthening increases $L_{pp}$ → IMO limits change → re-trial is mandatory.
- **Stability Check vs Seakeeping Check:** A ship with very high $\overline{GM}$ rolls fast (small $T_\phi$) — good for roll but bad for **shipping green water**; a low $\overline{GM}$ rolls slowly (large $T_\phi$) — good comfort, poor course stability. Designers target **intermediate $\overline{GM}$**.
- **Operational Limits:** Heave acc $\le 0.2 g$ (crew effectiveness); roll $\le 30^\circ$ (cargo shift); pitch $\le 4°$–$6°$ (slamming).
- **Class & Statutory Documents to Reference:** IMO MSC.137(76), IACS S1, ISO 2631, ABS Guide for Vessel Maneuverability, DNV-RP-C205 (Environmental Conditions).
- **For KTU exam answers,** always **state assumptions, write the governing equation symbolically, then substitute numbers with units, and conclude with an engineering interpretation** (numerical answer alone rarely fetches full marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rudder Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rudder Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rudder:** A vertically oriented, hydrodynamically shaped control surface fitted at the **stern (afterbody)** of a ship, pivoted about a vertical axis (the **rudder stock**), and used to generate a **lateral (side) force** that yaws the vessel, thereby steering it on a desired course.

In the context of **Ship Conversion Technology**, rudder design is revisited whenever:

- The vessel's **mission profile** changes (e.g., a cargo ship being converted to a dynamic positioning (DP) vessel).
- The **installed power** is upgraded, raising service speed $V$ and rudder hydrodynamic loading.
- A **single screw** arrangement is being replaced by **twin screw / twin rudder** configuration.
- **Maneuverability standards** (IMO Resolution A.601(15), MSC.137(76)) are not met and have to be retrofitted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion-Specific Definition:** Rudder design during a ship conversion is the **iterative hydrodynamic–structural synthesis** of an after-body control surface whose **side force, torque, and stock stresses** match the *new* operational envelope of the converted ship, while remaining within the **hull envelope, dry-dock clearances, and classification society scantlings** of the existing structure.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Build the Intuition)

Imagine you are **sculling a canoe** with a single paddle dipped into the water near the stern. By angling the paddle, you push water sideways — and the canoe pivots. The **rudder does exactly the same thing**, except that:

| Canoe Paddle | Ship Rudder |
|---|---|
| Manually held by the helmsman | Mechanically held by the rudder stock |
| Small force, instant response | Large force, needs reduction gear / hydraulic tiller |
| Variable angle | Variable angle controlled from the bridge |
| Single blade | Often **high-aspect-ratio foil** with NACA profile |

A second analogy — a **weathervane on a rooftop**: the rudder, like a weathervane, has a **pivot point** (the stock) and a **fin** (the blade). When the ship tries to "swing" due to a disturbance, the relative flow of water hitting the angled blade creates a restoring force.

> [!TIP]
> **"Why does the ship turn when only the rudder at the back moves?"**
> The rudder generates a side force $F_N$ at the stern, *behind* the ship's pivot point (the centre of lateral resistance, roughly amidships). Because this force does **not** pass through the pivot, it creates a **yawing moment** that swings the bow in the opposite direction — a basic application of Newton's 3rd Law and the principle of moments.

---

## 1.3 Key Geometric & Performance Parameters (Bold High-Yield Terms)

- **Rudder Area $A_R$**: Total projected lateral area of the rudder, normally in the range **1.5 % to 3.0 %** of the lateral (midship) area $L \cdot T$ for a single-screw ship.
- **Aspect Ratio $\Lambda$**: Ratio of span squared to area, $\Lambda = h^2 / A_R$, typically **1.0 to 2.0** for a single-plate spade rudder.
- **Balance Ratio $k$**: Ratio of the *leading-edge part area* (forward of the stock) to the *total* rudder area; ranges from **0 (unbalanced)** to about **0.30 (semi-balanced)**.
- **Rudder Angle $\alpha$**: Angle between the chord and the ship centreline, with the **maximum legal/operational angle = 35°** under SOLAS/IMO (35° on either side).
- **Stock Diameter $d_s$**: Critical structural dimension, designed by **shear and combined stress** criteria.
- **Wake Fraction $w$**: Fraction of the ship's speed "lost" to the propeller race; typically **$w = 0.15$ to $0.40$**. It directly increases the **effective rudder inflow speed** behind the propeller.

---

## 1.4 Types of Rudders (Conversion-Relevant Classification)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Tip:** When converting a vessel, the **rudder type change is the single most expensive hull modification**. Always check whether the existing rudder can be retained with only stock / bearing upgrade before considering a full replacement.

1. **Unbalanced (Single-Plate) Rudder** — entire blade aft of the stock; simple, low cost, but very high torque.
2. **Balanced Rudder** — part of the blade forward of the stock to reduce torque.
3. **Semi-Balanced Rudder** — compromise; most common in modern ships.
4. **Spade Rudder** — mounted on a single stock, no hinge pintles at the hull.
5. **Twin Rudder (Schilling / High-lift)** — used in tugs, DP vessels, naval combatants.
6. **Flap Rudder** — main blade + trailing flap for high lift at low speed.
7. **Integrated Rudder–Propeller (CRP / Mewis / Becker)** — ducted propeller + rudder combination, excellent for heavily converted working vessels.

---

## 1.5 Relevance of Rudder Design in Ship Conversion

A ship conversion often re-tasks the vessel so that its **original rudder is mismatched** to the new duty:

- **Lengthening the hull** (mid-body insertion) shifts the pivot point — the original rudder may now be too small.
- **Engine upgrade** raises $V$ so $F_N \propto V^2$ — torque on the stock can **double or triple**.
- **Adding a bow thruster** may relax rudder area requirements, but the existing rudder may still need strengthening.
- **Conversion to DP-2 / DP-3 class** typically demands **twin high-lift rudders** with independent power units.

This module equips the student with the **design formulas, force/moment synthesis steps, and structural sizing** necessary to evaluate these scenarios.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hydrodynamic Picture — What Happens at the Rudder

When a ship moves forward at speed $V$ and the rudder is deflected by an angle $\alpha$:

1. Water flowing **along the hull** (boundary layer + wake) approaches the rudder.
2. The **propeller slipstream** accelerates this flow, especially in single-screw ships, by a factor of about **1.5 to 2.0** (this is why the rudder is placed *right behind* the propeller).
3. The **effective inflow speed** $V_R$ at the rudder is therefore:

$$V_R = V \cdot (1 - w) \cdot E_R$$

where $w$ is the wake fraction and $E_R$ is the **propeller race acceleration factor** (≈ 1.5 for single screw, ≈ 1.0 for twin screw).

4. The foil-shaped rudder, inclined at $\alpha$ to this inflow, generates a **normal (side) force** $F_N$ and a smaller **tangential (drag) force** $F_T$.

5. $F_N$ applied at the **centre of pressure (CP)** of the rudder, located a distance $r$ from the stock centreline, creates a **torque** $T$ on the rudder stock.

---

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Standard Rudder Force Equation

We start with the generic lift / side-force formula from 2-D thin-aerofoil theory, then **patch** it with empirical corrections for **finite aspect ratio**, **propeller slipstream**, and **rudder–hull interaction**.

**Step-by-step logic:**

- **Step 1 — 2-D Lift on a foil** (per unit span): $L' = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_R^2 \, c \, C_\ell$
- **Step 2 — Integrate over span** $h$ to get total side force on a rectangular plate: $F_N = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_R^2 \, A_R \, C_N$
- **Step 3 — The 2-D slope $2\pi$ rad⁻¹** of the lift curve must be reduced for finite aspect ratio using the **Helmholtz–Kutta–Joukowski finite-wing correction**:

$$C_{N,\Lambda} = \frac{C_{N,2D}}{1 + \dfrac{C_{N,2D}}{\pi \Lambda}}$$

- **Step 4 — Multiply by the propeller race factor** $K_T$ (1.5–1.8 typical) and the **rudder–hull interaction factor** $K_{QR}$ (1.0–1.05).
- **Step 5 — Final working equation**:

$$F_N = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_R^2 \, A_R \, C_N \cdot K_T \cdot K_{QR}$$

- **Step 6 — Tangential force** (much smaller, ≈ 0.1 to 0.2 of $F_N$):

$$F_T = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_R^2 \, A_R \, C_T \cdot K_T$$

- **Step 7 — Torque on stock** (sign convention: positive = tends to *increase* rudder angle):

$$T = F_N \cdot r + F_T \cdot t_c / 2 \;\;\;\; \text{(tangential arm is small and usually ignored)}$$

> **The 'How'**: in design practice the term $K_T$ and the wake-correction $(1-w)$ together raise the effective dynamic pressure by a factor of about **2 to 3** versus a "free" rudder — this is why rudder forces are surprisingly large.

---

## 2.3 Torque Components — Why the Balance Ratio Matters

For an **unbalanced rudder** ($k = 0$), the centre of pressure lies at about **0.30 chord** aft of the leading edge — the **full moment arm** is the mean chord $c_m$:

$$T_{unbal} \approx F_N \cdot 0.30 \, c_m$$

For a **semi-balanced rudder** ($k \approx 0.20$), the stock is placed at about **0.20 chord** from the leading edge, so the CP sits *very close* to the stock, and the moment arm collapses to roughly **0.10 chord**:

$$T_{bal} \approx F_N \cdot 0.10 \, c_m$$

A reduction factor of **3 to 4×** in stock torque is the direct reason balance is introduced in conversion designs where stock replacement must be avoided.

---

## 2.4 Stock Design (Structural Synthesis)

The stock must transmit the torque without yielding. For a **solid circular stock**:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d_s^3} \;\le\; \frac{\tau_{yield}}{n}$$

where:
- $T$ = design torque (N·m)
- $d_s$ = stock diameter (m)
- $\tau_{yield}$ = yield stress of stock material (typically **250 MPa for carbon steel, 600 MPa for bronze**)
- $n$ = safety factor (typically **2.5 to 3.0** for Class scantlings)

A combined **bending + shear + torsion** check using von-Mises equivalent stress is mandatory for class approval:

$$\sigma_{vM} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3 \tau^2} \;\le\; \sigma_{allow}$$

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All the following formulas have been **verified** and are the minimum required set for any numerical problem on Rudder Design in the KTU ESE. Use $\vert$ for absolute value to keep markdown tables intact.

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Typical Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Effective inflow speed at rudder | $V_R$ | $V \cdot (1 - w) \cdot E_R$ | m/s; $w = 0.15$–$0.40$, $E_R = 1.0$–$1.8$ |
| 2 | Rudder area (rule of thumb) | $A_R$ | $(0.018$ to $0.030) \cdot L \cdot T$ | m² |
| 3 | Aspect ratio | $\Lambda$ | $h^2 / A_R$ | 1.0 – 2.0 |
| 4 | 2-D normal-force coefficient (linear) | $C_{N,2D}$ | $2\pi \sin \alpha$ | per radian; valid $\vert \alpha \vert \le 15°$ |
| 5 | 3-D normal-force coefficient | $C_{N,3D}$ | $\dfrac{C_{N,2D}}{1 + C_{N,2D}/(\pi \Lambda)}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | **Total side force on rudder** | $F_N$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_R^2 \, A_R \, C_{N,3D} \, K_T \, K_{QR}$ | N |
| 7 | Tangential force | $F_T$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_R^2 \, A_R \, C_T \, K_T$ | N; $C_T \approx 0.1$–$0.2$ |
| 8 | Torque on stock (general) | $T$ | $F_N \cdot r$ | N·m; $r = 0.10 c_m$ (balanced) to $0.30 c_m$ (unbal.) |
| 9 | Stock shear stress (solid) | $\tau$ | $16 T / (\pi d_s^3)$ | Pa; $\tau_{allow} = \tau_{yield}/n$ |
| 10 | von-Mises combined stress | $\sigma_{vM}$ | $\sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3 \tau^2}$ | Pa |
| 11 | Required rudder horsepower | $P_{req}$ | $T \cdot \dot{\alpha} / \eta$ | W; $\eta = 0.6$–$0.8$ |
| 12 | Turning diameter (empirical) | $D_T$ | $\dfrac{2 L}{\sin \alpha_{max}} \cdot f(\Lambda)$ | m; $f \approx 0.7$–$1.0$ |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Maneuvering prediction** (turning circle, IMO MSC.137(76) standards): every $F_N$ calculated above feeds a **Manoeuvring Modelling Group (MMG) simulation** to certify the converted vessel.
- **Steering gear sizing**: $P_{req}$ determines the **hydraulic ram / rotary vane actuator** capacity.
- **Classification society approval** (DNV, LR, BV, ABS, IRS): the formulae are the **first step** before FEM-based scantling checks on the actual stock and bearing housing.
- **Conversion project economics**: rudder replacement is one of the **largest cost lines** in a major conversion (₹ 1.5 to 4 Crore depending on size). Using the formulas above, the designer can quickly decide between *strengthening the existing stock* vs *full replacement*.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate Active:** Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. No truncation, no skipped steps. All multi-line equations are wrapped in `\begin{aligned} ... \end{aligned}` inside a single `$$` block, with one blank line before and after.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rudder Side Force $F_N$ from First Principles

We begin with the **2-D Kutta–Joukowski theorem** for a thin foil in steady, incompressible flow.

**Given:**
- Fluid density of seawater $\rho = 1025 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- Effective inflow speed at rudder $V_R$ (m/s)
- Mean chord $c_m$ (m), span $h$ (m), so $A_R = c_m \cdot h$
- Deflection angle $\alpha$ (rad)
- Aspect ratio $\Lambda = h / c_m = h^2 / A_R$
- Propeller race factor $K_T$, hull–rudder interaction factor $K_{QR}$

**Goal:** Derive the working formula for total side force $F_N$.

### Step 1 — Starting point: 2-D lift per unit span

From thin-aerofoil theory, lift per unit span for a flat plate inclined at $\alpha$:

$$\frac{dF_N}{dh}\bigg|_{2D} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_R^2 \, c_m \, \frac{dC_\ell}{d\alpha} \, \alpha$$

For a flat plate, $\dfrac{dC_\ell}{d\alpha} = 2\pi \; \text{rad}^{-1}$, so:

$$\frac{dF_N}{dh}\bigg|_{2D} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_R^2 \, c_m \, 2\pi \sin \alpha$$

### Step 2 — Integrate over the span $h$ to obtain 2-D total

$$F_{N,2D} = \int_0^h \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_R^2 \, c_m \, 2\pi \sin \alpha \; dh = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_R^2 \, A_R \, 2\pi \sin \alpha$$

So the 2-D normal-force coefficient is:

$$C_{N,2D} = 2\pi \sin \alpha$$

### Step 3 — Correct for **finite aspect ratio** (Prandtl lifting-line correction)

A 2-D plate is infinitely long. A real rudder has span $h$ and tip vortices shed from the top and bottom, reducing lift. The classical Prandtl–Lanchester correction gives:

$$C_{N,3D} = \frac{C_{N,2D}}{1 + \dfrac{C_{N,2D}}{\pi \Lambda}}$$

Substituting $C_{N,2D} = 2\pi \sin \alpha$:

$$C_{N,3D} = \frac{2\pi \sin \alpha}{1 + \dfrac{2 \sin \alpha}{\Lambda}}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the **propeller slipstream** correction

The propeller accelerates the flow into the rudder by factor $E_R$, so dynamic pressure rises by $E_R^2$. We lump this into $K_T$ (a dimensionless multiplier on the force), so $K_T \approx E_R^2$ for the side force component.

### Step 5 — Apply the **rudder–hull interaction** correction

The hull boundary layer and bilge vortex near the rudder may either augment or reduce the effective side force. The empirical factor $K_{QR}$ captures this and is typically **0.95 to 1.10**.

### Step 6 — Assemble the **final working equation**

$$F_N = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_R^2 \, A_R \, C_{N,3D} \, K_T \, K_{QR}$$

**Substituting $C_{N,3D}$ explicitly:**

$$\boxed{F_N = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_R^2 \, A_R \, \left[\frac{2\pi \sin \alpha}{1 + \dfrac{2 \sin \alpha}{\Lambda}}\right] \, K_T \, K_{QR}}$$

> **Validation check:** For $\Lambda \to \infty$ (very long rudder), the bracket $\to 2\pi \sin \alpha$, recovering the 2-D value — correct. For $\Lambda = 1$ and $\alpha = 30°$ ($\sin \alpha = 0.5$), the bracket gives $2\pi(0.5)/(1 + 1/\pi) \approx 2.20$, a 30 % drop from the 2-D value of $3.14$ — physically realistic.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque on the Rudder Stock

**Given:** $F_N$ from above, lever arm $r$ from the stock centreline to the centre of pressure (CP).

### Step 1 — Locate the centre of pressure for a flat-plate rudder

For a flat plate of chord $c_m$ at incidence $\alpha$ in 2-D flow, the centre of pressure lies at a fraction:

$$\frac{x_{CP}}{c_m} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{\pi}{8 \Lambda \sin \alpha} \quad (\text{for finite aspect ratio})$$

For a typical single-screw ship with $\Lambda = 1.6$, $\alpha = 35°$:

$$x_{CP}/c_m \approx 0.25 + 0.245 \approx 0.495$$

So the CP is roughly **mid-chord** in the worst case (hard-over, 35°).

### Step 2 — Lever arm from the stock

If the stock is placed at a fraction $k$ of the chord from the leading edge (i.e. **balance ratio = $k$**), then the lever arm from the stock to the CP is:

$$r = (x_{CP} - k) \cdot c_m$$

For an **unbalanced** rudder ($k = 0$, stock at leading edge) at $\alpha = 35°$:

$$r_{unbal} \approx 0.495 \, c_m$$

For a **semi-balanced** rudder ($k = 0.20$):

$$r_{bal} \approx (0.495 - 0.20) \, c_m = 0.295 \, c_m$$

> Notice: as the rudder hardens over, the CP migrates **forward**, so the lever arm *shrinks* for an unbalanced rudder. This self-stabilising behaviour is why a "balanced" rudder can sometimes become unstable and *flap* — the CP passes *ahead* of the stock and reverses the torque sign.

### Step 3 — Torque equation

$$T = F_N \cdot r + F_T \cdot (t_c/2)$$

The tangential arm $t_c/2$ is typically $< 5\,\%$ of $r$, so it is **neglected** in preliminary design:

$$\boxed{T \approx F_N \cdot r = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_R^2 \, A_R \, C_{N,3D} \, K_T \, K_{QR} \cdot (x_{CP} - k) \, c_m}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Required Stock Diameter (Solid Circular Stock)

**Given:** Design torque $T$ (N·m), yield stress $\sigma_y$ (Pa), safety factor $n$.

### Step 1 — Pure torsion of a solid circular shaft

The maximum shear stress in a circular shaft of diameter $d_s$ under torque $T$ is:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot r_{outer}}{J} = \frac{T \cdot (d_s/2)}{\pi d_s^4 / 32} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d_s^3}$$

### Step 2 — Allowable shear stress

$$\tau_{allow} = \frac{\tau_{yield}}{n} = \frac{0.577 \, \sigma_y}{n} \quad (\text{using von-Mises shear equivalent})$$

### Step 3 — Set $\tau_{max} = \tau_{allow}$ and solve for $d_s$

$$\frac{16 T}{\pi d_s^3} = \frac{0.577 \, \sigma_y}{n}$$

Rearranging:

$$d_s^3 = \frac{16 T \, n}{\pi \, 0.577 \, \sigma_y} = \frac{27.7 \, T \, n}{\pi \, \sigma_y}$$

$$\boxed{d_s = \left(\frac{27.7 \, T \, n}{\pi \, \sigma_y}\right)^{1/3}}$$

### Step 4 — Add the **bending** component

The stock also bends under the side force $F_N$, treated as a cantilever of length $\ell_s$ from the lower bearing:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{F_N \cdot \ell_s \cdot 32}{\pi d_s^4}$$

### Step 5 — von-Mises combined check

$$\sigma_{vM} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3 \tau^2} \le \frac{\sigma_y}{n}$$

This is the **final acceptance criterion** submitted to the classification society.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> A single-screw cargo ship is being **converted** by lengthening the hull. The new parameters are:
>
> - Service speed $V = 16$ knots $= 8.23 \; \text{m/s}$
> - Wake fraction $w = 0.25$
> - Propeller race factor $E_R = 1.6$
> - Rudder area $A_R = 18 \; \text{m}^2$
> - Mean chord $c_m = 1.8 \; \text{m}$, span $h = 10 \; \text{m}$
> - Aspect ratio $\Lambda = 100 / 18 = 5.56$
> - Hard-over angle $\alpha = 35°$, $\sin \alpha = 0.574$
> - Semi-balanced rudder, $k = 0.20$, $K_{QR} = 1.02$
> - Material: carbon steel, $\sigma_y = 250 \; \text{MPa}$, $n = 2.5$

**Step A — Effective inflow speed**

$$V_R = 8.23 \cdot (1 - 0.25) \cdot 1.6 = 8.23 \cdot 0.75 \cdot 1.6 = 9.88 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step B — 3-D normal force coefficient**

$$C_{N,2D} = 2\pi \sin 35° = 2\pi \cdot 0.574 = 3.605$$

$$C_{N,3D} = \frac{3.605}{1 + \dfrac{3.605}{\pi \cdot 5.56}} = \frac{3.605}{1 + 0.2065} = 2.988$$

**Step C — Propeller race factor on force**

$$K_T = E_R^2 = 1.6^2 = 2.56$$

**Step D — Total side force**

$$F_N = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 9.88^2 \cdot 18 \cdot 2.988 \cdot 2.56 \cdot 1.02$$

Compute term by term:

$$0.5 \cdot 1025 = 512.5$$

$$9.88^2 = 97.61$$

$$512.5 \cdot 97.61 = 50{,}025$$

$$50{,}025 \cdot 18 = 900{,}450$$

$$900{,}450 \cdot 2.988 = 2{,}690{,}544$$

$$2{,}690{,}544 \cdot 2.56 = 6{,}887{,}793$$

$$6{,}887{,}793 \cdot 1.02 = 7{,}025{,}549 \; \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{F_N \approx 7.03 \; \text{MN}}$$

**Step E — Lever arm**

$$x_{CP}/c_m = 0.25 + \frac{\pi}{8 \cdot 5.56 \cdot 0.574} = 0.25 + 0.123 = 0.373$$

$$r = (0.373 - 0.20) \cdot 1.8 = 0.173 \cdot 1.8 = 0.311 \; \text{m}$$

**Step F — Torque on stock**

$$T = F_N \cdot r = 7{,}025{,}549 \cdot 0.311 = 2{,}185{,}000 \; \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 2.19 \; \text{MN·m}}$$

**Step G — Stock diameter**

$$d_s = \left(\frac{27.7 \cdot 2.185 \times 10^6 \cdot 2.5}{\pi \cdot 250 \times 10^6}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$= \left(\frac{27.7 \cdot 2.185 \times 10^6 \cdot 2.5}{7.854 \times 10^8}\right)^{1/3}$$

Numerator: $27.7 \cdot 2.185 \times 10^6 = 60.52 \times 10^6$; times $2.5 = 151.3 \times 10^6$.

$$= \left(\frac{1.513 \times 10^8}{7.854 \times 10^8}\right)^{1/3} = (0.1926)^{1/3} = 0.577 \; \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{d_s \approx 580 \; \text{mm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering check:** a 580 mm stock is **very large** — typical merchant ship stocks are 250–450 mm. This confirms that the conversion (lengthening + speed-up) has placed the existing rudder stock **outside its design envelope**, justifying a **full rudder + stock replacement** in the conversion specification.

---

## 3.5 Rudder Area Selection — Empirical Rules Summary

| Ship Type | $A_R / (L \cdot T)$ Range | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Slow general cargo (single screw) | 0.020 – 0.030 | Conservative |
| Fast container ship (single screw) | 0.018 – 0.024 | Often with trim flap |
| Twin-screw cargo | 0.012 – 0.018 | Two rudders share load |
| Tanker, bulk carrier (single screw) | 0.016 – 0.022 | High $L/B$ ratio |
| Naval combatant (twin screw) | 0.025 – 0.040 (per rudder) | High manoeuvrability |
| Tug / DP vessel | 0.040 – 0.060 (per rudder) | High-lift flap type |

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Rudder Force & Torque Synthesis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START RUDDER DESIGN]) --> in1[INPUT PARAMETERS L T V w E_R]
    in1 --> calc1[CALCULATE EFFECTIVE INFLOW V_R = V TIMES 1 MINUS w TIMES E_R]
    calc1 --> calc2[CALCULATE ASPECT RATIO LAMBDA = h SQUARED DIVIDED BY A_R]
    calc2 --> calc3[CALCULATE 2D C_N = 2 PI SIN ALPHA]
    calc3 --> corr1[APPLY 3D ASPECT RATIO CORRECTION C_N3D = C_N2D DIVIDED BY 1 PLUS C_N2D DIVIDED BY PI LAMBDA]
    corr1 --> corr2[APPLY PROPELLER RACE K_T = E_R SQUARED]
    corr2 --> corr3[APPLY HULL INTERACTION K_QR]
    corr3 --> out1[OUTPUT SIDE FORCE F_N]
    out1 --> cp[CALCULATE CENTRE OF PRESSURE X_CP OVER C_M]
    cp --> lev[CALCULATE LEVER ARM R = X_CP MINUS K TIMES C_M]
    lev --> torq[OUTPUT TORQUE T = F_N TIMES R]
    torq --> stk[CALCULATE STOCK DIAMETER d_s = 27.7 T n DIVIDED BY PI SIGMA_y TO POWER ONE THIRD]
    stk --> comb[VON MISES COMBINED STRESS CHECK SIGMA_VM LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO SIGMA_y DIVIDED BY n]
    comb --> dec{DESIGN ACCEPTABLE?}
    dec -- YES --> finalA[FINAL RUDDER SCANTLINGS APPROVED]
    dec -- NO --> it[ITERATE BALANCE RATIO k OR RUDDER AREA A_R]
    it --> in1
    finalA --> stopA([END])
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Rudder Types and Conversion Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    req[CONVERSION MISSION REQUIREMENTS] --> q1{NEED HIGH MANOEUVRABILITY}
    q1 -- YES --> t1[TWIN HIGH LIFT RUDDER]
    q1 -- NO --> q2{SERVICE SPEED INCREASED ABOVE 20 KNOTS}
    q2 -- YES --> t2[SPADE RUDDER WITH NACA PROFILE]
    q2 -- NO --> q3{HULL LENGTHENED ABOVE 15 PERCENT}
    q3 -- YES --> t3[REPLACE WITH LARGER SEMI BALANCED RUDDER]
    q3 -- NO --> t4[RETAIN EXISTING UNBALANCED RUDDER]
    q1 -- MAYBE --> t5[SEMI BALANCED SPADE RUDDER]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Stock Diameter Question):**
> The most common loss of marks occurs when students **forget the aspect-ratio correction** in $C_N$. The 2-D value $2\pi \sin\alpha$ over-predicts the force by **30 % to 50 %** for typical single-plate ship rudders. Examiners will deduct up to **4 out of 7 marks** in Part (b) if this correction is omitted. Second-most-common error: **forgetting to multiply $E_R$ (the velocity factor) twice** — once in $V_R$ and once as $K_T = E_R^2$ on the force. Marks lost here are usually **2 to 3**.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "balance ratio" of a rudder. What is its significance in the design of a rudder stock during ship conversion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **balance ratio $k$** of a rudder is defined as the ratio of the *leading-edge area* (the portion of the blade *forward* of the rudder stock) to the *total rudder area*:

$$k = \frac{A_{forward}}{A_{R}}$$

**Significance in ship conversion (1 mark each point):**

1. **Torque reduction:** Increasing $k$ from 0 (unbalanced) to about 0.20–0.30 (semi-balanced) reduces the lever arm between the centre of pressure and the stock, dropping the **stock torque by 60 % to 75 %**. This is critical when the conversion upgrades the engine, because $T \propto V^2$ rises sharply.
2. **Stock reusability:** A higher balance ratio allows the **existing stock** to remain serviceable, avoiding the very expensive replacement of the rudder, stock, and bearings.
3. **Stability limit:** Beyond $k \approx 0.35$ the rudder becomes **aerodynamically unstable** — the centre of pressure migrates ahead of the stock, reversing the torque sense and causing *self-hard-over*. This bounds the practical value of $k$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**With the aid of neat sketches, differentiate between a spade rudder and a flap rudder. State one ship-conversion scenario where each would be preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Spade Rudder | Flap Rudder |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Single rigid blade mounted on one stock | Main blade + a hinged trailing flap |
| Lift mechanism | Pure camber + deflection | Main blade deflects; flap further deflects to multiply lift |
| Aspect ratio | Typically 1.0 – 2.0 | Main blade 1.5; total effective 2.5+ |
| Torque | Moderate | High (two hinges) |
| Cost | Low | High |

**Conversion scenarios (1 mark each):**

- **Spade rudder preferred:** Conversion of a single-screw general cargo ship to a **higher service speed** (e.g., 18 → 22 knots). The existing spade stock can be retained with a stronger material, minimising hull modification.
- **Flap rudder preferred:** Conversion of a supply vessel to a **DP-2 offshore support vessel**. The high-lift flap provides the **low-speed, high-thrust manoeuvring** essential for station-keeping.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, both Question A and Question B are to be printed and the student answers **either** A **or** B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive, from first principles using thin-aerofoil theory with the **Prandtl finite-aspect-ratio correction**, the working equation for the **side force $F_N$** acting on a ship rudder. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 25,000 DWT single-screw bulk carrier is being **converted** by replacing its slow-speed engine with a medium-speed diesel that raises the service speed from 14 to 18 knots. The new rudder must be designed. Given:

- $L_{pp} = 160 \; \text{m}$, $T = 9.5 \; \text{m}$
- Wake fraction $w = 0.28$
- Propeller race factor $E_R = 1.55$
- Rudder area to be used: $A_R = 26 \; \text{m}^2$ (spade, semi-balanced, $k = 0.20$)
- Aspect ratio $\Lambda = 1.65$
- Hard-over angle $\alpha = 35°$
- Material: forged carbon-steel, $\sigma_y = 260 \; \text{MPa}$, $n = 2.5$, $K_{QR} = 1.0$

Compute:
- (i) the side force $F_N$ **(3 marks)**
- (ii) the torque on the stock $T$ **(2 marks)**
- (iii) the required **stock diameter** $d_s$ **(2 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

1. **State the 2-D thin-aerofoil result** (1 mark): $C_{N,2D} = 2\pi \sin \alpha$.
2. **Apply the Prandtl finite-aspect-ratio correction** with the induced-angle factor (2 marks):

$$C_{N,3D} = \frac{2\pi \sin \alpha}{1 + \dfrac{2 \sin \alpha}{\Lambda}}$$

3. **Assemble the total side force** including propeller slipstream and hull interaction factors (2 marks):

$$F_N = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_R^2 \, A_R \, C_{N,3D} \, K_T \, K_{QR}$$

with $V_R = V(1-w)E_R$ and $K_T = E_R^2$.

4. **List assumptions** (1 mark): (i) steady, incompressible flow; (ii) flat-plate approximation valid for $\alpha \le 35°$; (iii) the rudder operates fully within the propeller slipstream; (iv) hull–rudder interaction is captured by a single factor $K_{QR}$; (v) the rudder has a rectangular planform.

5. **Discuss limitations** (1 mark): the formula is a *preliminary design* tool; final class-approved scantlings require **CFD/RANS** verification (typical for high-speed or DP conversions).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert service speed**

$$V = 18 \; \text{knots} = 18 \cdot 0.5144 = 9.26 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Effective inflow speed at rudder**

$$V_R = 9.26 \cdot (1 - 0.28) \cdot 1.55 = 9.26 \cdot 0.72 \cdot 1.55 = 10.33 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — 2-D and 3-D normal force coefficients**

$$C_{N,2D} = 2\pi \sin 35° = 2\pi \cdot 0.5736 = 3.604$$

$$C_{N,3D} = \frac{3.604}{1 + \dfrac{3.604}{\pi \cdot 1.65}} = \frac{3.604}{1 + 0.6956} = 2.126$$

**Step 4 — Propeller race factor on force**

$$K_T = E_R^2 = 1.55^2 = 2.4025$$

**Step 5 — Side force (i)**

$$F_N = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 10.33^2 \cdot 26 \cdot 2.126 \cdot 2.4025 \cdot 1.0$$

Compute term by term:

$$0.5 \cdot 1025 = 512.5$$

$$10.33^2 = 106.71$$

$$512.5 \cdot 106.71 = 54{,}689$$

$$54{,}689 \cdot 26 = 1{,}421{,}914$$

$$1{,}421{,}914 \cdot 2.126 = 3{,}022{,}929$$

$$3{,}022{,}929 \cdot 2.4025 = 7{,}263{,}287 \; \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{F_N \approx 7.26 \; \text{MN} \quad \text{(3 marks)}}$$

**Step 6 — Centre of pressure and lever arm**

$$\frac{x_{CP}}{c_m} = 0.25 + \frac{\pi}{8 \cdot 1.65 \cdot 0.5736} = 0.25 + 0.414 = 0.664$$

Mean chord: $c_m = A_R / h$. With $\Lambda = 1.65$ and $A_R = 26$ m²:

$$h = \sqrt{\Lambda \cdot A_R} = \sqrt{1.65 \cdot 26} = \sqrt{42.9} = 6.55 \; \text{m}$$

$$c_m = 26 / 6.55 = 3.97 \; \text{m}$$

Lever arm: $r = (0.664 - 0.20) \cdot 3.97 = 0.464 \cdot 3.97 = 1.842 \; \text{m}$

**Step 7 — Torque on stock (ii)**

$$T = 7.263 \times 10^6 \cdot 1.842 = 13.38 \times 10^6 \; \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 13.4 \; \text{MN·m} \quad \text{(2 marks)}}$$

**Step 8 — Stock diameter (iii)**

$$d_s = \left(\frac{27.7 \cdot 13.38 \times 10^6 \cdot 2.5}{\pi \cdot 260 \times 10^6}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$= \left(\frac{27.7 \cdot 13.38 \times 10^6 \cdot 2.5}{8.168 \times 10^8}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$= \left(\frac{9.264 \times 10^8}{8.168 \times 10^8}\right)^{1/3} = (1.134)^{1/3} = 1.0426$$

$$\boxed{d_s \approx 1.04 \; \text{m} = 1040 \; \text{mm} \quad \text{(2 marks)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 1.04 m stock is **impractical for a single-stock spade rudder** on a 25,000 DWT bulk carrier. This shows that the **conversion cannot be done with a simple engine upgrade** — the rudder system must be re-engineered, e.g. by adopting a **twin-screw / twin-rudder** layout, each rudder carrying half the side force and stock torque.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain, with diagrams, the **types of rudders** used in merchant ships, and classify them by **balance ratio**. For each, state one advantage and one disadvantage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship conversion project replaces a single-screw, single-rudder cargo vessel with a **twin-screw, twin-rudder** configuration. Each new rudder has:

- Area $A_R = 12 \; \text{m}^2$, mean chord $c_m = 1.6 \; \text{m}$
- Aspect ratio $\Lambda = 1.8$, balance ratio $k = 0.25$
- Service speed $V = 20 \; \text{knots}$, wake fraction $w = 0.20$, $E_R = 1.05$ (no slipstream acceleration for outboard rudders)
- Rudder angle $\alpha = 30°$, $\sin 30° = 0.5$, $K_{QR} = 1.0$

Compute for one rudder:
- (i) the side force $F_N$ **(3 marks)**
- (ii) the torque $T$ at hard-over, and the required stock diameter using $\sigma_y = 250 \; \text{MPa}$ and $n = 3.0$ **(4 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

1. **Unbalanced rudder ($k = 0$):** Stock at the leading edge. *Advantage:* strong, simple, robust; *Disadvantage:* very high torque, large steering gear. (1.5 marks)
2. **Semi-balanced rudder ($k = 0.15$ to $0.30$):** Stock placed ≈ 20 % chord from leading edge. *Advantage:* torque reduced by 60–75 %; *Disadvantage:* slight reduction in lift efficiency. (1.5 marks)
3. **Fully balanced rudder ($k = 0.30$ to $0.45$):** *Advantage:* minimum torque, ideal for large ships; *Disadvantage:* potential for self-hard-over instability, sensitive to fouling. (1.5 marks)
4. **Spade rudder:** Single stock, no pintles. *Advantage:* high lift, low drag; *Disadvantage:* stock carries all bending + torque. (1 mark)
5. **Flap / high-lift rudder:** Main blade + flap. *Advantage:* high lift at low speed; *Disadvantage:* complex hinge mechanism. (1 mark)
6. **Twin rudder (outboard):** *Advantage:* excellent manoeuvrability at low speed; *Disadvantage:* vulnerability to grounding damage. (0.5 marks)

> Diagrams: a labelled **plan view of each** is expected for full marks — include arrows showing stock position and CP.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Service speed in m/s**

$$V = 20 \cdot 0.5144 = 10.29 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Effective inflow at outboard rudder**

$$V_R = 10.29 \cdot (1 - 0.20) \cdot 1.05 = 10.29 \cdot 0.80 \cdot 1.05 = 8.64 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — 2-D and 3-D force coefficients**

$$C_{N,2D} = 2\pi \sin 30° = 2\pi \cdot 0.5 = 3.142$$

$$C_{N,3D} = \frac{3.142}{1 + \dfrac{3.142}{\pi \cdot 1.8}} = \frac{3.142}{1 + 0.5556} = 2.022$$

**Step 4 — $K_T$ for outboard rudder**

$$K_T = E_R^2 = 1.05^2 = 1.1025$$

**Step 5 — Side force (i)**

$$F_N = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 8.64^2 \cdot 12 \cdot 2.022 \cdot 1.1025 \cdot 1.0$$

$$0.5 \cdot 1025 = 512.5$$

$$8.64^2 = 74.65$$

$$512.5 \cdot 74.65 = 38{,}258$$

$$38{,}258 \cdot 12 = 459{,}096$$

$$459{,}096 \cdot 2.022 = 928{,}352$$

$$928{,}352 \cdot 1.1025 = 1{,}023{,}468 \; \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{F_N \approx 1.02 \; \text{MN} \quad \text{(3 marks)}}$$

**Step 6 — Centre of pressure**
