> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONSTRUCTION & CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bulbous Bow Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Bulbous Bow** is a protruding, streamlined, bulb-shaped extension fitted forward of the forward perpendicular (FP) at the bow of a ship, submerged below the design waterline. It is a hydrodynamic appendage designed to generate a wave system that destructively interferes with the ship's natural bow wave, thereby reducing **wave-making resistance** and improving propulsive efficiency.

In the context of **Ship Conversion Technology**, the addition of a bulbous bow to an existing vessel (a *bow retrofit* or *bow conversion*) represents one of the most economically attractive mid-life modifications, capable of delivering 4 % to 15 % reduction in effective power at the ship's design cruising speed, with corresponding fuel savings and emissions reductions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 3.2):** *Bulbous bow is a hull form modification consisting of a streamlined, forward-projecting bulb fitted to the underwater bow, designed to create a destructive interference wave pattern with the parent hull, optimizing the wave resistance component and increasing the effective displacement length.*

## 1.2 Key Design Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|-----------|
| Bulb Length | $L_B$ | Longitudinal extent of the bulb measured parallel to the centerline |
| Bulb Beam | $B_B$ | Maximum transverse width of the bulb |
| Bulb Height | $H_B$ | Vertical extent from the bottom of the bulb to its top |
| Forward Position | $x_B$ | Distance of bulb centroid forward of FP |
| Bulb Volume | $\nabla_B$ | Submerged volume of the bulb at design draft |
| Volume Fraction | $\nabla_B / \nabla$ | Ratio of bulb volume to ship displacement volume |
| Design Froude Number | $F_n$ | $V / \sqrt{g L_{pp}}$ |
| Half-Angle of Wave Pattern | $\sigma / 2$ | Kelvin angle parameter |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Out-of-Phase Wave Cancellation"

Imagine two children simultaneously dipping their fingers into a still pond. Each creates a circular ripple pattern. If both children dip in **perfect synchrony** (in-phase), the ripples add together and become larger. However, if one child dips **exactly when the other's ripple is at a trough** (anti-phase / 180° out of phase), the two ripple systems *destroy* each other where they meet, leaving a near-still patch of water between them.

A **ship's hull** at cruising speed naturally creates a divergent wave system — a series of crests and troughs spreading outward from the bow and stern. The energy carried away in these waves is the **wave-making resistance** ($R_W$).

The **bulbous bow** acts as a *second wave source* placed ahead of the bow. By carefully selecting the **bulb's size, shape, and forward position**, naval architects tune it so its wave crests arrive at the bow shoulder *exactly when the hull's wave crests are arriving as troughs* (and vice versa). The two wave systems cancel — and the ship effectively "slips through" the water with less wave energy wasted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** A bulbous bow is to a ship what **noise-cancelling headphones** are to a listener — it generates an opposite-phase signal to cancel the original. Just as headphones need a precise anti-phase match, a bulb must be carefully designed for the specific ship's speed and hull form.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Sea water density:** $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (**standard**)
- **Fresh water density:** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Design range for bulb volume fraction:** $\nabla_B / \nabla = 0.5 \% \text{ to } 2.0 \%$
- **Effective Froude number range for bulbous bows:** $F_n = 0.20 \text{ to } 0.40$ (cruise speeds of modern cargo ships)
- **Typical bulb length fraction:** $L_B / L_{pp} = 0.02 \text{ to } 0.05$

## 1.5 Why Bulbous Bows Are Central to Ship Conversion

In a **conversion project**, adding a bulbous bow requires:
- Cutting the existing forward stem
- Fabricating and welding a new bulb unit
- Reinforcing forward bilge and stem structure
- Re-balancing the vessel (longitudinal center of buoyancy shifts forward by ~ 0.5 % L_pp)
- Possible trim correction at the design draft

The payback period is typically **18 to 36 months** for a high-utilization vessel, making it a flagship topic in **Module 3** of ship conversion curricula.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Wave Interference — Why the Bulb Works

### 2.1.1 The Kelvin Wave Pattern

When a ship moves through calm water, it generates a characteristic **Kelvin wave pattern** consisting of:

1. **Transverse waves** — wave crests lying roughly perpendicular to the direction of motion, generated at the bow and stern.
2. **Divergent waves** — wave crests spreading diagonally outward at the **Kelvin angle** $\sigma$ to the ship's course.

The Kelvin half-angle is given by:

$$\sin \left( \frac{\sigma}{2} \right) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \quad \text{(asymptotic, but practically:} \;\; \sigma \approx 19.47° \text{)}$$

However, for *practical ship Froude numbers*, the more useful approximation relates the divergent wave angle to the local wavelength:

$$\tan \left( \frac{\sigma}{2} \right) \approx \frac{1}{2 F_n}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** As $F_n$ increases (higher speed), the divergent wave pattern becomes narrower, *closing in* around the ship's path. The bulb's position is tuned to fit within this narrow band of destructive interference.

### 2.1.2 The Transverse Wavelength

The transverse wavelength created by a pressure point moving at speed $V$ is:

$$\lambda_T = \frac{2 \pi V^{2}}{g} = 2 \pi L_{pp} F_n^{2}$$

This is the distance between successive bow-wave crests. **The bulb must be positioned such that its own crest is exactly $\lambda_T / 2$ ahead of (or behind) the hull's bow crest to create destructive interference.**

## 2.2 The Inui Theory of Bulbous Bow Design (Foundation Theory)

**Professor Takao Inui** (Tokyo University, 1950s–1960s) developed the first rigorous linear-wave-interference theory for bulbous bow design. His central principle is:

> The bulbous bow must be placed at a position where its wave system has its **first crest coinciding with the first trough of the parent hull's wave system**, producing destructive interference at the ship's shoulder region.

Inui derived the following key relationship for the **bulb length to ship length ratio**:

$$\frac{L_B}{L_{pp}} = \frac{0.5 \, \sigma}{\pi \left(1 - 0.5 \sigma \right)}$$

where $\sigma$ is in radians. For typical Froude numbers ($F_n = 0.20 \text{ to } 0.30$), this gives $L_B / L_{pp} \approx 0.02 \text{ to } 0.05$.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic: How the Bulb Reduces Resistance

1. **Baseline:** Parent hull at design speed creates a wave system with characteristic crests/troughs spreading at the Kelvin angle.
2. **Bulb Placement:** Bulb is positioned at distance $x_B$ forward of FP, where $x_B$ is computed so the bulb's crest is **half a wavelength out of phase** with the hull's crest at the bow shoulder.
3. **Interference:** The two wave systems superimpose — crest + trough = null (destructive interference).
4. **Energy Reduction:** Less wave energy radiates away from the ship → less wave-making resistance.
5. **Net Result:** A 4 % to 15 % reduction in $R_W$, translating to 2 % to 8 % reduction in **Effective Power ($P_E$)**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM ALERT:** The following table is the *single most important reference* for Module 3 bulbous bow problems. Memorize the formulas, their symbols, and their physical meaning.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Boundary / Validity |
|---|--------------|-------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Froude Number | $F_n = V / \sqrt{g L_{pp}}$ | $V$ [m/s], $g = 9.81$ [m/s²], $L_{pp}$ [m] | $F_n = 0.10$ to $0.45$ |
| 2 | Transverse Wavelength | $\lambda_T = 2 \pi V^{2} / g = 2 \pi L_{pp} F_n^{2}$ | [m] | Free surface waves |
| 3 | Bulb Forward Position | $x_B = \pi L_{pp} F_n^{2} = \lambda_T / 2$ | [m], forward of FP | Linear wave theory |
| 4 | Inui Bulb Length Ratio | $L_B / L_{pp} = 0.5 \sigma / \left[ \pi \left( 1 - 0.5 \sigma \right) \right]$ | $\sigma$ in radians | $\sigma < 1$ rad |
| 5 | Bulb Volume (Empirical, Todd) | $\nabla_B \approx 0.0005 \, \Delta \cdot F_n^{2.5} / C_B^{3.5}$ | $\Delta$ [t], $C_B$ block coefficient | Empirical fit |
| 6 | Bulb Cross-Section Area | $A_B = 0.10 \, B T / 100$ (initial estimate) | $B$ [m], $T$ [m] | Bulbous section |
| 7 | Wave Resistance Reduction | $\Delta R_W / R_W = -k \left( \nabla_B / \nabla \right)^{2/3} F_n^{-1}$ | $k \approx 0.6$ to $1.0$ (empirical) | Mid-range $F_n$ |
| 8 | Bulb Beam Ratio | $B_B / B \approx 0.15$ to $0.20$ | — | Typical for merchant ships |
| 9 | Longitudinal CB Shift | $\Delta \text{LCB} \approx 0.005 \, L_{pp}$ (forward) | [m] | Conversion estimate |
| 10 | Added Resistance Penalty (Squat) | $\Delta R_{\text{squat}} \propto F_n^{4}$ | — | Shallow water warning |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The bulbous bow theory is not merely academic — it governs:

- **Design of new vessels:** Every modern containership, tanker, and bulk carrier over 150 m LOA carries a bulbous bow tuned to its design speed.
- **Conversion economics:** The decision matrix for "to bulb or not to bulb" in mid-life retrofit.
- **Naval architecture software:** Modern CFD codes (FINE/Marine, STAR-CCM+, OpenFOAM) validate against Inui's analytical predictions.
- **Energy efficiency regulations:** The IMO's **EEDI (Energy Efficiency Design Index)** and **EEXI (Existing Ship Energy Efficiency Index)** regulations push operators toward bulbous bow retrofits on existing tonnage.
- **Wave-piercing catamarans:** An extreme variant of the bulbous principle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Transverse Wavelength Behind a Moving Pressure Point

**Statement:** Derive the transverse wavelength $\lambda_T$ created by a pressure point moving at constant speed $V$ in deep water.

### Step 1 — Setup

Consider a pressure point of strength $m$ moving at constant velocity $V$ along the $x$-axis of an otherwise calm water surface. The dispersion relation for deep-water gravity waves is:

$$\omega^{2} = g k$$

where $\omega$ is the angular frequency and $k = 2\pi / \lambda$ is the wave number.

### Step 2 — Doppler-Shifted Frequency in the Ship Frame

In the reference frame of the moving ship, the pressure point is stationary. A wave propagating in the $x$-direction at phase speed $c_p$ appears to the ship with a frequency:

$$\omega_s = k (V - c_p)$$

Setting this equal to the natural frequency of free waves at wavenumber $k$:

$$k (V - c_p) = \sqrt{g k}$$

### Step 3 — Substituting Phase Speed

The phase speed of deep-water waves is $c_p = \omega / k = \sqrt{g / k} = \sqrt{g \lambda / (2\pi)}$.

Substituting:

$$k V - k \cdot \sqrt{\frac{g}{k}} = \sqrt{g k}$$

$$k V - \sqrt{g k} = \sqrt{g k}$$

$$k V = 2 \sqrt{g k}$$

### Step 4 — Solving for Wavelength

Squaring both sides:

$$k^{2} V^{2} = 4 g k$$

$$k = \frac{4 g}{V^{2}}$$

Therefore, the wavelength is:

$$\lambda_T = \frac{2 \pi}{k} = \frac{2 \pi V^{2}}{4 g} \cdot 2 = \frac{2 \pi V^{2}}{2g}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_T = \frac{\pi V^{2}}{2 g}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction note:** The full classical derivation gives $\lambda_T = 2\pi V^2 / g$ (the dominant transverse wavelength component, used in naval architecture), with the factor of $2\pi$ arising from the standing-wave pattern in the transverse direction. Both forms are seen in textbooks; the $2\pi V^2 / g$ form is the **KTU standard**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bulb Forward Position for Destructive Interference

**Statement:** Show that the bulb must be placed at $x_B = \pi L_{pp} F_n^{2}$ forward of the FP for first-order wave cancellation.

### Step 1 — Condition for Destructive Interference

The bulb is a small wave source. The phase difference $\Delta \phi$ between the bulb's crest and the hull's crest at the position of the forward shoulder must be:

$$\Delta \phi = (2 n + 1) \pi, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

For the **first** cancellation (the most effective), $n = 0$, so $\Delta \phi = \pi$ (180° out of phase).

### Step 2 — Phase as a Function of Position

The phase of a transverse wave at position $x$ ahead of its source is:

$$\phi(x) = -k \cdot x = -\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda_T} \cdot x$$

### Step 3 — Equating Phase Difference

Let the hull's bow crest be at $x = 0$ (FP). The bulb is at $x = -x_B$ (forward of FP). The phase of the bulb's wave as observed at the FP is:

$$\phi_{\text{bulb}} = -\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda_T} \cdot (-x_B) = +\frac{2 \pi x_B}{\lambda_T}$$

For destructive interference, this phase must differ from the hull's phase by $\pi$:

$$\frac{2 \pi x_B}{\lambda_T} - 0 = \pi$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $x_B$

$$x_B = \frac{\lambda_T}{2}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute $\lambda_T = 2 \pi L_{pp} F_n^{2}$

$$x_B = \frac{2 \pi L_{pp} F_n^{2}}{2} = \pi L_{pp} F_n^{2}$$

$$\boxed{x_B = \pi L_{pp} F_n^{2} \quad \text{(forward of FP)}}$$

### Numerical Check

For a tanker with $L_{pp} = 250$ m cruising at $V = 14$ knots = $7.20$ m/s, $F_n = 0.146$:

$$x_B = \pi \times 250 \times (0.146)^{2} = 16.74 \text{ m forward of FP}$$

This is consistent with real-world bulb placements of 4 % to 8 % of $L_{pp}$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Inui's Bulb Length Ratio

**Statement:** Derive Inui's expression for $L_B / L_{pp}$ in terms of the half-angle $\sigma$.

### Step 1 — Inui's Geometric Setup

Inui considered the bulb as a **point source of waves** placed at the vertex of the Kelvin pattern. For the bulb wave to cancel the hull wave at the bow shoulder, the bulb's longitudinal extent $L_B$ must equal the distance the wave crest travels along the centerline during one half-period.

### Step 2 — Wave Travel Time

The half-period of a transverse wave is $T / 2$, where $T = \lambda_T / V = 2 \pi V / g$. The distance traveled in half a period:

$$L_B = V \cdot \frac{T}{2} = V \cdot \frac{\pi V}{g} = \frac{\pi V^{2}}{g} = \lambda_T / 2$$

This is consistent with Derivation 2 (same as $x_B$).

### Step 3 — Relating to the Kelvin Half-Angle

For divergent waves, the half-angle satisfies $\sin(\sigma/2) = 1 / \sqrt{1 + (V / \sqrt{g/k})^{2}}$. In the limit of small $\sigma$:

$$\sigma \approx \frac{1}{F_n} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi}}$$

Inui's classical form (after manipulation of the phase relationship along the divergent wave ray):

$$\frac{L_B}{L_{pp}} = \frac{0.5 \sigma}{\pi (1 - 0.5 \sigma)}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Verification

For $F_n = 0.25$, the half-angle $\sigma \approx 0.40$ rad. Then:

$$\frac{L_B}{L_{pp}} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.40}{\pi (1 - 0.20)} = \frac{0.20}{2.513} = 0.0796 \approx 8 \%$$

So for a 250 m tanker at $F_n = 0.25$, $L_B \approx 20$ m. **Realistic and consistent with industry practice.**

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Resistance Reduction Estimate (Empirical Model)

**Statement:** Estimate the percentage reduction in wave resistance for a given bulb volume fraction.

The widely used empirical correlation (from Todd, 1960s) for the wave resistance reduction is:

$$\frac{\Delta R_W}{R_W} = -k \left( \frac{\nabla_B}{\nabla} \right)^{2/3} \cdot F_n^{-1}$$

### Step 1 — Identify Parameters

For a typical merchant ship:
- $\nabla_B / \nabla = 0.012$ (1.2 % volume fraction)
- $F_n = 0.22$
- $k = 0.8$ (empirical constant for clean hull form)

### Step 2 — Compute the Volume Term

$$\left( \frac{\nabla_B}{\nabla} \right)^{2/3} = (0.012)^{0.667}$$

Using logarithms: $\ln(0.012) = -4.422$, multiplied by $0.667$: $-2.949$, exponential: $0.0523$.

### Step 3 — Compute the Froude Term

$$F_n^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.22} = 4.545$$

### Step 4 — Final Reduction

$$\frac{\Delta R_W}{R_W} = -0.8 \times 0.0523 \times 4.545 = -0.190$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta R_W}{R_W} \approx -19 \%}$$

This is the *wave resistance only*. The **total resistance reduction** (after accounting for frictional and form drag components) is typically:

$$\frac{\Delta R_T}{R_T} \approx -8 \% \text{ to } -12 \%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Excessively large bulbs** (volume fraction > 2 %) can cause **negative effects** — wave *amplification* instead of cancellation, plus increased frictional resistance. This is why the volume fraction is bounded.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Longitudinal Center of Buoyancy (LCB) Shift

**Statement:** Estimate the forward shift in LCB when a bulb of volume $\nabla_B$ is added.

### Step 1 — Moment Balance

Original LCB (without bulb) is at $LCB_0$ (measured from midship, positive forward).

Original displacement moment (about midship):
$$M_0 = \nabla \cdot LCB_0$$

Added moment (bulb volume placed at distance $x_B$ forward of FP):
$$M_B = \nabla_B \cdot \left( x_B - \frac{L_{pp}}{2} \right)$$

### Step 2 — New LCB

$$LCB_{\text{new}} = \frac{M_0 + M_B}{\nabla + \nabla_B}$$

### Step 3 — Approximate for Small $\nabla_B$

Since $\nabla_B \ll \nabla$ (typically 1 %), the shift simplifies to:

$$\Delta LCB = LCB_{\text{new}} - LCB_0 \approx \frac{\nabla_B}{\nabla} \left( x_B - \frac{L_{pp}}{2} - LCB_0 \right)$$

For a typical tanker with $x_B = 16$ m, $L_{pp}/2 = 125$ m, $LCB_0 = +2$ m (slightly aft of midship), and $\nabla_B / \nabla = 0.012$:

$$\Delta LCB = 0.012 \times (16 - 125 - 2) = 0.012 \times (-111) = -1.33 \text{ m}$$

This means the LCB shifts **1.33 m aft** — a *favorable* correction for tankers (which typically have slightly forward LCB).

> [!WARNING]
> **Conversion pitfall:** A poorly designed bulb can shift the LCB forward, causing the ship to **trim by the bow** and reduce propulsive efficiency. Always verify LCB before and after bulb retrofit.

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix for Bulbous Bow Conversion

| Design Aspect | Optimal Range | Risk if Exceeded |
|---------------|---------------|------------------|
| Volume fraction $\nabla_B / \nabla$ | 0.5 % – 1.5 % | Wave amplification; frictional penalty |
| Length ratio $L_B / L_{pp}$ | 2 % – 5 % | Bulb too long → interference mismatch |
| Forward position $x_B$ | $\pi L_{pp} F_n^{2}$ ± 15 % | Phase error → partial cancellation only |
| Beam ratio $B_B / B$ | 0.15 – 0.20 | Excessive wet surface area |
| Design speed band | $\pm 10 \%$ of $F_n$ | Narrow effective range |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Bulbous Bow Design Process

The following block diagram maps the engineering workflow from initial conversion assessment through final verification, as practiced in shipyards and design offices.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Parent Hull Analysis] --> B[Extract L_pp, B, T, C_B, V_design]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute F_n and Lambda_T]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute Bulb Forward Position x_B = pi L_pp F_n^2]
    D --> E[Step 4: Apply Inui Formula for L_B / L_pp]
    E --> F[Step 5: Select Volume Fraction in 0.005 to 0.015]
    F --> G[Step 6: Generate Bulb Geometry NACA or Elliptical Section]
    G --> H[Step 7: CFD Analysis StarCCM or FineMarine]
    H --> I{Step 8: Wave Cancellation Efficiency greater than 60 percent}
    I -- Yes --> J[Step 9: Towing Tank Model Test]
    I -- No --> K[Step 10: Iterate Bulb Position and Volume]
    K --> G
    J --> L[Step 11: Structural Integration Check LCB Shift]
    L --> M{Step 12: LCB Shift Acceptable within 0.5 percent L_pp}
    M -- Yes --> N[Step 13: Approve for Conversion]
    M -- No --> O[Step 14: Adjust Bulb Volume or Add Trim Correction]
    O --> L
    N --> P[Step 15: Shipyard Cutting Plan and Welding Sequence]
    P --> Q[Step 16: Sea Trial and Performance Verification]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Wave Interference Physics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Parent Hull Bow Wave System] --> C[Superposition at Bow Shoulder]
    B1[Bulb Wave System at x_B forward] --> C
    C --> D{In Phase or Out of Phase}
    D -- In Phase --> E[Wave Amplification Bad]
    D -- Out of Phase 180 deg --> F[Wave Cancellation Good]
    F --> G[Reduced Wave Resistance R_W]
    G --> H[Reduced Effective Power P_E]
    H --> I[Fuel Savings and EEDI Compliance]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Conversion Modification Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Existing Ship in Service] --> B1[Hull Survey and Strength Assessment]
    B1 --> C1[Bow Section Cutting Plan]
    C1 --> D1[Fabrication of Bulb Unit in Shop]
    D1 --> E1[Drydock Hull Preparation]
    E1 --> F1[Cutting Forward Stem and Existing Bow]
    F1 --> G1[Pre-fitting and Alignment of Bulb]
    G1 --> H1[Welding and NDT Inspection]
    H1 --> I1[Reinforcement of Forward Bilge and Stem]
    I1 --> J1[Paint and Cathodic Protection]
    J1 --> K1[Sea Trial and Wave Pattern Measurement]
    K1 --> L1[Performance Comparison with Baseline]
    L1 --> M1{Improvement greater than 5 percent}
    M1 -- Yes --> N1[Conversion Successful Sign Off]
    M1 -- No --> O1[Investigate and Iterate]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Block Representation — Bulbous Bow Geometry (KTU Nomenclature)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Bulbous Bow Assembly] --> B0[Streamlined Forward Tip]
    A0 --> C0[Maximum Section at Mid-Length]
    A0 --> D0[Tail Connection to Hull]
    B0 --> B1[Elliptical Leading Edge]
    C0 --> C1[Bulb Beam B_B Maximum 0.18 B]
    C0 --> C2[Section Thickness 0.012 L_pp]
    D0 --> D1[Fairing Wedge to Existing Hull]
    D0 --> D2[Structural Doubler Plates]
    A0 --> E0[Volume Fraction 0.5 to 1.5 percent]
    A0 --> F0[Submerged at Design Draft T]
    A0 --> G0[Clearance from Keel about 0.05 T]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Q: Define a bulbous bow. State two of its primary functions in the context of ship conversion.**

**Model Answer:**

A **bulbous bow** is a streamlined, forward-projecting bulb fitted below the waterline at the bow of a ship, ahead of the forward perpendicular (FP). It is an underwater appendage designed primarily to **reduce wave-making resistance** and improve **propulsive efficiency**.

**Two primary functions in ship conversion:**

1. **Resistance Reduction:** By generating a wave system 180° out of phase with the parent hull's bow wave, the bulb creates destructive interference, reducing wave-making resistance by 4 % to 15 % and effective power by 2 % to 8 %.

2. **Fuel Economy & Emissions Compliance:** A retrofitted bulbous bow reduces fuel consumption, helping the vessel comply with IMO **EEXI** and **CII** regulations without reducing cargo capacity.

**Other acceptable functions:** Increased displacement volume (cargo benefit), improved course stability, reduced bow wave height.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q: What is the Inui condition for the design of a bulbous bow? State the formula for the forward position of the bulb.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Inui condition** states that the bulb must be placed at a longitudinal position such that the **transverse wave generated by the bulb is exactly 180° out of phase** (i.e., half a wavelength displaced) with the **transverse wave generated by the parent hull's bow**, producing destructive wave interference at the bow shoulder.

**Formula for the forward position of the bulb:**

$$x_B = \frac{\lambda_T}{2} = \pi \, L_{pp} \, F_n^{2}$$

where:
- $\lambda_T$ = transverse wavelength of the bow wave
- $L_{pp}$ = length between perpendiculars of the parent ship
- $F_n$ = design Froude number of the vessel

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the principle of wave interference in bulbous bow design. Derive the expression for the forward position of the bulb in terms of ship speed and length. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier has $L_{pp} = 200$ m, design speed $V = 13.5$ knots, block coefficient $C_B = 0.78$, and displacement $\Delta = 42{,}000$ tonnes. A bulbous bow with volume fraction $\nabla_B / \nabla = 1.0 \%$ is proposed. Calculate: (i) the design Froude number, (ii) the forward position of the bulb, (iii) the bulb length using Inui's formula (take $\sigma = 0.40$ rad), and (iv) the expected percentage reduction in wave resistance (take $k = 0.8$). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Wave Interference Principle and Derivation:**

The principle of wave interference in bulbous bow design is based on **linear superposition of wave systems**. When a ship moves through water, the bow and stern generate a characteristic **Kelvin wave pattern** consisting of transverse and divergent wave systems. The energy radiated away in these waves constitutes the **wave-making resistance** $R_W$.

By placing a streamlined bulb at a position ahead of the FP, an **additional wave system** is generated. If the bulb's volume, shape, and longitudinal position are chosen correctly, the bulb's wave crests will arrive at the bow shoulder **exactly when the parent hull's wave troughs are arriving there** (and vice versa). The two wave systems undergo **destructive interference**, partially cancelling the wave field and reducing $R_W$.

**Derivation of bulb forward position:**

Consider a transverse wave of wavelength $\lambda_T$ created by the parent hull moving at speed $V$. The phase of this wave at any position $x$ ahead of the source is:

$$\phi(x) = -\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda_T} \cdot x$$

For destructive interference between the bulb wave and the hull wave at the bow shoulder, the **phase difference** must be $\pi$ (180°). With the bulb placed at $x = -x_B$ (forward of FP), the phase of the bulb's wave as seen at the FP is:

$$\phi_{\text{bulb}} = +\frac{2 \pi x_B}{\lambda_T}$$

Setting the phase difference equal to $\pi$:

$$\frac{2 \pi x_B}{\lambda_T} = \pi \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x_B = \frac{\lambda_T}{2}$$

The transverse wavelength for a pressure point moving at speed $V$ is $\lambda_T = 2 \pi V^{2} / g$. Substituting:

$$\boxed{x_B = \frac{\pi V^{2}}{g} = \pi L_{pp} F_n^{2}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $L_{pp} = 200$ m
- $V = 13.5$ knots $= 13.5 \times 0.5144 = 6.944$ m/s
- $C_B = 0.78$
- $\Delta = 42{,}000$ t
- $\nabla_B / \nabla = 0.010$
- $\sigma = 0.40$ rad
- $k = 0.8$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³

**(i) Design Froude Number:**

$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g L_{pp}}} = \frac{6.944}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 200}} = \frac{6.944}{\sqrt{1962}} = \frac{6.944}{44.29} = 0.1568$$

**(ii) Forward Position of Bulb:**

$$x_B = \pi L_{pp} F_n^{2} = \pi \times 200 \times (0.1568)^{2} = \pi \times 200 \times 0.02459 = 15.45 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Bulb Length (Inui's Formula):**

$$\frac{L_B}{L_{pp}} = \frac{0.5 \sigma}{\pi (1 - 0.5 \sigma)} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.40}{\pi \times (1 - 0.20)} = \frac{0.20}{\pi \times 0.80} = \frac{0.20}{2.513} = 0.0796$$

$$L_B = 0.0796 \times 200 = 15.92 \text{ m}$$

**(iv) Expected Reduction in Wave Resistance:**

$$\frac{\Delta R_W}{R_W} = -k \left( \frac{\nabla_B}{\nabla} \right)^{2/3} \cdot F_n^{-1} = -0.8 \times (0.010)^{0.667} \times (0.1568)^{-1}$$

Computing the volume term: $(0.010)^{0.667}$. Using $\ln(0.010) = -4.605$, multiplied by $0.667$: $-3.071$, exponential: $0.0464$.

Computing the Froude term: $(0.1568)^{-1} = 6.378$.

$$\frac{\Delta R_W}{R_W} = -0.8 \times 0.0464 \times 6.378 = -0.237 = -23.7 \%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** A common error is computing $V$ in knots directly into the Froude formula. **Always convert $V$ to m/s** before using $V / \sqrt{g L_{pp}}$. Marks lost here are unrecoverable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Students often confuse the bulb position formula $x_B = \pi L_{pp} F_n^2$ with the Inui bulb length formula. These are **different formulas** — the first gives $x_B$ (position), the second gives $L_B$ (length). Mixing them up costs a full 3 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Describe the various types of bulbous bows used in modern ship design. Explain the criteria for selecting a bulb type for a given ship during a conversion project. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A container vessel with $L_{pp} = 300$ m, $B = 42.8$ m, $T = 14.5$ m, and design speed $V = 24$ knots is to be retrofitted with a bulbous bow. The estimated bulb volume is $1250$ m³ and the ship displacement volume is $78{,}500$ m³. Calculate: (i) the Froude number, (ii) the bulb forward position, (iii) the bulb length using Inui's formula (take $\sigma = 0.50$ rad), and (iv) the expected total resistance reduction. Comment on whether the conversion is economically justified. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Types of Bulbous Bows:**

There are several geometric classifications of bulbous bows, each suited to different speed regimes and hull forms:

1. **Normal / Spherical Bulb (Type A):** A simple, rounded bulb resembling a stretched sphere. Best suited to **low to mid Froude numbers** ($F_n = 0.18$ to $0.25$). Common on bulk carriers and tankers.

2. **Pointed / Torpedo Bulb (Type B):** A more elongated, pointed shape resembling a torpedo. Used at **higher Froude numbers** ($F_n = 0.25$ to $0.35$) where the wavelength is longer. Common on container ships and naval frigates.

3. **Hybrid / Hybrid-Streamlined Bulb (Type C):** A combination of rounded leading edges and a tapered trailing section. Used for **wide speed-range** operations.

4. **Delta Bulb (Type D):** A downward-pointing, wedge-shaped bulb designed for **fine-form** hulls (e.g., high-speed container ships, research vessels).

5. **Whale Bulb:** A large, voluminous bulb extending well below the keel — used on **VLCC tankers** at low $F_n$ ($< 0.15$).

**Selection criteria for conversion:**

- **Design Froude number** of the vessel (governs wavelength to cancel).
- **Hull block coefficient** $C_B$ (fine hulls favor smaller, more pointed bulbs; full hulls favor larger, rounder bulbs).
- **Operational speed profile** (single vs. multi-speed service).
- **Structural feasibility** of cutting and welding the forward stem.
- **LCB shift tolerance** and trim correction capability.
- **Economic payback period** (typically < 36 months).

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $L_{pp} = 300$ m
- $B = 42.8$ m
- $T = 14.5$ m
- $V = 24$ knots $= 24 \times 0.5144 = 12.346$ m/s
- $\nabla_B = 1250$ m³
- $\nabla = 78{,}500$ m³
- $\sigma = 0.50$ rad

**(i) Froude Number:**

$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g L_{pp}}} = \frac{12.346}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 300}} = \frac{12.346}{\sqrt{2943}} = \frac{12.346}{54.25} = 0.2276$$

**(ii) Bulb Forward Position:**

$$x_B = \pi L_{pp} F_n^{2} = \pi \times 300 \times (0.2276)^{2} = \pi \times 300 \times 0.0518 = 48.81 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Bulb Length (Inui's Formula):**

$$\frac{L_B}{L_{pp}} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.50}{\pi \times (1 - 0.25)} = \frac{0.25}{\pi \times 0.75} = \frac{0.25}{2.356} = 0.1061$$

$$L_B = 0.1061 \times 300 = 31.84 \text{ m}$$

**(iv) Expected Total Resistance Reduction:**

Volume fraction: $\nabla_B / \nabla = 1250 / 78{,}500 = 0.01592$

Froude inverse: $F_n^{-1} = 1 / 0.2276 = 4.393$

Using $k = 0.8$ (typical for container ship hull forms):

$$\frac{\Delta R_W}{R_W} = -0.8 \times (0.01592)^{2/3} \times 4.393$$

Computing $(0.01592)^{2/3}$: $\ln(0.01592) = -4.139$, $\times 0.667 = -2.760$, exponential $= 0.0633$.

$$\frac{\Delta R_W}{R_W} = -0.8 \times 0.0633 \times 4.393 = -0.2225 = -22.3 \%$$

The **total resistance reduction** (including frictional and form components) is typically **50 % to 60 % of the wave resistance reduction**:

$$\frac{\Delta R_T}{R_T} \approx 0.55 \times (-22.3 \%) \approx -12.3 \%$$

**Economic Justification:**

For a container vessel consuming ~ 200 tonnes of fuel per day at 24 knots service speed:
- Daily fuel savings at -12.3 %: ~ 24.6 tonnes/day
- Annual savings (350 days operation): ~ 8610 tonnes
- At fuel cost USD 600/tonne: ~ USD 5.17 million/year
- Typical bulb conversion cost: USD 3 to 5 million
- **Payback period: 7 to 12 months**

> **Conclusion:** The conversion is **strongly economically justified** for this vessel.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** When asked about "total" resistance reduction, do **not** quote the wave resistance figure directly. Apply the **typical 50 % to 60 % attenuation factor** to get the realistic total resistance reduction. Quoting only wave resistance reduction (e.g., -22 %) without explaining the total resistance context will cost 1 to 2 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #4:** In economic justification problems, always state the **annual fuel cost saved** explicitly. A vague "it saves fuel" answer is insufficient. Show the calculation chain: daily consumption → daily savings → annual savings → payback period.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Bulbous Bow Design**

- **Definition:** Bulbous bow is a streamlined underwater appendage fitted forward of FP to reduce wave-making resistance via destructive wave interference.
- **Core Principle (Inui's Theory):** Bulb must be placed where its wave is 180° out of phase with the parent hull's bow wave.
- **Position Formula:** $x_B = \pi L_{pp} F_n^{2}$ (forward of FP).
- **Inui Bulb Length Formula:** $L_B / L_{pp} = 0.5 \sigma / [\pi(1 - 0.5 \sigma)]$ where $\sigma$ is the Kelvin half-angle in radians.
- **Transverse Wavelength:** $\lambda_T = 2\pi V^2 / g = 2\pi L_{pp} F_n^2$.
- **Volume Fraction:** $\nabla_B / \nabla = 0.5 \%$ to $1.5 \%$ (optimal range).
- **Resistance Reduction (Empirical):** $\Delta R_W / R_W = -k (\nabla_B / \nabla)^{2/3} F_n^{-1}$.
- **Typical Speed Band:** $F_n = 0.20$ to $0.40$.
- **LCB Shift:** Bulb retrofit shifts LCB by ~ 0.5 % $L_{pp}$ (may require trim correction).
- **Bulb Types:** Normal/Spherical, Pointed/Torpedo, Hybrid, Delta, Whale.
- **Selection Criteria:** Froude number, block coefficient, speed profile, structural feasibility.
- **Conversion Steps:** Hull survey → bow cutting → bulb fabrication → drydock fit-up → welding → NDT → reinforcement → sea trial.
- **Regulatory Driver:** IMO EEDI / EEXI / CII compliance.
- **Typical Savings:** 4 % to 15 % in $R_W$, 2 % to 8 % in effective power, payback 7 to 36 months.
- **Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
  1. Forgetting to convert $V$ from knots to m/s in Froude formula.
  2. Confusing $x_B$ (position) with $L_B$ (length).
  3. Reporting only wave resistance reduction, not total resistance reduction.
  4. Ignoring LCB shift and trim correction in conversion economics.
  5. Designing bulb for the wrong Froude number (bulb is speed-specific).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stern Forms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stern Forms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stern Form (KTU 2024 Definition):** The *stern form* refers to the geometric configuration of the **afterbody** of a ship, comprising the shape of the hull behind the maximum section (parallel mid-body termination) up to the **sternpost** or **transom edge**. It is defined by the waterline shape, buttock lines, and the profile of the propeller aperture region, and is quantified by form coefficients such as the **prismatic coefficient of the afterbody** ($C_{PA}$) and the **after-body shape parameter**.

The stern form is a critical hull design parameter in ship conversion because it directly governs the **wake field**, **propulsive efficiency**, **course stability**, and **resistance characteristics** of the vessel. Any change in the stern geometry during conversion (e.g., adding a bulb, lengthening the parallel middle body, or reshaping the buttocks) alters the boundary layer behaviour and pressure distribution around the hull.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 3 of PENST415 (Ship Conversion Technology), stern forms are studied under the broader topic of *Hull Form Modifications* with a focus on how pre-conversion stern geometry constrains or enables specific conversion options.

### 1.2 Common Stern Form Classifications

The principal stern forms encountered in merchant naval architecture are:

1. **Cruiser Stern (Round / Elliptical Stern)** – Waterlines converge to a fine point at the centreline.
2. **Transom Stern (Square / Flat Stern)** – A wide, flat, or slightly rounded vertical (or near-vertical) surface closing the hull.
3. **Elliptical Stern** – Smoothly faired elliptical waterline closure.
4. **Counter Stern** – A composite of an upper counter (concave) above a flatter lower section.
5. **Canoe Stern** – Hull lines continue aft to a fine end (rare, used in some sailing vessels).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Think of a swimming pool's deep end wall. A *transom stern* is like cutting a swimming pool with a flat vertical wall — abrupt, simple, structurally efficient, but causes turbulent water (slipstream) behind the ship. A *cruiser stern* is like gradually tapering the pool's floor and walls toward a point — smoother flow, less disturbance, but harder to build. Ship conversion engineers often must *trade-off* between structural simplicity, hydrodynamic smoothness, and available dock/building constraints — exactly the same compromise an architect faces when designing a kayak versus a flat-bottomed barge.

### 1.4 Key Geometric & Performance Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| After-body prismatic coefficient | $C_{PA}$ | 0.55 – 0.80 | Volume distribution aft of midship |
| Waterline half-angle (entrance of stern) | $\phi$ | 5° – 25° | Flow acceleration into propeller |
| Stern clearance | $C_s$ | 0.10$R$ – 0.30$R$ | Gap between hull and propeller tip |
| Taylor wake fraction | $w_T$ | 0.20 – 0.55 | Powering prediction |
| Stern form parameter (van Lammeren) | $i_E$ | 10 – 30 | Empirical afterbody fullness |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Why and How

#### A. Why the Stern Form Matters in Ship Conversion

When a vessel undergoes conversion (lengthening, re-engining, bulbous bow addition, block-coefficient change), the **stern form is one of the most sensitive parameters** because:

- It controls the **wake field** entering the propeller disk. A change in wake fraction directly changes **quasi-propulsive efficiency** ($\eta_D$) and hence **EHP/SHP ratio**.
- The stern is structurally the **heavily reinforced region** (rudder horn, stern frame, propeller bossing, bilge keel endings). Conversion is expensive here.
- The stern dictates the **propeller-hull interaction** (P-H interaction) and the **pressure pulse** that the hull experiences.
- The **course-keeping** and **course stability** are functions of the lateral waterplane area, which is concentrated at the stern for transom forms.

#### B. How Each Stern Form Influences Performance

**1. Cruiser Stern**
- *Why:* Smooth, symmetric waterline closure produces a thin, axial wake.
- *How:* Boundary layer remains attached longer → lower form resistance, low $w_T$, higher propulsive efficiency at moderate Froude numbers.
- *Conversion implication:* Preferred for high-speed or fuel-efficient conversions; but requires more steel forming.

**2. Transom Stern**
- *Why:* Abrupt termination separates the flow and creates a **transom wave** and a **Kármán-like vortex street** behind the hull.
- *How:* Higher wake non-uniformity, larger $w_T$, increased **revolutions per minute (RPM) reduction risk**, but lower structural cost and easier stern tube alignment.
- *Conversion implication:* Common in tugboats, fishing vessels, and small craft; conversion can add a **bulbous stern* (reverse bulb) to recover efficiency.

**3. Elliptical Stern**
- *Why:* A compromise — partial transom above waterline, full cruiser closure below.
- *How:* Maintains favourable wake with reduced structural cost above WL.
- *Conversion implication:* Frequently seen in naval auxiliaries; allows easy addition of a **squat stern sonar dome** or **azimuth thruster recess**.

**4. Counter Stern**
- *Why:* The upper counter provides additional buoyancy and freeboard aft.
- *How:* Creates a region of **flow separation** above the design waterline; not recommended for fine-form cargo ships.
- *Conversion implication:* Common in pre-1980s cargo vessels; conversion projects often involve *faired* counter → cruiser conversion for EEDI compliance.

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are examinable under Module 3 of PENST415. Memorise the symbols and the physical interpretation of each.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{PA} &= \frac{V_{A}}{A_{M} \cdot L_{A}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_T &= 1 - \frac{V_{A} + V_{S}}{V_{M}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{D} &= \eta_{O} \cdot \eta_{H} \cdot \eta_{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{H} &= \frac{1 - w_T}{1 - w}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{E} &= \frac{R_{T} \cdot V_{S}}{75} \quad \text{(metric HP, knots)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{D} &= \frac{P_{E}}{\eta_{D}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{n} &= \frac{V_{S}}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{E} &= \frac{C_{PA} + 0.5 \cdot \nabla \text{ correction factor}}{L / \nabla^{1/3}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** In the wake fraction equation, $V_A$, $V_S$, and $V_M$ are velocities of advance, ship, and model respectively — NOT volumes. Mixing these in a problem solution costs full marks.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Formula | LaTeX Form | Physical Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | After-body prismatic coefficient | $C_{PA}$ | Volumetric fullness of afterbody | dimensionless |
| 2 | Taylor wake fraction | $w_T$ | Frictional + form wake at propeller disk | dimensionless |
| 3 | Hull efficiency | $\eta_H$ | Ratio of thrust work to rotor work | dimensionless |
| 4 | Propulsive coefficient | $\eta_D$ | Overall propulsive efficiency | dimensionless |
| 5 | Froude number | $F_n$ | Wave-making tendency | dimensionless |
| 6 | Effective HP | $P_E$ | Power to overcome total resistance | kW or HP |
| 7 | Delivered HP | $P_D$ | Power at propeller flange | kW or HP |
| 8 | Stern clearance | $C_s / R$ | Propeller-hull tip gap ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | Reynolds number | $R_n$ | Viscous flow regime indicator | dimensionless |
| 10 | Form factor (Hoerner's) | $(1 + k)$ | Form drag multiplier on 2D friction | dimensionless |

> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used during *powering prediction studies* in any conversion project where the afterbody is modified. They are also the foundation of **ISO 15016** speed/power trials analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Relationship Between Stern Form and Wake Fraction

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **favourite 14-mark question** in KTU ESE. Walk through it carefully.

**Starting Point:** The wake at the propeller disk comprises three components:

$$
w = w_F + w_{form} + w_{wave}
$$

where
- $w_F$ = frictional wake
- $w_{form}$ = form (potential-flow induced) wake
- $w_{wave}$ = wave wake (oscillatory component)

**Step 1 — Express the frictional wake component:**

For a turbulent boundary layer on a flat plate (ITTC-78 correlation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_F &= \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The frictional wake from the boundary layer displacement thickness is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{F} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_F \cdot \frac{S_{aft}}{A_D}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the form wake using slender-body theory:**

For a slender afterbody, the potential-flow wake is approximated by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{form} &= \frac{1}{4\pi} \int_{x_{m}}^{L/2} \frac{d^{2}y(x)}{dx^{2}} \cdot \frac{dx}{x_{p} - x}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $y(x)$ is the half-breadth of the hull and $x_p$ is the propeller longitudinal position.

**Step 3 — Wave wake component:**

The wave wake oscillates and is bounded by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{wave} &\approx 0.04 \cdot F_{n}^{2} \quad \text{(for moderate-speed vessels)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total wake at the propeller disk:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w &= w_F + w_{form} + w_{wave}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Effect on hull efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_H &= \frac{1 - w_T}{1 - w} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \eta_H \uparrow \text{ as } w \downarrow
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** A *cruiser* stern reduces $w_{form}$ (because the second derivative $d^{2}y/dx^{2}$ is small near the tip), so it yields **lower $w$** and **higher $\eta_H$**. A *transom* stern increases $w_{form}$ sharply at the transom edge, increasing $w$ and **lowering $\eta_H$**. This is why transom-stern vessels often need a *propeller nozzle* or *duct* during conversion to recover efficiency.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Estimating Wake Change After Stern Conversion

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A bulk carrier has a cruiser stern with $w_T = 0.32$ and $\eta_H = 1.20$. During conversion, the stern is replaced by a transom form. The new estimated $w_T$ is **0.42**. The original SHP is **12 000 kW** at service speed $V_S = 14$ knots. Assuming EHP remains constant, estimate the new SHP and the percentage increase in fuel consumption at the same speed.

**Step 1 — Original delivered power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{D,\text{orig}} &= \eta_{O} \cdot \eta_{H,\text{orig}} \cdot \eta_{R} \\
&= 0.65 \cdot 1.20 \cdot 1.00 \\
&= 0.78
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{D,\text{orig}} &= \frac{P_E}{\eta_{D,\text{orig}}} = \frac{P_E}{0.78}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — New hull efficiency with transom stern:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{H,\text{new}} &\approx \frac{1 - w_{T,\text{new}}}{1 - w_{T,\text{orig}}} \cdot \eta_{H,\text{orig}} \\
&\approx \frac{1 - 0.42}{1 - 0.32} \cdot 1.20 \\
&\approx \frac{0.58}{0.68} \cdot 1.20 \\
&\approx 1.024
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — New overall propulsive coefficient (assuming $\eta_O$ and $\eta_R$ unchanged):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{D,\text{new}} &= 0.65 \cdot 1.024 \cdot 1.00 = 0.666
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — New delivered power for same EHP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{D,\text{new}} &= \frac{P_E}{0.666}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage increase in SHP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \Delta P_D &= \left( \frac{0.78}{0.666} - 1 \right) \cdot 100 \\
&\approx 17.1 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Converting from a cruiser to a transom stern at the same EHP requires **~17 % more SHP**. This is why such conversions are usually accompanied by a **nozzle**, **grate ducts**, or a **re-shaped counter stern** to recover efficiency.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Stern Forms vs. Conversion Suitability

| Stern Form | Wake Fraction ($w_T$) | $\eta_H$ Range | Resistance Penalty | Best Conversion Use | Avoid For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cruiser** | 0.20 – 0.32 | 1.20 – 1.35 | Low | High-speed container, naval | Heavy weather (slamming) |
| **Elliptical** | 0.25 – 0.35 | 1.15 – 1.28 | Low–Moderate | General cargo, navy aux. | Extreme shallow draft |
| **Transom** | 0.35 – 0.55 | 0.95 – 1.15 | High (if not optimised) | Tugs, fishing, harbour craft | Long-range, EEDI ships |
| **Counter** | 0.30 – 0.45 | 1.05 – 1.20 | Moderate | Old cargo conversion (EEDI) | High-speed operations |
| **Canoe** | 0.18 – 0.28 | 1.30 – 1.45 | Very Low | Sailing/specialised vessels | Conventional merchant |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Stern Form Selection Logic

The following diagram maps the **decision flow** that naval architects follow when choosing a stern form for a conversion project:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Conversion Goal] --> nodeB{Vessel Type?}
    nodeB -- High Speed --> nodeC[Target F_n > 0.30]
    nodeB -- Low Speed --> nodeD[Target F_n < 0.20]
    nodeB -- Medium Speed --> nodeE[Target F_n 0.20 to 0.30]

    nodeC --> nodeF[Choose CRUISER Stern]
    nodeF --> nodeF1[Verify Wake Fraction 0.20 to 0.32]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF2[Check Structural Forming Cost]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF3[End: High-Speed Stern Selected]

    nodeD --> nodeG[Choose TRANSOM Stern]
    nodeG --> nodeG1[Verify Wake Fraction 0.35 to 0.55]
    nodeG1 --> nodeG2{Propeller Nozzle Available?}
    nodeG2 -- Yes --> nodeG3[Add Nozzle, Reduce w_T]
    nodeG2 -- No --> nodeG4[Accept Higher SHP]
    nodeG3 --> nodeG5[End: Transom with Nozzle]
    nodeG4 --> nodeG5

    nodeE --> nodeH[Choose ELLIPTICAL Stern]
    nodeH --> nodeH1[Verify Wake Fraction 0.25 to 0.35]
    nodeH1 --> nodeH2[Check Counter Region Above WL]
    nodeH2 --> nodeH3[End: Elliptical Stern Selected]
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Wake Field Variation by Stern Type

The following block diagram represents the **wake topology** at the propeller disk for each stern form (numerical scales show typical axial wake fraction $w$):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA1[Cruiser Stern] --> nodeB1[Axial wake: 0.20 to 0.32]
    nodeA2[Elliptical Stern] --> nodeB2[Axial wake: 0.25 to 0.35]
    nodeA3[Counter Stern] --> nodeB3[Axial wake: 0.30 to 0.45]
    nodeA4[Transom Stern] --> nodeB4[Axial wake: 0.35 to 0.55]

    nodeB1 --> nodeC1[eta_H: 1.20 to 1.35]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC2[eta_H: 1.15 to 1.28]
    nodeB3 --> nodeC3[eta_H: 1.05 to 1.20]
    nodeB4 --> nodeC4[eta_H: 0.95 to 1.15]

    nodeC1 --> nodeD1[Best for: High-speed conversion]
    nodeC2 --> nodeD2[Best for: General cargo conversion]
    nodeC3 --> nodeD3[Best for: Legacy cargo EEDI retrofit]
    nodeC4 --> nodeD4[Best for: Tug and fishing conversion]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Conversion Workflow When Stern Form is Modified

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Conversion Project Initiated] --> stepB[Baseline Stern Form Identified]
    stepB --> stepC{Modification Required?}
    stepC -- No --> stepD[Proceed with Other Modules]
    stepC -- Yes --> stepE[New Stern Form Selected]
    stepE --> stepF[Compute New w_T via Holtrop or CFD]
    stepF --> stepG[Recompute eta_H and P_D]
    stepG --> stepH{Within SHP Margin?}
    stepH -- No --> stepI[Re-engine or add nozzle]
    stepH -- Yes --> stepJ[Structural Drawing Update]
    stepI --> stepF
    stepJ --> stepK[End: Stern Conversion Approved]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "stern form" in shipbuilding. List any four common types of stern forms used in merchant vessels.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** The stern form is the geometric shape of the afterbody of a ship, bounded by the waterlines, buttock lines, and the stern post or transom edge. It determines the wake field, propulsive efficiency, and resistance characteristics of the vessel.

> **Four common stern forms (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
> 1. Cruiser stern (round / elliptical waterline closure)
> 2. Transom stern (flat vertical or near-vertical termination)
> 3. Elliptical stern (smooth elliptical waterline shape)
> 4. Counter stern (concave upper region above a flat lower region)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is Taylor's wake fraction? State its typical range and explain how it is influenced by the stern form.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Taylor's wake fraction $w_T$ is defined as the ratio of the difference between the speed of advance of the propeller $V_A$ and the ship speed $V_S$ to the ship speed, expressed as $w_T = (V_S - V_A) / V_S = 1 - V_A / V_S$.

> **Typical range (1 mark):** For merchant vessels, $w_T$ ranges between **0.20 and 0.55** depending on the stern form and loading condition.

> **Influence of stern form (1 mark):** Cruiser and elliptical sterns produce a thin, axial wake with low $w_T$ (0.20–0.32). Transom and counter sterns produce higher $w_T$ (0.35–0.55) due to flow separation and vortex shedding. Lower $w_T$ yields higher hull efficiency and better propulsive performance.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In stern-form questions, students often confuse the **propulsive coefficient** ($\eta_D$) with the **hull efficiency** ($\eta_H$). Always state which efficiency you are computing and the assumptions used (e.g., $\eta_R = 1.00$ for free-running model). Marks are awarded for *defining symbols* before plugging in numbers.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, describe the **four principal stern forms** used in merchant shipbuilding. Discuss the effect of each form on the wake fraction and hull efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A container ship of length 220 m and service speed 22 knots is undergoing conversion. The existing cruiser stern has $w_T = 0.30$ and $\eta_H = 1.25$. The proposed elliptical stern conversion is expected to give $w_T = 0.34$. Assuming $\eta_O = 0.62$, $\eta_R = 1.00$, and EHP = 18 000 kW, calculate:
1. The new hull efficiency.
2. The new overall propulsive coefficient.
3. The new delivered power and the change in SHP as a percentage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — 7 marks:**

*Cruiser stern (1.5 marks):* The waterlines converge smoothly to a fine point at the centreline. The buttocks are also faired smoothly, producing an axisymmetric wake. Wake fraction is low (0.20–0.32), and $\eta_H$ is high (1.20–1.35). Best for high-speed vessels and fuel-efficient conversions.

*Transom stern (1.5 marks):* A wide, flat or slightly rounded vertical surface closes the hull. Causes flow separation at the transom edge, generating a transom wave and vortex street. Wake fraction is high (0.35–0.55), and $\eta_H$ is low (0.95–1.15). Best for tugs, fishing vessels, and harbour craft; usually fitted with a nozzle.

*Elliptical stern (1.5 marks):* A compromise between cruiser and transom — partial transom above WL and full cruiser closure below. Moderate wake fraction (0.25–0.35) and moderate $\eta_H$ (1.15–1.28). Versatile; common in general cargo and naval auxiliaries.

*Counter stern (1.5 marks):* A composite stern with a concave upper region (the "counter") above a flatter lower section. Provides additional buoyancy and freeboard aft but introduces flow separation above WL. Wake fraction 0.30–0.45; $\eta_H$ 1.05–1.20. Often faired into a cruiser form during EEDI-compliance retrofits.

*Common effect summary (1 mark):* The smoother and more axisymmetric the afterbody closure, the lower the wake fraction and the higher the hull efficiency. Abrupt terminations (transom) create the most non-uniform wake.

**(b) — 7 marks:**

*Step 1: New hull efficiency (2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{H,\text{new}} &= \eta_{H,\text{old}} \cdot \frac{1 - w_{T,\text{new}}}{1 - w_{T,\text{old}}} \\
&= 1.25 \cdot \frac{1 - 0.34}{1 - 0.30} \\
&= 1.25 \cdot \frac{0.66}{0.70} \\
&= 1.179
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2: New overall propulsive coefficient (2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{D,\text{new}} &= \eta_{O} \cdot \eta_{H,\text{new}} \cdot \eta_{R} \\
&= 0.62 \cdot 1.179 \cdot 1.00 \\
&= 0.731
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3: New delivered power (2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{D,\text{new}} &= \frac{P_E}{\eta_{D,\text{new}}} = \frac{18\,000}{0.731} \\
&\approx 24\,624 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 4: Percentage change in SHP (1 mark)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{D,\text{old}} &= \frac{18\,000}{0.62 \cdot 1.25 \cdot 1.00} = 23\,226 \text{ kW} \\
\% \Delta P_D &= \left( \frac{24\,624}{23\,226} - 1 \right) \cdot 100 \\
&\approx 6.0 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Converting from a cruiser to an elliptical stern requires **~6 % more SHP** for the same EHP. This is the typical penalty band for "mild" stern-form conversions.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative Module 3 Question]**

**(a)** Explain the role of the **afterbody prismatic coefficient** $C_{PA}$ in the design and conversion of merchant ships. How does $C_{PA}$ change when a vessel is lengthened by inserting a parallel middle body? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a conversion project, a 30-year-old tanker with a counter stern is being refitted for EEDI compliance. The original parameters are: $L_{WL} = 180$ m, $V_S = 15$ knots, $C_B = 0.78$, $C_{PA} = 0.72$, $w_T = 0.42$, $\eta_O = 0.58$, $\eta_R = 1.00$. The new design proposes a cruiser stern with $C_{PA} = 0.66$ and $w_T = 0.30$. Compute:
1. The new hull efficiency (assuming $\eta_{H,\text{old}} = 1.08$).
2. The new overall propulsive coefficient.
3. The percentage reduction in delivered power at the same EHP. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — 7 marks:**

*Definition (2 marks):* $C_{PA}$ is the ratio of the volume of the afterbody $V_A$ to the product of the midship section area $A_M$ and the afterbody length $L_A$:
$$C_{PA} = V_A / (A_M \cdot L_A)$$

*Role in design and conversion (3 marks):*
- $C_{PA}$ governs the **wake fraction** and the **form resistance** of the vessel.
- A higher $C_{PA}$ (0.75–0.85) indicates a **fuller** afterbody, common in low-speed tankers and bulk carriers. It produces a larger wake fraction but better course stability.
- A lower $C_{PA}$ (0.55–0.68) indicates a **finer** afterbody, used in high-speed container ships and naval frigates. It reduces wake fraction and improves propulsive efficiency.
- During conversion, $C_{PA}$ is a key parameter for evaluating whether a re-shaping of the afterbody (e.g., adding a parallel middle body, fitting a bulbous stern, or fairing the counter) is necessary.

*Effect of lengthening with parallel middle body (2 marks):*
- Inserting a parallel middle body **lengthens $L_A$** without changing $A_M$ or $V_A$ in the new parallel region.
- If the insertion is purely amidships (not affecting the afterbody), $C_{PA}$ remains approximately constant.
- If the insertion is partly aft of the mid-section, $L_A$ increases and $V_A$ changes, leading to a **reduction in $C_{PA}$** — i.e., the afterbody becomes effectively finer. This typically **reduces $w_T$** and improves $\eta_H$ but may slightly increase wave resistance.

**(b) — 7 marks:**

*Step 1: New hull efficiency (2.5 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{H,\text{new}} &= \eta_{H,\text{old}} \cdot \frac{1 - w_{T,\text{new}}}{1 - w_{T,\text{old}}} \\
&= 1.08 \cdot \frac{1 - 0.30}{1 - 0.42} \\
&= 1.08 \cdot \frac{0.70}{0.58} \\
&= 1.303
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2: New overall propulsive coefficient (2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{D,\text{new}} &= \eta_{O} \cdot \eta_{H,\text{new}} \cdot \eta_{R} \\
&= 0.58 \cdot 1.303 \cdot 1.00 \\
&= 0.756
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3: Percentage reduction in delivered power (2.5 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{D,\text{old}} &= 0.58 \cdot 1.08 \cdot 1.00 = 0.626 \\
\% \Delta P_D &= \left(1 - \frac{\eta_{D,\text{old}}}{\eta_{D,\text{new}}}\right) \cdot 100 \\
&= \left(1 - \frac{0.626}{0.756}\right) \cdot 100 \\
&\approx 17.2 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Converting a counter stern to a cruiser form in this tanker scenario yields a **17.2 % reduction in SHP** for the same EHP. This is a major fuel-saving retrofit, justifying the higher structural conversion cost.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Stern form** = geometry of the afterbody from midship termination to the sternpost/transom.
- **Four principal merchant stern forms:** cruiser, transom, elliptical, counter.
- **Cruiser stern** → low $w_T$ (0.20–0.32), high $\eta_H$ (1.20–1.35), preferred for high-speed and EEDI conversions.
- **Transom stern** → high $w_T$ (0.35–0.55), low $\eta_H$ (0.95–1.15), used for tugs and harbour craft; needs a nozzle to recover efficiency.
- **Elliptical stern** → compromise; moderate $w_T$ (0.25–0.35) and $\eta_H$ (1.15–1.28); versatile.
- **Counter stern** → moderate $w_T$ (0.30–0.45); common in pre-1980s cargo ships; often retrofitted to cruiser for EEDI.
- **Key formula:** $w_T = 1 - V_A / V_S$.
- **Hull efficiency:** $\eta_H = (1 - w_T) / (1 - w)$.
- **Propulsive coefficient:** $\eta_D = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R$.
- **Afterbody prismatic coefficient:** $C_{PA} = V_A / (A_M \cdot L_A)$.
- **Conversion rule of thumb:** A 0.10 increase in $w_T$ corresponds to roughly 15–20 % increase in SHP at the same EHP.
- **Always define symbols** before substituting into equations — this is a KTU marking requirement.
- **EEDI compliance** drives modern conversions toward cruiser/elliptical sterns.
- **Nozzle / duct addition** is the standard method to recover efficiency when transom forms are retained.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** In any 14-mark question involving stern forms, structure your answer as: (1) definition + sketch, (2) effect on $w_T$ and $\eta_H$, (3) numerical calculation with clearly labelled steps, (4) engineering interpretation of the result. Examiners reward *clarity of reasoning* over raw computation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conventional Method of Lines Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Conventional Method of Lines Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Conventional Method of Lines Design** (also called the **Lines Plan**, **Lines Drawing**, or **Table of Offsets** method) is the classical, centuries-old naval architecture technique used to define, document, and communicate the complex three-dimensional (3D) geometry of a ship's hull surface by projecting it onto three mutually perpendicular, principal two-dimensional (2D) reference planes. The resulting graphical document is called the **Lines Plan**, and it is the *primary contractual and engineering drawing* of a vessel's form.

In the context of **Ship Conversion Technology (PENST415)**, the Lines Plan of the *parent ship* serves as the foundational geometric baseline from which all modification, lengthening, deepening, or re-shaping operations are mathematically and graphically derived. Without a properly faired and dimensioned Lines Plan, no conversion feasibility study or structural re-design can be performed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> The Conventional Method of Lines Design is a graphical procedure by which the geometry of a ship's hull is described by a set of mutually orthogonal sectional curves — **Waterlines (WL)**, **Transverse Stations (Sections)**, and **Longitudinal Buttocks (BL)** — projected onto the Profile (Sheer) Plan, the Body Plan, and the Half-Breadth Plan, with the intersection of any two such curves yielding a point on the hull surface called an **"Offset"** (or **"Co-ordinate"**).

## 1.2 The Three Principal Reference Planes

The 3D hull is projected onto three standard orthogonal planes:

1. **Profile (Sheer) Plan** — Projection onto the longitudinal vertical centreline plane (X-Z plane). Shows the *side view* of the ship.
2. **Body Plan** — Projection onto the transverse vertical plane at amidships (Y-Z plane). Shows the *front view* of the ship, with forward sections on the right and aft sections on the left.
3. **Half-Breadth Plan** — Projection onto the horizontal water plane (X-Y plane). Shows the *top view* of the ship, usually depicting only the starboard (right-hand) side by KTU 2024 convention.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Origami Ship in Three Mirrors"**
> 
> Imagine placing a small solid model of a ship's hull inside a glass box. The box has **three perpendicular mirrors** — one on the floor, one on the back wall, and one on the side wall. When the hull is illuminated, its outline is simultaneously "stamped" onto each of the three mirror surfaces:
> - The **floor mirror** captures the silhouette from above → this is the **Half-Breadth Plan** (top-down view).
> - The **back wall mirror** captures the silhouette from the side → this is the **Profile/Sheer Plan**.
> - The **side wall mirror** captures the silhouette from the front → this is the **Body Plan** (split into forward view and aft view).
> 
> Now, instead of just the silhouette, suppose we **slice the hull with horizontal "watermelon-knife" cuts** (the Waterlines), **vertical "bread-knife" cuts** at regular intervals along the length (the Stations), and **vertical "cheese-wire" cuts** running fore-and-aft (the Buttocks). Every slice leaves a curved line on the surface. By projecting every slice onto the correct mirror, the entire curved hull surface is completely described by the network of these intersecting curves. **This network is the Lines Plan.**

## 1.4 Key Physical Metrics & Standard Spacing

In the KTU 2024 scheme and in compliance with **Lloyd's Register Rules** and **ISO 7462 (Shipbuilding — Shipbuilding Drawings — Lines Plan)**, the following constants govern the design:

| Parameter | Standard Notation | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Length Between Perpendiculars** | $L_{PP}$ | 0.85 to 0.95 of $L_{OA}$ |
| **Length Overall** | $L_{OA}$ | Maximum hull dimension |
| **Design Waterline Length** | $L_{WL}$ | Length at the Design Waterline (DWL) |
| **Standard Station Spacing** | $\Delta x$ | $\dfrac{L_{PP}}{20}$ (Lloyd's Rule of 20) |
| **Standard Waterline Spacing** | $\Delta z$ | 4 to 6 equally spaced WLs between keel and DWL |
| **Number of Stations** | $N_s$ | 21 (stations 0 to 20 inclusive) |
| **Half-Beam Maximum** | $B/2$ | Half of the maximum moulded breadth |
| **Moulded Depth** | $D$ | Keel to main deck at side (amidships) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lloyd's Rule of 20 (Mandatory at KTU):**
> The number of transverse stations along $L_{PP}$ is exactly **20 equal intervals**, giving **21 station lines** (numbered 0 at the Aft Perpendicular to 20 at the Forward Perpendicular). This is a non-negotiable convention expected in all KTU board examination solutions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Sets of Cutting Planes (The "Curvilinear Grid")

The hull surface is uniquely defined by the intersection of three families of curves, each family lying in a plane parallel to one of the three principal projection planes:

### 2.1.1 Transverse Stations (Sections) — The "Bread-Knife" Cuts
- **Definition:** Vertical planes perpendicular to the longitudinal centreline, spaced at regular intervals along the length of the ship.
- **Number:** 21 stations (per Lloyd's Rule of 20).
- **Naming Convention:**
  - Station **0** → Aft Perpendicular (AP)
  - Station **10** → Amidships (mid-station)
  - Station **20** → Forward Perpendicular (FP)
- **Output Curves:** When projected onto the Body Plan, each station yields a closed curve showing the hull cross-section (typically U/V-shape forward, U-shape amidships, becoming nearly circular at the stern).
- **Engineering Utility:** Used directly for structural scantling, plate development, and stability/bonjean cross-curve generation.

### 2.1.2 Waterlines (WLs) — The "Watermelon-Knife" Cuts
- **Definition:** Horizontal planes cutting the hull at fixed vertical intervals from the baseline (keel) upward.
- **Standard Spacing:** $\Delta z$ typically = $D / 5$ to $D / 6$ (5 or 6 waterlines between keel and design waterline).
- **Naming Convention:** WL 0 (Baseline/Keel), WL 1, WL 2, ..., WL DWL (Design Waterline), WL LWL (Load Waterline), WL S (Sheer line / main deck side).
- **Output Curves:** When projected onto the Half-Breadth Plan, each WL yields a closed waterplane area curve.
- **Engineering Utility:** Used to compute waterplane area, TPC (Tonnes Per Centimetre), and longitudinal centre of flotation (LCF).

### 2.1.3 Longitudinal Buttocks (BLs) — The "Cheese-Wire" Cuts
- **Definition:** Vertical planes parallel to the longitudinal centreline plane, offset to port and starboard at specific distances.
- **Standard Spacing:** Typically 3 to 5 buttocks at $y = 0$ (centreline), $y = B/4$, $y = B/2$ (side), and sometimes intermediate.
- **Output Curves:** When projected onto the Profile Plan, each buttock yields a longitudinal curvature line.
- **Engineering Utility:** Used for longitudinal strength analysis, fairing, and bilge keel alignment.

## 2.2 The Concept of an "Offset" (Co-ordinate)

An **offset** is the unique 3D coordinate $(x, y, z)$ of a point on the hull surface, obtained as the intersection of a specific Station and a specific Waterline (or Station and Buttock, etc.). Offsets are tabulated in the **Table of Offsets**, which is the numerical data sheet that accompanies the Lines Plan.

For a point at Station $i$ on Waterline $j$:
- $x_i$ = longitudinal position (constant for the whole station)
- $z_j$ = vertical position (constant for the whole waterline)
- $y_{i,j}$ = **variable half-breadth** (this is the dimension that uniquely defines the hull form)

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Three-View Convention

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three views?**
> 
> A single 2D view cannot uniquely describe a 3D curved surface because **information is lost** when a 3D object is projected onto a plane (the depth dimension is collapsed). However, by combining three orthogonal 2D projections and using the principle of "**point projection**" (where two curves crossing in a 3D sense project as two curves crossing in *each* 2D view), the original 3D surface can be **fully reconstructed** mathematically. This is precisely the same logic used in engineering drawing (first-angle or third-angle projection) and in computer-aided 3D modelling (where the model is stored as a parametric mesh of $u, v$ coordinates analogous to stations and waterlines).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lloyd's Station Spacing** | $\Delta x = \dfrac{L_{PP}}{20}$ | metres (m) | Defines longitudinal grid resolution |
| **Number of Stations** | $N_s = \dfrac{L_{PP}}{\Delta x} + 1 = 21$ | dimensionless | Total vertical cut planes |
| **Standard Waterline Spacing** | $\Delta z = \dfrac{D}{5}$ to $\dfrac{D}{6}$ | m | Defines vertical grid resolution |
| **Design Waterline Half-Breadth (max)** | $y_{max} = \dfrac{B}{2}$ | m | Maximum beam of hull at DWL |
| **Offset Co-ordinate** | $P_{i,j} = (x_i, y_{i,j}, z_j)$ | m | Defines a unique point on hull |
| **Block Coefficient** | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T}$ | dimensionless | Hull fullness indicator |
| **Prismatic Coefficient** | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{PP}}$ | dimensionless | Distribution of volume along length |
| **Midship Section Coefficient** | $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | dimensionless | Fullness of midship section |
| **Displacement Volume** | $\nabla = \int_{0}^{L_{PP}} A(x) \, dx$ | $\text{m}^3$ | Total underwater volume |
| **Waterplane Area Coefficient** | $C_{WP} = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{L_{WL} \cdot B}$ | dimensionless | Fullness of waterplane |
| **TPC (Tonnes per cm immersion)** | $TPC = \dfrac{A_{WP} \cdot \rho_{sw}}{100}$ | t/cm | Loading sensitivity |
| **LCF (Long. Centre of Flotation)** | $LCF = \dfrac{\int x \cdot y_{WL}(x) \, dx}{A_{WP}}$ | m from midship | Pitching centre |
| **Station 10 = Amidships** | $x_{10} = \dfrac{L_{PP}}{2}$ | m | Reference for symmetry |
| **Area by Simpson's 1st Rule** | $A = \dfrac{h}{3}[y_0 + 4(y_1+y_3+...) + 2(y_2+y_4+...) + y_n]$ | $\text{m}^2$ | Numerical integration of offsets |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When computing areas or volumes from offsets using Simpson's Rules, the standard KTU convention requires **21 ordinates** (stations 0 to 20) for the First Rule and **multiples of 2 + 1 = odd numbers of stations** for the standard application. Always state which Simpson's rule variant (1st, 2nd, or 3rd) is being used.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Ship Conversion

In a **Ship Conversion Project**, the Conventional Lines Plan of the parent ship is the *single most important document* because:

1. **It is the geometric baseline** for inserting a new midbody (lengthening by inserting a parallel section).
2. **It is the source of all hydrostatic data** ($C_B$, $C_P$, TPC, LCF) needed to assess the converted vessel's performance.
3. **It enables fairing checks** before and after modification — any unfairness introduced by the conversion is immediately visible in the lines.
4. **It is the input for computational tools** (Hullspeed, Maxsurf, Rhino, NAPA) that require an initial set of 3D control points to mesh the surface.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion of a Table of Offsets into Displacement Volume

This is a classic KTU 14-mark derivation expected in board examinations. We derive the **displacement volume $\nabla$** of a ship from its Table of Offsets using successive application of Simpson's Rules (a double-integration).

### 3.1.1 Step 1: Set Up the Problem

The displacement volume is the integral of the cross-sectional area $A(x)$ along the ship's length:

$$
\nabla = \int_{0}^{L_{PP}} A(x) \, dx
$$

The cross-sectional area at any station $x_i$ is itself the integral of the half-breadth $y_{i}(z)$ from the keel to the waterline (twice, for both sides):

$$
A(x_i) = 2 \int_{0}^{T} y_i(z) \, dz
$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2: Apply Simpson's First Rule Lengthwise

Let $\Delta x$ = station spacing. The longitudinal integration becomes:

$$
\nabla = \int_{0}^{L_{PP}} A(x) \, dx \approx \frac{\Delta x}{3} \left[ A_0 + 4(A_1 + A_3 + \dots + A_{19}) + 2(A_2 + A_4 + \dots + A_{18}) + A_{20} \right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of the coefficients:** The "4" coefficients are at *odd* station indices, and "2" coefficients are at *even* (non-endpoint) station indices. This is the standard Simpson's 1st Rule pattern, which requires an **odd number of ordinates** (here, 21).

### 3.1.3 Step 3: Apply Simpson's First Rule Vertically at Each Station

For each station $i$, the cross-sectional area $A_i$ is obtained by integrating half-breadths $y_{i,j}$ vertically from the keel (WL 0) to the DWL. Let $n$ be the number of waterlines (must be odd, typically 5 or 9), and $h$ = waterline spacing.

$$
A_i = 2 \int_{0}^{T} y_i(z) \, dz \approx 2 \cdot \frac{h}{3} \left[ y_{i,0} + 4(y_{i,1} + y_{i,3} + \dots) + 2(y_{i,2} + y_{i,4} + \dots) + y_{i,n} \right]
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4: Combine into the Double-Integration Master Formula

Substituting Step 3 into Step 2:

$$
\nabla = \frac{2 \cdot h \cdot \Delta x}{9} \sum_{i=0}^{20} W_i \sum_{j=0}^{n} w_j \, y_{i,j}
$$

where $W_i$ are the longitudinal Simpson's weights $\{1, 4, 2, 4, 2, \dots, 4, 1\}$ and $w_j$ are the vertical Simpson's weights $\{1, 4, 2, 4, 2, \dots, 4, 1\}$.

### 3.1.5 Step 5: Worked Numerical Skeleton (KTU-style)

**Given:** A vessel with $L_{PP} = 100$ m, 21 stations (Lloyd's spacing $\Delta x = 5$ m), 5 waterlines with $h = 2$ m, maximum half-breadth $B/2 = 10$ m.

**Station Spacing:**
$$
\Delta x = \frac{L_{PP}}{20} = \frac{100 \text{ m}}{20} = 5 \text{ m}
$$

**Vertical Spacing (assume 5 WLs, 0–4):**
$$
h = \frac{D}{n - 1} = \frac{D}{4}
$$

**Simpson's 1st Rule — Vertical (5 ordinates: WL 0, 1, 2, 3, 4):**
$$
A_i = 2 \cdot \frac{h}{3} \left[ y_{i,0} + 4 y_{i,1} + 2 y_{i,2} + 4 y_{i,3} + y_{i,4} \right]
$$

**Simpson's 1st Rule — Longitudinal (21 stations):**
$$
\nabla = \frac{\Delta x}{3} \sum_{i=0}^{20} W_i \, A_i
$$

**Putting it together** (using $W_i = \{1, 4, 2, 4, 2, \dots, 4, 1\}$):
$$
\nabla = \frac{2 h \Delta x}{9} \sum_{i=0}^{20} \sum_{j=0}^{4} W_i \, w_j \, y_{i,j}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the factor 2?** The factor of 2 in front accounts for the fact that the Table of Offsets usually gives only **half-breadths** (port side only), so we multiply by 2 to get the full breadth. The denominator $9$ comes from $3 \times 3$ (two applications of Simpson's 1/3 rule).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Reading the Three Views

| If you know the ... | ... and you need the ... | ... you look up the value in the ... | ... by the following projection |
|---|---|---|---|
| Station index $i$ and Waterline $j$ | Half-breadth $y$ | Body Plan (waterlines) and Half-Breadth Plan (stations) | Read $y$ from the Body Plan curve for station $i$ at WL $j$ |
| Station index $i$ and Waterline $j$ | Longitudinal position $x$ | Profile (Sheer) Plan | The whole station $i$ has a fixed $x$-value |
| Station index $i$ and Waterline $j$ | Vertical position $z$ | Profile (Sheer) Plan or Body Plan | The whole waterline $j$ has a fixed $z$-value |
| Point in Half-Breadth Plan | Its corresponding point in Body Plan | Drop a vertical projector down to the Body Plan | Standard third-angle projection |

## 3.3 Derivation: Lloyd's Rule of 20 vs. Increased Number of Stations

Some modern vessels (especially when a midship section is being inserted during conversion) use **21 equal intervals + 1**, i.e., 22 stations for fine resolution. The general form:

$$
N_s = \frac{L_{PP}}{\Delta x} + 1
$$

If a 22nd station is added amidships (for higher accuracy in the parallel midbody region):

$$
\Delta x = \frac{L_{PP}}{21}, \quad N_s = 22
$$

**Conversion Implication:** When a parallel midbody of length $L_m$ is inserted amidships, the new station spacing becomes:

$$
\Delta x_{new} = \frac{L_{PP,new}}{20} = \frac{L_{PP} + L_m}{20}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** The original 21 stations are retained and new stations are *interpolated* in the inserted midbody to maintain the Lloyd's spacing. The Table of Offsets must be re-tabulated for the converted vessel.

## 4.4 Description of the Conventional Lines Plan Layout (Since Physical Drawing is Not Rendered)

In an actual shipyard or KTU exam paper, the Lines Plan is laid out with:

- **Profile (Sheer) Plan** in the **upper-right quadrant** of the drawing sheet.
- **Body Plan** in the **upper-left quadrant** (showing Aft Sections on the LEFT side and Forward Sections on the RIGHT side; both are mirror images because they are viewed from astern and ahead respectively).
- **Half-Breadth Plan** in the **lower-right quadrant**, with the **forward end on the right side** of the drawing and the **after end on the left** (consistent with the Profile Plan above it).
- The **lower-left quadrant** is reserved for the **Table of Offsets** (numerical data sheet) and notes.

The **Starboard side of the hull** is conventionally drawn with **solid lines** (visible), and the **Port side** with **dashed lines** (hidden), as the entire representation is a "right-hand-side view" of the hull unfolded onto the paper.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Drawing Convention Reminder:**
> 1. Stations are numbered **0 at AP to 20 at FP** (in the Profile Plan, AP is on the LEFT and FP is on the RIGHT).
> 2. Waterlines are numbered **0 at the Keel**, increasing upward.
> 3. The **centerline is drawn as a long-short-long dashed line** (per ISO 128).
> 4. The **moulded baseline** is the reference for all vertical dimensions.
