# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the "Conventional Method of Lines Design" and state the three principal views used in a ship's Lines Plan.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Conventional Method of Lines Design is a classical naval architecture technique used to define the 3D geometry of a ship's hull by projecting it onto three mutually perpendicular 2D reference planes, generating a Lines Plan. The three principal views are:
1. **Profile (Sheer) Plan** — projection onto the longitudinal vertical centreline plane showing the side view.
2. **Body Plan** — projection onto the transverse vertical plane showing transverse sections (forward and aft views).
3. **Half-Breadth Plan** — projection onto the horizontal plane showing the top view of the hull (typically starboard side only).
The intersection of two cutting-plane curves (e.g., a station and a waterline) yields an "offset" which uniquely defines a point on the hull surface. *(3 marks — 1 for definition + 1 for listing views + 1 for the offset concept)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**What is Lloyd's Rule of 20? Why is it essential in ship conversion design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Lloyd's Rule of 20** is the standard convention that defines the **station spacing** along the Length Between Perpendiculars ($L_{PP}$) as $\Delta x = L_{PP} / 20$, resulting in **21 transverse station lines** (numbered 0 at AP to 20 at FP, with station 10 at amidships). It is essential in ship conversion because:
- It provides a **standardised, internationally accepted grid** for representing hull geometry, enabling comparison and reuse of parent-ship data.
- It allows **fairness checking** of modified hull forms.
- It forms the **input framework** for hydrostatic calculations and for inserting a new midbody during lengthening conversions.
*(3 marks — 1 for the rule + 1 for the count + 1 for conversion relevance)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - When drawing the Body Plan, students often draw the **aft and forward station curves on the same side** of the centreline — this is WRONG. The convention is that **aft sections are on the LEFT of the Body Plan's vertical centreline and forward sections on the RIGHT** (because they are "viewed" from behind and ahead respectively, projected onto the same plane).
> - Many students forget the **factor of 2** when computing areas/volumes from half-breadth offsets.
> - Do not confuse **waterline spacing** $\Delta z = D/5$ with the **number of waterlines** (which is 6 if spacing is D/5, since there are 5 intervals).
> - The **Table of Offsets** is the numerical data, NOT the Lines Plan drawing itself.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Option Set 1*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three principal views** of a ship's Lines Plan, including the orientation of each view, the curves plotted on each, and the standard KTU drawing conventions. State Lloyd's Rule of 20 and explain how station spacing is determined. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A vessel has $L_{PP} = 120$ m, $B = 18$ m, and design draft $T = 6.5$ m. The Table of Offsets gives half-breadths at five waterlines (WL 0 to WL 4) at stations 0, 5, 10, 15, and 20 as follows (all in metres):

| Station | WL 0 | WL 1 | WL 2 | WL 3 | WL 4 (DWL) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (AP) | 0.0 | 0.6 | 0.9 | 1.1 | 1.2 |
| 5 | 0.0 | 2.5 | 3.8 | 4.6 | 5.0 |
| 10 (Mid) | 0.0 | 5.0 | 7.5 | 8.5 | 9.0 |
| 15 | 0.0 | 2.5 | 3.8 | 4.6 | 5.0 |
| 20 (FP) | 0.0 | 0.6 | 0.9 | 1.1 | 1.2 |

Waterline spacing: $h = 6.5 / 4 = 1.625$ m. Station spacing: $\Delta x = 120/20 = 6$ m. Compute the **displacement volume $\nabla$** using double Simpson's First Rule integration. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks:**

**1. The Profile (Sheer) Plan** *(2 marks)*
- **Plane:** Longitudinal vertical centreline plane (X-Z).
- **Orientation:** Forward perpendicular (FP) is drawn on the **right side** of the drawing; AP on the **left**.
- **Curves shown:** Sheer line (top side profile), straight line of the keel, buttock curves (longitudinal vertical sections at $y = 0, B/4, B/2$), and the design waterline as a near-horizontal line.
- **Tick marks:** Stations are marked as short vertical tick lines crossing the waterlines.

**2. The Body Plan** *(2 marks)*
- **Plane:** Transverse vertical plane at amidships (Y-Z).
- **Orientation:** Aft sections (stations 0 to 10) are drawn on the **LEFT** side of the centreline; forward sections (stations 10 to 20) on the **RIGHT** side. (This is the standard "abnormal section" convention.)
- **Curves shown:** Transverse section curves of the hull at each station, with each section showing the full hull cross-section (keel at bottom, sheer at top).
- **Waterlines:** Horizontal lines crossing all sections.

**3. The Half-Breadth Plan** *(2 marks)*
- **Plane:** Horizontal water plane (X-Y).
- **Orientation:** Same as Profile Plan (FP on right, AP on left).
- **Curves shown:** Waterline contours (closed curves of the hull at each waterline), station tick marks, and the centreline as a long-dash-short-dash line.
- **Starboard convention:** Only the starboard half is drawn with solid lines; port side is dashed or omitted.

**4. Lloyd's Rule of 20** *(1 mark)*
- Station spacing: $\Delta x = L_{PP} / 20$, giving 21 stations numbered 0 to 20.

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Compute the cross-sectional area at each station using vertical Simpson's 1st Rule** *(3 marks)*

For 5 waterlines (WL 0 to WL 4), Simpson's weights are $\{1, 4, 2, 4, 1\}$:

$$
A_i = 2 \cdot \frac{h}{3} \left[ y_{i,0} + 4y_{i,1} + 2y_{i,2} + 4y_{i,3} + y_{i,4} \right]
$$

$$
A_0 = 2 \cdot \frac{1.625}{3} [0 + 4(0.6) + 2(0.9) + 4(1.1) + 1.2]
$$

$$
A_0 = \frac{3.25}{3} [2.4 + 1.8 + 4.4 + 1.2] = \frac{3.25}{3} (9.8) = 10.617 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_5 = \frac{3.25}{3} [0 + 10.0 + 7.6 + 18.4 + 5.0] = \frac{3.25}{3} (41.0) = 44.417 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{10} = \frac{3.25}{3} [0 + 20.0 + 15.0 + 34.0 + 9.0] = \frac{3.25}{3} (78.0) = 84.500 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{15} = 44.417 \text{ m}^2 \quad \text{(by symmetry)}
$$

$$
A_{20} = 10.617 \text{ m}^2 \quad \text{(by symmetry)}
$$

**Step 2: Apply longitudinal Simpson's 1st Rule using 5 ordinates (stations 0, 5, 10, 15, 20)** *(3 marks)*

Weights: $\{1, 4, 2, 4, 1\}$ with $\Delta x = 6$ m:

$$
\nabla = \frac{\Delta x}{3} [A_0 + 4A_5 + 2A_{10} + 4A_{15} + A_{20}]
$$

$$
\nabla = \frac{6}{3} [10.617 + 4(44.417) + 2(84.500) + 4(44.417) + 10.617]
$$

$$
\nabla = 2 \cdot [10.617 + 177.668 + 169.000 + 177.668 + 10.617]
$$

$$
\nabla = 2 \cdot 545.570 = 1091.14 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3: Displacement in tonnes (assuming salt water $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³)** *(1 mark)*

$$
W = \nabla \cdot \rho = 1091.14 \times 1.025 \approx 1118.4 \text{ tonnes}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Option Set 2*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** What is an **"offset"** in the context of the Conventional Method of Lines Design? Explain how a complete hull surface is reconstructed from a Table of Offsets. Describe, with the aid of a schematic, the relationship between a station, a waterline, and a buttock. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** During a **lengthening conversion**, a parallel midbody of length $L_m = 24$ m is inserted amidships into a vessel of original $L_{PP} = 120$ m. The original vessel had 21 stations per Lloyd's Rule. **Calculate the new station spacing** and **state the new number of stations** required for the converted vessel, assuming the original station spacing pattern is preserved in the parent-ship fore and aft sections, and new stations are interpolated in the inserted midbody. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks:**

**1. Definition of an Offset** *(2 marks)*
An **offset** (or **co-ordinate**) is the unique three-dimensional Cartesian coordinate $(x_i, y_{i,j}, z_j)$ of a point on the hull surface, defined by the intersection of a transverse **station $i$** (which fixes $x$) and a horizontal **waterline $j$** (which fixes $z$). The variable component is the **half-breadth $y$**, which is the distance from the longitudinal centreline to the hull surface at that point.

**2. Reconstruction of the Hull Surface** *(3 marks)*
The hull is reconstructed by:
- **Step (i):** Establishing a 3D orthogonal grid of $21 \times (n+1)$ gridlines (stations × waterlines).
- **Step (ii):** Reading the $y$-value (half-breadth) at each grid intersection from the Table of Offsets.
- **Step (iii):** Plotting these $(x, y, z)$ triplets in 3D space; the result is a **point cloud** that defines the hull surface.
- **Step (iv):** Joining adjacent points with smooth surface patches (lofted surface, modernly called a NURBS surface).
- The principle is identical to that used in modern 3D CAD, where a hull is stored as a mesh of $(u, v)$ parametric coordinates.

**3. Relationship Between Station, Waterline, and Buttock** *(2 marks)*
- A **station** is a vertical plane $\perp$ to the longitudinal axis.
- A **waterline** is a horizontal plane $\parallel$ to the design waterline.
- A **buttock** is a vertical plane $\parallel$ to the longitudinal centreline.
- The three families are mutually orthogonal. A point on the hull is the intersection of one member of each family: a specific station, a specific waterline, and a specific buttock. The **offset** gives the perpendicular distance from the centreline to the hull along the buttock direction.

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Original Configuration** *(2 marks)*
- Original $L_{PP} = 120$ m
- Original station spacing: $\Delta x_{old} = L_{PP} / 20 = 120/20 = 6$ m
- Original number of stations = 21

**Step 2: New Length After Conversion** *(2 marks)*
$$
L_{PP,new} = L_{PP} + L_m = 120 + 24 = 144 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: New Station Spacing (Preserving Lloyd's Rule)** *(2 marks)*
To maintain the standard Lloyd's rule of 20 intervals across the new length:

$$
\Delta x_{new} = \frac{L_{PP,new}}{20} = \frac{144}{20} = 7.2 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: New Number of Stations** *(1 mark)*
$$
N_{s,new} = \frac{L_{PP,new}}{\Delta x_{new}} + 1 = \frac{144}{7.2} + 1 = 20 + 1 = 21 \text{ stations}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Note:** The total number of stations remains 21 (because the spacing has been *increased* to 7.2 m). The parent-ship stations (originally at 0, 6, 12, ..., 120) are renumbered to 0, 7.2, 14.4, ..., 144, and **no new stations are interpolated** in the inserted midbody. If finer resolution is required in the midbody, an additional 3 to 5 stations can be added locally (giving $N_s = 24$ or $N_s = 26$).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- [ ] **Lines Plan = 3 views**: Profile (Sheer) + Body + Half-Breadth.
- [ ] **Lloyd's Rule of 20** → 21 stations, $\Delta x = L_{PP}/20$.
- [ ] **Station 0 = AP, Station 20 = FP, Station 10 = Amidships (Midship).**
- [ ] **Body Plan Layout:** Aft sections on LEFT, Forward sections on RIGHT of centreline.
- [ ] **Half-Breadth Plan:** Starboard side solid, Port side dashed. Forward on RIGHT.
- [ ] **An Offset** = $(x, y, z)$ point on hull defined by intersection of station + waterline.
- [ ] **Standard WL Spacing:** $\Delta z = D/5$ to $D/6$.
- [ ] **Factor of 2** is essential in area/volume calculations from half-breadths.
- [ ] **Simpson's 1st Rule** uses weights $\{1, 4, 2, 4, 2, \dots, 4, 1\}$ — needs an **odd** number of ordinates.
- [ ] **Displacement** $\nabla = \int_0^{L_{PP}} A(x) dx$ computed by **double Simpson's rule**.
- [ ] **Conversion Implication:** New $L_{PP} = L_{PP} + L_m$; spacing and station count must be re-decided.
- [ ] **Reference Plane Names:** Three mutually perpendicular planes are the **baseline plane** (X-Y), the **centreline plane** (X-Z), and the **midship transverse plane** (Y-Z).
- [ ] **Drawing Sheet Layout:** Profile (top-right), Body (top-left), Half-Breadth (bottom-right), Table of Offsets (bottom-left).
- [ ] **Fairing** = smoothing of all curves so that adjacent sections and waterlines are mutually consistent.
- [ ] **Modern CAD Equivalence:** The Lines Plan is the 2D analog of a 3D NURBS hull surface in modern software (Maxsurf, Rhino, NAPA).
- [ ] **Moulded dimensions** are used in the Lines Plan (not the as-built, or "shell plate", dimensions).

> **End of Module 3 — Conventional Method of Lines Design Notes**
> *(Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Examination Pattern, NEP 2020 Outcomes, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy Levels L1–L4)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propeller Clearances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Propeller Clearances

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Propeller Clearances** are the carefully engineered spatial dimensions maintained between the rotating propeller assembly and the adjacent hull structure, appendages, rudder, baseline (keel), and the sea bed, in order to ensure hydrodynamic efficiency, structural safety, cavitation-free operation, and adequate accessibility for inspection, maintenance, and class survey.

In the context of **Ship Conversion Technology**, propeller clearances represent one of the most decisive geometric constraints imposed on any major conversion, re-engining, or hull form modification project. When a ship is converted (e.g., re-engined, lengthened, or fitted with a new bulbous bow), the new propulsion characteristics — including the propeller diameter $D$, the new design draft $T$, the new block coefficient $C_B$, and the new service speed $V$ — frequently demand a re-validation of the after-body geometry.

The principal propeller clearances considered by **Classification Societies** (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, Bureau Veritas, IRS) and ship designers are:

1. **Tip Clearance to Hull Plating ($C_{TH}$)** — vertical/horizontal distance from the propeller tip to the closest hull surface.
2. **Tip Clearance to Baseline / Keel ($C_{TB}$)** — vertical distance from the propeller tip to the baseline of the ship.
3. **Propeller-to-Rudder Clearance ($C_{PR}$)** — longitudinal spacing between the propeller disc plane and the leading edge of the rudder.
4. **Shaft Line Clearance to Hull ($C_{SH}$)** — clearance between the propeller shaft (including the boss) and the hull plating or shaft bossing.
5. **Skeg / Stern Frame Clearance** — lateral distance from the propeller tip to the nearest skeg, side strut, or stern frame web.
6. **Propeller-to-Sea Bed Clearance ($C_{SG}$)** — vertical distance from the propeller tip to the harbour/sea bed at the design operating draft.
7. **Appendage Clearance** — distance between the propeller tip and any appendage such as a stabilizer fin, bilge keel, or sonar dome.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Ceiling Fan in a Confined Room**

Imagine installing a large ceiling fan inside a small, low-ceiling room. If the fan blades come too close to the walls, the air swirls chaotically, the motor vibrates, dust accumulates, and the fan cannot be cleaned without dismantling the ceiling. Similarly, when a ship's propeller rotates at high RPM in a region surrounded by hull plating, rudders, and sea water, insufficient clearances cause:

- **Cavitation** (analogous to the "rushing wind" of a poorly placed fan)
- **Hull vibration** (vibrating motor analogy)
- **Efficiency loss** (swirling, non-uniform flow)
- **Maintenance nightmares** (cannot inspect the blade tips)

**Propeller clearances are therefore the "breathing space" that allows the propeller to do its job efficiently, safely, and inspectably.**

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The governing physical metric is the **Propeller Diameter $D$** (in metres). All clearances in shipbuilding are expressed as **dimensionless ratios of $D$**, because ships range from 5 m harbour tugs to 400 m ULCCs, and a single absolute millimetre value is meaningless across this size spectrum.

| Constant | Symbol | Typical Range | Standard Body |
|----------|--------|---------------|----------------|
| Propeller Diameter | $D$ | 1.5 m – 10 m | All classes |
| Design Draft | $T$ | 0.05 m – 25 m | All classes |
| Block Coefficient | $C_B$ | 0.55 – 0.90 | All classes |
| Tip Clearance to Hull | $C_{TH}/D$ | **0.20 – 0.30** | DNV / LR / ABS |
| Tip Clearance to Baseline | $C_{TB}/D$ | **0.15 – 0.20** | DNV / LR / ABS |
| Propeller-to-Rudder | $C_{PR}/D$ | **0.20 – 0.30** | LR Pt 4 Ch 8 |
| Aft Peak to Propeller | $L_{AP}/D$ | 0.25 – 0.40 | Practical design |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU Module 3, students are expected to (a) define each clearance, (b) recall the empirical ratio, and (c) explain the hydrodynamic and structural consequences of violating the minimum clearance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Clearances Matter

The propeller operates in a **non-uniform wake field** behind the ship's hull. The flow entering the propeller disc is the product of the ship's forward speed and the wake fraction $w$:

$$
V_A \;=\; V \; (1 - w)
$$

where:
- $V_A$ = speed of advance of the propeller (m/s)
- $V$ = ship service speed (m/s)
- $w$ = Taylor wake fraction (typically 0.20 – 0.45 for single-ship, lower for fine hulls)

When the propeller tip is too close to the hull, the local wake field becomes even more distorted, leading to:

- **Increased pressure fluctuations** on the hull plating → fatigue cracks
- **Sheet cavitation** on the blade tips → erosion of hull paint, vibration
- **Inefficient thrust conversion** → loss of 2 – 5 % propulsive efficiency
- **Propeller-induced excitation of hull resonances** → structural failure

### 2.2 The Six Critical Clearances — Step-by-Step Logic

**Clearance 1 — Propeller Tip to Hull Plating ($C_{TH}$)**

- **Why:** Prevents the blade tip from "singing" against the hull plating and limits the boundary layer ingestion.
- **Logic:** The tip vortex must develop fully before striking the hull. Viscous diffusion requires distance.
- **Empirical Rule:**

$$
C_{TH} \;\geq\; 0.20 \, D \quad \text{(Minimum for high-power single-screw ships)}
$$

$$
C_{TH} \;\geq\; 0.25 \, D \quad \text{(DNV recommended for unrestricted service)}
$$

**Clearance 2 — Propeller Tip to Baseline / Keel ($C_{TB}$)**

- **Why:** Prevents the propeller from striking the sea bed in shallow water, dry-dock blocks, or harbour approaches.
- **Logic:** The propeller must clear the dock floor even with the ship fully ballasted down to its **Load Line draft**.
- **Empirical Rule:**

$$
C_{TB} \;\geq\; 0.15 \, D \quad \text{(minimum, in fully loaded condition)}
$$

**Clearance 3 — Propeller to Rudder ($C_{PR}$)**

- **Why:** Allows the rudder to operate in the **propeller slipstream** without being blanketed by cavitating tip vortices. Closer is better for manoeuvrability, but too close causes cavitation erosion on the rudder leading edge.
- **Logic:** The slipstream contract diameter $D_s \approx 0.70 D$ defines the effective propeller disc the rudder must be downstream of.

$$
C_{PR} \;\geq\; 0.20 \, D \quad \text{(Lloyd's Pt 4, Ch 8)}
$$

**Clearance 4 — Shaft Bossing Clearance ($C_{SB}$)**

- **Why:** Allows fairing of the shaft exit point; prevents line-shaft whipping vibration.
- **Logic:** Bossing internal radius $R_b$ should be $\geq 1.5 \times$ shaft diameter.
- **Empirical Rule:**

$$
R_b \;\geq\; 1.5 \, d_s
$$

where $d_s$ = propeller shaft diameter.

**Clearance 5 — Stern Frame / Skeg to Propeller Tip ($C_{SF}$)**

- **Why:** In twin-screw ships with a centerline skeg, the side propeller must clear the skeg face.
- **Logic:** Prevents cavitation erosion on the skeg, and provides for class survey.

$$
C_{SF} \;\geq\; 0.30 \, D \quad \text{(ABS Guide for Twin-Screw vessels)}
$$

**Clearance 6 — Propeller Tip to Sea Bed ($C_{SG}$)**

- **Why:** Operational safety; in shallow ports, channels, and locks, the propeller must not strike the bottom.
- **Logic:** Calculated at **maximum operating draft** $T_{max}$:

$$
C_{SG} \;=\; H_{water} \;-\; T_{max} \;-\; z_{tip}
$$

where $H_{water}$ = water depth at berth, $T_{max}$ = maximum draft, $z_{tip}$ = vertical distance from baseline to propeller tip.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Clearance | Empirical Formula | Source / Class | Units | Remarks |
|---|-----------|-------------------|----------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Tip to Hull | $C_{TH} \;\geq\; 0.20\,D$ | DNV Pt 3 Ch 3 | m | High power FPP |
| 2 | Tip to Hull (large ships) | $C_{TH} \;\geq\; 0.25\,D$ | DNV Pt 3 Ch 3 | m | $D > 8$ m |
| 3 | Tip to Baseline | $C_{TB} \;\geq\; 0.15\,D$ | LR Pt 4 Ch 8 | m | At scantling draft |
| 4 | Propeller to Rudder | $C_{PR} \;\geq\; 0.20\,D$ | LR Pt 4 Ch 8 | m | Min for FPP |
| 5 | Tip to Rudder (alternative) | $C_{PR} \;\geq\; 0.30\,D$ | DNV | m | Cavitation-free |
| 6 | Aft Peak Length | $L_{AP} \;\geq\; 0.25\,D$ | Practical | m | Stern frame position |
| 7 | Bossing radius | $R_b \;\geq\; 1.5\,d_s$ | LR Pt 4 | m | Shaft clearance |
| 8 | Skeg to Tip (twin) | $C_{SF} \;\geq\; 0.30\,D$ | ABS | m | Twin-screw |
| 9 | Sea-bed clearance | $C_{SG} \;=\; H_w - T_{max} - z_{tip}$ | Operational | m | Shallow water |
| 10 | Wake fraction | $w \;\approx\; 0.5\,C_B - 0.05$ | Taylor | — | Single-screw V-form |
| 11 | Thrust deduction | $t \;\approx\; 0.6\,w$ | Taylor | — | Single-screw |
| 12 | Slipstream contraction | $D_s \;\approx\; 0.70\,D$ | Empirical | m | Behind propeller |
| 13 | Block coefficient | $C_B \;=\; \nabla \,/\,(L \, B \, T)$ | Standard | — | Hull fullness |
| 14 | Prismatic coefficient | $C_P \;=\; \nabla \,/\,(A_M \, L)$ | Standard | — | After-body shape |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember:** The *why* behind each formula is worth more marks in KTU exams than blind substitution. Always state the *hydrodynamic reason* first, then the number.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Propeller clearance engineering is critical in:

- **Ship Conversion:** When re-engining, the new MCR may require a larger $D$, demanding stern modifications.
- **Shallow-Water Operations:** River-sea vessels and VLCCs in partially loaded channels.
- **Naval Architecture:** Cavitation tunnel testing, EFD validation.
- **Port Design:** Determining dredging depth for new terminals.
- **Offshore Wind & Towing:** Where manoeuvrability requires a close-coupled rudder.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Propeller Clearance Verification for a Bulk Carrier

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example Statement (KTU 2024 Style):**
> A Panamax bulk carrier is being converted with a re-engining project. The new propeller diameter is $D = 6.20 \, \text{m}$. The design draft (scantling) is $T = 14.50 \, \text{m}$. The block coefficient is $C_B = 0.82$. The ship is single-screw, with FPP and a high-block hull form. The water depth at the intended loading terminal is $H_w = 17.5 \, \text{m}$. The propeller is positioned with its centreline at vertical distance $z_c = 5.40 \, \text{m}$ above the baseline.
>
> **Required:** Verify all six propeller clearances and confirm class compliance.

#### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Clearance 1: Tip to Hull ($C_{TH}$)

The propeller tip is at a vertical distance from the centreline of:
- $z_{tip} = D/2 = 6.20/2 = 3.10 \, \text{m}$ from the propeller centreline.
- The hull plating at the propeller location (counter-stern) is approximately $z_{hull} = 2.50 \, \text{m}$ above the propeller centreline (from the lines plan, station 0).

Therefore the actual tip-to-hull clearance is:
$$
C_{TH} \;\text{(actual)} \;=\; z_{hull} \;-\; \frac{D}{2} \;=\; 2.50 \;-\; 3.10 \;=\; -0.60 \, \text{m}
$$

**This is a negative clearance — the propeller would strike the hull!**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Decision:** The conversion project must either (a) increase the propeller centreline height by ≥ 0.85 m by re-cutting the stern, or (b) reduce the propeller diameter. This is the central engineering trade-off in ship conversion.

Let us redesign: place the propeller centreline at $z_c = 5.40 \, \text{m}$ above baseline. The hull counter at that station now has $z_{hull} = 5.40 + 2.50 = 7.90 \, \text{m}$ above baseline. Re-check:

$$
C_{TH} \;\text{(new)} \;=\; \left(z_c + z_{hull,\text{local}}\right) \;-\; z_c \;-\; \frac{D}{2}
$$

In a coordinate system where $z = 0$ is the propeller centreline:

$$
C_{TH,\text{new}} \;=\; 2.50 \;-\; 3.10 \;+\; 0.90 \;=\; 0.30 \, \text{m}
$$

Wait — let me restart the geometry cleanly. Using absolute heights above baseline:

- Propeller tip **lowest** point: $z_{tip,\text{low}} = z_c - D/2 = 5.40 - 3.10 = 2.30 \, \text{m}$ above baseline.
- Propeller tip **highest** point: $z_{tip,\text{high}} = z_c + D/2 = 5.40 + 3.10 = 8.50 \, \text{m}$ above baseline.
- Hull counter (lowest) at propeller station: $z_{hull} = 8.20 \, \text{m}$ above baseline (from lines plan).

Tip-to-hull clearance = $z_{hull} - z_{tip,\text{high}} = 8.20 - 8.50 = -0.30 \, \text{m}$.

**Still negative — the conversion is geometrically impossible without hull modification.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Conversion Engineering Truth:** In real conversion projects, when the new propeller is too large, the shipyard must **re-cut the stern frame** (a "shoe-piece" extension) to gain the required 0.30 m of clearance. This is one of the most expensive operations in a re-engining project, often costing several million USD.

Let us assume a re-cut stern achieves the following revised geometry:
- Revised hull counter at propeller station: $z_{hull,\text{new}} = 9.00 \, \text{m}$ above baseline.
- Tip-to-hull: $C_{TH} = 9.00 - 8.50 = 0.50 \, \text{m}$.

Now verify against the class rule:
$$
\frac{C_{TH}}{D} \;=\; \frac{0.50}{6.20} \;=\; 0.0806
$$

The DNV minimum is $0.20 D = 0.20 \times 6.20 = 1.24 \, \text{m}$.

**The clearance of 0.50 m is still NON-COMPLIANT with DNV's $0.20D$ rule.**

**Conclusion:** The diameter $D = 6.20$ m cannot be accommodated. The conversion team must downsize the propeller to:
$$
D_{\max} \;=\; \frac{C_{TH,\text{achieved}}}{0.20} \;=\; \frac{0.50}{0.20} \;=\; 2.50 \, \text{m}
$$

This is an absurdly small propeller for a Panamax bulk carrier. The original hull is simply too small for this diameter. **The conversion project would be rejected at this stage.**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** The 0.20D rule is a hard rule for unrestricted service. Some classification societies allow a relaxation to 0.15D for restricted-service vessels, but NEVER below 0.12D under any circumstances.

#### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Clearance 2: Tip to Baseline ($C_{TB}$)

The lowest tip point is at $z_{tip,\text{low}} = 2.30 \, \text{m}$ above baseline.

$$
C_{TB} \;=\; 2.30 \, \text{m}
$$

$$
\frac{C_{TB}}{D} \;=\; \frac{2.30}{6.20} \;=\; 0.371
$$

Class rule: $C_{TB}/D \geq 0.15$, so $0.371 \geq 0.15$ — **COMPLIANT** by a large margin.

#### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Clearance 3: Propeller to Rudder ($C_{PR}$)

From the general arrangement, the rudder leading edge is located $2.40 \, \text{m}$ aft of the propeller disc plane.

$$
C_{PR} \;=\; 2.40 \, \text{m}
$$

$$
\frac{C_{PR}}{D} \;=\; \frac{2.40}{6.20} \;=\; 0.387
$$

Class rule: $C_{PR}/D \geq 0.20$, so $0.387 \geq 0.20$ — **COMPLIANT**.

#### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Sea-Bed Clearance ($C_{SG}$)

Using the formula:
$$
C_{SG} \;=\; H_w \;-\; T_{\max} \;-\; z_{tip,\text{low}}
$$

Substituting:
$$
C_{SG} \;=\; 17.5 \;-\; 14.5 \;-\; 2.30 \;=\; 0.70 \, \text{m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Rule of Thumb:** The sea-bed clearance should be at least $0.10 D$ for safe operation. Here $0.10 D = 0.62 \, \text{m}$, and $C_{SG} = 0.70 \, \text{m} \geq 0.62 \, \text{m}$ — **MARGINALLY COMPLIANT**, but the port should be informed that this vessel can only enter the terminal at high tide.

#### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Wake Fraction (Taylor's formula for single-screw)

$$
w \;=\; 0.5 \, C_B \;-\; 0.05
$$

Substituting $C_B = 0.82$:
$$
w \;=\; 0.5 \times 0.82 \;-\; 0.05 \;=\; 0.41 \;-\; 0.05 \;=\; 0.36
$$

This high wake fraction is typical of a full-form bulk carrier and confirms the high block coefficient.

#### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Thrust Deduction (Taylor)

$$
t \;=\; 0.6 \, w \;=\; 0.6 \times 0.36 \;=\; 0.216
$$

This is used in the **hull efficiency calculation**:
$$
\eta_H \;=\; \frac{1 - t}{1 - w} \;=\; \frac{1 - 0.216}{1 - 0.36} \;=\; \frac{0.784}{0.640} \;=\; 1.225
$$

(The hull efficiency > 1 because the wake is non-uniform, with higher wake at the top of the disc.)

### 3.2 Summary Matrix — Conversion Decision Sheet

| # | Clearance | Calculated | Required (Class) | Status | Action |
|---|-----------|-----------|------------------|--------|--------|
| 1 | $C_{TH}/D$ (tip-hull) | 0.0806 | $\geq 0.20$ | ❌ FAIL | Re-cut stern or reduce $D$ |
| 2 | $C_{TB}/D$ (tip-baseline) | 0.371 | $\geq 0.15$ | ✅ PASS | None |
| 3 | $C_{PR}/D$ (prop-rudder) | 0.387 | $\geq 0.20$ | ✅ PASS | None |
| 4 | $C_{SG}$ (sea-bed) | 0.70 m | $\geq 0.10D = 0.62$ m | ⚠️ MARGINAL | Tide restriction |
| 5 | $w$ (wake) | 0.36 | 0.20 – 0.45 | ✅ Normal | None |
| 6 | $t$ (thrust deduction) | 0.216 | 0.10 – 0.25 | ✅ Normal | None |

### 3.3 Final Conversion Verdict

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The propeller tip-to-hull clearance failure is the **single largest conversion-blocker**. The conversion project must:
> 1. Reduce $D$ to $\leq 2.50$ m (impractical), **OR**
> 2. Re-cut the stern to gain an additional $0.74$ m of vertical clearance (expensive), **OR**
> 3. Replace the FPP with a **CPP (Controllable Pitch Propeller)** of smaller diameter, **OR**
> 4. Accept restricted-service class notation (sailing in fair-weather routes only).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Propeller Clearances and Their Interdependencies

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the six propeller clearances, their governing Classification Society rules, the conversion-time decision triggers, and the hydrodynamic consequences. This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** rather than a physical drawing, per KTU schematic requirements.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Ship Conversion Project Initiated]
    nodeB[Re-engining or Hull Modification]
    nodeC[New Propeller Diameter D Selected]
    nodeD[Wake Field Re-calculated]
    nodeE[Tip to Hull Clearance C TH]
    nodeF[Tip to Baseline Clearance C TB]
    nodeG[Propeller to Rudder Clearance C PR]
    nodeH[Shaft Bossing Clearance]
    nodeI[Skeg to Tip Clearance C SF]
    nodeJ[Sea Bed Clearance C SG]
    nodeK[DNV Class Rule C TH over D greater than 0.20]
    nodeL[LR Class Rule C PR over D greater than 0.20]
    nodeM[Operational Rule C SG over 0.10 D]
    nodeN[Conversion Compliant]
    nodeO[Conversion Non Compliant]
    nodeP[Stern Frame Re Cut]
    nodeQ[Reduce Propeller Diameter]
    nodeR[Accept Restricted Service Notation]
    nodeS[Hull Cavitation Erosion]
    nodeT[Loss of Propulsive Efficiency]
    nodeU[Shaft Whipping Vibration]
    nodeV[Ground Strike Risk in Shallow Water]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeD --> nodeI
    nodeD --> nodeJ
    nodeE --> nodeK
    nodeG --> nodeL
    nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeK --> nodeN
    nodeK --> nodeO
    nodeL --> nodeN
    nodeL --> nodeO
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeM --> nodeO
    nodeO --> nodeP
    nodeO --> nodeQ
    nodeO --> nodeR
    nodeE -. Inadequate .-> nodeS
    nodeE -. Inadequate .-> nodeT
    nodeH -. Inadequate .-> nodeU
    nodeJ -. Inadequate .-> nodeV
    nodeP --> nodeA
    nodeQ --> nodeA
    nodeR --> nodeN
```

### 4.2 Companion Block Diagram — Stern Region Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Hull Plating Above Propeller]
    nodeB[Propeller Disc Plane]
    nodeC[Propeller Centreline at Height zc]
    nodeD[Propeller Tip Lowest Point]
    nodeE[Propeller Tip Highest Point]
    nodeF[Rudder Leading Edge Aft]
    nodeG[Stern Frame / Skeg]
    nodeH[Shaft Bossing Exit]
    nodeI[Baseline Keel Plate]
    nodeJ[Sea Bed Level]

    nodeA -- C TH gap --> nodeE
    nodeB -- C PR distance --> nodeF
    nodeB -- C SF distance --> nodeG
    nodeC -- D over 2 --> nodeD
    nodeC -- D over 2 --> nodeE
    nodeH -- R b over 1.5 ds --> nodeA
    nodeD -- C TB distance --> nodeI
    nodeI -- C SG distance --> nodeJ
```

### 4.3 Diagram Interpretation Guide

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read the diagrams for KTU exams:**

1. The first diagram is a **decision-flow schematic**. Use it when answering "What happens if a clearance fails?" type questions.
2. The second diagram is a **geometric map**. Use it when answering "Define the clearance $X$" type questions in Part A.

Both diagrams use the alphanumeric node ID convention required by KTU schematic guidelines.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
Define the term **Propeller Tip Clearance to Hull** ($C_{TH}$) and state the minimum value prescribed by **DNV** for an unrestricted-service single-screw merchant vessel.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Propeller Tip Clearance to Hull ($C_{TH}$) is the perpendicular distance measured between the **highest point of the propeller tip circle** and the **lowest point of the hull plating (or counter-stern)** in the propeller disc region. It governs the boundary-layer interaction between the blade tip vortex and the hull surface.
>
> For unrestricted-service single-screw vessels, **DNV Pt 3 Ch 3** prescribes:
>
> $$C_{TH} \;\geq\; 0.20 \, D$$
>
> where $D$ is the propeller diameter in metres. For vessels with $D > 8$ m, a stricter rule of $0.25 D$ is recommended. **(3 marks)**

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
Why is it necessary to maintain a **minimum propeller-to-rudder clearance** in single-screw ships? State the empirical rule from **Lloyd's Register**.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The propeller-to-rudder clearance ($C_{PR}$) is the longitudinal distance from the **propeller disc plane** to the **leading edge of the rudder**. The clearance is necessary for the following reasons:
>
> 1. **Hydrodynamic:** Allows the rudder to operate inside the **propeller slipstream** (contracted diameter $\approx 0.70 D$), maximising turning forces. Insufficient clearance causes the rudder leading edge to be hit by tip vortices, inducing cavitation erosion.
> 2. **Structural:** Prevents the rudder stock and propeller blade from physically contacting during hard-over manoeuvres.
> 3. **Maintenance:** Provides access for class survey of the rudder bearing and propeller hub.
>
> **Lloyd's Register Pt 4, Chapter 8** prescribes:
>
> $$C_{PR} \;\geq\; 0.20 \, D$$
>
> **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has TWO sub-parts, each carrying 7 marks. The internal choice requires students to attempt either **Question A** or **Question B** in full.

#### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled schematic, explain the **six principal propeller clearances** required in a single-screw merchant ship. For each clearance, state the empirical ratio with respect to the propeller diameter $D$ and identify the relevant classification society rule. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Handymax bulk carrier is being converted with a new engine requiring a propeller of diameter $D = 5.40 \, \text{m}$. The design draft is $T = 11.20 \, \text{m}$. The propeller centreline is at $z_c = 4.80 \, \text{m}$ above the baseline. The hull counter (lowest point) at the propeller station is at $z_{hull} = 7.65 \, \text{m}$ above the baseline. The rudder leading edge is $1.80 \, \text{m}$ aft of the propeller disc plane. The intended loading terminal has water depth $H_w = 14.0 \, \text{m}$.
>
> **Calculate and verify against class rules:**
> 1. Propeller tip-to-hull clearance
> 2. Propeller tip-to-baseline clearance
> 3. Propeller-to-rudder clearance
> 4. Propeller tip-to-sea-bed clearance
>
> Use DNV rules. State clearly whether the conversion is class-compliant. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Six Clearances:** Provide the definitions, the empirical ratios, and the rule sources as in the SECTION 1 and SECTION 2 of these notes. Key ratios to state:

- $C_{TH}/D \geq 0.20$ (DNV)
- $C_{TB}/D \geq 0.15$ (LR)
- $C_{PR}/D \geq 0.20$ (LR)
- $C_{SF}/D \geq 0.30$ (ABS, twin-screw)
- $C_{SG} \geq 0.10 D$ (operational)
- $R_b \geq 1.5 d_s$ (shaft bossing)

Include the labelled Mermaid-style block diagram showing the geometric layout (refer SECTION 4).

**(b) Step-by-step calculation:**

**Step 1 — Tip-to-hull clearance $C_{TH}$:**

- Propeller tip highest point: $z_{tip,high} = z_c + D/2 = 4.80 + 2.70 = 7.50 \, \text{m}$
- Hull counter lowest point: $z_{hull} = 7.65 \, \text{m}$
- Clearance: $C_{TH} = z_{hull} - z_{tip,high} = 7.65 - 7.50 = 0.15 \, \text{m}$
- Ratio: $C_{TH}/D = 0.15 / 5.40 = 0.0278$
- DNV requires $\geq 0.20 D = 1.08 \, \text{m}$
- **Status: ❌ NON-COMPLIANT (grossly insufficient)**

**Step 2 — Tip-to-baseline $C_{TB}$:**

- Propeller tip lowest point: $z_{tip,low} = z_c - D/2 = 4.80 - 2.70 = 2.10 \, \text{m}$
- Clearance: $C_{TB} = 2.10 \, \text{m}$
- Ratio: $C_{TB}/D = 2.10 / 5.40 = 0.389$
- LR requires $\geq 0.15 D = 0.81 \, \text{m}$
- **Status: ✅ COMPLIANT**

**Step 3 — Propeller-to-rudder $C_{PR}$:**

- Clearance: $C_{PR} = 1.80 \, \text{m}$
- Ratio: $C_{PR}/D = 1.80 / 5.40 = 0.333$
- LR requires $\geq 0.20 D = 1.08 \, \text{m}$
- **Status: ✅ COMPLIANT**

**Step 4 — Tip-to-sea-bed $C_{SG}$:**

$$
C_{SG} \;=\; H_w \;-\; T \;-\; z_{tip,low} \;=\; 14.0 \;-\; 11.2 \;-\; 2.10 \;=\; 0.70 \, \text{m}
$$

- Operational rule requires $\geq 0.10 D = 0.54 \, \text{m}$
- **Status: ✅ COMPLIANT (marginally)**

**Final Verdict:** The **tip-to-hull clearance is the only failing parameter**. The conversion is NOT class-compliant without stern modification. The team must re-cut the stern to gain an additional $\approx 0.93 \, \text{m}$ of vertical clearance at the propeller station. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches how **cavitation erosion**, **hull vibration**, and **propulsive efficiency** are each affected by inadequate propeller tip-to-hull clearance. In your answer, derive the relationship between wake fraction $w$ and the **block coefficient $C_B$** for single-screw ships. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a single-screw container ship with $C_B = 0.65$, propeller diameter $D = 7.20 \, \text{m}$, and design speed $V = 24 \, \text{knots}$, calculate:
> 1. The wake fraction $w$
> 2. The thrust deduction $t$
> 3. The speed of advance $V_A$
> 4. The minimum tip-to-hull clearance as per DNV
>
> Comment on the adequacy of this clearance for unrestricted ocean service. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Effects of inadequate tip-to-hull clearance:**

- **Cavitation erosion:** When the blade tip vortex is too close to the hull, the low-pressure core of the vortex impinges on the hull paint, causing local cavitation collapse erosion. The hull surface develops pitting and roughness, leading to a 1–2 % increase in frictional resistance over the service life.
- **Hull vibration:** The pressure fluctuations from the tip vortex are transmitted through the hull plating as **forced vibrations**. If the excitation frequency coincides with a hull natural frequency (typically 5–25 Hz for merchant ships), structural resonance can crack weld seams. The rule of thumb is to keep the tip at least $0.20 D$ away to limit the forcing function amplitude.
- **Propulsive efficiency:** Insufficient clearance causes the propeller to operate in a **distorted, non-uniform wake**, reducing the effective advance coefficient $J$ and increasing the rotative coefficient $K_Q$. The overall propulsive efficiency $\eta_D$ drops by 2–5 %.

**Derivation of $w$ vs $C_B$ (Taylor's empirical formula):**

The wake fraction is defined as:
$$
w \;=\; \frac{V - V_A}{V} \;=\; 1 - \frac{V_A}{V}
$$

Taylor's empirical relation for single-screw ships is:
$$
w \;=\; 0.5 \, C_B \;-\; 0.05
$$

The thrust deduction factor is then:
$$
t \;=\; 0.6 \, w
$$

**Justification (qualitative):** Higher block coefficient (fuller hull) → larger wake volume behind the stern → higher wake fraction. The minus 0.05 term accounts for the mean of empirical data. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical calculation:**

Given: $C_B = 0.65$, $D = 7.20 \, \text{m}$, $V = 24 \, \text{knots} = 24 \times 0.5144 = 12.35 \, \text{m/s}$.

**Step 1 — Wake fraction $w$:**
$$
w \;=\; 0.5 \times 0.65 \;-\; 0.05 \;=\; 0.325 \;-\; 0.05 \;=\; 0.275
$$

**Step 2 — Thrust deduction $t$:**
$$
t \;=\; 0.6 \times 0.275 \;=\; 0.165
$$

**Step 3 — Speed of advance $V_A$:**
$$
V_A \;=\; V \, (1 - w) \;=\; 12.35 \times (1 - 0.275) \;=\; 12.35 \times 0.725 \;=\; 8.95 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum tip-to-hull clearance (DNV):**
$$
C_{TH,\min} \;=\; 0.20 \times D \;=\; 0.20 \times 7.20 \;=\; 1.44 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Conversion to knots of $V_A$ (sanity check):**
$$
V_A \;=\; \frac{8.95}{0.5144} \;=\; 17.40 \, \text{knots}
$$

**Comment on adequacy:** The minimum tip-to-hull clearance of **1.44 m** is appropriate for a container ship of moderate $C_B = 0.65$ in unrestricted ocean service. Container ships have fine hull forms (lower $C_B$) and operate at high speeds, generating strong hull-pressure fluctuations. The $0.20 D$ rule provides a margin against:
- Wake-induced vibration at the propeller disc
- Tip vortex cavitation
- Class survey access

For vessels with $D > 8$ m or in heavy-weather service, a stricter $0.25 D = 1.80 \, \text{m}$ would be advisable. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the units:** $C_{TH}/D$ is **dimensionless**; $C_{TH}$ alone is in metres. Students often write "$C_{TH} \geq 0.20$" without stating the dimensionless ratio. **Deduct 0.5 mark.**
>
> 2. **Mixing DNV and LR rules:** DNV uses $0.25 D$ for large propellers; LR uses $0.20 D$ for restricted service. Always **state the rule source** explicitly.
>
> 3. **Sign error in clearance subtraction:** The tip-to-hull clearance is $z_{hull} - z_{tip,high}$. A common error is computing $z_{tip} - z_{hull}$ and getting a negative number "from the wrong side." **Deduct 1 mark.**
>
> 4. **Confusing tip-to-hull with tip-to-baseline:** These are **two different clearances**. Tip-to-baseline is the more lenient ($0.15 D$); tip-to-hull is the stricter ($0.20 D$). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the conversion context:** A question on "ship conversion" expects the student to discuss **what changes during conversion** — not just the static rules. Always mention re-engining, stern re-cut, or diameter reduction as the conversion decision.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the wake-thrust relationship:** The hull efficiency formula $\eta_H = (1-t)/(1-w)$ is the bridge between propeller clearances and overall ship performance. Examiners love to test it.
>
> 7. **Skipping the units check in the sea-bed formula:** The formula $C_{SG} = H_w - T - z_{tip,low}$ requires all four variables in **metres**. Mixing metres and feet loses 1 mark.
>
> 8. **No labelled diagram in Part B (a):** KTU examiners deduct up to 2 marks if a "labelled sketch" is requested but not provided. Use the Mermaid block diagram from SECTION 4 as a guide.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Propeller Clearances**

- [ ] **Six clearances to memorise:** Tip-to-Hull, Tip-to-Baseline, Propeller-to-Rudder, Shaft-Bossing, Skeg-to-Tip, Sea-Bed.
- [ ] **Key empirical ratios:** $C_{TH}/D \geq 0.20$, $C_{TB}/D \geq 0.15$, $C_{PR}/D \geq 0.20$, $C_{SF}/D \geq 0.30$, $C_{SG} \geq 0.10 D$.
- [ ] **Shaft bossing rule:** $R_b \geq 1.5 d_s$.
- [ ] **Wake fraction (Taylor):** $w = 0.5 C_B - 0.05$ (single-screw).
- [ ] **Thrust deduction:** $t = 0.6 w$.
- [ ] **Hull efficiency:** $\eta_H = (1-t)/(1-w)$.
- [ ] **Slipstream contraction:** $D_s \approx 0.70 D$.
- [ ] **Conversion triggers:** Re-engining, lengthening, hull-form change — all require re-validation of $C_{TH}$.
- [ ] **Stern re-cut is expensive:** Often 5–10 % of total conversion cost.
- [ ] **Sea-bed clearance uses maximum draft, not design draft.**
- [ ] **Restricted-service vessels may relax $C_{TH}/D$ to 0.15 (DNV).**
- [ ] **Always state the Classification Society rule source explicitly in KTU answers.**
- [ ] **Use the Mermaid block diagram from SECTION 4 as the standard "labelled sketch" answer.**
- [ ] **Sign convention:** Clearances are positive when the geometry is feasible. Negative clearance = conversion blocker.
- [ ] **Operational clearance $C_{SG}$ is governed by water depth, not class rules.**

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** The propeller tip-to-hull clearance $C_{TH}/D \geq 0.20$ is the **single most-tested rule** in Module 3. Memorise it as a dimensionless ratio, state the rule source (DNV Pt 3 Ch 3), and always link it to the conversion context.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propeller Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Propeller Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — PROPELLER DESIGN
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Propeller Design** is the systematic engineering process of determining the geometry (diameter, pitch, blade area, blade section shape, thickness distribution, and rake/skew) of a marine screw propeller such that it converts the rotational mechanical power delivered by the main engine into a useful thrust force at a prescribed ship speed, while satisfying constraints on efficiency, cavitation, vibration, and structural strength.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context (under Ship Conversion Technology), propeller design is treated as the final design step of a **conversion project** where an existing hull is paired with a re-rated engine, and the propeller must be matched to the new EHP/SHP and service profile. The governing reference is the **Wageningen B-Series** (also called the **Troost Series**) for open-water characteristics of series propellers, supplemented by lifting-line and lifting-surface corrections for heavily loaded or non-standard designs.

### 1.2 Fundamental Geometry of a Marine Propeller

A propeller is a **rotating hydrofoil system** consisting of blades attached to a hub. The principal geometric descriptors are:

- **D (Diameter)** — Tip-to-tip diameter measured at the propeller centreline. Typical range for merchant ships: **3 m – 10 m**.
- **Z (Number of Blades)** — Usually 3, 4, 5, or 6; higher Z improves efficiency and reduces cavitation but raises cost and weight.
- **P/D (Pitch Ratio)** — Pitch of the propeller divided by its diameter. Merchant ship values: **0.5 – 1.2**.
- **EAR (Expanded Area Ratio)** — Ratio of the total developed (expanded) blade area $A_E$ to the propeller disc area $A_0 = \pi D^2/4$. Typical range: **0.30 – 1.05**.
- **Rake** — Aftward (positive) or forward tilt of the blade measured at the tip, in degrees.
- **Skew** — Angular offset of the blade mid-chord line to reduce vibration and cavitation.

### 1.3 Real-World Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Underwater Windmill:**
> Imagine a windmill. A windmill converts the linear flow of air into rotational power. A marine propeller does the **opposite**: it takes rotational power from the engine and pushes water backward (Newton's Third Law), generating a forward thrust on the ship — exactly as a screw pulls itself into a piece of wood. The faster the screw turns, the harder it "bites" into the water. Propeller design is the art of choosing the right "bite" — not too shallow (which wastes power in slipstream), not too deep (which causes cavitation bubbles and erosion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Trade-off (KTU Board Favourite):**
> A propeller must deliver a required **thrust (T)** at a given **advance speed (V_A)** without cavitation, with the **highest possible open-water efficiency (η₀)**. Designers balance three conflicting parameters: **diameter, RPM, and blade area**. Larger diameter = higher efficiency but risk of hull clearance issues and cavitation at the tip. Higher RPM = smaller, cheaper propeller but lower efficiency and tip-speed cavitation.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants Used

| Symbol | Quantity | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\rho$ | Density of sea water | **1025 kg/m³** (standard) |
| $\rho$ | Density of fresh water | **1000 kg/m³** |
| $\nu$ | Kinematic viscosity of sea water | $\approx 1.05 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s |
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | **9.81 m/s²** |
| 1 knot | Ship speed unit | **0.5144 m/s** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Environment of a Propeller

A propeller operating behind a ship does **not** see the ship's speed $V_S$ directly. It operates in a complex three-dimensional flow field shaped by the hull boundary layer. The key corrections are:

- **Wake Fraction (w):** Fraction of ship speed "lost" to the hull boundary layer. The wake is **non-uniform** across the propeller disc, but for design purposes an effective or Taylor wake fraction is used.
  $$V_A = V_S \, (1 - w)$$

- **Thrust Deduction (t):** Because the propeller accelerates flow into the rotor, the pressure on the hull stern drops, increasing hull resistance. The total thrust the propeller must produce therefore exceeds the bare-hull resistance.
  $$T = \frac{R}{1 - t}$$
  where $R$ is the bare-hull resistance at ship speed $V_S$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Context:** When a ship is **converted** (e.g., lengthened, re-engined, re-shaped), the wake and thrust deduction must be **re-estimated** from model tests or CFD. KTU expects students to recognise that propeller matching in a conversion project begins with re-deriving $w$ and $t$, not from the original design.

### 2.2 Non-Dimensional Coefficients (The "Big Three")

These are the **three pillars** of propeller open-water characteristics. All KTU numerical problems are solved in this non-dimensional framework.

- **Advance Coefficient (J):** Measure of how fast the ship moves relative to the propeller rotation.
  $$J = \frac{V_A}{nD} = \frac{V_S (1-w)}{nD}$$

- **Thrust Coefficient (K_T):**
  $$K_T = \frac{T}{\rho \, n^2 \, D^4}$$

- **Torque Coefficient (K_Q):**
  $$K_Q = \frac{Q}{\rho \, n^2 \, D^5} = \frac{Q}{\rho \, n^2 \, D^5}$$
  where $Q$ is the delivered torque at the propeller shaft.

### 2.3 Open-Water Efficiency

The ideal open-water efficiency of a propeller — the figure of merit students must maximise — is:
$$\eta_0 = \frac{T \cdot V_A}{2 \pi n Q} = \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q}$$

In practice, behind-hull efficiency is slightly higher due to wake augmentation (the propeller sees $V_A < V_S$, so the same $T$ is more useful).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TABLE A — MASTER FORMULA SHEET (keep this in your exam memory)**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Advance speed | $V_A = V_S (1-w)$ | m/s |  |
| 2 | Required thrust | $T = R / (1-t)$ | N |  |
| 3 | Advance coefficient | $J = V_A / (nD)$ | dimensionless |  |
| 4 | Thrust coefficient | $K_T = T / (\rho n^2 D^4)$ | dimensionless |  |
| 5 | Torque coefficient | $K_Q = Q / (\rho n^2 D^5)$ | dimensionless |  |
| 6 | Open-water efficiency | $\eta_0 = (J / 2\pi) \cdot (K_T / K_Q)$ | dimensionless |  |
| 7 | Propulsive efficiency | $\eta_D = \eta_0 \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R$ | dimensionless | where $\eta_H = (1-t)/(1-w)$ and $\eta_R$ = rotative rel. eff. |
| 8 | Disc area | $A_0 = \pi D^2 / 4$ | m² |  |
| 9 | Expanded area | $A_E = EAR \cdot A_0$ | m² |  |
| 10 | Delivered power | $P_D = 2 \pi n Q$ | W |  |
| 11 | Thrust power | $P_T = T \cdot V_A$ | W |  |
| 12 | Thrust loading | $C_T = T / (0.5 \rho V_A^2 A_0)$ | dimensionless | Used for cavitation screening |
| 13 | Cavitation number | $\sigma = (p_0 - p_v) / (0.5 \rho V_A^2)$ | dimensionless |  |
| 14 | Reynolds number (blade section) | $Re = c \cdot V_{rel} / \nu$ | dimensionless |  |

**Critical Reminder — No Pipes in Tables:** In the above, $K_T$ uses division — never write absolute value with the pipe symbol inside a table cell.

### 2.5 Wageningen B-Series (KTU Mandatory)

The **Wageningen B-Series** is the universal series used worldwide for merchant-ship propellers. It expresses $K_T$ and $K_Q$ as polynomial functions of $J$, $P/D$, $Z$, and $EAR/A_E$:

$$K_T = \sum_{s,t,u,v} C_{s,t,u,v} \, J^{s} \, (P/D)^{t} \, Z^{u} \, (EAR)^{v}$$

$$K_Q = \sum_{s,t,u,v} C'_{s,t,u,v} \, J^{s} \, (P/D)^{t} \, Z^{u} \, (EAR)^{v}$$

The coefficients $C$ are tabulated (47 terms for $K_T$, 47 for $K_Q$) in standard marine texts. The range of validity is:

$$J \in [0, 1.5], \quad P/D \in [0.5, 1.4], \quad Z \in [2, 7], \quad EAR \in [0.3, 1.05]$$

For design, the engineer typically:
1. Picks a target $J$ (from the $K_T - K_Q$ chart at maximum $\eta_0$).
2. Reads off the matching $K_T$ and $K_Q$.
3. Back-calculates $P/D$ and $EAR$ required.

### 2.6 Cavitation Check (Burrill / Lerbs Criterion)

For preliminary design, the **minimum blade area** to suppress cavitation is given by the Burrill diagram, or the empirical Lerbs formula:
$$\frac{A_E}{A_0} \geq \frac{(1.3 + 0.3 Z) T / (A_0 \cdot p_0) - \sigma}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{rel}^2 / p_0 - \sigma}$$

Where $p_0$ is the ambient pressure (depth + atmospheric) and $V_{rel}$ is the local relative velocity at the design radius.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Open-Water Efficiency

**Starting point:** The useful power delivered to the ship by the propeller is the *thrust power* $P_T = T \cdot V_A$. The power consumed by the propeller is the *delivered power* $P_D = 2 \pi n Q$ (rotational power in = thrust power out + losses).

$$
\eta_0 = \frac{P_T}{P_D} = \frac{T \cdot V_A}{2 \pi n Q}
$$

**Substitute** $T = K_T \rho n^2 D^4$ and $Q = K_Q \rho n^2 D^5$:

$$
\eta_0 = \frac{(K_T \rho n^2 D^4) \cdot V_A}{2 \pi n \cdot (K_Q \rho n^2 D^5)}
$$

**Cancel** common factors $\rho$, $n^2$ appear as $\rho n^2$ in numerator and $\rho n^2$ in denominator; $D^4 / D^5 = 1/D$; $n^2 / n = n$ cancels with $V_A / nD$:

$$
\eta_0 = \frac{K_T \cdot V_A}{2 \pi n D \cdot K_Q} = \frac{K_T}{K_Q} \cdot \frac{V_A}{2 \pi n D}
$$

**Recognise** the second fraction as $J / 2 \pi$:

$$
\boxed{\eta_0 = \frac{J}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance for KTU:** This is the *defining* efficiency equation. It tells us that for a given $J$, maximising $\eta_0$ means minimising the ratio $K_Q / K_T$, which is the design objective of every B-Series chart.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Propeller Matching for a Converted Vessel

**Given (post-conversion data):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_S &= 15 \text{ knots} \\
P_E &= 5000 \text{ kW} \quad \text{(Effective Power, from model test)} \\
n &= 120 \text{ rpm} \\
w &= 0.30 \quad \text{(re-estimated after conversion)} \\
t &= 0.15 \quad \text{(re-estimated after conversion)} \\
Z &= 4 \\
\rho &= 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Convert ship speed to SI:**

$$
V_S = 15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute advance speed:**

$$
V_A = V_S \cdot (1 - w) = 7.716 \times (1 - 0.30) = 7.716 \times 0.70 = 5.401 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute required thrust.**

Bare-hull resistance $R = P_E / V_S$:

$$
R = \frac{5000 \times 10^3}{7.716} = 6.480 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 648.0 \text{ kN}
$$

Required thrust:

$$
T = \frac{R}{1 - t} = \frac{6.480 \times 10^5}{1 - 0.15} = \frac{6.480 \times 10^5}{0.85} = 7.624 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 762.4 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Choose diameter (KTU exam convention).**

Assume the ship is constrained by hull clearance. We try $D = 5.0$ m. (The KTU board typically provides $D$ or asks for an iterative trial.)

**Step 5 — Compute the advance coefficient:**

$$
J = \frac{V_A}{n D} = \frac{5.401}{(120/60) \times 5.0} = \frac{5.401}{10.0} = 0.540
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the thrust loading coefficient:**

$$
K_T = \frac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4} = \frac{7.624 \times 10^5}{1025 \times (2.0)^2 \times (5.0)^4} = \frac{7.624 \times 10^5}{1025 \times 4 \times 625} = \frac{7.624 \times 10^5}{2.5625 \times 10^6} = 0.2976
$$

**Step 7 — Read the Wageningen B-Series $K_T - K_Q - J$ chart at $J = 0.54$, $Z = 4$.**

Trial $P/D = 0.8$, $EAR = 0.55$:
- $K_T = 0.198$
- $K_Q = 0.0315$

(Values interpolated from B-Series charts.)

**Step 8 — Compute open-water efficiency:**

$$
\eta_0 = \frac{J}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q} = \frac{0.540}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{0.198}{0.0315} = 0.0859 \times 6.286 = 0.5403 \approx 54.0\%
$$

**Step 9 — Compute torque and delivered power:**

$$
Q = K_Q \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^5 = 0.0315 \times 1025 \times 4 \times 3125 = 4.031 \times 10^5 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
P_D = 2 \pi n Q = 2 \pi \times 2.0 \times 4.031 \times 10^5 = 5.066 \times 10^6 \text{ W} = 5066 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 10 — Check cavitation (Burrill / sigma).** At the design radius $r/R = 0.7$, the local relative velocity is approximately $1.5 V_A$, so:

$$
V_{rel} \approx 1.5 \times 5.401 = 8.10 \text{ m/s}
$$

At a shaft immersion of 6 m, $p_0 \approx 101325 + 1025 \times 9.81 \times 6 = 1.617 \times 10^5$ Pa, and $p_v = 1700$ Pa (cold sea water). Then:

$$
\sigma = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{0.5 \rho V_{rel}^2} = \frac{1.617 \times 10^5 - 1700}{0.5 \times 1025 \times 65.6} = \frac{1.600 \times 10^5}{33620} = 4.76
$$

Since $\sigma$ is large, the design is **non-cavitating** — the chosen $EAR = 0.55$ is adequate. The design is **ACCEPTABLE**.

### 3.3 Analytical Design Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Iteration Loop — KTU Examination Format**

| Step | Decision Variable | Source / Equation | Trial Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_S$ | Ship specification | Given |
| 2 | $P_E$ | Model test or parent hull data | Given |
| 3 | $w, t$ | Model test / empirical | Given or estimated |
| 4 | $V_A = V_S(1-w)$ | Wake correction | Computed |
| 5 | $R = P_E / V_S$ | $P = F \cdot v$ | Computed |
| 6 | $T = R/(1-t)$ | Thrust deduction | Computed |
| 7 | $n$ | Engine MCR + gearbox | Given or chosen |
| 8 | $D$ | Hull clearance / empirical formula | Try $D$ |
| 9 | $J, K_T$ | From $V_A, n, D, T$ | Computed |
| 10 | $P/D, EAR$ | Wageningen chart at max $\eta_0$ | Read from chart |
| 11 | $K_Q$ | Chart at chosen $P/D, EAR$ | Read from chart |
| 12 | $\eta_0$ | Formula (Sec 2.3) | Computed |
| 13 | $Q, P_D$ | Formula (Sec 2.4) | Computed |
| 14 | Cavitation check | Burrill / sigma | Accept / iterate |
| 15 | $A_E = EAR \cdot A_0$ | Definition | Computed |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Propeller Design Workflow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Conversion Project Inputs]) --> node1[Ship Speed VS and Effective Power PE]
    node1 --> node2[Estimate Wake w and Thrust Deduction t]
    node2 --> node3[Compute Advance Velocity VA = VS times 1 minus w]
    node3 --> node4[Compute Hull Resistance R = PE divided by VS]
    node4 --> node5[Compute Required Thrust T = R divided by 1 minus t]
    node5 --> node6[Choose Engine RPM n and Number of Blades Z]
    node6 --> node7[Trial Propeller Diameter D from Hull Clearance]
    node7 --> node8[Compute J = VA divided by nD]
    node8 --> node9[Compute KT = T divided by rho n squared D to the 4]
    node9 --> node10[Enter Wageningen B Series Chart with J, Z, KT]
    node10 --> node11[Read Pitch Ratio P over D for Maximum Efficiency]
    node11 --> node12[Read Expanded Area Ratio EAR for Cavitation Margin]
    node12 --> node13[Read KQ from Same Chart]
    node13 --> node14[Compute Open Water Efficiency eta0 = J over 2 pi times KT over KQ]
    node14 --> node15{Cavitation Check Pass?}
    node15 -- No --> node7
    node15 -- Yes --> node16[Compute Torque Q and Delivered Power PD]
    node16 --> node17[Define Blade Section Geometry: Chord, Thickness, Camber, Rake, Skew]
    node17 --> node18[Verify Propulsive Efficiency etaD and Engine Matching]
    node18 --> node19([Final Propeller Design Output])
```

### 4.2 Geometry-Definition Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    propBlockA[Propeller Geometry] --> propBlockB[Diameter D: Tip to Tip Span]
    propBlockA --> propBlockC[Pitch P: Axial Advance per Revolution]
    propBlockA --> propBlockD[Number of Blades Z: Typically 3 to 6]
    propBlockA --> propBlockE[Expanded Area Ratio EAR: AE over A0]
    propBlockA --> propBlockF[Rake: Aftward Tip Tilt in Degrees]
    propBlockA --> propBlockG[Skew: Midchord Angular Offset]
    propBlockE --> subDef1[AE: Developed Blade Area]
    propBlockE --> subDef2[A0: Disc Area pi D squared over 4]
    propBlockD --> perfLink[Performance Map: KT, KQ, eta0 as functions of J, P/D, EAR, Z]
    propBlockB --> perfLink
    propBlockC --> perfLink
    propBlockE --> perfLink
    perfLink --> wagChart[Wageningen B Series Open Water Chart]
```

### 4.3 Cavitation / Performance Trade-off Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    tradeStart([Design Objective: High eta0 AND No Cavitation]) --> t1[Increase Diameter D]
    t1 --> t1a[Improves eta0] --> t1b[Risk: Tip vortex cavitation and hull clearance]
    t2[Increase Blade Area EAR] --> t2a[Suppresses cavitation] --> t2b[Risk: Added drag, weight, cost]
    t3[Increase Number of Blades Z] --> t3a[Improves efficiency and cavitation] --> t3b[Risk: Higher cost, weight, hub complexity]
    t4[Reduce RPM n] --> t4a[Improves efficiency] --> t4b[Risk: Larger propeller needed]
    t1b --> optimize[Iterate: Wageningen Chart and Burrill Diagram]
    t2b --> optimize
    t3b --> optimize
    t4b --> optimize
    optimize --> finalDec([Final Acceptable Design])
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define the following with reference to propeller design: (a) Advance coefficient $J$, (b) Thrust deduction fraction $t$, (c) Expanded area ratio $EAR$.**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Advance Coefficient ($J$):** The non-dimensional ratio of the ship's advance velocity to the product of propeller rotational speed and diameter:
$$J = \frac{V_A}{nD}$$
It is the primary independent variable in propeller open-water characteristics.

(b) **Thrust Deduction Fraction ($t$):** The fractional reduction in effective thrust caused by the low-pressure zone created at the ship's stern by the propeller. The total thrust $T$ required from the propeller is greater than the bare-hull resistance $R$:
$$T = \frac{R}{1 - t}$$

(c) **Expanded Area Ratio ($EAR$):** The ratio of the total developed (expanded) blade area $A_E$ to the propeller disc area $A_0 = \pi D^2 / 4$:
$$EAR = \frac{A_E}{A_0}$$
Higher $EAR$ reduces cavitation but adds drag and weight. **[3 marks]**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] What is the Wageningen B-Series? State its range of validity and the four independent parameters on which $K_T$ and $K_Q$ depend.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Wageningen B-Series** (developed at the Netherlands Ship Model Basin, Wageningen, in the 1960s) is the internationally accepted universal series of open-water test data for marine propellers. It expresses $K_T$ and $K_Q$ as polynomial functions of:
- Advance coefficient $J$
- Pitch ratio $P/D$
- Number of blades $Z$
- Expanded area ratio $EAR$

**Range of validity:** $J \in [0, 1.5]$, $P/D \in [0.5, 1.4]$, $Z \in [2, 7]$, $EAR \in [0.3, 1.05]$. The series was derived from systematic tests of 20 model propellers in open water and forms the backbone of all modern merchant-ship propeller design. **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Module 3 Internal Choice]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

A bulk carrier is undergoing a **conversion refit** with a re-rated main engine. The following data are obtained from post-conversion model tests:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_S &= 14.5 \text{ knots} \\
P_E &= 4200 \text{ kW} \\
n &= 130 \text{ rpm} \\
w &= 0.32 \\
t &= 0.14 \\
Z &= 4 \\
\rho &= 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3 \\
D &= 4.6 \text{ m (assumed from hull clearance)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **(a) Compute the advance velocity $V_A$, the required thrust $T$, and the advance coefficient $J$. [7 marks]**

**Step 1:** Convert $V_S$ to m/s.
$$V_S = 14.5 \times 0.5144 = 7.459 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Advance velocity.
$$V_A = V_S (1 - w) = 7.459 \times (1 - 0.32) = 7.459 \times 0.68 = 5.072 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Bare-hull resistance.
$$R = \frac{P_E}{V_S} = \frac{4200 \times 10^3}{7.459} = 5.630 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 563.0 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4:** Required thrust.
$$T = \frac{R}{1 - t} = \frac{5.630 \times 10^5}{1 - 0.14} = \frac{5.630 \times 10^5}{0.86} = 6.547 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 654.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5:** Advance coefficient.
$$J = \frac{V_A}{n D} = \frac{5.072}{(130/60) \times 4.6} = \frac{5.072}{9.967} = 0.509$$

**[7 marks]**

#### **(b) From the Wageningen B-Series chart, $P/D = 0.80$ and $EAR = 0.60$ give $K_T = 0.181$ and $K_Q = 0.0302$ at the computed $J$. Calculate (i) the open-water efficiency $\eta_0$, (ii) the delivered torque $Q$, and (iii) the delivered power $P_D$. Comment on whether the design is acceptable. [7 marks]**

**Step 1:** Open-water efficiency.
$$\eta_0 = \frac{J}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q} = \frac{0.509}{2 \pi} \times \frac{0.181}{0.0302} = 0.0810 \times 5.993 = 0.4856 \approx 48.6\%$$

**Step 2:** Torque coefficient gives torque.
$$Q = K_Q \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^5 = 0.0302 \times 1025 \times (2.1667)^2 \times (4.6)^5$$
Computing term by term: $n^2 = 4.694$, $D^5 = 4.6^5 = 2059.6$.
$$Q = 0.0302 \times 1025 \times 4.694 \times 2059.6 = 3.001 \times 10^5 \text{ N·m} = 300.1 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3:** Delivered power.
$$P_D = 2 \pi n Q = 2 \pi \times 2.1667 \times 3.001 \times 10^5 = 4.087 \times 10^6 \text{ W} = 4087 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4:** Propulsive efficiency.
$$\eta_H = \frac{1 - t}{1 - w} = \frac{0.86}{0.68} = 1.265$$
$$\eta_D = \eta_0 \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R = 0.486 \times 1.265 \times 1.0 = 0.615$$
$$P_E \text{ required} = \eta_D \cdot P_D = 0.615 \times 4087 = 2514 \text{ kW}$$

**Comment:** Since the required effective power (2514 kW) is well below the available 4200 kW, the propeller is **over-designed**. The design team should either (i) reduce diameter, (ii) reduce blade area, or (iii) increase pitch to operate at a more efficient point. The current design is therefore **not optimal** but mechanically acceptable.

**[7 marks]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) Explain the Wageningen B-Series method of propeller design. List the inputs required, the iterative steps, and the outputs. [7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The Wageningen B-Series method is a **semi-empirical, chart-based design procedure** for marine propellers, using the universal B-Series test data.

**Inputs:**
- Ship speed $V_S$, effective power $P_E$, RPM $n$, wake $w$, thrust deduction $t$, number of blades $Z$, sea water density $\rho$, maximum allowable diameter $D_{max}$ (from hull clearance).

**Iterative Steps:**
1. Compute $V_A = V_S(1-w)$, $R = P_E/V_S$, $T = R/(1-t)$.
2. Trial a diameter $D \leq D_{max}$.
3. Compute $J = V_A / (nD)$ and $K_T = T/(\rho n^2 D^4)$.
4. Enter the B-Series $K_T - K_Q - J$ chart with these values; identify the curve of constant $P/D$ that passes through the design point and the curve of constant $EAR$ corresponding to the cavitation-free design.
5. Read off $K_Q$ from the chart.
6. Compute $\eta_0 = (J/2\pi)(K_T/K_Q)$.
7. If $\eta_0$ is too low or cavitation is unacceptable, **iterate** by changing $D$, $P/D$, or $EAR$.

**Outputs:**
- Diameter $D$, pitch $P$, expanded area $A_E$, open-water efficiency $\eta_0$, delivered torque $Q$, delivered power $P_D$, blade section geometry (chord, thickness, camber distribution).

The B-Series method is preferred for **merchant ship** propellers because of its simplicity and proven reliability.

**[7 marks]**

#### **(b) Derive the open-water efficiency $\eta_0$ of a propeller in terms of $J$, $K_T$ and $K_Q$. State the physical significance of each term. [7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The derivation is the same as Section 3.1 above, with the following significance statement:

- $J = V_A / (nD)$ represents the **kinematic state** of the propeller — fast ship and slow rotation give high $J$.
- $K_T = T / (\rho n^2 D^4)$ is the **dimensionless thrust loading** — higher $K_T$ means more thrust per unit of rotational energy.
- $K_Q = Q / (\rho n^2 D^5)$ is the **dimensionless torque loading** — it represents the resistance to rotation (drag of the blades).

A well-designed propeller has **high $K_T$ and low $K_Q$** at the operating $J$, which maximises the ratio $K_T / K_Q$. The factor $J / 2\pi$ is purely kinematic — it converts the rotational energy input into axial thrust energy.

$$\boxed{\eta_0 = \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q}}$$

**[7 marks]**

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on Propeller Design Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert knots to m/s.** $V_S$ is almost always given in knots; multiplying by **0.5144** is non-optional. Students losing 1–2 marks per question.
> 2. **Mixing up $R$ and $T$.** $R$ is the *hull resistance*; $T$ is the *propeller thrust*. Many forget the $(1-t)$ correction and end up with thrust too low by 10–15%.
> 3. **Using $V_S$ instead of $V_A$.** The propeller sees $V_A = V_S(1-w)$, **not** $V_S$. This is the single most common mistake in numerical problems.
> 4. **Ignoring the units of $n$.** $n$ in formulae is in **revolutions per second**, not rpm. Divide by 60.
> 5. **Forgetting $D^5$ in $K_Q$.** Many students write $K_Q = Q/(\rho n^2 D^4)$, losing the dimensional consistency. The power of $D$ must be 5.
> 6. **Skipping the cavitation check.** The KTU board awards 2 marks in long questions for explicitly computing the cavitation number $\sigma$ or checking against the Burrill diagram.
> 7. **Drawing the chart wrong.** In Wageningen-chart reading, the curves are $K_T$ vs $J$, and the constant-$P/D$ and constant-$EAR$ lines are nested. Misreading a curve loses up to 4 marks.
> 8. **Not writing units in the final answer.** Always end with "$T = 762.4$ **kN**" or "$Q = 4.03 \times 10^5$ **N·m**".

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — PROPELLER DESIGN**
>
> - **Three pillars:** $J$, $K_T$, $K_Q$ — all dimensionless; $J$ is the *input*, $K_T$ and $K_Q$ are the *outputs* of the B-Series chart.
> - **The defining equation:** $\eta_0 = (J/2\pi) \cdot (K_T / K_Q)$ — **memorise verbatim**.
> - **Conversion context:** In a ship conversion, the propeller matching loop **starts from $w$ and $t$**, not from the propeller itself.
> - **$V_A$ is not $V_S$.** Always $V_A = V_S(1-w)$.
> - **$T$ is not $R$.** Always $T = R/(1-t)$.
> - **$n$ in SI is rev/s.** $n(\text{rps}) = n(\text{rpm}) / 60$.
> - **Wageningen B-Series ranges:** $J \in [0, 1.5]$, $P/D \in [0.5, 1.4]$, $Z \in [2, 7]$, $EAR \in [0.3, 1.05]$.
> - **Burrill / Lerbs:** Always verify that the chosen $EAR$ is above the cavitation-limit line.
> - **Trade-offs (often asked as 3-mark questions):** $D \uparrow \Rightarrow \eta_0 \uparrow$ but hull clearance and weight $\uparrow$. $n \uparrow \Rightarrow D \downarrow$ but cavitation risk $\uparrow$. $EAR \uparrow \Rightarrow$ cavitation suppressed but drag and cost $\uparrow$. $Z \uparrow \Rightarrow$ efficiency and cavitation improved but cost and weight $\uparrow$.
> - **Final design outputs to report:** $D$, $P/D$, $EAR$, $A_E$, $\eta_0$, $Q$, $P_D$, blade section chord and thickness distribution.
> - **Common unit conversions:** 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s; 1 kW = 1000 N·m/s; 1 shp = 0.7457 kW; 1 tonne = 1000 kg; $\rho_{sea} = 1025$ kg/m³.
> - **Sigma check (cavitation):** $\sigma = (p_0 - p_v)/(0.5 \rho V_{rel}^2)$. High $\sigma$ = low cavitation risk.
> - **Propulsive efficiency:** $\eta_D = \eta_0 \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R = \eta_0 \cdot (1-t)/(1-w) \cdot \eta_R$. Hull efficiency $\eta_H$ can exceed 1.0 (it is *not* a thermodynamic efficiency).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
