**Step 2 — Current schedule — pre and post man-hours.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MH_{pre,old} &= 0.45 \cdot W_{outfit} \cdot 88 = 0.45 \cdot 1976.47 \cdot 88 = 78{,}277 \text{ MH} \\
MH_{post,old} &= 0.55 \cdot W_{outfit} \cdot 125 = 0.55 \cdot 1976.47 \cdot 125 = 135{,}882 \text{ MH}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total current:

$$
MH_{old} = 78{,}277 + 135{,}882 = 214{,}159 \text{ MH}
$$

**Step 3 — Proposed schedule.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MH_{pre,new} &= 0.70 \cdot 1976.47 \cdot 88 = 121{,}758 \text{ MH} \\
MH_{post,new} &= 0.30 \cdot 1976.47 \cdot 125 = 74{,}118 \text{ MH} \\
MH_{new} &= 121{,}758 + 74{,}118 = 195{,}876 \text{ MH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Saving.**

$$
\Delta MH = 214{,}159 - 195{,}876 = 18{,}283 \text{ MH}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage saving.**

$$
\%\text{Saving} = \dfrac{18{,}283}{214{,}159} \cdot 100 = 8.54\,\%
$$

> [!NOTE] **Final answer:** Approximately **8.5 % man-hour saving** is achieved by shifting 25 % of outfitting work from post-launch to pre-launch. This represents a major productivity win and is a KTU-favourite numerical type.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Module 3]**
*(a)* Discuss the **principles of planning hull outfitting work in a shipyard**, with specific reference to:
    (i) Critical path items
    (ii) Pre-outfitting strategy
    (iii) Work-area allocation
**(7 Marks)**

*(b)* Explain the **special considerations for hull outfitting during ship conversion** as distinct from new-building, with reference to gas-freeing, hot-work permits, and confined-space work. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a) — Planning Principles (7 Marks)

**(i) Critical Path Items (2.5 marks)**
Items whose installation gates a downstream milestone (launch, trials). They are monitored weekly and are non-negotiable for date adherence. Examples:
- Stern frame, rudder trunk, shaft line
- Sea chests, shell valves
- Engine-room and deck-machinery foundations
- Bow thruster tunnel
- Main hull penetrations

**(ii) Pre-Outfitting Strategy (2.5 marks)**
The yard's "upstream outfitting" policy: shift outfit work to the earliest possible stage, in the panel shop or block shop, where productivity is 1.0 index, cost is baseline, and quality is highest. Practical enablers:
- Pre-cut pipe spools and ventilation ducts
- Welded-on foundations before erection
- Pre-installed cable trays
- Modular accommodation units lifted as blocks

**(iii) Work-Area Allocation (2 marks)**
Distinct, non-overlapping work zones (platers' berth, fitters' bay, pipe shop, outfitting quay) sized using:

$$
A_{outfit} = \dfrac{W_{outfit}}{D_{yard} \cdot \eta_{area}}
$$

where $D_{yard}$ is the area density (t/m²) and $\eta_{area}$ is the utilisation factor. The plan also sequences crane usage to avoid contention between block erection and outfit lifts.

---

#### Solution to B(b) — Conversion-Specific Outfitting Considerations (7 Marks)

| # | Aspect | New-Building | Ship Conversion | Engineering Implication |
|---|--------|--------------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Gas-freeing | Not required (empty hull) | **Mandatory** before hot work | Tanks, cofferdams must be inerted, vented, tested (LEL, O₂) |
| 2 | Hot-work permit | Workshop standard | **Ship-specific permit** | Fire watch, fire-fighting equipment, gas-test records |
| 3 | Confined-space work | Moderate | **Extensive** (tanks, duct keels, double bottoms) | Rescue plan, man-down alarm, ventilation, lighting |
| 4 | Existing services | None | **Active / partially active** | Must isolate, lock-out / tag-out electrical and hydraulic systems |
| 5 | Penetration cutting | Planned from design | **Field-cut** on existing shell | NDT 100 % of new penetrations; class surveyor attendance |
| 6 | Surveyor / class | Standard hold-points | **Continuous survey** | Every modification is class-approved (DNV / LR / ABS) |
| 7 | Outfitting intensity | Evenly distributed | **Clustered** at conversion scope | Man-hour peaks; need temporary labour / work-front expansion |

> [!NOTE] **Concluding statement:** Conversion hull outfitting is **3 to 5 times more safety-sensitive and 1.4 to 2.2 times more expensive per man-hour** than new-building outfitting. The conversion yard must build explicit HSE, gas-test, and lock-out/tag-out protocols into the outfitting plan from day one.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Hull-Outfitting Questions (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Confusing hull outfitting with machinery outfitting** — the former is on the hull, the latter is engine/machinery assembly. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if categories are mixed.
> 2. **Forgetting the outfitting weight fraction (α)** — many students write $W_{outfit} = 0.30$ tonnes literally; the correct form is $W_{outfit} = 0.30 \cdot W_{LWT}$. Always express fractions as products.
> 3. **Using post-launch productivity in pre-launch cost estimates** — the productivity index is **stage-specific**; using a single global MH/t is the #1 valuation trap.
> 4. **Ignoring gas-freeing in conversion questions** — a 7-mark conversion question with no mention of gas-test, LEL, O₂, or hot-work permit is automatically capped at ~4 marks.
> 5. **Treating the sea chest as a non-critical item** — sea chest welding is **launch-critical**; misclassifying it costs 1 mark in any critical-path question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hull outfitting = all non-primary-structural work** done on the hull, from panel stage to delivery.
- **Benchmark fractions (memorise):** $W_{outfit} \approx 30\,\% \cdot W_{LWT}$, hull-outfit share of total outfit $\approx 50\,\%$, outfit man-hours $\approx 35\,\% \cdot MH_{total}$.
- **Four stages:** Panel/Block → Erection-Area → Pre-Launch → Afloat. **Modern yards do ≥ 70 % pre-launch.**
- **Critical launch items:** Stern frame, rudder trunk, sea chest, shell valve, shaft, bow thruster tunnel.
- **Conversion-specific risks:** Gas-freeing (LEL < 1 %, O₂ 20 – 22 %), hot-work permit, confined-space rescue, lock-out/tag-out, class continuous survey.
- **Pre-outfitting is the single biggest productivity lever** — a 25 % shift from post to pre saves roughly 8 – 12 % of outfit man-hours (the KTU-style 7-mark numerical).
- **Key formulas (memorise verbatim):**
   - $W_{outfit} = \alpha \cdot W_{LWT} = \dfrac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha} \cdot W_{steel}$
   - $MH_{outfit} = P_{outfit} \cdot W_{outfit}$
   - $\%\text{Saving} = \dfrac{MH_{old} - MH_{new}}{MH_{old}} \cdot 100$
- **Productivity index (memorise):** Pre-launch = **1.0**, Post-launch = **0.6 – 0.7**, Conversion afloat = **0.4 – 0.5**.
- **Cost index:** Pre-launch = **1.0 ×**, Post-launch = **1.4 ×**, Conversion = **1.8 – 2.2 ×**.
- **Always remember:** In conversion, every new equipment → new foundation → new penetration → new outfitting work. This is the KTU 2024 Module 3 "conversion amplifier" of outfitting work content.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Outfitting Production - Machinery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Outfitting Production - Machinery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of ship conversion technology, **Outfitting** refers to all the work performed on a vessel after the structural fabrication/conversion stages (cutting, welding, blasting, painting of hull blocks and the dry-dock/launch phase) are complete, and before the vessel is delivered for sea trials. **Outfitting Production — Machinery** is the sub-discipline that deals with the planning, scheduling, transport, positioning, installation, alignment, hook-up, testing, and commissioning of all mechanical, electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, and thermal systems installed inside the ship.

According to standard shipbuilding classification (aligned with the KTU 2024 syllabus descriptor for Module 3), machinery outfitting is broadly grouped into:

- **Hull Outfitting** — related to the shell and structural integrity.
- **Machinery Outfitting (E-engineering Outfitting)** — installation of propulsion, auxiliary, and ship-service systems.
- **Electrical Outfitting** — cabling, switchboards, automation.
- **Outfit (Accommodation) Outfitting** — joinery, insulation, domestic systems.

The **Machinery Outfitting** branch specifically includes the **main engine, gear box, shaft line, propeller, generators, boilers, purifiers, pumps, compressors, heat exchangers, valves, piping, deck machinery (winches, capstans, windlasses, cranes), HVAC plants, fuel/lub oil systems, and steering gear**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PENST415 Module 3):**
> The examiner expects students to clearly distinguish between **Pre-Outfitting (PE)** and **Post-Outfitting (PO)** of machinery, understand the **zone/sectional outfitting strategy**, and appreciate the **work-package based planning** that integrates with the **Integrated Production System (IPS)** and **Computer-Aided Ship Production Planning (CASPP)** frameworks taught in earlier modules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are renovating a large hospital building. The contractors first complete the brickwork, plastering, and structural skeleton (this is the **hull stage** of a ship). Once the shell is up, electricians, plumbers, HVAC technicians, and equipment installers come in to bring the building to life — fitting lifts, MRI machines, generators, oxygen pipelines, sterilizers, etc. **This 'fitting the building with life-support systems' is exactly what machinery outfitting does for a ship.**

A ship, like a floating city, must generate its own power, treat its own water, move its own cargo, and keep its crew alive — all while battling salt, motion, and corrosion. The **machinery outfitting phase is therefore the 'organs-transplant and nervous-system' stage of ship conversion**, transforming an empty steel shell into a functioning vessel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metric:** In modern shipyards, the ratio of **outfitting man-hours to total construction man-hours** typically lies between **30 % and 50 %**. For complex naval or offshore conversion projects, this share may exceed **55 %**, which is why outfitting is the single largest cost driver after steel.

## 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Outfitting share of total man-hours | 30 – 50 | % |
| Pre-outfitting (PE) target | 60 – 80 | % of total outfitting work |
| Unit (zone) outfitting target | 70 – 90 | % of zone work before block joining |
| Outfitting density (commercial tanker) | 0.8 – 1.2 | t of outfit steel per m³ |
| Pipe shop prefabrication target | 70 – 85 | % of total piping |
| Tolerances for shaft alignment (cold) | 0.05 – 0.10 | mm/1000 mm |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Machinery Outfitting

The machinery outfitting process can be decomposed into **six logical sub-processes**, each with its own engineering deliverables:

### A. Work-Package Planning (Front-Loaded Engineering)
- **Why:** To allow parallel rather than sequential execution.
- **How:** Engineering drawings, isometric pipe spool drawings, and 3D models are broken into **zone work packages (ZWP)**. Each ZWP contains a bill of materials (BOM), tools, procedures, and man-hour budget.
- **Logic step:**
  1. Read 3D model and extract component list.
  2. Generate Zone Outfit Drawing (ZOD).
  3. Generate Pipe Spool Isometric.
  4. Generate Cable Transit & Tray Layout.
  5. Issue work package to shop floor via **MES (Manufacturing Execution System)**.

### B. Prefabrication & Pre-Outfitting (PE)
- **Why:** To move work from congested shipboard locations to climate-controlled workshops.
- **How:** Pipe spools, ventilation ducting, cable looms, equipment foundations, switchboard cubicles, and pump baseplates are pre-assembled in the workshop.
- **Benefit:** PE typically achieves **2.5× to 4× productivity gain** compared with post-outfitting.

### C. Logistics & Material Flow
- **Why:** Shipyards are space-constrained.
- **How:** **Just-In-Time (JIT)** delivery using tugger trains, AGVs, or cranes; staging areas called **outfitting quays or pre-erection berths**.

### D. Shipboard Installation
- **Why:** The components must be physically placed and aligned.
- **How:** Use of overhead cranes, gantry cranes, mono-rails, and roller beds; foundation alignment by **chock-fast** or **epoxy-resin grouting**.

### E. Hook-Up, Alignment & Cabling
- **Why:** Individual units must be connected into functioning systems.
- **How:** Cold alignment of shafting using **laser alignment or reverse-dial-indicator method**; piping flange torque-wrench tightening; cable termination.

### F. Testing, Commissioning & Trials
- **Why:** To prove the system to class and owner.
- **How:** Stage-by-stage **FMEA-based commissioning**; **HAT (Harbour Acceptance Test)** and **SAT (Sea Acceptance Test)**.

## 2.2 The Pre-Outfitting vs. Post-Outfitting Trade-Off

| Aspect | Pre-Outfitting (PE) | Post-Outfitting (PO) |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Workshops & assembly halls | On board (berth/dock) |
| Productivity | High (workshop conditions) | Low (weather, access) |
| Access to components | Excellent | Restricted by hull closure |
| Man-hour cost factor | 0.8 – 1.0 × | 1.5 – 2.5 × |
| Accuracy | High (jigs, fixtures) | Moderate |
| Risk of damage | Lower | Higher |
| Preferred share | Maximize | Minimize |

## 2.3 Zone / Unit Outfitting Strategy

The ship is divided into **zones** (e.g., Zone 11 = Engine Room, Zone 21 = Bridge, Zone 31 = Pump Room). **Unit outfitting** further aggregates machinery into 3D "units" — e.g., the lube-oil purifier unit, the HVAC self-contained unit, the steering gear unit. Each unit is pre-assembled, tested in the workshop, lifted aboard, and connected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Aim for **70 % or more of zone outfitting work to be completed before the zone is joined to the main hull block.** This is the key performance indicator of a modern shipyard.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Variables | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{tot} = T_{steel} + T_{outfit} + T_{paint} + T_{trials}$ | Total project time components | days / weeks | Used in project scheduling |
| 2 | $M_{outfit} = M_{tot} \times f_{outfit}$ | Man-hours outfitting | man-hours | $f_{outfit}$ typically **0.30 – 0.50** |
| 3 | $P_{pe} = \dfrac{H_{pe}}{H_{pe} + H_{po}} \times 100\%$ | PE ratio | % | Target $\geq$ 60 % |
| 4 | $N_{shift} = \dfrac{M_{outfit}}{H_{shift} \times D \times W}$ | Crew sizing | workers | $H_{shift}$ = man-hours per worker per day |
| 5 | $D_{crane} = k \times L_{comp} \times \rho_{comp}$ | Crane size for machinery | t | $k$ = safety factor 1.2 – 1.5 |
| 6 | $\delta_{cold} = \dfrac{\Delta L_{shaft}}{L_{shaft}} \times 10^{6}$ | Cold alignment | µm/m | Class requirement 50 – 100 µm/m |
| 7 | $Q_{HVAC} = \dot{m}_{air} \times c_{p} \times \Delta T$ | HVAC capacity | kW | Conditioning air for compartments |
| 8 | $A_{flow} = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{C \times 3600}$ | Pipe cross-section | m² | For pump discharge sizing |
| 9 | $h_{pump} = \dfrac{P_{out} - P_{in}}{\rho g}$ | Pump head | m | NPSH verification mandatory |
| 10 | $T_{cycle} = T_{steel} + T_{outfit}$ | Critical-path cycle time | months | Used in Gantt tracking |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Do not confuse **man-hours** with **calendar weeks**. A 100 000-man-hour outfitting task performed by a 50-person crew working 40 h/week is **50 weeks** of calendar time, not 100 000 weeks.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Ship Conversion

In a typical VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier) lengthening or offshore platform conversion, the **machinery outfitting phase** decides whether the project is delivered **on-time and on-budget**. Yard planners use these relationships to:

- Bid tenders accurately.
- Decide the **level of PE** to commit to.
- Calculate **dock/berth occupancy**.
- Negotiate with class surveyors and engine makers.
- Plan for **dry-dock window** availability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Outfitting Man-Hour Allocation Between PE and PO

We want to show mathematically why a yard targets a high PE share. The total outfitting man-hours $H_{tot}$ is split into shop (PE) and shipboard (PO) portions:

$$
H_{tot} = H_{pe} + H_{po}
$$

Let $p$ be the **productivity ratio** between PO and PE, defined as the man-hour multiplier required to do the same work shipboard versus in the workshop. A typical yard reports:

$$
p = \frac{H_{po}^{\text{per unit}}}{H_{pe}^{\text{per unit}}} \approx 1.8 \;\; \text{to} \;\; 2.5
$$

Define the **PE share** $\alpha$:

$$
\alpha = \frac{H_{pe}}{H_{tot}}
$$

Hence:

$$
1 - \alpha = \frac{H_{po}}{H_{tot}}
$$

### Step 1 — Express actual man-hours in terms of an "effective" base

Let the base work content be $W$. Then:

$$
W = \frac{H_{pe}}{1} = \frac{H_{po}}{p}
$$

Therefore:

$$
H_{pe} = W, \quad H_{po} = pW
$$

### Step 2 — Total man-hour cost

$$
H_{tot} = W + pW = W(1 + p)
$$

### Step 3 — Express $H_{pe}$ and $H_{po}$ in terms of $\alpha$

From the definition of $\alpha$:

$$
H_{pe} = \alpha H_{tot}
$$

Substituting $H_{tot} = W(1+p)$:

$$
\alpha H_{tot} = W \quad \Rightarrow \quad W = \alpha W(1+p)
$$

Solving for $\alpha$:

$$
1 = \alpha(1+p) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \frac{1}{1+p}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication:** The PE share is **bounded by the productivity ratio itself.** With $p = 2$, $\alpha = 1/3 \approx 33\%$. With $p = 5$ (very poor yard), $\alpha = 1/6 \approx 17\%$. The mathematical lesson: **a yard with poor shipboard productivity is forced to pre-outfit more — but cannot exceed the structural limit** given by $1/(1+p)$.

### Step 4 — Man-hour saving from increasing PE

Suppose we increase PE from $\alpha_1$ to $\alpha_2$. The base work content reduces from $W_1$ to $W_2$ because more work is done in the efficient workshop:

$$
H_{tot}^{(1)} = W_1(1+p), \quad H_{tot}^{(2)} = W_2(1+p)
$$

The reduction in total man-hours:

$$
\Delta H = H_{tot}^{(1)} - H_{tot}^{(2)} = (W_1 - W_2)(1+p)
$$

But the base work content itself changes because the share of efficient work changed. Assuming the same total scope, we write:

$$
\frac{H_{pe}^{(1)}}{\alpha_1} = \frac{H_{pe}^{(2)}}{\alpha_2}
$$

Solving:

$$
H_{pe}^{(2)} = H_{pe}^{(1)} \cdot \frac{\alpha_2}{\alpha_1}
$$

This equation is the **budget-allocation tool** used by planners to redistribute man-hours between PE and PO when targets change.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example

A naval frigate conversion project has a planned outfitting scope of $H_{tot} = 200\,000$ man-hours. The yard targets a $p = 2.0$ productivity multiplier. The current PE share is $\alpha_1 = 50\%$. Management wants to raise it to $\alpha_2 = 70\%$.

**Step 1 — Current base work content:**

$$
W_1 = \frac{H_{pe}^{(1)}}{1} = 0.5 \times 200\,000 = 100\,000 \text{ man-hours (PE portion)}
$$

**Step 2 — New PE man-hours required at higher share:**

$$
H_{pe}^{(2)} = H_{pe}^{(1)} \cdot \frac{\alpha_2}{\alpha_1} = 100\,000 \times \frac{0.7}{0.5} = 140\,000 \text{ man-hours}
$$

**Step 3 — New PO man-hours:**

$$
H_{po}^{(2)} = (1 - 0.7) \times 200\,000 = 60\,000 \text{ man-hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Check with productivity ratio:**

$$
W_2 = \frac{H_{po}^{(2)}}{p} = \frac{60\,000}{2.0} = 30\,000 \text{ man-hours}
$$

But $W_2$ should equal $H_{pe}^{(2)}$ for the model to be consistent. We see $140\,000 \ne 30\,000$, which means **the absolute scope must shrink** for the higher PE share to be meaningful. The planner's correct interpretation is:

> The yard must **re-engineer the work** so that more of the *physical scope* is executed in PE, not just the same work in different locations.

**Step 5 — Realistic scope redistribution:**

Let the physical scope remain the same but $p$ drops to $1.6$ because better shipboard access is achieved via improved temporary staging:

$$
W_2 = 140\,000 \text{ man-hours (PE)}, \quad p = 1.6
$$

$$
H_{po}^{(2)} = p W_2 = 1.6 \times 140\,000 = 224\,000
$$

This exceeds the planned $H_{tot}$, indicating that **scope reduction or schedule extension is mandatory**. The planner now negotiates with the ship-owner.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Strategy | Pre-Outfitting Share | Yard Man-Hour Multiplier | Total Man-Hours | Schedule Risk | Skill Required |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Conventional (1960s) | 20 % | 2.5 | 1.40 $W$ | High | Low |
| Mixed (1990s) | 50 % | 2.0 | 1.50 $W$ | Medium | Medium |
| Modern IPS (2020+) | 75 % | 1.4 | 1.52 $W$ | Low | High |
| Maximum PE | 90 % | 1.2 | 1.08 $W$ | Very Low | Very High |

> [!NOTE]
> The total man-hours do not always drop monotonically with higher PE because of **re-work, transport, and increased handling effort**. A balanced 60 – 75 % PE target is usually optimal for general commercial vessels.

## 3.4 Numerical Application: Crane Selection for Engine Lift

A main engine of mass $m = 180$ t must be lifted into the engine room. Length $L = 7.5$ m, breadth $B = 3.0$ m, height $H = 4.0$ m. Two cranes will be used in tandem.

**Step 1 — Load per crane (gross):**

$$
W_{crane} = \frac{m \cdot g \cdot k}{n} = \frac{180 \times 9.81 \times 1.3}{2} = 1147.7 \text{ kN}
$$

where $k = 1.3$ is the dynamic + sling factor and $n = 2$ is the number of cranes.

**Step 2 — Convert to tonne-force:**

$$
T = \frac{1147.7 \times 10^{3}}{9.81 \times 10^{3}} = 117 \text{ t per crane}
$$

**Step 3 — Crane selection:** A **150 t crawler crane** with a 12 m radius is the appropriate choice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a KTU exam, students often forget the **dynamic factor $k$**. Always include it in lifting calculations: $k = 1.25$ for slow careful lifts, $k = 1.5$ for fast lifts.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Outfitting Production Workflow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Engineering 3D Model] --> B[Work Package Planning ZWP]
    B --> C[Material Procurement and Staging]
    C --> D1[Pipe Spool Prefabrication Shop]
    C --> D2[Equipment Unit Assembly Shop]
    C --> D3[Cable Loom and Tray Prefab]
    C --> D4[HVAC Duct Prefabrication]
    D1 --> E[Pre Outfitting PE on Block]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Block Erection on Slipway]
    F --> G[Dock Phase Block Joining]
    G --> H[Post Outfitting PO on Ship]
    H --> I[Hook Up and Alignment]
    I --> J[System Commissioning HAT]
    J --> K[Sea Trials SAT]
    K --> L[Delivery to Owner]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The process flows from design (left) to delivery (right). The four parallel branches in the middle represent **simultaneous prefabrication streams** — a hallmark of the **Integrated Production System (IPS)**.

## 4.2 Machinery Outfitting Classification (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Machinery Outfitting] --> P[Propulsion Plant]
    M --> Aux[Auxiliary Systems]
    M --> Deck[Deck Machinery]
    M --> ShipSv[Ship Service Systems]
    P --> ME[Main Engine]
    P --> GB[Gearbox]
    P --> SH[Shaft Line and Propeller]
    Aux --> GE[Generators]
    Aux --> BO[Boilers and Steam Plant]
    Aux --> PM[Pumps and Compressors]
    Aux --> HX[Heat Exchangers]
    Deck --> WN[Winches Capstans]
    Deck --> WL[Windlass]
    Deck --> CR[Cranes]
    Deck --> STG[Steering Gear]
    ShipSv --> HV[HVAC Plant]
    ShipSv --> FO[Fuel and Lub Oil Systems]
    ShipSv --> FW[Fresh Water Generator]
    ShipSv --> SL[Sewage and Bilge System]
```

## 4.3 PE/PO Trade-Off Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Item to be Outfitted] --> Q1{Size > 2 tons?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Lifting access available?}
    Q1 -- No --> PEA[Pre Outfit in Workshop]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Tolerance critical < 0.1 mm?}
    Q2 -- No --> POA[Post Outfit on Ship]
    Q3 -- Yes --> PEA
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Weather sensitive work?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> PEA
    Q4 -- No --> POA
```

## 4.4 Shaft Alignment Workflow (Key Machinery Activity)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Set Engine on Chocks] --> S2[Rough Centering]
    S2 --> S3[Hard Chock and Grout]
    S3 --> S4[Cold Alignment by Laser]
    S4 --> S5{Shaft Within 50 um per m?}
    S5 -- No --> S6[Re Shim and Realign]
    S6 --> S4
    S5 -- Yes --> S7[Coupling Bolting]
    S7 --> S8[Hot Alignment Check after Run In]
    S8 --> S9[Final Class Survey]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shaft alignment** is the **most mark-yielding question** in KTU Module 3 examinations. Memorise that class typically requires **cold alignment $\le 0.05$ mm per 1000 mm** for main propulsion shafting.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define "Pre-Outfitting" and "Post-Outfitting" in the context of ship machinery installation. State the typical target share of pre-outfitting in a modern shipyard.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Pre-Outfitting (PE):** The work of fabricating, assembling, and fitting outfit components (pipe spools, equipment units, cable looms, ducting) onto hull blocks **in the workshop or on the pre-erection berth** before the block is erected and joined to the rest of the hull. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Post-Outfitting (PO):** The work of installing and connecting outfit components on the **erected ship at the building dock, outfitting quay, or alongside berth** after hull closure. *(1 mark)*
- **Target PE share:** In a modern yard, PE should constitute **60 % – 80 %** of the total outfitting man-hours. *(0.5 mark)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is a "Zone Work Package" (ZWP)? Why is it important in machinery outfitting planning?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Zone Work Package is a **bundled set of drawings, BOMs, procedures, and man-hour budgets** covering all outfitting work to be performed inside a specific **shipboard zone** (e.g., Zone 11 = Engine Room, Zone 21 = Pump Room). *(1.5 marks)*
- It enables **parallel work** across multiple zones. *(0.5 mark)*
- It provides a **single planning unit** for material, crew, and schedule. *(0.5 mark)*
- It allows accurate **progress measurement** and class-survey coordination. *(0.5 mark)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat flowchart, the **sequence of operations in machinery outfitting** for a main propulsion engine installation. Highlight the alignment procedure. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A ship conversion project has a planned outfitting scope of **$180\,000$ man-hours**. Currently, the **PE share is 45 %** and the **productivity ratio $p = 2.2$**. The yard plans to raise the PE share to **65 %** while keeping the absolute physical scope unchanged. Assuming the PO man-hours reduce proportionally to maintain the base work, calculate the **new total outfitting man-hours** and the **man-hour saving**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Sequence of operations (7 marks):**

1. **Foundation preparation** — cleaning engine-seat plate, checking level, ensuring chock pockets are clean. *(1 mark)*
2. **Engine arrival on board** — lifted by gantry/overhead crane, set on temporary supports. *(1 mark)*
3. **Rough centering** — engine aligned approximately with shaft line using wire and scale. *(1 mark)*
4. **Hard chocking** — steel chocks driven and seal-welded. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Grouting** — epoxy or cementitious grout poured between chock plate and ship foundation. *(0.5 mark)*
6. **Cold alignment by laser or reverse-dial** — adjust shims until offset $\le 50 \;\mu\text{m/m}$ and angular $\le 0.05 \;\text{mm/m}$. *(1.5 marks)*
7. **Coupling bolt tightening** — to torque specified by maker. *(0.5 mark)*
8. **Hot alignment verification** — after test run, re-measure and shim if needed. *(0.5 mark)*
9. **Class survey and final chock certificate.** *(0.5 mark)*

**Flowchart (included in answer):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Foundation Cleaning] --> A2[Engine Lift Aboard]
    A2 --> A3[Rough Centering]
    A3 --> A4[Hard Chock and Weld]
    A4 --> A5[Epoxy Grouting]
    A5 --> A6[Cold Alignment Laser]
    A6 --> A7{Class Tolerance}
    A7 -- No --> A8[Re Shim]
    A8 --> A6
    A7 -- Yes --> A9[Coupling Bolt]
    A9 --> A10[Hot Alignment Check]
    A10 --> A11[Final Class Approval]
```

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:**
- $H_{tot} = 180\,000$ man-hours
- $\alpha_1 = 0.45$, $p = 2.2$
- $\alpha_2 = 0.65$
- Absolute physical scope unchanged.

**Step 1 — Current PE and PO man-hours:**

$$
H_{pe}^{(1)} = 0.45 \times 180\,000 = 81\,000 \text{ man-hours}
$$

$$
H_{po}^{(1)} = 0.55 \times 180\,000 = 99\,000 \text{ man-hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Base work content $W$:**

$$
W = \frac{H_{po}^{(1)}}{p} = \frac{99\,000}{2.2} = 45\,000 \text{ man-hours}
$$

**Step 3 — New PE man-hours at $\alpha_2 = 0.65$:**

Since the absolute physical scope is unchanged, $H_{po}$ must drop. We keep $W$ constant at $45\,000$ man-hours and recompute:

$$
H_{po}^{(2)} = pW = 2.2 \times 45\,000 = 99\,000 \text{ man-hours}
$$

This is unchanged, which means **raising the PE share without changing the scope** is impossible unless we change the work location. Let us re-interpret: the planner wants **to transfer 20 % of the current PO man-hours into PE.** So:

$$
H_{po}^{(2)} = 99\,000 - 36\,000 = 63\,000 \text{ man-hours}
$$

$$
H_{pe}^{(2)} = 81\,000 + 36\,000 = 117\,000 \text{ man-hours}
$$

**Step 4 — New total man-hours:**

$$
H_{tot}^{(2)} = 117\,000 + 63\,000 = 180\,000 \text{ man-hours (unchanged)}
$$

The **man-hour saving in calendar terms** comes from productivity gain. If previously 99 000 man-hours were spent at 1.0 (PE) effective, but 99 000 PO man-hours would have needed only $W = 45\,000$ if done in PE, the saving is:

$$
\Delta H = 99\,000 - 45\,000 = 54\,000 \text{ man-hours saved}
$$

**Step 5 — Effective total after relocation:**

$$
H_{eff}^{(2)} = 117\,000 + 45\,000 = 162\,000 \text{ man-hours}
$$

**Result:** The new total *effective* man-hours is **162 000** with a **saving of 18 000 man-hours** (≈ 10 % reduction). *(7 marks distributed across the steps)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** Most students forget that **the nominal total man-hours (180 000) does not change** when only the location changes; only the **effective cost** changes. Writing "the new total is now $117\,000 + 63\,000 = 180\,000$" is correct, but the examiner wants the *saving* in **effective man-hours** (or calendar weeks), not just the location swap. Marks are deducted if the productivity ratio $p$ is not used.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` — **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **planning of machinery outfitting** for a naval auxiliary ship conversion. Cover work-breakdown, pre-fabrication strategy, and the role of computer-aided planning tools. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A pipe spool prefabrication shop has **40 workers working 8-hour shifts, 5 days a week**. The total pipe scope is **$12\,000$ spools** with an average prefabrication time of **6 man-hours per spool**. Calculate the **time in weeks** required to complete prefabrication. If the yard wants to **reduce this by 25 %**, how many additional workers are needed (assume same hours and days)? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Work-Breakdown Structure (3 marks):**
The planning is broken down hierarchically:
- **Level 1:** Total ship machinery outfitting.
- **Level 2:** Propulsion, Auxiliary, Deck, Ship-service.
- **Level 3:** Main engine, gear box, shaft line, propellers.
- **Level 4:** Sub-assemblies (e.g., lube-oil purifier unit).
- **Level 5:** Pipe spools, brackets, cable runs, foundations.
- **Level 6 (Work Package):** Specific ZWP for a zone.

**Pre-Fabrication Strategy (2.5 marks):**
- Pipe spool drawings extracted from 3D model (e.g., **AVEVA Marine / E3D / Tribon**).
- Spools fabricated in workshop with **orbital welding**, hydro-tested, painted, then delivered to ship by **JIT system**.
- Aim for **70 – 85 % pipe spool prefabrication ratio**.
- Equipment units (HVAC, lube-oil, fuel-oil) assembled in workshop, function-tested, then installed as a single lift.

**Computer-Aided Planning Tools (1.5 marks):**
- 3D CAD for clash detection and ZOD generation.
- **CASPP (Computer-Aided Ship Production Planning)** for sequencing.
- **MES (Manufacturing Execution System)** for shop-floor tracking.
- **ERP (e.g., SAP)** for material and procurement.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:**
- Workers $N = 40$, Shift = 8 h, Days/week = 5
- Total spools = 12 000, time per spool = 6 man-hours

**Step 1 — Total man-hours required:**

$$
H_{total} = 12\,000 \times 6 = 72\,000 \text{ man-hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Man-hours per week per worker:**

$$
H_{week} = 8 \times 5 = 40 \text{ man-hours per worker per week}
$$

**Step 3 — Crew man-hours per week:**

$$
H_{crew} = 40 \times 40 = 1600 \text{ man-hours per week}
$$

**Step 4 — Time required:**

$$
T = \frac{72\,000}{1600} = 45 \text{ weeks}
$$

**Step 5 — 25 % reduction target time:**

$$
T_{new} = 0.75 \times 45 = 33.75 \text{ weeks}
$$

**Step 6 — Required crew man-hours per week:**

$$
H_{req} = \frac{72\,000}{33.75} = 2133.33 \text{ man-hours per week}
$$

**Step 7 — Additional workers needed:**

$$
N_{new} = \frac{2133.33}{40} = 53.33 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; 54 \text{ workers}
$$

$$
\Delta N = 54 - 40 = \mathbf{14 \text{ additional workers}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** A common mistake is to write 53 workers and stop. Always **round UP** to the next whole worker because you cannot hire half a person. Also, students often confuse **man-hours** with **hours** — remember to multiply by the number of workers.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Outfitting** is everything done to the ship after structural work, and **machinery outfitting** specifically covers engines, auxiliaries, deck machinery, and ship-service systems.
- **Pre-Outfitting (PE)** = workshop; **Post-Outfitting (PO)** = on board. Modern yards target **60 – 80 % PE share**.
- The relationship $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{1+p}$ gives a **theoretical upper bound** to the PE share based on the productivity ratio $p$.
- **Zone Work Packages (ZWP)** are the planning unit of choice; they enable parallel work, accurate progress tracking, and class coordination.
- **Unit outfitting** (3D pre-assembled modules) is the single biggest productivity booster in modern shipyards.
- **Pipe spool prefabrication ratio** target = **70 – 85 %** of total piping.
- **Crane sizing** always includes a dynamic + sling factor $k$ of **1.25 – 1.5**.
- **Shaft cold alignment tolerance** for main propulsion = **$\le 50 \;\mu\text{m/m}$** (offset and angular).
- **Total outfitting man-hours** typically equal **30 – 50 %** of total shipbuilding man-hours, and as high as **55 %** for complex conversions.
- For man-hour / time calculations, remember: $T = H_{total} / (N \times H_{shift} \times D)$, and always **round up** the worker count.
- **IPS (Integrated Production System)** + **CASPP** + **MES** = the modern digital toolchain for outfitting planning.
- A common exam trap: confusing **calendar weeks** with **man-hours** — they are **not** the same dimension.
- Always include the **dynamic factor** in any lifting / crane calculation; forgetting it loses at least 1 mark.
- **Chock-fast** and **epoxy grouting** are the two accepted foundation methods — class surveyors will reject un-grouted machinery.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Main Engine Based on the Revised Powering and Shaft Calculations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Selection of Main Engine Based on the Revised Powering and Shaft Calculations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

In the context of **Ship Conversion Technology**, the **Selection of Main Engine Based on Revised Powering and Shaft Calculations** is the integrated engineering procedure by which a prime mover (diesel, gas, or dual-fuel engine) is chosen, and the associated propulsion shaft line (intermediate shaft, thrust shaft, tail shaft, and propeller shaft) is dimensioned, using the **revised Effective Horsepower (EHP)** obtained after hull form modification, appendage re-arrangement, deadweight redistribution, and route profile re-evaluation.

The selection must satisfy three concurrent engineering obligations:

1. **Power Obligation:** Brake Power (BHP) developed at the Maximum Continuous Rating (MCR) must overcome the revised Effective Power, total propulsive losses, and the prescribed **Service Margin (15–25 %)**.
2. **Geometric Obligation:** The shaft line diameter (derived from a torsional–empirical formula) must transmit the MCR torque with a **shear stress within the permissible limit of the shaft material (typically 49–56 MPa for forged steel)**.
3. **Regulatory Obligation:** The choice must conform to the **classification society rules (IRS / DNV / Lloyd's Register / BV / ABS)**, the **MARPOL Annex VI NOx Tier** requirement, and the **SOLAS** redundancy clause for passenger/conversion vessels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 expects students to be able to (i) recompute EHP after a conversion, (ii) escalate EHP to BHP, (iii) apply the correct Service Margin, (iv) size the shaft using the standard K/D empirical constant, and (v) justify the engine on the basis of MCR, RPM, SFOC, and Type.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Choosing a New Heart for a Transplant Patient"

Think of a ship conversion as a **cardiac transplant** for the vessel:

- The **old hull** is the patient's body — it survives, but it is now carrying **new responsibilities** (new route, new deadweight, possibly a new bow form).
- The **new main engine** is the **donor heart** — it must pump the **revised blood flow (EHP)** with adequate reserve (**Service Margin**) and at a comfortable stroke rate (**engine RPM**).
- The **shaft line** is the **aorta** — its diameter must be wide enough to carry the new cardiac output without bursting, but not so wide that the body wastes energy pushing blood through it.
- A **stethoscope check** = the model test or the **self-propulsion test** in the towing tank.

If the donor heart is too small, the patient tires on the slightest climb (the ship cannot maintain service speed in a head sea). If the heart is oversized, fuel is wasted, lubricating oil consumption rises, and the propeller cavitates violently. The doctor — *the Naval Architect* — must therefore choose **just the right size**, with a reserve called the *Service Margin*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary Lock-In for KTU:**
> - **EHP (Effective Horsepower)** — the *useful* power required to tow the ship at service speed.
> - **DHP (Delivered Horsepower)** — power reaching the propeller after hull losses.
> - **BHP (Brake Horsepower)** — power developed at the engine flywheel.
> - **MCR (Maximum Continuous Rating)** — the upper limit at which the engine may run indefinitely.
> - **NCR (Normal Continuous Rating)** — the rating at which the engine *normally* operates at service speed (typically **85–90 % of MCR**).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

The following are the **standardised numerical constants** used throughout this topic. Memorise them — they are guaranteed to appear in a KTU calculation question:

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value | Unit |
|--------|----------|---------------|------|
| $S_M$ | Service Margin (merchant) | **15–20 %** | — |
| $S_M$ | Service Margin (naval) | **20–25 %** | — |
| $\eta_H$ | Hull efficiency | **1.0 – 1.20** | — |
| $\eta_R$ | Rotative efficiency | **0.95 – 0.98** | — |
| $\eta_G$ | Gearbox / transmission efficiency | **0.97 – 0.99** | — |
| $t$ | Thrust deduction fraction | **0.10 – 0.20** | — |
| $w$ | Wake fraction | **0.20 – 0.40** | — |
| $K_{IS}$ | Intermediate shaft (steel) constant | **275** | mm·(kW·min/rpm)$^{-1/3}$ |
| $K_{TS}$ | Tail shaft (bronze) constant | **240** | mm·(kW·min/rpm)$^{-1/3}$ |
| $\tau_{all}$ | Allowable shear stress (forged steel) | **49–56** | MPa |
| $\sigma_{all}$ | Allowable bending stress (steel) | **70–84** | MPa |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Revised Powering Chain (EHP → BHP)

After a ship undergoes conversion (e.g., lengthening, bulbous bow addition, route change from coastal to deep-sea, addition of upper-deck cargo), the **resistance curve changes**. A new model test (or CFD validation) gives a new $R_T$ vs. $V$ curve, and the **revised EHP** at the new service speed $V_S$ is:

$$EHP_{revised} = \frac{R_T \cdot V_S}{75} \quad \text{(metric HP)}$$

or

$$EHP_{revised} = \frac{R_T \cdot V_S}{0.7355} \quad \text{(kW)}$$

This EHP must be **propagated back** through the propulsion chain to find the **BHP** the main engine must deliver:

| Stage | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|-------|---------|------------------|
| Thrust Power (TP) | $TP = (1 - t) \cdot EHP$ | Power lost overcoming thrust deduction |
| Delivered Power (DHP) | $DHP = \dfrac{TP}{\eta_R} = \dfrac{(1-t) \cdot EHP}{\eta_R}$ | Power actually arriving at the propeller disc |
| Brake Power (BHP) | $BHP = \dfrac{DHP}{\eta_G}$ | Power leaving the engine flywheel |
| Service Power (SP) | $SP = BHP$ | Power required at service speed (no margin) |
| Maximum Continuous Rating (MCR) | $MCR = \dfrac{SP}{1 - S_M}$ | Power that the engine must *be capable* of delivering |
| Normal Continuous Rating (NCR) | $NCR = (0.85 \text{ to } 0.90) \cdot MCR$ | Rating at which the engine actually runs at service speed |

## 2.2 Engine Selection Decision Tree

The engineer must answer **four binary questions** in sequence:

1. **Two-stroke vs Four-stroke?**
   - *Two-stroke low-speed (60–120 rpm)*: Direct drive, no gearbox, longer stroke = better thermal efficiency (~50 %), lower SFOC (~160 g/kWh). Preferred for **bulk carriers, tankers, container ships** where a new propeller can be fitted to the existing shaft line.
   - *Four-stroke medium-speed (400–1000 rpm)*: Geared drive, shorter, lighter, but consumes slightly more fuel (~175–185 g/kWh). Preferred for **naval auxiliaries, FPSO, offshore vessels** or when the existing shaft line RPM cannot be altered.

2. **Diesel vs Dual-Fuel (LNG/methanol)?**
   - Driven by IMO 2020 sulphur cap and forthcoming carbon intensity (CII) regulations. A conversion project lasting > 20 years is **strongly advised** to evaluate dual-fuel.

3. **Cylinder count and MCR?**
   - Use the calculated $MCR$ to look up manufacturer catalogues (MAN B&W, Wärtsilä, Caterpillar Marine). Cylinder power for modern 2-stroke units: 1000–4500 kW/cyl.

4. **Propeller RPM matching?**
   - The B-series (Wageningen) propeller must operate at an **advance coefficient** $J = V_A / (nD)$ between **0.4 and 0.7** for an efficient open-water condition.

## 2.3 Shaft Calculation — The Empirical Cubic-Root Formula

The diameter of a marine propulsion shaft is sized primarily for **torsional strength** under the steady torque at MCR. The standard empirical formula is:

$$d = K \cdot \left(\frac{P}{n}\right)^{1/3}$$

where:
- $d$ = shaft diameter in **mm**
- $P$ = transmitted power in **kW** (at MCR)
- $n$ = shaft rotational speed in **rpm**
- $K$ = empirical constant depending on **material and keyway** (see table below)

### 2.4 Standard Empirical Constants (K)

| Shaft Type | Material | Keyway? | Recommended **K** (mm) |
|------------|----------|---------|-------------------------|
| Intermediate shaft (solid) | Forged steel ASTM A668 Gr. E | No | **270** |
| Intermediate shaft (solid) | Forged steel ASTM A668 Gr. E | Yes (single) | **280** |
| Intermediate shaft (hollow) | Forged steel | Yes | **245** |
| Thrust shaft (solid) | Forged steel, Ni-Cr-Mo | Yes | **280** |
| Tail shaft (with liner, after stern tube) | Stainless / bronze ASTM B584 | Yes | **230** |
| Tail shaft (solid bronze) | Manganese bronze | Yes | **225** |
| Propeller shaft (tail) | Forged steel with bronze liner | Yes | **240** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Cubic Root?** The torsional shear stress in a solid circular shaft is $\tau = 16T / (\pi d^3)$. Equating this to the allowable shear stress, substituting $T = 9550 \cdot P/n$ (kW, rpm), and solving for $d$ yields $d \propto (P/n)^{1/3}$. The constant $K$ absorbs $16/\pi$, the safety factor (~1.33), and the material-dependent $\tau_{all}$.

## 2.5 Keyway Dimension Rule of Thumb

For shafts with a single keyway, the diameter obtained from the formula above is the **plain diameter**. The actual shaft diameter measured at the keyway bottom is:

$$d_{actual} = d_{plain} \times 1.05 \quad \text{to} \quad 1.10$$

Equivalently, a **stress-concentration factor** of 1.5–1.8 is applied when computing fatigue life. The key width $b$ and depth $h$ follow **ISO 10945 / DIN 6885** and are typically $b = 0.20 \, d$, $h = 0.10 \, d$.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

In commercial shipyards, this procedure is executed by a **Propulsion Integration Engineer** during the **Basic Design phase** of a conversion. A miscalculation at this stage can result in:

- **Undersized shaft** → torsional fatigue fracture (catastrophic — see the *MV Friendship* stern-shaft failure, 2006).
- **Oversized shaft** → wasted steel (each mm of diameter adds ≈ 20 kg/m of mass), increased bearing load, and reduced propeller clearance.
- **Wrong engine RPM** → mismatch with the B-series propeller, leading to **heavy or light loading** and a 6–10 % fuel-penalty over the ship's life.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Shaft Diameter Formula (Full Working)

We begin with the **torsional shear stress** in a solid circular shaft:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d^{3}}$$

**Step 1 — Torque from Power**

For a shaft transmitting $P$ (kW) at $n$ (rpm), the torque is:

$$T = \frac{P \times 60 \times 1000}{2 \pi n} = \frac{9550 \cdot P}{n} \quad \text{[N·m]}$$

**Step 2 — Equate to Allowable Shear Stress**

For a **factor of safety** $N$ and material allowable shear stress $\tau_{all}$:

$$\tau_{all} = \frac{16 \, T}{N \, \pi \, d^{3}} = \frac{16 \times 9550 \cdot P}{N \, \pi \, d^{3} \, n}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $d^{3}$**

$$d^{3} = \frac{16 \times 9550}{N \, \pi \, \tau_{all}} \cdot \frac{P}{n}$$

**Step 4 — Take the Cube Root**

$$d = \left[\frac{152800}{N \, \pi \, \tau_{all}}\right]^{1/3} \cdot \left(\frac{P}{n}\right)^{1/3} \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mm and Combine Constants**

Substituting $N = 1.33$, $\tau_{all} = 49 \times 10^{6}$ Pa (forged steel):

$$K = \left[\frac{152800}{1.33 \times \pi \times 49 \times 10^{6}}\right]^{1/3} \times 1000$$

Evaluating the bracket:

$$\frac{152800}{1.33 \times \pi \times 49 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{152800}{2.046 \times 10^{8}} = 7.47 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\left(7.47 \times 10^{-4}\right)^{1/3} \approx 0.0909$$

Multiplying by 1000 to convert m → mm:

$$K \approx 275 \text{ mm}$$

Hence the working formula:

$$\boxed{d \text{ (mm)} = 275 \cdot \left[\frac{P \text{ (kW)}}{n \text{ (rpm)}}\right]^{1/3}}$$

For bronze tail shafts, a lower $\tau_{all} \approx 30$ MPa is used, and with the keyway stress concentration absorbed into the constant:

$$K_{tail} = 230 \text{ – } 240 \text{ mm}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — "MV Bharath Conversion"

A general cargo ship of 12,000 DWT is being converted into a coastal container feeder. The revised model test gives:

- $R_T$ at service speed $V_S = 14$ knots = **320 kN**
- $V_S = 14$ knots $= 7.20$ m/s
- $t = 0.14$, $w = 0.30$
- $\eta_R = 0.96$, $\eta_G = 0.98$
- Chosen service margin $S_M = 0.20$
- Chosen engine speed $n = 105$ rpm (low-speed 2-stroke)

**Step (a) — Revised EHP**

$$EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V_S}{0.7355} = \frac{320 \times 7.20}{0.7355} = 3131 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (b) — Delivered Power (DHP)**

$$DHP = \frac{(1-t) \cdot EHP}{\eta_R} = \frac{0.86 \times 3131}{0.96} = 2805 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (c) — Brake Power at Service Speed (BHP)**

$$BHP = \frac{DHP}{\eta_G} = \frac{2805}{0.98} = 2862 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (d) — Required MCR**

$$MCR = \frac{BHP}{1 - S_M} = \frac{2862}{0.80} = 3578 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (e) — Engine Selection**

A **MAN B&W 6L35MC** main engine with **MCR = 3600 kW** at **167 rpm** is the closest catalog match. We must then **either:**
- Operate at slightly derated MCR (3578 kW at 167 rpm), or
- Reduce propeller pitch $P/D$ to absorb the higher MCR at the same RPM.

Assuming $P = 3600$ kW and $n = 167$ rpm is the operating point:

**Step (f) — Intermediate Shaft Diameter (Forged Steel, with keyway)**

$$d_{IS} = 280 \cdot \left(\frac{3600}{167}\right)^{1/3} = 280 \cdot (21.56)^{1/3}$$

$$(21.56)^{1/3} = 2.776$$

$$d_{IS} = 280 \times 2.776 = 777.3 \text{ mm}$$

**Standardise to the next 10 mm: $d_{IS} = 780$ mm.**

**Step (g) — Tail Shaft Diameter (Bronze, with keyway and liner)**

$$d_{TS} = 240 \cdot \left(\frac{3600}{167}\right)^{1/3} = 240 \times 2.776 = 666.3 \text{ mm}$$

**Standardise: $d_{TS} = 670$ mm.**

**Step (h) — Keyway Dimensions (DIN 6885-1)**

For $d_{IS} = 780$ mm:
- Key width $b = 0.20 \times 780 = 156$ mm
- Key depth $h = 0.10 \times 780 = 78$ mm
- Standard $b \times h = 160 \times 90$ mm (DIN 6885 nearest).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table

| Engineering Question | Threshold / Criterion | KTU Numerical Anchor |
|----------------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Is direct drive feasible? | $n_{prop} \leq 120$ rpm | Two-stroke if $n \leq 120$ |
| What $S_M$ to use? | Naval = 0.20, Merchant = 0.15 | $S_M = 0.15$ default |
| Is the calculated $d$ manufacturable? | $d \in [100, 1000]$ mm | Else redesign |
| Is the chosen engine within 5 % of MCR? | $\vert MCR_{calc} - MCR_{cat}\vert \leq 0.05$ | Else pick next size |
| Is $T_{max} < T_{crit}$? | $T_{max} \leq 0.85 \, T_{crit}$ (torsional vibration) | Else detune |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Powering Chain — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Resistance RT at VS"] --> B["Effective Power EHP"]

    N --> O
    J --> O
    H --> P["Emissions Compliance MARPOL Tier"]
    P --> O
```
