# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 (3 marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define Service Margin and state the typical range used for (i) a merchant vessel and (ii) a naval auxiliary vessel.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Service Margin** is a multiplicative reserve factor applied to the **Service Power (SP)** to determine the **Maximum Continuous Rating (MCR)** the engine must be capable of delivering. It accounts for:
- Wind and wave allowance not captured in the model test
- Hull fouling during drydock intervals
- Future route extension or minor deadweight increase
- Margin for auxiliary load variability

Typical values:
- **Merchant vessel:** $S_M = 0.15$ (i.e., 15 %)
- **Naval auxiliary:** $S_M = 0.20$ to $0.25$ (i.e., 20–25 %), because combat vessels must operate at MCR for sustained periods and tolerate heavier weather routing.

$$MCR = \frac{SP}{1 - S_M}$$

---

### Question 2 (3 marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Why is the empirical constant $K = 240$ used for a bronze tail shaft but $K = 280$ for a forged steel intermediate shaft, even though both transmit the same torque?**

**Model Answer:**

The constant $K$ is **inversely proportional to the cube root of the material's allowable shear stress** $\tau_{all}$. Forged carbon-manganese steel (ASTM A668) has $\tau_{all} \approx 49$–$56$ MPa, while manganese bronze (ASTM B584) used for tail shafts has $\tau_{all} \approx 30$–$35$ MPa. Since bronze has a *lower* allowable stress, a *larger* diameter is required for the same torque, which mathematically means the constant $K$ should be *larger* for bronze. However, the empirical formula uses $K = 240$ for bronze and $K = 280$ for steel because:

1. The **tail shaft is often inside a bronze liner** (or is itself a steel shaft with a bronze liner), and the empirical constant was back-calculated from in-service catalogues.
2. The **stress concentration** of the keyway is more severe on a steel shaft (higher modulus) — hence a higher $K$ for steel-with-keyway.
3. The **empirical constants incorporate the safety factor** of 1.33–1.5 differently for the two materials.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often think lower $K$ means weaker material. **The opposite is true in this empirical formulation** — a *lower* $K$ means a *smaller* diameter for the same $P/n$, indicating the material can carry the load more efficiently. Always cross-check with $\tau_{all}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is mandatory.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** A 15,000 DWT general cargo ship is being converted for a new coastal container feeder service. The revised model test gives the following data at the new service speed:
- $V_S = 15$ knots = 7.72 m/s
- $R_T = 340$ kN
- Thrust deduction $t = 0.16$, Wake fraction $w = 0.32$
- $\eta_R = 0.97$, $\eta_G = 0.98$
- Service Margin $S_M = 0.20$

Compute the **revised EHP, DHP, BHP, and required MCR** in kW. (7 marks)

**(b)** For the same vessel, if the chosen main engine is a **Wärtsilä 6L46F** running at **120 rpm** with **MCR = 5400 kW**, design the **intermediate shaft (forged steel with keyway)** and the **tail shaft (bronze, with keyway)** diameters. State the keyway dimensions per DIN 6885. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**Step 1 — EHP**

$$EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V_S}{0.7355} = \frac{340 \times 7.72}{0.7355} = 3568.4 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — DHP**

$$DHP = \frac{(1-t) \cdot EHP}{\eta_R} = \frac{0.84 \times 3568.4}{0.97} = 3089.7 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — BHP (Service Power)**

$$BHP = \frac{DHP}{\eta_G} = \frac{3089.7}{0.98} = 3152.8 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — MCR**

$$MCR = \frac{BHP}{1 - S_M} = \frac{3152.8}{0.80} = 3941.0 \text{ kW}$$

**Summary Table**

| Quantity | Value (kW) |
|----------|------------|
| EHP | **3568.4** |
| DHP | **3089.7** |
| BHP (SP) | **3152.8** |
| MCR | **3941.0** |

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Step 1 — Intermediate shaft diameter (forged steel with keyway, $K = 280$)**

$$d_{IS} = 280 \cdot \left(\frac{5400}{120}\right)^{1/3} = 280 \cdot (45)^{1/3}$$

$$(45)^{1/3} = 3.5569$$

$$d_{IS} = 280 \times 3.5569 = 995.9 \text{ mm}$$

**Standardise to next 10 mm: $d_{IS} = 1000$ mm.**

**Step 2 — Tail shaft diameter (bronze with keyway, $K = 240$)**

$$d_{TS} = 240 \cdot (45)^{1/3} = 240 \times 3.5569 = 853.7 \text{ mm}$$

**Standardise: $d_{TS} = 860$ mm.**

**Step 3 — Keyway dimensions (DIN 6885-1, parallel type)**

For $d = 1000$ mm: standard key $b \times h = 200 \times 110$ mm.
For $d = 860$ mm: standard key $b \times h = 180 \times 100$ mm.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Where You Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $1 - S_M$ in the denominator** when computing MCR (very common — students write $MCR = BHP \times S_M$).
> 2. **Using $K = 275$ instead of 280** for an intermediate shaft with a single keyway. The 5 mm change translates to ~1.5 % of diameter, and examiners are strict.
> 3. **Not standardising** to a manufacturing-friendly dimension (e.g., presenting 995.9 mm instead of 1000 mm).
> 4. **Confusing** intermediate and tail shaft constants — write both clearly in your answer script.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Alternative]

**(a)** A bulk carrier of 28,000 DWT is being lengthened by 14 m to improve her block coefficient. After the conversion, the **revised EHP** at 14 knots is 4200 kW. With $t = 0.18$, $w = 0.35$, $\eta_R = 0.96$, $\eta_G = 0.98$, and a service margin of 15 %, determine the **required MCR** and select a suitable main engine from the **MAN B&W catalogue** (assume 6 cylinders, 2200 kW/cyl available). (7 marks)

**(b)** For the selected engine running at 90 rpm at MCR, calculate the **intermediate shaft diameter** using $K = 280$ mm. If the keyway ratio is $b = 0.20d$ and $h = 0.10d$, comment on whether the **key length** should be limited to 1.6 × the shaft diameter (DIN 6885 limit). (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

**Step 1 — Compute DHP**

$$DHP = \frac{(1-0.18) \times 4200}{0.96} = \frac{0.82 \times 4200}{0.96} = 3587.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Compute BHP**

$$BHP = \frac{3587.5}{0.98} = 3660.7 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — MCR**

$$MCR = \frac{3660.7}{1 - 0.15} = \frac{3660.7}{0.85} = 4306.7 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Engine Selection**

Per cylinder power = 2200 kW. For 6 cylinders:

$$MCR_{cat} = 6 \times 2200 = 13200 \text{ kW}$$

This is **vastly over-rated**. We should select a **smaller per-cylinder** option, e.g.:

- 4 cylinders × 1100 kW = 4400 kW ✓ (within 5 % of 4306.7 kW)

So the selection is: **4-cylinder 4L-series engine** with 1100 kW/cyl, MCR = 4400 kW, at 90 rpm.

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Step 1 — Intermediate shaft diameter**

$$d_{IS} = 280 \cdot \left(\frac{4400}{90}\right)^{1/3} = 280 \cdot (48.89)^{1/3}$$

$$(48.89)^{1/3} = 3.652$$

$$d_{IS} = 280 \times 3.652 = 1022.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Standardise: $d_{IS} = 1030$ mm.**

**Step 2 — Keyway dimensions**

$$b = 0.20 \times 1030 = 206 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{use standard } 200 \text{ mm}$$

$$h = 0.10 \times 1030 = 103 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{use standard } 110 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Key length check**

DIN 6885 recommends **maximum key length** $L \leq 1.6 \times d$ to prevent uneven load distribution.

$$L_{max} = 1.6 \times 1030 = 1648 \text{ mm} \approx 1650 \text{ mm}$$

The key is **acceptable** as long as it is fitted in a single piece (not sectional). Beyond 1.6 d, a **double keyway at 180°** should be used, with each key sized for 0.5 T.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):**
> 1. **Skipping the 0.5 × step** for the engine selection — if 6 cylinders are over-rated, state it explicitly and recommend 4 cylinders.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1.6 d key length check** — this is a favourite 2-mark sub-question in KTU.
> 3. **Wrong sign** in thrust deduction — students occasionally write $DHP = EHP / (1-t)$ instead of $DHP = (1-t) EHP / \eta_R$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Service Margin** is *not* added to the EHP — it is applied at the *BHP-to-MCR* stage via $MCR = BHP / (1 - S_M)$.
- The **propulsion chain is sequential**: $EHP \to (1-t) \to \eta_R \to \eta_G \to MCR$. Each step has its own physics.
- The **shaft diameter formula** $d = K (P/n)^{1/3}$ is **empirical**, not derived from first principles in KTU exams — but you should be able to derive the $K$ constant on demand.
- **Standard K values (memorise):** Intermediate shaft steel with keyway = **280**; Tail shaft bronze with keyway = **240**.
- **Always standardise** the final diameter to the next 10 mm (or as per yard practice: next 5 mm below 200, next 10 mm above 200).
- **Keyway per DIN 6885-1** for marine shafts: $b \approx 0.20 d$, $h \approx 0.10 d$, maximum key length $L_{max} = 1.6 d$ (or use double keyways).
- **Engine selection priority order:** (i) Match MCR ± 5 %, (ii) Match RPM to propeller advance coefficient, (iii) Check SFOC and emissions, (iv) Verify weight and dimensions against engine room constraints.
- **For conversions specifically:** The existing shaft line RPM *cannot* always be changed — engine RPM must be matched to the *existing* gear ratio and propeller.
- **Classification society rules** (IRS / DNV) require an additional **fatigue check** (combined bending + torsion), and torsional vibration analysis by an approved software (e.g., MAN-CEAS).
- **Safety Factor** of 1.33 is *built into* the $K$ constant; do not add it again.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Verdict:** This topic is a **guaranteed 14-marker** in any ESE. Master the powering chain, the cubic-root formula, and the keyway check — three questions, three sub-parts, three confident 7-mark answers. The examiner wants to see clean steps, standard values, and a justified final dimension. **Practise at least one full numerical before the exam.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hull-Propeller Interaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hull-Propeller Interaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Hull-Propeller Interaction** refers to the mutual hydrodynamic influence between a ship's hull and its propeller when the vessel is operating at a steady speed in calm water. It describes how the presence of the hull modifies the inflow velocity seen by the propeller, and how the propeller's suction in turn alters the resistance experienced by the hull. This interaction is quantitatively captured by two non-dimensional parameters:

- **Wake Fraction ($w$)** — the fractional reduction in the velocity of the water approaching the propeller disc compared with the ship's speed.
- **Thrust Deduction Factor ($t$)** — the fractional loss of thrust delivered to the ship relative to the total thrust generated by the propeller.

These two quantities combine to define the **Hull Efficiency ($\eta_H$)**, one of the three canonical efficiencies that together yield the overall **Propulsive Coefficient ($\eta_D$)** of a ship.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** In Module 4, the conversion of a vessel is heavily judged on its ability to absorb installed power. *Hull-Propeller Interaction* dictates whether the original engine + propeller combination remains optimal after a lengthening, re-engining, or hull-form modification operation. A conversion that ignores this interaction invariably fails the power-trial guarantee.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Escort and Trailer" Picture

Imagine a cyclist (the **ship**) pedalling along a flat road at speed $V$. Directly behind the cyclist, in the slipstream, a small electric fan (the **propeller**) blows air forward, pushing the cyclist. The fan never "sees" the still, undisturbed road air — instead, it inhales the turbulent, slow-moving air that the cyclist drags along. So the fan has to spin faster than it would in open air to produce the same thrust.

Simultaneously, the suction created by the fan reduces the pressure behind the cyclist, effectively dragging him backwards — this is the **thrust deduction** effect.

In ship terms:

| Picture Element | Naval Architecture Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Cyclist on road | Ship hull advancing through calm water |
| Slow turbulent wake behind cyclist | Velocity deficit at propeller disc |
| Fan spinning in disturbed air | Propeller operating in non-uniform wake |
| Backward pull on cyclist | Thrust deduction |
| Net forward force actually used | Effective Thrust $T_E = T(1 - t)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Wake Fraction**
> $$w \;=\; \frac{V - V_A}{V}$$
> where $V$ is the ship speed and $V_A$ is the speed of advance of the propeller (velocity of water at the disc).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Thrust Deduction Factor**
> $$t \;=\; \frac{T - T_E}{T}$$
> where $T$ is the total thrust produced at the propeller and $T_E$ is the effective thrust useful to the ship.

---

## 1.3 Why Conversion Technology Treats This as a "Pillar" Topic

When a ship is *converted* (lengthened, re-engined, or had its hull form altered), the **wetted surface area**, **block coefficient ($C_B$)**, and **afterbody shape** all change. Each of these directly shifts $w$ and $t$. A naive conversion that replaces the propeller with one of identical geometry — without re-evaluating the interaction — will typically see:

- Engine overload (RPM too high because effective thrust drops),
- Excessive slip, cavitation, and vibration, or
- Under-utilisation of the new engine power.

Hence the topic is a *prerequisite gate* for any successful conversion project.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Power Transmission Chain (Conceptual)

The thermodynamic power generated inside the engine cylinders must travel through five stages before it becomes useful work overcoming the ship's total resistance. Each stage incurs a loss:

$$
\boxed{\;P_B \;\xrightarrow{\eta_S}\; P_D \;\xrightarrow{\eta_R}\; P_T \;\xrightarrow{\eta_H}\; P_E\;}
$$

Where each symbol is:

| Symbol | Name | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $P_B$ | **Brake Power** | Power measured at engine crankshaft / output coupling |
| $P_D$ | **Delivered Power** | Power actually reaching the propeller disc ($P_D = 2 \pi n Q$) |
| $P_T$ | **Thrust Power** | Useful fluid power: $P_T = T \cdot V_A$ |
| $P_E$ | **Effective Power** | Useful power overcoming resistance: $P_E = R_T \cdot V$ |
| $\eta_S$ | **Shaft Efficiency** | Mechanical losses in shaft, bearings, gear |
| $\eta_R$ | **Rotative / Propeller Efficiency** | Conversion of $P_D$ into $P_T$ in open water ($\eta_O = \eta_R$ in KTU notation) |
| $\eta_H$ | **Hull Efficiency** | Conversion of $P_T$ into $P_E$ (this section's focus) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention Note:** In Indian naval-architecture textbooks, **$\eta_O$** (open-water propeller efficiency) and **$\eta_R$** (rotative efficiency) are sometimes combined into a single **Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient $\eta_D$**. The most exam-safe formulation is:
> $$\eta_D \;=\; \eta_H \cdot \eta_O \cdot \eta_R \;=\; \frac{P_E}{P_D}$$

---

## 2.2 Components of Hull Efficiency

Hull efficiency is the *ratio of useful power to thrust power*:

$$
\eta_H \;=\; \frac{P_E}{P_T} \;=\; \frac{R_T \cdot V}{T \cdot V_A}
$$

Because the ship moves at $V$ but the propeller "sees" a slower stream $V_A$, we may write $V_A = V(1 - w)$. Substituting, and using the relation $T_E = R_T$ (effective thrust balances resistance at constant speed):

$$
\eta_H \;=\; \frac{T_E / T}{(V_A / V)} \;=\; \frac{1 - t}{1 - w}
$$

### 2.2.1 Wake Fraction ($w$) — Physical Drivers

Wake is created by three physical mechanisms. KTU examiners commonly test these as a 3-mark short question.

1. **Potential (Wave) Wake ($w_P$):** Caused by the wave system generated by the hull, which displaces water outward near the stern. Typical magnitude: $\approx 0.05$.
2. **Frictional (Boundary-Layer) Wake ($w_F$):** Caused by the velocity deficit inside the turbulent boundary layer flowing along the hull. Dominant contribution, typically $\approx 0.7\,w$.
3. **Wake Scale Effect:** $w$ is **larger for fuller hull forms** (higher $C_B$), as the boundary layer is thicker.

Total wake:
$$w_T \;=\; w_P + w_F - w_P w_F \;\approx\; w_P + w_F$$

**Taylor's Wake Estimation** (very common KTU question):
$$w \;=\; 0.5\,C_B - 0.05 \quad (\text{for single-screw merchant ships})$$

### 2.2.2 Thrust Deduction Factor ($t$) — Physical Drivers

- **Suction effect:** The low pressure on the stern of the hull created by the propeller's rotational flow produces a force that adds to resistance.
- **Empirical bounds for single-screw ships:** $t \approx 0.4\,w$ to $0.6\,w$.
- A **fuller stern** (typical of conversion-lengthened vessels) increases $t$.

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion Insight:** After a mid-body insertion, both $w$ and $t$ increase because the afterbody remains unchanged but the parallel middle body becomes longer — making the afterbody effectively "fuller" relative to the new LWL.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Safety:** All absolute-value bars and set-membership symbols use `\vert` / `\mid` so the table layout does not break.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Key | Typical Range |
|:-:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wake Fraction | $w = \dfrac{V - V_A}{V}$ | $V_A$ = speed of advance | $0.20 - 0.40$ |
| 2 | Thrust Deduction | $t = \dfrac{T - T_E}{T}$ | $T_E = R_T$ at constant $V$ | $0.10 - 0.25$ |
| 3 | Hull Efficiency | $\eta_H = \dfrac{1 - t}{1 - w}$ | Combined effect | $1.05 - 1.30$ |
| 4 | Effective Power | $P_E = R_T \cdot V$ | $R_T$ in kN, $V$ in m/s, $P_E$ in kW | — |
| 5 | Thrust Power | $P_T = T \cdot V_A$ | $T$ in kN, $V_A$ in m/s | — |
| 6 | Delivered Power | $P_D = 2\pi n Q$ | $n$ in rps, $Q$ in kN·m | — |
| 7 | Shaft Efficiency | $\eta_S = \dfrac{P_D}{P_B}$ | Mechanical losses | $0.97 - 0.99$ |
| 8 | Propulsive Coeff. | $\eta_D = \dfrac{P_E}{P_D}$ | Overall propulsion | $0.55 - 0.70$ |
| 9 | Taylor Wake Estimate | $w = 0.5\,C_B - 0.05$ | Empirical | Single-screw |
| 10 | Effective Power Ratio | $\dfrac{P_E}{P_B} = \eta_S \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_O \cdot \eta_H$ | Full chain | $0.50 - 0.65$ |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

The **propulsive coefficient $\eta_D$** is the single number that determines fuel-burn per nautical mile. In a typical tanker conversion:

- A 1% improvement in $\eta_D$ saves roughly **1% in bunker cost** over a year.
- Hull-propeller interaction tuning (e.g., fitting a **duct (Kort nozzle)**, **grating ducts**, or a **pre-swirl fin**) can shift $\eta_H$ from 1.20 to 1.28 — a **6-7% gain** without changing the engine.

This is why *every* conversion feasibility study includes an interaction audit.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Hull Efficiency $\eta_H$

**Starting Point — definition of hull efficiency:**

By definition, hull efficiency is the ratio of the useful *effective power* delivered to the ship to the *thrust power* developed by the propeller at its disc:

$$
\eta_H \;=\; \frac{P_E}{P_T}
$$

**Step 1 — Express $P_E$ in terms of resistance and ship speed.**

The effective power is the resistance force multiplied by the speed at which the ship is actually moving:

$$
P_E \;=\; R_T \cdot V
$$

where $R_T$ is the total resistance in newtons and $V$ is the ship's speed in m/s.

**Step 2 — Express $P_T$ in terms of thrust and speed of advance.**

The thrust power is the propeller thrust multiplied by the velocity at which the propeller disc actually ingests water:

$$
P_T \;=\; T \cdot V_A
$$

where $T$ is the total propeller thrust and $V_A$ is the *speed of advance*.

**Step 3 — Apply the steady-state thrust–resistance balance.**

In straight-line, constant-speed operation, the effective thrust propelling the ship equals the total resistance:

$$
T_E \;=\; R_T
$$

**Step 4 — Rewrite $\eta_H$ using thrust ratio and velocity ratio.**

$$
\eta_H \;=\; \frac{T_E \cdot V}{T \cdot V_A}
\;=\; \frac{T_E / T}{V_A / V}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the definitions of $t$ and $w$.**

By definition:
$$
t \;=\; \frac{T - T_E}{T} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{T_E}{T} = 1 - t
$$
$$
w \;=\; \frac{V - V_A}{V} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{V_A}{V} = 1 - w
$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form.**

$$
\boxed{\;\eta_H \;=\; \frac{1 - t}{1 - w}\;}
$$

This elegant expression shows that hull efficiency exceeds unity whenever $t < w$ — a near-universal situation for displacement-mode single-screw ships.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Conversion Project Power Audit

**Problem:** A single-screw bulk carrier is to be lengthened by 10 m. After conversion the following data are estimated:

- Ship speed: $V = 15.5$ knots $= 7.97$ m/s
- Block coefficient (new): $C_B = 0.78$
- Total resistance (new): $R_T = 1\,180$ kN
- Taylor wake estimate still valid: $w = 0.5\,C_B - 0.05$
- Assume $t = 0.55\,w$ (typical empirical correlation)

Calculate: **(a)** Wake fraction, **(b)** Thrust deduction factor, **(c)** Hull efficiency, **(d)** Thrust required, **(e)** Effective power, **(f)** Thrust power.

### Solution

**Step (a) — Wake Fraction:**

$$
w \;=\; 0.5 \times 0.78 - 0.05 \;=\; 0.39 - 0.05 \;=\; 0.34
$$

**Step (b) — Thrust Deduction:**

$$
t \;=\; 0.55 \times 0.34 \;=\; 0.187
$$

**Step (c) — Hull Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_H \;=\; \frac{1 - 0.187}{1 - 0.34} \;=\; \frac{0.813}{0.66} \;=\; 1.232
$$

**Step (d) — Thrust Required:**

Using $T_E = R_T = 1180$ kN and $T_E = T(1 - t)$:

$$
T \;=\; \frac{T_E}{1 - t} \;=\; \frac{1180}{1 - 0.187} \;=\; \frac{1180}{0.813} \;=\; 1451.4 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step (e) — Effective Power:**

$$
P_E \;=\; R_T \cdot V \;=\; 1180 \times 7.97 \;=\; 9\,404.6 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step (f) — Thrust Power:**

First compute $V_A$:

$$
V_A \;=\; V(1 - w) \;=\; 7.97 \times (1 - 0.34) \;=\; 7.97 \times 0.66 \;=\; 5.2602 \text{ m/s}
$$

Then:

$$
P_T \;=\; T \cdot V_A \;=\; 1451.4 \times 5.2602 \;=\; 7\,634.6 \text{ kW}
$$

**Verification using $\eta_H$:**

$$
\eta_H \;=\; \frac{P_E}{P_T} \;=\; \frac{9404.6}{7634.6} \;=\; 1.232 \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Full Propulsive Chain

$$
\eta_D \;=\; \frac{P_E}{P_D}
\;=\; \underbrace{\frac{P_E}{P_T}}_{\eta_H} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{P_T}{P_D}}_{\eta_O \cdot \eta_R}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_B \;=\; \frac{P_E}{\eta_S \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_O \cdot \eta_H}
$$

This is the *master equation* used by KTU examiners when sizing a replacement engine after a conversion.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Before vs. After Conversion

| Parameter | Before Conversion | After Lengthening (L + 10 m) | Effect on $\eta_H$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_B$ | $0.72$ | $0.78$ | $w \uparrow$ |
| Wake $w$ | $0.31$ | $0.34$ | $1-w \downarrow$ |
| $t$ | $0.171$ | $0.187$ | $1-t \downarrow$ |
| $\eta_H$ | $\frac{0.829}{0.69}=1.20$ | $\frac{0.813}{0.66}=1.23$ | **Improves** $\uparrow$ |
| $P_E$ (kW) | $8\,900$ | $9\,405$ | Demand $\uparrow$ |
| Notes | — | Full afterbody → larger $\eta_H$ | Favourable conversion |

> [!TIP]
> **Take-away:** Counter-intuitively, lengthening often **increases** hull efficiency — but the gain is overwhelmed by the larger $P_E$ demand, so a larger or more efficient engine is still required.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hull-Propeller Interaction

The Mermaid diagram below traces the *power-flow architecture* showing how hull and propeller feed back on each other. It is rendered using only safe alphanumeric node identifiers and raw uppercase labels (no markdown inside quoted labels) in compliance with KTU-PREMIER rendering rules.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[ENGINE CRANKSHAFT] --> A2[SHAFTLINE GEARING]
    A2 --> A3[TAIL SHAFT BEARINGS]
    A3 --> A4[PROPELLER DISC]
    A4 --> A5[THRUST BEARING TO HULL]
    A5 --> A6[SHIP HULL WETTED SURFACE]

    B1[CALM WATER V INF] --> B2[HULL BOUNDARY LAYER]
    B2 --> B3[POTENTIAL FLOW WAVE WAKE]
    B3 --> B4[COMBINED WAKE FIELD]
    B4 --> A4

    A4 --> C1[PROP SUCTION LOW PRESSURE]
    C1 --> C2[STERN PRESSURE DROP]
    C2 --> C6[ADDED HULL RESISTANCE]

    A4 --> A7[THRUST T KILO N]
    A7 --> A8[EFFECTIVE THRUST T E]
    A8 --> A6

    A7 --> D1[WAKE FRACTION W]
    A8 --> D2[THRUST DEDUCTION T]
    D1 --> D3[HULL EFFICIENCY ETA H]
    D2 --> D3

    D3 --> E1[PROPULSIVE COEFF ETA D]
    A4 --> E2[OPEN WATER EFF ETA O]
    A2 --> E3[SHAFT EFF ETA S]
    E2 --> E1
    E3 --> E1

    E1 --> F1[BUNKER CONSUMPTION T PER DAY]
    E1 --> F2[POWERING GUARANTEE CHECK]
    E1 --> F3[EMISSION INDEX G PER TON NM]
```

---

## 4.2 Component-Interaction Flow (Stern-View Block Diagram)

The next diagram zooms into the *interaction loop* between hull afterbody and propeller disc, showing the two opposing coupling arrows.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HULL_BLOCK
        H1[HULL AFTERBODY FORM]
        H2[BOUNDARY LAYER VELOCITY DEFICIT]
        H3[POTENTIAL WAKE COMPONENT]
    end

    subgraph DISC_BLOCK
        D1[PROPELLER DISC]
        D2[ROTATING BLADE WAKE]
        D3[CAVITATION REGION]
    end

    H1 --> H2
    H2 --> D1
    H3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D1
    D1 --> D3
    D3 --> H1

    H2 -.->|WAKE FRACTION W| M1[MUTUAL INTERACTION CORE]
    D3 -.->|THRUST DEDUCTION T| M1
    M1 -->|ETA H| OUT[OUTPUT HULL EFFICIENCY]
```

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Recap of the Architecture

1. **Forward path (top-down):** Mechanical power travels from engine → shaft → propeller disc → thrust bearing → hull.
2. **Feedback path (bottom-up):** The hull's boundary layer and wave system slow the inflow to the disc → this is **wake**.
3. **Side path:** The propeller's suction lowers the stern pressure → this is **thrust deduction**, which increases hull resistance.
4. **Output path:** Both feedback arrows combine into the **hull efficiency $\eta_H$**, which feeds the propulsive coefficient $\eta_D$ and ultimately governs fuel consumption.

> [!NOTE]
> The two feedback loops (wake and suction) are the *only* reasons a hull efficiency value of $1.0$ is *never* achievable. A perfectly clean installation would give $\eta_H = 1.0$; in reality we live in the regime $1.05 \le \eta_H \le 1.30$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define **Wake Fraction** and **Thrust Deduction Factor**. State their typical ranges for single-screw merchant ships.

**Model Answer:**

> **Wake Fraction ($w$):** The fractional reduction of the velocity of water at the propeller disc compared with the ship's speed:
> $$w \;=\; \frac{V - V_A}{V}$$
> **Thrust Deduction Factor ($t$):** The fractional loss of effective thrust available to overcome ship resistance relative to the total thrust produced at the propeller:
> $$t \;=\; \frac{T - T_E}{T}$$
> **Typical ranges (single-screw merchant ships):**
> - $w \approx 0.20$ to $0.40$ (fuller forms at the higher end).
> - $t \approx 0.10$ to $0.25$, with $t \approx 0.5\,w$ to $0.6\,w$ as a rule of thumb.
> *(3 marks: definition 1 m + formula 1 m + ranges 1 m)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is **Hull Efficiency**? Why does it usually exceed unity for single-screw displacement ships?

**Model Answer:**

Hull efficiency is defined as:
$$\eta_H \;=\; \frac{1 - t}{1 - w}$$
It is the ratio of effective power to thrust power. Because the wake $w$ is almost always numerically greater than the thrust-deduction factor $t$ (i.e., $t < w$), the numerator $(1-t)$ is larger than the denominator $(1-w)$, so $\eta_H > 1$. Physically, the propeller is "helped" by the fact that it ingests slow-moving water (less work to push the ship at speed $V$), while the suction drag is a smaller penalty than that benefit. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — *(14 Marks)*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

> **(a)** Derive an expression for **Hull Efficiency** in terms of wake fraction $w$ and thrust deduction factor $t$, clearly stating the assumptions used. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A single-screw bulk carrier has $V = 14.0$ knots, $C_B = 0.74$, $R_T = 980$ kN, and $t = 0.55\,w$. Using Taylor's wake estimate, compute (i) the wake fraction, (ii) the hull efficiency, (iii) the propeller thrust, (iv) the effective power, and (v) the thrust power. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):** Full derivation reproduced as in **§3.1** above. State assumptions:
- Constant speed, straight-line motion.
- $T_E = R_T$ (force balance).
- Uniform inflow at the disc (Taylor-style simplification).
- Quasi-steady propeller operation.

The examiner expects 5-6 numbered algebraic steps, each carrying partial credit.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Convert speed: $V = 14.0 \text{ knots} = 14.0 \times 0.5144 = 7.2016$ m/s.

*(i) Wake fraction (Taylor):*
$$w = 0.5\,C_B - 0.05 = 0.5 \times 0.74 - 0.05 = 0.37 - 0.05 = 0.32$$

*(ii) Thrust deduction factor:*
$$t = 0.55 \times 0.32 = 0.176$$

*(iii) Hull efficiency:*
$$\eta_H = \frac{1 - 0.176}{1 - 0.32} = \frac{0.824}{0.68} = 1.212$$

*(iv) Propeller thrust:* With $T_E = R_T = 980$ kN:
$$T = \frac{980}{1 - 0.176} = \frac{980}{0.824} = 1189.3 \text{ kN}$$

*(v) Effective power:*
$$P_E = R_T \cdot V = 980 \times 7.2016 = 7057.6 \text{ kW}$$

*(vi) Thrust power:* First compute $V_A$:
$$V_A = V(1 - w) = 7.2016 \times 0.68 = 4.8971 \text{ m/s}$$
$$P_T = T \cdot V_A = 1189.3 \times 4.8971 = 5824.7 \text{ kW}$$

*Verification:* $\eta_H = P_E / P_T = 7057.6 / 5824.7 = 1.212$ ✓

---

### Question B — *(14 Marks)* *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2023, Module 4]**

> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **components of wake** experienced by a ship's propeller. How does wake affect the design choice of propeller diameter during conversion? *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** The following trial data are available for a twin-screw naval auxiliary after a length-by-5 m conversion:
>
> - Brake power $P_B = 6200$ kW
> - Shaft efficiency $\eta_S = 0.98$
> - Open-water efficiency $\eta_O = 0.62$
> - Rotative efficiency $\eta_R = 0.99$
> - Effective power $P_E = 3850$ kW
>
> Compute (i) Delivered power $P_D$, (ii) Propulsive coefficient $\eta_D$, and (iii) Hull efficiency $\eta_H$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Components of wake (7 marks):** Refer to **§2.2.1**.

- **Frictional wake ($w_F$):** Created by the turbulent boundary layer along the hull. Largest contributor, ≈ 70 % of total $w$. Mention that this is a function of Reynolds number and hull roughness.
- **Potential (wave) wake ($w_P$):** Created by the divergent wave system radiating from the hull's bow and stern. Smaller contribution, ≈ 5 %–10 %.
- **Wake distribution** — at the disc, $w$ is *non-uniform* both radially and circumferentially. Upper-blade regions see higher $w$ than lower-blade — this leads to **non-uniform inflow** and possible cavitation if not designed for.

**Conversion design implication:** Higher $w$ reduces $V_A$, so the propeller must absorb the same $P_D$ at lower advance coefficient $J$. To avoid over-loading, designers either (i) **reduce propeller pitch**, (ii) **increase diameter** (if aperture permits), or (iii) **add a duct** to recover rotational energy.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

*(i) Delivered power:*
$$P_D = \eta_S \cdot P_B = 0.98 \times 6200 = 6076 \text{ kW}$$

*(ii) Propulsive coefficient:*
$$\eta_D = \frac{P_E}{P_D} = \frac{3850}{6076} = 0.6336$$

*(iii) Hull efficiency:* From $\eta_D = \eta_S \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_O \cdot \eta_H$:
$$\eta_H = \frac{\eta_D}{\eta_S \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_O} = \frac{0.6336}{0.98 \times 0.99 \times 0.62}$$
$$\eta_H = \frac{0.6336}{0.6015} = 1.053$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):** Students frequently confuse $V$ (ship speed in m/s) with $V$ (in knots) and pick up a **unit-conversion** deduction of 1 mark. Always convert knots → m/s using the factor **0.5144** *before* computing power. A second common loss is in **sign convention** for $w$ and $t$: both are *positive fractions*, so $(1-w)$ and $(1-t)$ are both *less than* unity. If a student writes $\eta_H < 1$ they will lose 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section before your exam.**

- ✅ **Wake fraction** $w = (V - V_A)/V$ — propeller "sees" slower water.
- ✅ **Thrust deduction** $t = (T - T_E)/T$ — propeller suction drags hull back.
- ✅ **Hull efficiency** $\eta_H = (1-t)/(1-w)$ — typically **1.05–1.30** for single-screw.
- ✅ **Effective power** $P_E = R_T \cdot V$ — what the ship *needs*.
- ✅ **Thrust power** $P_T = T \cdot V_A$ — what the propeller *produces as fluid work*.
- ✅ **Delivered power** $P_D = 2\pi n Q$ — power at the propeller hub.
- ✅ **Brake power** $P_B = P_E / (\eta_S \eta_R \eta_O \eta_H)$ — what the engine *delivers*.
- ✅ **Taylor wake estimate** $w = 0.5\,C_B - 0.05$ — single-screw merchant rule of thumb.
- ✅ **Conversion rule:** Lengthening $\Rightarrow C_B \uparrow \Rightarrow w \uparrow \Rightarrow \eta_H \uparrow$, but $P_E$ also $\uparrow$, so engine still needs re-rating.
- ✅ **Two feedback loops in any ship:** (1) hull → propeller (wake), (2) propeller → hull (suction/thrust deduction).
- ✅ **Examiner favourite unit:** Convert **knots → m/s** by multiplying by **0.5144**.
- ✅ **Cavitation alert:** High local wake + high blade tip speed = cavitation risk → may need **section skew** or **tip vane** modifications during conversion.

> **End of Module 4 — Hull-Propeller Interaction Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ship Resistance Under Trial Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ship Resistance Under Trial Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ship Resistance Under Trial Conditions** refers to the total hydrodynamic resistance offered by a vessel during its official **Sea Trials**, performed to verify contractual speed and power guarantees between the shipbuilder and the shipowner. The trial results are then analytically corrected to **Standard Reference Conditions** (deep, calm, unrestricted water) using internationally accepted procedures defined by the **International Towing Tank Conference (ITTC 1978 / 2014)** and **ISO 15016:2015**.

The total resistance encountered by a ship moving at speed $V$ through water is the **Total Resistance ($R_T$)**, decomposed as:

$$R_T = R_F + R_R + R_{APP} + R_{W} + R_{A}$$

where:
- $R_F$ = **Frictional Resistance** (viscous skin friction)
- $R_R$ = **Residual Resistance** (wave-making + wave-breaking + viscous-pressure)
- $R_{APP}$ = **Appendage Resistance** (rudder, bilge keels, shaft brackets)
- $R_W$ = **Wave-added Resistance** due to sea state during trial
- $R_A$ = **Air Resistance** (wind contribution)

During trials, all five components are *implicitly bundled* into the **Effective Horsepower (EHP)**; then corrections are mathematically peeled away to obtain the **Contract Equivalence** the buyer demands.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Driving a New Car on a Delivery Run**
> Imagine you buy a car advertised at "max speed 180 km/h, fuel efficiency 15 km/L." The dealer delivers the car, and on a windy, hilly day, it does only 165 km/h and gives 13 km/L. The company doesn't shrug and say "sorry, your car is faulty." Instead, they *standardise* the run: subtract the headwind effect, subtract the uphill slope, add a correction for the cold engine, and report the **corrected** values 180 / 15. This is exactly what happens during **Ship Trials**.
>
> A ship is built and contracted to do 20 knots at 25,000 BHP in **still water, deep sea, no wind, no waves, clean hull**. Trial day comes with headwind, 1.5 m waves, and a current. The ship only does 19.4 knots. The Naval Architect then applies **corrections** to mathematically erase the wind, waves, current, and shallow-water effects — proving the ship would have hit 20+ knots under the contract.

This correction step is the single most important deliverable of a Sea Trial.

---

## 1.3 Why Trials and Corrections Matter in Conversion Projects

In the context of **Ship Conversion Technology** (a repowering or major alteration project), trial-condition analysis is critical because:

| Conversion Event | Trial Concern |
|---|---|
| Engine replacement (repowering) | New EHP required must match new trial-derived resistance |
| Hull form modification (bulbous bow, stern appendage) | Changes residual resistance — new trial cycle mandatory |
| Re-engining with hybrid propulsion | EHP/SHP ratio shifts — requires fresh correlation |
| Block-coefficient change from lengthening | $C_B$ change alters wave resistance drastically |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Favourite:** When asked "Why are trial corrections applied?" — the answer is ALWAYS: *"To enable a fair, repeatable comparison between ships of different classes and trial environments, ensuring contractual speed/power guarantees are evaluated under identical standard reference conditions."*

---

## 1.4 Reference Standards — The "Big Four"

The following standards govern trial-condition corrections; examiners expect students to name them:

1. **ITTC 1978 / 2014 Procedure** — Friction line & form factor
2. **ISO 15016:2015** — Ships and marine technology — Sea trials
3. **IACS SVR (Statutory Verification of Required Power)** — For EEDI/EEXI
4. **ISO 3046** — Reciprocating internal combustion engines — Power declaration

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants Used in This Module

- **Sea water density:** $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1025 \ kg/m^3}$ (standard, 35‰ salinity, 15 °C)
- **Kinematic viscosity of sea water:** $\nu_{sw} = \mathbf{1.1883 \times 10^{-6} \ m^2/s}$
- **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = \mathbf{9.81 \ m/s^2}$
- **1 nautical mile:** $1852 \ m$
- **1 metric horsepower:** $1 \ HP = \mathbf{0.7355 \ kW}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Total Resistance

The hydrodynamic resistance of a ship is a *superposition* of independent physical phenomena:

### 2.1.1 Frictional Resistance ($R_F$)

Generated by the **viscous shear** of water flowing along the wetted hull surface. Always present, dominant at low-to-medium Froude numbers.

$$R_F = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S \cdot C_F$$

where $S$ is the **wetted surface area** and $C_F$ is the **frictional resistance coefficient**.

### 2.1.2 Residual Resistance ($R_R$)

Everything that is *not* friction: wave-making, wave-breaking, eddy-making, hull-pressure viscous effects. Determined experimentally from model tests.

$$R_R = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S \cdot C_R$$

### 2.1.3 Appendage Resistance ($R_{APP}$)

Bonus resistance from shaft brackets, rudders, stabiliser fins, bilge keels, sonar domes. Often expressed as a multiplier on $R_F$:

$$R_{APP} = (1 + k_{app}) \cdot R_F, \quad k_{app} \approx 0.04 \text{ to } 0.08$$

### 2.1.4 Correlation Allowance ($R_{CA}$)

A fudge factor (up to +15% to +20% in old practice) accounting for the unavoidable discrepancy between model basin predictions and full-scale trials. ITTC 2014 prefers using Reynolds-number-corrected form factor instead of $R_{CA}$.

---

## 2.2 The ITTC 1978 Friction Line (Mandatory Formula)

The ITTC standard correlation for frictional coefficient is:

$$C_{F,ITTC} = \frac{0.075}{\left( \log_{10} R_n - 2 \right)^2}$$

The **Reynolds number** for the ship is based on length between perpendiculars $L_{PP}$:

$$R_n = \frac{V \cdot L_{PP}}{\nu}$$

where $\nu = 1.1883 \times 10^{-6} \ m^2/s$ for standard sea water at 15 °C.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This formula is the **single most-tested equation** in KTU Module 4. Memorise it. Note the *exact* constants 0.075 and the squared denominator — changing them changes the answer significantly.

---

## 2.3 Form Factor (1 + k)

The ratio between model total resistance coefficient and model frictional coefficient gives the **form factor**, which is assumed to scale from model to ship:

$$1 + k = \frac{C_{T,model}}{C_{F,model}}$$

The full-scale total resistance coefficient is then:

$$C_{T,ship} = (1 + k) \cdot C_{F,ship} + C_{R,ship}$$

The term $(1+k) \cdot C_{F,ship}$ is often called the **"frictional + form resistance"** and is the ITTC 2014 substitute for the older $R_{CA}$.

---

## 2.4 Effective Horsepower (EHP)

The power needed to tow the ship at speed $V$ through still water:

$$\boxed{EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{75} \quad \text{(metric HP, if $R$ in kgf and $V$ in m/s)}}$$

Or in SI units:

$$P_E = R_T \cdot V \quad [W]$$

Conversion: $1 \ \text{metric HP} = 735.5 \ W = 0.7355 \ kW$

---

## 2.5 The Three Horsepowers (Vital Hierarchy)

| Symbol | Name | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **EHP** | Effective Horsepower | $R_T \times V$ — pure hydrodynamic |
| **DHP** | Delivered Horsepower | EHP + mechanical losses in shaft/propeller line |
| **BHP / SHP** | Brake / Shaft Horsepower | DHP + bearing & gearbox losses |

Relation:

$$SHP = \frac{EHP}{\eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_S} = \frac{EHP}{\eta_D}$$

where $\eta_D$ is the **propulsive coefficient** (typically 0.55 – 0.70 for modern single-screw ships).

---

## 2.6 Trial-Condition Correction Theory

Trial-day readings are **corrected** to standard reference conditions in two stages:

### Stage 1 — Speed Correction (Add/subtract environmental effects)

$$V_{corrected} = V_{measured} + \Delta V_{wind} + \Delta V_{wave} + \Delta V_{current} + \Delta V_{shallow}$$

Each $\Delta V$ term is *positive* if it decelerates the ship (wind/wave/current against), *negative* if along.

### Stage 2 — Power Correction (Cubic law — proportional to $V^3$)

$$\boxed{\frac{P_{corrected}}{P_{measured}} = \left( \frac{V_{corrected}}{V_{measured}} \right)^3}$$

The **cube law** arises because $P \propto R_T \cdot V \propto V \cdot V^2 = V^3$.

---

## 2.7 Complete KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_n = \dfrac{V L_{PP}}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless |
| 2 | $C_{F,ITTC} = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log R_n - 2)^2}$ | ITTC friction line | dimensionless |
| 3 | $R_F = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_F$ | Frictional resistance | N |
| 4 | $1+k = \dfrac{C_{T,m}}{C_{F,m}}$ | Form factor | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_T = (1+k)C_F + C_R + C_A + C_{APP}$ | Total coeff. | dimensionless |
| 6 | $EHP = \dfrac{R_T V}{75}$ | Effective HP (metric) | HP |
| 7 | $SHP = \dfrac{EHP}{\eta_D}$ | Shaft HP | HP |
| 8 | $\dfrac{P_{corr}}{P_{meas}} = \left( \dfrac{V_{corr}}{V_{meas}} \right)^3$ | Power correction (cube law) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $C_A = \dfrac{\Delta^{2/3} V^3}{P}$ | Admiralty coefficient | varies |
| 10 | $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g L_{PP}}}$ | Froude number | dimensionless |
| 11 | $k_{app} \approx 0.04$–$0.08$ | Appendage factor | dimensionless |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Contractual binding** — Speed/power guarantee legally hinges on corrected trial values.
- **EEDI / EEXI compliance** — $P_{ME}$ declared for IMO Carbon Intensity Index relies on corrected trial SHP.
- **Warranty benchmarking** — Engine OEMs (MAN B&W, Wärtsilä) use trial-corrected SHP for specific fuel-oil-consumption guarantees.
- **Hull-form iteration** — Trial residuals guide designers to refine bulbous bow, stern shape, or trim.
- **Conversion cost–benefit** — Repowering projects compare **new trial EHP** vs **old trial EHP** to validate payback.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the ITTC Friction-Line Coefficient

The ITTC 1978 line was synthesised from the **Schönherr friction line** (1932) and **Tatinclaux–Mercier** corrections, fitted to the form:

$$C_F = \frac{A}{(log_{10} R_n - B)^2}$$

A **least-squares regression** on the model–ship correlation data gave the constants:

$$A = 0.075, \quad B = 2$$

These constants are not derived from first principles; they are an empirical fit that has been ratified by every ITTC conference since 1957 and confirmed in ITTC 2014. The **theoretical underpinning** is that for turbulent boundary layers, $C_F \sim R_n^{-1/5}$ (Prandtl's 1/5-power law), and $1/(log R_n)^2$ closely approximates that decay.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Cube Law for Power Correction

**Starting premise:** Power is force times velocity.

$$P = R_T \cdot V$$

**Assumption:** For small variations $\delta V$ around the trial speed, $R_T$ scales approximately as $V^2$ (because $R_T \propto V^2 \cdot C_T(V)$, and $C_T$ is treated as approximately constant over a small speed increment).

$$R_T(V) \approx k V^2$$

Therefore:

$$P(V) \approx k V^2 \cdot V = k V^3$$

Taking the ratio of corrected to measured:

$$\frac{P_{corr}}{P_{meas}} = \frac{k V_{corr}^3}{k V_{meas}^3} = \left( \frac{V_{corr}}{V_{meas}} \right)^3$$

This completes the derivation. The cube exponent is exact **only** if $C_T$ is constant over the speed interval; in practice it is an excellent approximation for $\Delta V/V < 5\%$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example #1 — Trial Speed Correction

**Given:**
A bulk carrier is on sea trial. Measured speed = **18.4 knots**. Environmental deltas:
- Headwind = 15 knots → $\Delta V_{wind} = +0.35$ knots
- Beam wave, $H_{1/3} = 1.5$ m → $\Delta V_{wave} = +0.42$ knots
- Following current = 0.6 knots → $\Delta V_{current} = -0.6$ knots
- Deep water, no shallow correction.

**Find:** Corrected trial speed.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

$$\Delta V_{total} = \Delta V_{wind} + \Delta V_{wave} + \Delta V_{current}$$

$$\Delta V_{total} = (+0.35) + (+0.42) + (-0.60)$$

$$\Delta V_{total} = +0.17 \ \text{knots}$$

Applying speed correction:

$$V_{corrected} = V_{measured} + \Delta V_{total}$$

$$V_{corrected} = 18.4 + 0.17 = \mathbf{18.57 \ \text{knots}}$$

The ship actually performed better than the measurement suggested — environmental drag slowed it by 0.17 knots.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example #2 — Cube-Law Power Correction

**Given (continuing Example 1):**
Measured SHP at 18.4 knots = **12,400 kW**. Find corrected SHP at $V_{corr} = 18.57$ knots.

**Solution:**

$$\frac{P_{corr}}{P_{meas}} = \left( \frac{V_{corr}}{V_{meas}} \right)^3$$

$$\frac{P_{corr}}{12400} = \left( \frac{18.57}{18.4} \right)^3$$

Step A — Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{18.57}{18.40} = 1.009239$$

Step B — Cube it:

$$(1.009239)^3 = 1.02787$$

Step C — Multiply by measured power:

$$P_{corr} = 12400 \times 1.02787 = \mathbf{12745.6 \ kW}$$

The corrected SHP is **12,746 kW**, ~346 kW higher than measured — meaning the trial environment was *helping* the engine (current was favourable). Without this correction, the builder would over-credit the engine and under-credit the environment.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example #3 — EHP from $R_T$ and Speed

**Given:**
$L_{PP} = 200$ m, $S = 9000$ m², $V = 15$ knots = $7.716$ m/s, $\nu = 1.1883 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $C_{T} = 3.8 \times 10^{-3}$, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³.

**Find:** EHP in kW.

**Step 1:** Convert speed:

$$V = 15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716 \ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute total resistance:

$$R_T = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_T$$

$$R_T = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (7.716)^2 \times 9000 \times 3.8 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_T = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 59.54 \times 9000 \times 0.0038$$

$$R_T = \mathbf{1{,}044{,}950 \ N} \quad (\approx 1045 \ kN)$$

**Step 3:** Effective power in W:

$$P_E = R_T \cdot V = 1044950 \times 7.716$$

$$P_E = 8{,}063{,}930 \ W$$

**Step 4:** Convert to kW:

$$\boxed{P_E \approx 8064 \ kW}$$

**Step 5 (verification, optional):** In metric HP:

$$EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{75 \cdot 9.81} = \frac{8063930}{735.75} \approx 10{,}960 \ \text{HP}$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example #4 — ITTC Friction Coefficient and Frictional Resistance

**Given:**
Same vessel, $V = 7.716$ m/s, $L_{PP} = 200$ m, $\nu = 1.1883 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $S = 9000$ m², $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³.

**Step 1:** Reynolds number:

$$R_n = \frac{V \cdot L_{PP}}{\nu} = \frac{7.716 \times 200}{1.1883 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R_n = \frac{1543.2}{1.1883 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.299 \times 10^9$$

**Step 2:** ITTC friction coefficient:

$$\log_{10} R_n = \log_{10}(1.299 \times 10^9) = 9.1136$$

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(9.1136 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(7.1136)^2} = \frac{0.075}{50.603}$$

$$C_F = 1.4823 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3:** Frictional resistance:

$$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (7.716)^2 \times 9000 \times 1.4823 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 59.54 \times 9000 \times 0.001482$$

$$R_F \approx \mathbf{407{,}400 \ N}$$

**Observation:** $R_F$ is ~39% of $R_T$, confirming friction is a major but not dominant component at $F_n \approx 0.17$.

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example #5 — Admiralty Coefficient Check

**Given:**
Displacement $\Delta = 35{,}000$ tonnes, $V = 18.57$ knots (corrected), $SHP_{corr} = 12{,}746$ kW.

**Find:** Admiralty coefficient.

$$C_A = \frac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3}{P}$$

$$\Delta^{2/3} = (35000)^{2/3} = (35000^{1/3})^2$$

$$35000^{1/3} \approx 32.77 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (32.77)^2 \approx 1073.9$$

$$V^3 = (18.57)^3 = 6402.5$$

$$C_A = \frac{1073.9 \times 6402.5}{12746} = \frac{6{,}876{,}148}{12746}$$

$$\boxed{C_A \approx 539.5 \quad \text{(arbitrary units)}}$$

Sister-ship comparisons within ±5% confirm the propulsion package is performing as designed.

---

## 3.8 The "Order of Magnitude" Sanity-Check Matrix

| Quantity | Typical Range | Sanity Flag |
|---|---|---|
| $C_F$ (ITTC) | $1.3 \times 10^{-3}$ to $2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ | If $<10^{-4}$ → check $\log R_n$ |
| $C_T$ (full scale) | $3 \times 10^{-3}$ to $6 \times 10^{-3}$ | Bulker lower, tanker higher |
| $\eta_D$ | 0.55 – 0.70 | Single screw low, twin screw higher |
| $F_n$ trial | 0.15 – 0.30 | Above 0.30 → planing/hydrofoil regime |
| $C_A$ (Admiralty) | 400 – 700 | Varies widely by ship type |
| Cube-law error | $<2\%$ if $\Delta V/V < 5\%$ | Use full $\Delta V \cdot V^2$ method if larger |
