# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the standard reference conditions to which a ship's trial results are corrected. State the standard values of seawater density and kinematic viscosity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Reference conditions (2 marks):**
> 1. **Deep, unrestricted water** (depth > 10 × draft)
> 2. **Calm, still water** (no wind, no waves, no current)
> 3. **Standard seawater temperature** 15 °C, salinity 35 ‰
> 4. **Clean hull** with standard appendages
> 5. **Trial displacement** equal to design loadline displacement
>
> **Standard values (1 mark):**
> - $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1025 \ kg/m^3}$
> - $\nu_{sw} = \mathbf{1.1883 \times 10^{-6} \ m^2/s}$

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the "Cube Law" of power correction in sea trials? Under what condition is it valid?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Cube Law states that **the corrected power is to the measured power as the cube of the corrected speed is to the cube of the measured speed:**
>
> $$\frac{P_{corr}}{P_{meas}} = \left( \frac{V_{corr}}{V_{meas}} \right)^3$$
>
> It is derived from $P = R_T \cdot V$ with $R_T \propto V^2$ (1 mark for derivation).
>
> **Validity:** The law is valid only when the **fractional speed change $\Delta V/V$ is small (< 5%)** so that the total resistance coefficient $C_T$ can be treated as constant over the interval (1 mark).
> If $\Delta V/V > 5\%$, a **full $\Delta R_T(V)$ computation** using the ITTC friction line and form factor must be used (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Module Internal Choice — Select **ONE** of the two questions below

---

### **Question B.A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

A 30,000 DWT bulk carrier undergoes sea trials. The following data were recorded:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Length between perpendiculars, $L_{PP}$ | 190 m |
| Wetted surface area, $S$ | 8,400 m² |
| Measured speed, $V_m$ | 16.8 knots |
| Measured shaft power, $SHP_m$ | 9,800 kW |
| Headwind | 20 knots → $\Delta V_{wind} = +0.45$ knots |
| Beam sea, $H_{1/3}$ | 1.2 m → $\Delta V_{wave} = +0.30$ knots |
| Following current | 0.4 knots → $\Delta V_{current} = -0.4$ knots |
| Sea water density $\rho$ | 1025 kg/m³ |
| Kinematic viscosity $\nu$ | $1.1883 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s |
| ITTC friction coefficient $C_F$ at trial $R_n$ | $1.51 \times 10^{-3}$ |

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**(i)** Compute the corrected trial speed. **(ii)** Apply the cube law to obtain corrected shaft power. **(iii)** Compute the effective power $P_E$ at the corrected speed using $C_T = 4.0 \times 10^{-3}$ and $S = 8400$ m².

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**(i)** Compute the propulsive coefficient $\eta_D$. **(ii)** Compute the frictional resistance at the corrected speed using the updated Reynolds number. **(iii)** Comment on whether the result satisfies the contractual guarantee of 17 knots at 11,000 kW SHP.

---

#### Model Solution B.A

**Part (a) Solution:**

**(i) Corrected speed (2 marks):**

$$\Delta V_{total} = (+0.45) + (+0.30) + (-0.40) = +0.35 \ \text{knots}$$

$$V_{corr} = 16.8 + 0.35 = \mathbf{17.15 \ knots}$$

**(ii) Cube-law power correction (2 marks):**

$$\frac{P_{corr}}{P_{meas}} = \left( \frac{17.15}{16.80} \right)^3$$

$$\frac{17.15}{16.80} = 1.02083$$

$$(1.02083)^3 = 1.06366$$

$$SHP_{corr} = 9800 \times 1.06366 = \mathbf{10{,}423.9 \ kW}$$

**(iii) Effective power at corrected speed (3 marks):**

Convert: $V_{corr} = 17.15 \times 0.5144 = 8.824$ m/s

$$P_E = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_T$$

$$P_E = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (8.824)^2 \times 8400 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P_E = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 77.86 \times 8400 \times 0.004$$

$$P_E = \mathbf{1{,}340{,}690 \ W} = \mathbf{1340.7 \ kW}$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

**(i) Propulsive coefficient (2 marks):**

$$\eta_D = \frac{P_E}{SHP_{corr}} = \frac{1340.7}{10423.9}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_D = 0.1286 \approx 12.86\%}$$

**⚠ This is unrealistically low — see examiner warning below.**

**(ii) Frictional resistance at corrected speed (3 marks):**

New Reynolds number:

$$R_n = \frac{V_{corr} \cdot L_{PP}}{\nu} = \frac{8.824 \times 190}{1.1883 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R_n = \frac{1676.56}{1.1883 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.411 \times 10^9$$

Updated ITTC $C_F$:

$$\log R_n = 9.1496 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_F = \frac{0.075}{(9.1496-2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{51.108} = 1.4676 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (8.824)^2 \times 8400 \times 1.4676 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_F \approx 492{,}430 \ N \approx 492.4 \ kN$$

**(iii) Contractual verdict (2 marks):**
The contract required **17.0 knots at 11,000 kW SHP**. Achieved corrected values are **17.15 knots at 10,424 kW SHP**. Since the corrected trial speed **exceeds** contract by 0.15 knots AND corrected power is **below** contract by 576 kW, the builder has **substantially met the contract with margin**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall B.A:**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Students often *subtract* the current delta instead of recognising the sign convention. Headwind and head waves decelerate → add; following current accelerates → subtract.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert knots → m/s before computing $P_E = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_T$. Marks lost on every line of subsequent algebra.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Reporting $\eta_D$ as 0.1286 (12.86%) without flagging that this is physically absurd. **Real ships have $\eta_D = 0.55$–0.70.** The intended computation in this question used simplified coefficients; in a real exam, if $\eta_D$ falls outside 0.4–0.8, the student should note "the input $C_T$ value is low for a bulk carrier — actual full-scale $\eta_D$ would be much higher with realistic coefficients." This demonstrates examiner-grade critical thinking.
> - **Common mistake 4:** Reporting $V_{corr} = 17.15$ knots without writing the **corrected speed is what the ship would have done in standard conditions** — that explanatory sentence is worth 1 mark by itself.

---

### **Question B.B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

A 12,000 TEU container vessel undergoes post-conversion trial after installation of a **new bulbous bow and a high-efficiency propeller**. The following are recorded:

| Parameter | Pre-conversion (Old) | Post-conversion (New) |
|---|---|---|
| Trial corrected speed | 22.0 knots | 22.5 knots |
| Corrected SHP | 52,000 kW | 47,500 kW |
| Displacement $\Delta$ | 145,000 t | 145,000 t |
| $L_{PP}$ | 360 m | 360 m |
| Wetted surface $S$ | 28,000 m² | 27,200 m² |

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**(i)** Compute the Admiralty coefficient before and after conversion. **(ii)** State whether the propulsion efficiency has improved. **(iii)** If fuel specific consumption is 170 g/kWh, calculate the daily fuel saving at the new condition compared to the old.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**(i)** Compute the ITTC frictional resistance for both conditions using appropriate Reynolds numbers. **(ii)** If the residual resistance coefficient is reduced from $C_R = 0.9 \times 10^{-3}$ to $C_R = 0.6 \times 10^{-3}$ due to the new bulbous bow, compute the new total resistance coefficient and the percentage reduction in $R_T$. **(iii)** Discuss whether this 12% SHP reduction justifies the conversion cost.

---

#### Model Solution B.B

**Part (a) Solution:**

**(i) Admiralty coefficients (3 marks):**

Pre-conversion:

$$C_{A,old} = \frac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3}{P_{old}}$$

$$\Delta^{2/3} = (145000)^{2/3}; \quad 145000^{1/3} \approx 52.50; \quad (52.50)^2 \approx 2756.3$$

$$V^3 = (22.0)^3 = 10648$$

$$C_{A,old} = \frac{2756.3 \times 10648}{52000} = \frac{29{,}347{,}000}{52000} \approx 564.4$$

Post-conversion:

$$V^3 = (22.5)^3 = 11390.6$$

$$C_{A,new} = \frac{2756.3 \times 11390.6}{47500} = \frac{31{,}394{,}700}{47500} \approx 661.0$$

**(ii) Efficiency verdict (2 marks):**
$C_A$ increased from 564 → 661 (≈ **+17.1%**), confirming a substantial improvement in overall propulsive efficiency. Higher $C_A$ at higher speed AND lower power is a textbook sign of successful conversion.

**(iii) Daily fuel saving (2 marks):**

$$\Delta SHP = 52000 - 47500 = 4500 \ kW$$

$$\Delta \dot{m}_{fuel} = 4500 \ kW \times 0.170 \ kg/kWh \times 24 \ h$$

$$\Delta \dot{m}_{fuel} = 108 \times 24 = \mathbf{18{,}360 \ kg/day}$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

**(i) Frictional resistance (3 marks):**

Convert speeds: $V_{old} = 22.0 \times 0.5144 = 11.32$ m/s; $V_{new} = 22.5 \times 0.5144 = 11.57$ m/s.

Old Reynolds:

$$R_{n,old} = \frac{11.32 \times 360}{1.1883 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.43 \times 10^9$$

$$\log R_{n,old} = 9.535; \quad C_{F,old} = \frac{0.075}{(9.535-2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{56.78} = 1.321 \times 10^{-3}$$

New Reynolds:

$$R_{n,new} = \frac{11.57 \times 360}{1.1883 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.50 \times 10^9$$

$$\log R_{n,new} = 9.544; \quad C_{F,new} = \frac{0.075}{(9.544-2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{56.97} = 1.317 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_{F,old} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (11.32)^2 \times 28000 \times 1.321 \times 10^{-3} \approx 2{,}432{,}000 \ N$$

$$R_{F,new} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (11.57)^2 \times 27200 \times 1.317 \times 10^{-3} \approx 2{,}331{,}000 \ N$$

**(ii) Total resistance coefficient (3 marks):**

Pre-conversion total (using old $C_R$):

$$C_{T,old} = C_{F,old} + C_{R,old} = 1.321 \times 10^{-3} + 0.9 \times 10^{-3} = 2.221 \times 10^{-3}$$

Post-conversion total:

$$C_{T,new} = C_{F,new} + C_{R,new} = 1.317 \times 10^{-3} + 0.6 \times 10^{-3} = 1.917 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\%\text{ reduction in } C_T = \frac{2.221 - 1.917}{2.221} \times 100\% = \mathbf{13.7\%}$$

(Note: Resistance scales with $V^2$, and $V$ changed by +2.3%, so $R_T$ reduction is slightly different — but the coefficient itself dropped by 13.7%.)

**(iii) Justification (1 mark):**
A **9.6% drop in fuel consumption (4500/47000 ≈ 9.6%)** with no reported speed loss is highly attractive. At 18.36 tonnes of fuel saved per day, with marine fuel at ~USD 600/tonne, daily savings ≈ **USD 11,016/day ≈ USD 4 million/year**. This typically pays back a bulbous-bow + propeller retrofit within 1.5–3 years, justifying the conversion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall B.B:**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Forgetting that Admiralty coefficient $C_A$ has *arbitrary units*; students often claim "661 is a unitless number." State explicitly that $C_A$ is conventionally compared between sister ships in the same units; absolute values are not absolute.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Computing $C_F$ with the wrong $\log$ base. The ITTC line uses **base-10** logarithm — writing $\ln$ instead of $\log_{10}$ changes $C_F$ by a factor of $\approx 2.3$ and is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Comparing $C_F$ values without re-computing Reynolds number at the *new* speed. Speed changed from 22.0 → 22.5 knots; using the old $R_n$ for the new condition is a frequent slip.
> - **Common mistake 4:** Reporting "% reduction in $C_T$" without explaining that this is the *coefficient*, not the absolute resistance — because $V$ also changed, the absolute $R_T$ change requires a separate computation. Examiners reward students who note this subtlety.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Cross-Cutting Pitfall List

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 8 places students lose marks on this topic (compiled from KTU valuation reports):**
>
> 1. **Sign convention** for $\Delta V$ corrections — students add wind correction but forget the *opposite* effect of following current.
> 2. **Knots ↔ m/s conversion** — most common single error; never skip it.
> 3. **Cube law assumption validity** — students apply it without checking $\Delta V/V < 5\%$.
> 4. **EHP units confusion** — metric HP ($V$ in m/s, divide by 75), or kW ($V$ in m/s, divide by 1000).
> 5. **Form factor 1+k** — students confuse it with appendage factor $k_{app}$ — they are different.
> 6. **Correlation allowance $R_{CA}$ vs form factor** — ITTC 1978 uses $R_{CA}$, ITTC 2014 uses $1+k$; examiner may specify which.
> 7. **Air resistance neglect** — trial correction procedure explicitly accounts for *wind* but not aerodynamic drag of superstructure in the standard formulation; mention it for completeness.
> 8. **Stating only the answer without the *contractual interpretation*** — the *engineering judgement* ("the contract is met" / "the contract is not met") is worth 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Total Resistance:** $R_T = R_F + R_R + R_{APP} + R_A + R_W$
- **ITTC 1978 Friction Line:** $\boxed{C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}}$ — base-10 log, sea-water $\nu = 1.1883 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s
- **Reynolds number:** $R_n = V \cdot L_{PP} / \nu$
- **Form factor:** $(1+k) = C_{T,model} / C_{F,model}$; full-scale $C_T = (1+k) C_{F,ship} + C_R$
- **Effective HP:** $EHP = R_T \cdot V$ (W) or $EHP = R_T \cdot V / (75 \times 9.81)$ in metric HP
- **SHP hierarchy:** $SHP = EHP / \eta_D$ where $\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_S$
- **Standard trial conditions:** deep water, calm sea, no wind/current, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.1883 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s
- **Speed correction:** $V_{corr} = V_{meas} + \Delta V_w + \Delta V_{wv} + \Delta V_c + \Delta V_{sh}$
- **Power correction (Cube Law):** $P_{corr} / P_{meas} = (V_{corr} / V_{meas})^3$
- **Validity of Cube Law:** only when $\Delta V / V < 5\%$
- **Admiralty coefficient:** $C_A = \Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3 / P$
- **Appendage factor:** $k_{app} \approx 0.04$–$0.08$ on top of $R_F$
- **Froude number:** $F_n = V / \sqrt{g L_{PP}}$
- **Standards to cite:** ITTC 1978/2014, ISO 15016:2015, IACS SVR, ISO 3046
- **Conversion relevance:** Always compare **corrected** trial results before and after; use Admiralty coefficient for single-number efficiency summary
- **Sign convention:** Environment *opposing* motion → positive $\Delta V$; environment *aiding* motion → negative $\Delta V$
- **Final check:** Always end with a **contractual verdict** — does the corrected performance meet the spec? This is the engineering takeaway.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Additional Resistance Under Service Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Additional Resistance Under Service Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Additional Resistance under Service Conditions (R_ADD)** is defined as the increment in the total resistance of a vessel when it operates in realistic, non-ideal environmental conditions (waves, wind, shallow water, fouling, current, ice) compared to the resistance measured or predicted in **deep, calm, clean water** at the same speed and displacement.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$R_{ADD} = R_{TOTAL}^{SERVICE} - R_{CALM}^{DEEP}$$

The total resistance in actual service is then:

$$R_{TOTAL} = R_C + R_{ADD}$$

where $R_C$ is the **calm water resistance** and $R_{ADD}$ is the **service-condition allowance**.

The key components of additional resistance are:

$$R_{ADD} = R_{AW} + R_{AA} + R_{SH} + R_{FOUL} + R_{TRIM} + R_{CURRENT} + \cdots$$

| Symbol | Component | Physical Driver |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $R_{AW}$ | Added resistance in **waves** | Ship motions + wave scattering |
| $R_{AA}$ | **Aerodynamic / wind** resistance | Above-water hull form |
| $R_{SH}$ | **Shallow water** correction | Restricted under-keel clearance |
| $R_{FOUL}$ | **Hull fouling** allowance | Biofouling & surface roughness |
| $R_{TRIM}$ | **Trim / draft** correction | Non-design hydrostatic state |
| $R_{CURRENT}$ | **Current** effect | Relative water velocity |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation, the typical acceptable components tested are **R_AW, R_AA, R_SH, and R_FOUL**, as they form the core of the ship-conversion power-budget calculation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a marathon runner trained on a flat, smooth indoor track. Their "calm water resistance" is just the effort to move forward. Now put that same runner in a real outdoor race:

- **Headwind** blowing against them → equivalent to **wind resistance (R_AA)**.
- **Puddles and mud on the road** → equivalent to **fouling (R_FOUL)**.
- **Rolling hills** they must climb → equivalent to **added resistance in waves (R_AW)**.
- **A narrow corridor that restricts stride** → equivalent to **shallow water (R_SH)**.
- **Carrying a backpack** heavier than during training → equivalent to **trim/draft change after conversion (R_TRIM)**.

The **total extra effort** in the real race is what a naval architect calls the *Additional Resistance under Service Conditions*. In ship conversion, we are essentially taking a ship designed (or pre-converted) for one set of conditions and asking: *"How much more power will the engine need to deliver the same service speed in the new operational environment?"*

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following reference values are universally used in KTU-evaluated problems:

- **Sea water density:** $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Air density (at standard sea level):** $\rho_a = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Kinematic viscosity of seawater (15 °C):** $\nu = 1.19 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

> [!TIP]
> Always check the assumed sea-state and **significant wave height $H_{1/3}$** given in the problem. KTU 2024 numerical questions typically use $H_{1/3} = 2 \text{ to } 6$ m with a Pierson–Moskowitz (PM) or JONSWAP spectrum.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Additional Resistance Matters in Ship Conversion

During ship conversion, the operational profile of the vessel changes. Examples relevant to **PENST415 (Ship Conversion Technology)**:

1. A general cargo ship is converted to a **coastal container feeder** → must operate in restricted / shallow waters → $R_{SH}$ matters.
2. A naval auxiliary is converted to a **long-range oceanographic vessel** → operates in heavy seas → $R_{AW}$ dominates.
3. A tanker is converted after 10 years of service with **biofouling** → $R_{FOUL}$ may add 15–25 % to frictional resistance.
4. A bulk carrier is converted with **increased deck cargo or new superstructure** → wind drag $R_{AA}$ increases.

The converted vessel's **Effective Power** must be re-estimated:

$$P_{E}^{SERVICE} = (R_{CALM} + R_{ADD}) \cdot V_S$$

A failure to account for $R_{ADD}$ leads to **under-powered conversions**, missed contractual speed guarantees, and expensive post-conversion engine up-ratings.

## 2.2 The Physical "Why" Behind Each Component

### 2.2.1 Added Resistance in Waves ($R_{AW}$)

In regular head waves, the ship undergoes **forced heave and pitch oscillations**, plus a steady second-order drift force. The drift force is non-zero when averaged over a wave cycle and produces a steady retarding force aligned with the ship's course.

There are three dominant physical mechanisms:
- **Diffraction term** — pressure asymmetry on hull due to incident wave.
- **Radiation term** — energy radiated by ship-generated waves (especially at resonance).
- **Reflection term** — waves reflected off the hull carry away momentum.

The result is a **mean added drag** that peaks when the **encounter frequency** $\omega_e$ matches the ship's natural heave or pitch frequency.

### 2.2.2 Wind Resistance ($R_{AA}$)

Above the waterline, the superstructure, deck cargo, and hull sides experience an aerodynamic drag. Since $\rho_a \ll \rho$, this is small at low speeds but grows with $V^2$ and can reach 5–15 % of total resistance for a heavily built superstructure or a slow, full-form vessel.

### 2.2.3 Shallow Water Effect ($R_{SH}$)

When the **under-keel clearance (UKC)** drops below roughly 1.5 × draft, the wave system generated by the ship is constrained by the seabed. The wave-making resistance increases sharply, and there is a critical speed beyond which sinkage and trim become unstable (**squat phenomenon**). A typical $R_{SH}/R_{DEEP}$ can rise to **1.5 – 2.5** near the critical speed.

### 2.2.4 Fouling Allowance ($R_{FOUL}$)

Marine growth (barnacles, slime, algae) increases the **hull roughness** $k_s$. The ITTC-78 friction line is replaced with an **effective roughness** that may give 30–50 % higher frictional resistance after 5 years without docking.

### 2.2.5 Trim / Draft Correction

A conversion that adds or removes weight shifts the operating draft and trim. Since frictional and residual resistance both depend on $T$ and trim angle, the calm-water resistance itself changes. This correction is often lumped into the additional resistance budget.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the single most important reference for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic. **Memorise it.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|:-:|:---|:---|
| 1 | $R_{TOTAL} = R_C + R_{ADD}$ | Master decomposition |
| 2 | $R_{AW}$ — ITTC 1978 head seas: $\dfrac{\Delta C}{1000} = 0.65 \cdot \dfrac{B}{L} \left(\dfrac{H_{1/3}}{L}\right)^{1.7} \left(\dfrac{L}{\lambda}\right)^{3.4} \cdot \dfrac{\cos\mu}{2}$ | Empirical head-sea AWR |
| 3 | $R_{AA} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_{AA} A_{FV} U_R^2$ | Wind drag with $C_{AA}\in[0.6,1.2]$ |
| 4 | $R_{SH} = (1 + k_{SH}) R_{DEEP}$ with $k_{SH}= f(h/T, F_{nh})$ | Shallow water factor |
| 5 | $R_{FOUL} = (1 + \Delta C_{F}/1000) R_F$ with $\Delta C_F \approx 0.4 (k_s/L)^{1/3} \cdot 10^3$ | Fouling-roughness correction |
| 6 | $C_F^{ITTC} = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10}Re - 2)^2}$ | Friction coefficient (clean) |
| 7 | $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Froude number |
| 8 | $\omega_e = \omega - \dfrac{\omega^2 V \cos\mu}{g}$ | Encounter frequency |
| 9 | $P_E = R \cdot V_S$ | Effective power (W) |
| 10 | $\mu$ = course angle w.r.t. waves | $\mu=0$ head, $\mu=180°$ following |

> **Notation key:**
> $L$ = length between perpendiculars (m), $B$ = breadth (m), $T$ = draft (m), $H_{1/3}$ = significant wave height (m), $\lambda$ = wavelength (m), $\mu$ = wave course angle (deg), $U_R$ = relative wind speed (m/s), $A_{FV}$ = frontal projected area (m²), $k_{SH}$ = shallow-water factor, $k_s$ = equivalent sand roughness (m).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Powering guarantee contracts** (EEDI, EEXI compliance) — service margin 15 % is typical.
- **Route planning** in weather-routing software (e.g., BVS, WR-paths) — picks course to minimize $R_{AW}$.
- **Engine selection** during ship conversion — main engine and shaft generator must be sized on $R_{CALM} + R_{ADD}$, not on $R_{CALM}$ alone.
- **Fuel-oil consumption (FOC) estimation** for the converted vessel.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Added Resistance in Regular Head Waves (Havelock Source Theory, Outline)

The full Havelock derivation proceeds from a distribution of **point sources** placed on the longitudinal centre plane of the ship, each with strength $\sigma(x)$ oscillating at the **encounter frequency** $\omega_e$. We present the streamlined version of the mean-drag result.

### Step 1 — Velocity potential

The total velocity potential is split into incident, diffracted, and radiated parts:

$$\Phi(x,y,z,t) = \Phi_I + \Phi_D + \Phi_R$$

### Step 2 — Linearised free-surface condition

$$\frac{\partial^2 \Phi}{\partial t^2} + g \frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial z}\bigg|_{z=0} = 0$$

### Step 3 — Second-order mean pressure

Averaging the Bernoulli pressure over one wave period:

$$\bar{p}_S = -\tfrac{1}{2}\rho \, \overline{\vert \nabla \Phi \vert^2} - \rho \, \overline{\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial t}}$$

### Step 4 — Integrate around the hull wetted surface $S$

$$R_{AW} = -\oint_S \bar{p}_S \, n_x \, dA$$

### Step 5 — Far-field (Havelock) form

Using Green's theorem and moving the integration to a control surface at infinity:

$$R_{AW} = \frac{\rho g^2 \zeta_a^2}{2 k} \int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} \left( P(\theta) \right)^2 \cos\theta \, d\theta$$

where $P(\theta)$ is the Kochin function (far-field amplitude). For a slender hull, $P(\theta)$ peaks near the Kelvin angle and the integral yields the famous Havelock result.

### Step 6 — Engineering form (Havelock thin-ship, head seas)

For a slender ship of length $L$ and beam $B$ in regular head waves of amplitude $\zeta_a$ and wavelength $\lambda$:

$$R_{AW} = \frac{\rho g^2 B^2 \zeta_a^2}{2 L} \cdot \frac{\pi^2}{\left(kL\right)^2} \cdot f\!\left(\frac{\omega_e^2 B}{g}\right)$$

For irregular seas, integrate the spectral form:

$$R_{AW}^{IRR} = \int_0^{\infty} R_{AW}(\omega) \cdot S(\omega) \, d\omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU-2024 typically asks for the **spectral integral evaluation** or the **empirical ITTC head-sea form** (Item 2 of the formula sheet), not the full Havelock derivation. The above is provided to satisfy the **outcome-based** expectation that students know the *physical origin* of $R_{AW}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Wind Resistance from First Principles

A naval architect treats the above-water portion of the ship as a **bluff body** in a uniform airstream. The classical drag formulation is derived as follows.

### Step 1 — Momentum balance on a control volume

Consider air of density $\rho_a$ approaching the superstructure at relative velocity $U_R$. By conservation of momentum in the streamwise direction, the drag equals the rate of momentum deficit leaving the control volume.

### Step 2 — Apply the Rankine-Blob / strip-theory form

Treat the superstructure as a 2-D bluff body of frontal area $A_{FV}$ and side area $A_{LV}$:

$$R_{AA} = C_{AA} \cdot q_a \cdot A_{FV}$$

where the **dynamic pressure** is:

$$q_a = \frac{1}{2}\rho_a U_R^2$$

### Step 3 — Insert the constant

$$R_{AA} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_{AA} A_{FV} U_R^2$$

### Step 4 — Numerical substitution

With $\rho_a = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $A_{FV} = 250 \text{ m}^2$, $C_{AA} = 0.8$, $U_R = 15 \text{ m/s}$:

$$R_{AA} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(0.8)(250)(15)^2 = 27{,}562.5 \text{ N} \approx 27.6 \text{ kN}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shallow-Water Resistance Correction

### Step 1 — Define depth Froude number

$$F_{nh} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g h}}$$

where $h$ = water depth.

### Step 2 — Apply the ITTC shallow-water correction factor

For $h/T \ge 1.5$ (typical of unrestricted service):

$$\frac{R_{SH}}{R_{DEEP}} = 1 + \alpha\left(\frac{T}{h}\right)^{\beta} F_{nh}^{\gamma}$$

Empirically $\alpha \approx 0.7$, $\beta \approx 1.5$, $\gamma \approx 2.0$ (Schlichting-type fit).

### Step 3 — Critical speed

The critical speed is reached when $F_{nh} \approx 1.0$ (or equivalently $V_{CR} = \sqrt{g h}$). At and above this speed, **squat and trim instability** set in and the resistance ratio $R_{SH}/R_{DEEP}$ may exceed 2.0.

### Step 4 — Worked example

For $V = 12$ kn ≈ 6.17 m/s, $h = 15$ m, $T = 8$ m, $R_{DEEP} = 600$ kN:

$$F_{nh} = \frac{6.17}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 15}} = 0.504$$

$$\frac{R_{SH}}{R_{DEEP}} = 1 + 0.7 \left(\frac{8}{15}\right)^{1.5} (0.504)^{2} = 1 + 0.7(0.296)(0.254) \approx 1.053$$

$$R_{SH} \approx 632 \text{ kN}$$

A 5.3 % increase — small in this case, but grows rapidly as $V \to V_{CR}$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Component

| Service Scenario | Dominant $R_{ADD}$ Component | Typical Magnitude (% of $R_C$) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Open ocean, head seas $H_{1/3}=3$ m | $R_{AW}$ | 30 – 80 % |
| Open ocean, beam seas | $R_{AW}$ | 50 – 100 % |
| Trade wind region | $R_{AA}$ | 5 – 12 % |
| Coastal / canal transit | $R_{SH}$ | 10 – 150 % (squat-critical) |
| Vessel > 5 yrs since last docking | $R_{FOUL}$ | 15 – 25 % of $R_F$ |
| Conversion changed loading condition | $R_{TRIM}$ | 2 – 8 % of $R_C$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Total Resistance Decomposition

The following block-level architecture shows how the calm-water baseline branches into all service-condition corrections that must be summed when sizing the propulsion plant for a converted ship.

```mermaid
graph TD
    RT["Total Resistance R_T in Service"] --> RC["Calm Water Resistance R_C"]
    RT --> RAD["Additional Resistance R_ADD"]

    RC --> RF["Frictional R_F (ITTC-78)"]
    RC --> RR["Residual R_R (wave-making + wave-breaking)"]
    RC --> RAP["Appendage R_APP"]

    RAD --> RAW["Added Resistance in Waves R_AW"]
    RAD --> RAA["Wind / Aerodynamic R_AA"]
    RAD --> RSH["Shallow Water Effect R_SH"]
    RAD --> RFO["Fouling Allowance R_FOUL"]
    RAD --> RTR["Trim / Draft Correction R_TRIM"]
    RAD --> RCU["Current / Ice Allowance R_CURRENT"]

    RAW --> R1["Diffraction term"]
    RAW --> R2["Radiation term"]
    RAW --> R3["Reflection / Drift term"]

    RAA --> A1["Front area A_FV"]
    RAA --> A2["Aerodynamic coeff C_AA"]
    RAA --> A3["Relative wind U_R"]

    RSH --> S1["Depth Froude F_nh"]
    RSH --> S2["UKC ratio h over T"]
    RSH --> S3["Critical speed V_CR"]

    RFO --> F1["Sand roughness k_s"]
    RFO --> F2["Docking interval t_D"]
    RFO --> F3["Effective CF correction"]

    PWR["Effective Power P_E = R_T * V_S"] --> ENG["Engine / Shaft Selection for Conversion"]
    ENG --> MAR["Service Speed Margin (15 percent)"]
```

> [!TIP]
> When solving a KTU 2024 problem, always start by drawing (or mentally tracing) this architecture. Identify which components are *given* or *implied* by the problem statement, and sum only those branches.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Encounter-Frequency Logic for $R_{AW}$

The added resistance in waves depends critically on the **encounter frequency** $\omega_e$, which determines whether the ship is in sub-critical, critical, or super-critical regime.

```mermaid
graph TD
    INC["Incident Wave Spectrum S omega"] --> WS["Ship Speed V and Course mu"]
    WS --> EFC["Encounter Frequency we = omega - omega_sq V cos mu over g"]
    EFC --> BR1["Sub-critical: we below natural heave"]
    EFC --> BR2["Critical: we matches natural heave or pitch"]
    EFC --> BR3["Super-critical: we above natural frequency"]
    BR1 --> L1["Low R_AW, mostly diffraction"]
    BR2 --> L2["Peak R_AW, large radiation + slamming"]
    BR3 --> L3["R_AW decreases, response attenuated"]
    L1 --> SUMM["Spectral integral R_AW_int"]
    L2 --> SUMM
    L3 --> SUMM
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Typical Conversion-Service Resistance Budget

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Original Ship R_C"] --> B["Conversion Changes"]
    B --> C1["New Hull Form"]
    B --> C2["New Superstructure"]
    B --> C3["New Draft / Trim"]
    B --> C4["New Operational Area"]
    C1 --> D["Updated R_C"]
    C2 --> E1["R_AA increases"]
    C3 --> E2["R_TRIM correction"]
    C4 --> E3["R_AW and R_SH new values"]
    D --> F["Re-evaluated Total R_T = R_C + R_ADD"]
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G["New Effective Power P_E"]
    G --> H["Engine / Propeller Re-Selection"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q.A1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**"Define the term *Additional Resistance under Service Conditions*. List any four of its components with a one-line physical cause for each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Additional Resistance is the difference between a ship's total resistance in real operating conditions and its calm-water deep-water resistance at the same speed and displacement:

$$R_{ADD} = R_{SERVICE} - R_{CALM}^{DEEP}$$

Four components:

1. **Added resistance in waves ($R_{AW}$)** — caused by ship motions (heave, pitch) and wave–hull interaction in a seaway.
2. **Wind resistance ($R_{AA}$)** — aerodynamic drag of the above-water hull, superstructure and deck cargo.
3. **Shallow-water effect ($R_{SH}$)** — enhanced wave-making resistance when under-keel clearance is restricted.
4. **Fouling allowance ($R_{FOUL}$)** — increase in frictional resistance due to marine growth and hull roughness.

*(Award full 3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for correct equation + 1 for the four components. 0.25 mark deduction per missing or wrongly attributed component.)*

---

### Q.A2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**"State the ITTC 1978 empirical formula for the added resistance of a ship in head seas. Identify each term."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\frac{\Delta C}{1000} = 0.65 \cdot \frac{B}{L}\left(\frac{H_{1/3}}{L}\right)^{1.7}\left(\frac{L}{\lambda}\right)^{3.4} \cdot \frac{\cos\mu}{2}$$

Identification of terms:

- $B$ = moulded breadth (m).
- $L$ = length between perpendiculars (m).
- $H_{1/3}$ = significant wave height (m).
- $\lambda$ = dominant wavelength (m).
- $\mu$ = course angle (deg), $\mu = 0$ for head seas.
- $\Delta C/1000$ is the added-resistance coefficient; multiply by $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S$ to obtain force.

*(Full 3 marks for formula + 1 bonus mark possible for stating the boundary condition $H_{1/3}/L \le 0.05$ where applicable.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks, Module 4 Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Do NOT omit the units** in the final numerical answer. Marks are deducted for "27.6" without "kN".
> - **Do NOT confuse $R_{AW}$ with the full resistance**. The question always asks for the *increment*, not the total.
> - **Always state the sea-state assumption** (e.g., "PM spectrum, $H_{1/3}$ = …") before applying the empirical formula.
> - **Show the substitution step-by-step** for partial credit.
> - In **head-seas questions**, $\cos\mu = 1$; in **beam seas**, the form differs and many students lose marks by misapplying the formula.

### Q.B1 — Choice A (14 Marks)

#### (a) `[KTU University Exam – Model 2024]`
**Explain the various methods used to predict the added resistance of a ship in waves. Compare their accuracy, range of validity and computational cost. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

The principal methods are:

1. **Empirical / Statistical methods**
   - ITTC 1978 (Strom-Teigen) head-sea formula.
   - - *Range:* Head seas only, $H_{1/3}/L \le 0.05$, $F_n \le 0.3$.
   - - *Cost:* Negligible (closed-form).
   - - *Accuracy:* ± 25 % on average.

2. **Semi-empirical / Spectral methods**
   - Gerritsma & Beukelman (1972); Fujii & Tsutsumi (1961).
   - - *Range:* Any course angle, broad $F_n$.
   - - *Cost:* Low; requires integration over wave spectrum.
   - - *Accuracy:* ± 20 %.

3. **Slender-body / Strip theory**
   - Havelock source method; Newman formulas.
   - - *Range:* Slender hulls; regular or irregular seas.
   - - *Cost:* Moderate (1-D integration along ship length).
   - - *Accuracy:* ± 15–20 % for slender ships.

4. **3-D Panel methods (potential)**
   - Codes like WAMIT, DIFFRACT, NLOADCT.
   - - *Range:* Arbitrary hull form, headings, water depth.
   - - *Cost:* High (CFD-grade).
   - - *Accuracy:* ± 5–10 % for moderate seas.

5. **RANS-CFD (viscous)**
   - Overset-grid or sliding-mesh methods with VOF waves.
   - - *Range:* Universal.
   - - *Cost:* Very high; days of HPC time.
   - - *Accuracy:* Best available, ± 5 % with proper validation.

*Comparison conclusion:* Empirical methods are used at the **conceptual design / conversion feasibility stage**; panel and CFD methods are used for the **final propulsion plant selection** in a serious conversion project. KTU answers should explicitly state the trade-off between accuracy and cost.

*(Awarding: 5 marks for explaining the 5 methods, 1 mark for comparison table, 1 mark for the engineering conclusion.)*

#### (b) `[KTU University Exam – Model 2024]`
**A bulk carrier of $L = 150$ m, $B = 22.5$ m, $T = 9.0$ m, $C_B = 0.78$, displacement $\Delta = 18{,}500$ t, is to be converted for ocean service in significant head seas with $H_{1/3} = 4$ m and dominant wavelength $\lambda = 90$ m. The calm-water effective power at service speed $V = 14$ kn is $P_{E,calm} = 5400$ kW. Compute the additional effective power required in the specified seaway using the ITTC 1978 formula. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert speed and identify the form factor**
$V = 14$ kn $= 14 \times 0.5144 = 7.20$ m/s.

**Step 2 — Compute the ITTC head-sea added-resistance coefficient (Item 2 of formula sheet)**

$$\frac{\Delta C}{1000} = 0.65 \cdot \frac{B}{L} \left(\frac{H_{1/3}}{L}\right)^{1.7} \left(\frac{L}{\lambda}\right)^{3.4} \cdot \frac{\cos\mu}{2}$$

Insert values (head seas → $\cos\mu = 1$):

- $B/L = 22.5/150 = 0.150$
- $H_{1/3}/L = 4/150 = 0.02667$
- $L/\lambda = 150/90 = 1.6667$

$$\left(\frac{H_{1/3}}{L}\right)^{1.7} = (0.02667)^{1.7}$$

Take logarithms: $\log_{10}(0.02667) = -1.574$, multiplied by 1.7 gives $-2.676$, so $(0.02667)^{1.7} \approx 0.002106$.

$$\left(\frac{L}{\lambda}\right)^{3.4} = (1.6667)^{3.4}$$

$\log_{10}(1.6667) = 0.2219$, times 3.4 = 0.7544, so $(1.6667)^{3.4} \approx 5.681$.

Assemble:

$$\frac{\Delta C}{1000} = 0.65 \cdot 0.150 \cdot 0.002106 \cdot 5.681 \cdot 0.5$$

$$= 0.65 \cdot 0.150 \cdot 0.002106 \cdot 2.8405$$

$$= 0.65 \cdot 0.150 \cdot 0.005984 = 0.0005834$$

**Step 3 — Convert $\Delta C$ to added resistance $R_{AW}$**
Wetted surface area (approximate): $S = L (2T + B) \sqrt{C_B} \approx 150(18 + 22.5)(0.883) \approx 5365$ m².

$$R_{AW} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S \cdot \Delta C = \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(7.20)^2 (5365)(0.0005834)$$

$\tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(51.84) = 26{,}568$; times $5365 = 1.425 \times 10^8$; times $0.0005834 = 83{,}139$ N $\approx 83.1$ kN.

**Step 4 — Convert to additional effective power**

$$\Delta P_E = R_{AW} \cdot V = 83{,}139 \times 7.20 = 598{,}600 \text{ W} \approx 599 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Final answer with service margin**

$$P_{E,total} = 5400 + 599 \approx 5999 \text{ kW} \approx 6.0 \text{ MW}$$

With a 15 % service margin: $6.0 \times 1.15 = 6.9$ MW shaft power at the engine output (after propeller and mechanical losses, the engine must be rated even higher).

*(Mark split: 2 for setting up, 2 for ITTC substitution, 1 for $R_{AW}$ conversion, 1 for power, 1 for final answer with margin.)*

---

### Q.B2 — Choice B (14 Marks)

#### (a) `[KTU University Exam – Model 2024]`
**Derive the expression for the wind (aerodynamic) resistance of a ship. Discuss how the relative wind speed $U_R$ is calculated from true wind and ship speed, and state the typical range of the aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_{AA}$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition**
The aerodynamic resistance of a ship is the component of total resistance that arises from the **above-water hull and superstructure** moving through air. Because $\rho_a \ll \rho$, it is small compared to hydrodynamic resistance but is non-negligible for low-speed, full-form vessels or those with bulky deck cargo.

**Step 2 — Vector addition of velocities (relative wind)**
The relative wind velocity is the vector difference between the **true wind** $\vec{V}_{TW}$ and the **ship's velocity** $\vec{V}_S$:

$$\vec{U}_R = \vec{V}_{TW} - \vec{V}_S$$

The magnitude is:

$$U_R = \sqrt{V_{TW}^2 + V_S^2 - 2 V_{TW} V_S \cos\psi_{TW}}$$

where $\psi_{TW}$ is the angle of the true wind measured from the ship's bow.

**Step 3 — Aerodynamic drag equation**
Treating the superstructure as a bluff body of frontal projected area $A_{FV}$:

$$R_{AA} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_{AA} A_{FV} U_R^2$$

**Step 4 — Range of $C_{AA}$**
For typical merchant ship superstructures:

- Streamlined container ship bridge: $C_{AA} \approx 0.6$
- Bulky tanker house: $C_{AA} \approx 0.8$–$1.0$
- General cargo with kingposts, masts, rigging: $C_{AA} \approx 1.0$–$1.4$

The coefficient also depends on **wind direction** (side winds give the largest $C_{AA}$).

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation**
In a ship conversion, adding a new superstructure (e.g., a reefer deck or a hangar) directly increases $A_{FV}$ and therefore $R_{AA}$. A complete power-budget review is mandatory.

*(Mark split: 2 for vector velocity, 2 for drag equation, 1 for ranges, 1 for engineering comment, 1 for the units.)*

#### (b) `[KTU University Exam – Model 2024]`
**A 12,000 DWT general-cargo vessel is to be converted for service in a coastal channel with under-keel clearance (UKC) of 1.2 m and water depth $h = 11$ m. The vessel's draft is $T = 9.8$ m and its deep-water effective power at the conversion design speed of 10 kn is 1850 kW. Using the ITTC shallow-water correction procedure, estimate the additional power required in the channel. Comment on the safety of the proposed UKC. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Geometry check**
$h/T = 11/9.8 = 1.122$. This is well below the unrestricted value of 1.5, so shallow-water correction definitely applies.

UKC = 1.2 m is **less than 15 % of draft** and is dangerously close to the squat-critical regime. The conversion specification should be reviewed.

**Step 2 — Speed conversion**
$V = 10$ kn $= 5.144$ m/s.

**Step 3 — Depth Froude number**

$$F_{nh} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gh}} = \frac{5.144}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 11}} = \frac{5.144}{10.385} = 0.495$$

**Step 4 — Apply the ITTC shallow-water factor**
Using $\alpha = 0.7$, $\beta = 1.5$, $\gamma = 2.0$:

$$\frac{R_{SH}}{R_{DEEP}} = 1 + 0.7\left(\frac{T}{h}\right)^{1.5} F_{nh}^2 = 1 + 0.7(0.891)^{1.5}(0.495)^2$$

$(0.891)^{1.5} = 0.841$; $(0.495)^2 = 0.245$.

$$\frac{R_{SH}}{R_{DEEP}} = 1 + 0.7(0.841)(0.245) = 1 + 0.1442 = 1.1442$$

**Step 5 — Power scaling**
Because power is proportional to resistance at constant speed:

$$P_{E,SH} = 1.1442 \times 1850 = 2117 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Additional power**

$$\Delta P_E = 2117 - 1850 = 267 \text{ kW} \quad (\approx 14.4 \text{ % increase})$$

**Step 7 — Comment on safety**
- UKC of 1.2 m at $T = 9.8$ m is only **12.2 % of draft**, well below the 15–20 % recommended safe margin.
- $F_{nh} = 0.495$ is approaching the critical value of 1.0; the vessel is in the regime where **squat** can cause sudden sinkage of 0.4–0.8 m.
- **Recommendation:** Either reduce the conversion design speed to 8 kn (recompute $F_{nh}$ = 0.40, more comfortable), or restrict operations to high-water slack-tide windows.

*(Mark split: 1 for geometry check, 2 for ITTC substitution, 1 for power conversion, 1 for additional power, 2 for the engineering comment.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> Use this checklist as a 5-minute pre-exam revision card.

- [x] **$R_{ADD} = R_{AW} + R_{AA} + R_{SH} + R_{FOUL} + R_{TRIM} + R_{CURRENT} + \ldots$** — write this decomposition first on every answer sheet.
- [x] **Calm water is the baseline.** Always compute $R_C$ (ITTC-78) and then **add** the service increments.
- [x] **Sea water density** $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, **air density** $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³ — do not mix them.
- [x] **ITTC 1978 head-sea formula** uses $H_{1/3}$ (significant) and the dominant wavelength $\lambda$.
- [x] **Course angle** $\mu = 0$ for head seas; the ITTC empirical form has $\cos\mu / 2$ as the heading factor — easy to forget.
- [x] **Wind resistance** scales as $U_R^2$; compute $U_R$ from the **vector** of true wind and ship speed.
- [x] **Shallow water** effect grows rapidly as $F_{nh} \to 1.0$ — recommend UKC ≥ 15 % of $T$ for safe conversion operations.
- [x] **Fouling** typically adds 15–25 % to $R_F$ after 4–5 years; conversion projects must declare the assumed docking interval.
- [x] **Encounter frequency** $\omega_e$ governs $R_{AW}$ peaks — write the equation before plugging in any value.
- [x] **Service margin** of 15 % is industry-standard — include it in the final engine selection.
- [x] Always **end with units** (kN, kW, m/s) — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
- [x] In **conversion context**, justify **why** each correction is needed by referring to the new operational profile.
- [x] Avoid **$R_{AW}$ vs $R_{TOTAL}$ confusion** — the question asks for the *increment* unless explicitly stated otherwise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line takeaway for ship conversion engineers:**
> *A successful ship conversion is not the one that matches the original calm-water contract speed — it is the one that delivers the **service speed** in the **realistic environmental envelope** of the new mission, with a quantified $R_{ADD}$ budget that the engine can sustain over the vessel's next operational decade.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rudder Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST415 - SHIP CONVERSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rudder Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rudder (KTU 2024 Standard Terminology):** A rudder is a hydrodynamically shaped, vertically oriented foil-like control surface mounted at the **sternpost**, **propeller post**, or **rudder horn** of a ship, pivoted about a vertical axis to generate a **side force (Normal Force, F_N)** that produces a **yaw moment** for directional control and maneuverability.

> **Key KTU Definition — Rudder Design:**
> Rudder design is the engineering process of determining the **rudder geometry (area, thickness, aspect ratio, profile)**, **structural scantlings (plate thickness, stock diameter)**, and **support arrangement (pintle/gudgeon bearings)** such that the steering gear can deliver adequate **torque (Q)** to withstand the maximum hydrodynamic load under the most severe service conditions prescribed by the **Classification Societies (IRS, LR, DNV, BV)** and **IMO Resolution A.601(15)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Think of the Rudder as the Steering Wheel of a Fish 🐟"**
>
> Imagine a fish swimming in water. A fish does not have a steering wheel — instead, it uses its **tail fin (caudal fin)**. When the fish swings its tail fin slightly to one side, water pushes the rear of the fish sideways, causing the nose to swing the opposite way. This is exactly how a ship rudder works:
>
> - The **rudder = fish's tail fin** (the moveable control surface).
> - The **flowing water = fish's swimming water** (created by the ship's forward motion).
> - The **side force (F_N) = sideways push from water on the fin**.
> - The **turning moment (M) = the "torque" that rotates the ship's nose**.
> - The **rudder stock = the muscles and bones of the fish's tail** — the strong central shaft that carries all the load.
>
> **Why a rudder needs strong DESIGN:** Just like a fish's tail fin must be strong enough not to break when it strikes a current, the ship's rudder must be designed to withstand the enormous water forces at high speeds without snapping, twisting, or vibrating. A poorly designed rudder can shear off — and a ship without a rudder cannot dock safely or avoid collisions.

### 1.3 Essential Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Symbol | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| **Design Speed (V)** | **Service speed (V_s)** in m/s or knots | The speed used for rudder force/torque calculation |
| **Block Coefficient (C_B)** | 0.50 – 0.85 | Affects wake fraction and rudder effectiveness |
| **Rudder Aspect Ratio (Λ)** | Λ = b² / A_R = **1.0 to 2.0** | Span (b)² divided by area |
| **Chord Length (c)** | 1.5 m – 6.0 m | Fore-aft dimension of rudder |
| **Material Density (Steel)** | **ρ = 7850 kg/m³** | Standard for rudder stock & plating |
| **Young's Modulus (Steel)** | **E = 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²** | Used in stiffness checks |
| **Yield Stress (Mild Steel)** | **σ_y = 235 N/mm²** | Per IRS Rules for shipbuilding steel |
| **Permissible Shear Stress** | **τ_allow ≈ 0.4 × σ_y** | For rudder stock per IRS Pt.3 Ch.9 |
| **Safety Factor (Yielding)** | **n = 1.4 – 1.6** | For rudder stock design per IMO A.601 |
| **Sea Margin on Torque** | **K = 1.25 to 1.50** | Multiplier on theoretical torque |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Requirements of a Rudder

A rudder, by **KTU / Classification Society (IRS) design philosophy**, must satisfy the following:

1. **Sufficient side force** (F_N) at the design speed to enable the ship to turn within SOLAS/Ch.5 specified **advance and tactical diameter**.
2. **Adequate structural strength** to withstand hydrodynamic, inertial, and steering gear reaction forces.
3. **Adequate fatigue life** (minimum **10⁸ cycles**) under repeated wave and maneuvering loads.
4. **Sufficient stiffness** so that resonant vibration with hull/wake frequencies is avoided.
5. **Corrosion allowance** (typically **2.0 mm to 3.0 mm** added to plate thickness).
6. **Maintainability** — pintle bearings must be replaceable, often via underwater removal.

### 2.2 Classification of Rudders (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Based on **support configuration**, rudders are classified (KTU PENST415 Module 4) as:

1. **Unbalanced Rudder (Single Plate / Double Plate)**
   - Entire surface ahead of the rudder stock.
   - Larger torque required → bigger steering gear.
   - Common in older or simple cargo vessels.
2. **Balanced Rudder (Semi-Balanced / Spade)**
   - 15%–30% of area is forward of the stock axis → reduces torque.
   - Most common in modern merchant ships.
3. **Spade Rudder**
   - No hull support; only top bearing and pintle bearing → high efficiency.
   - Used in single-screw high-speed vessels.
4. **Twin Rudder**
   - Two rudders, one behind each propeller (used in twin-screw ships like tankers).
5. **Schilling Rudder / High-Performance Rudder**
   - Used for specialized vessels (e.g., research vessels, tugs).

### 2.3 Why a Ship Needs Rudder Force (Physics of the "Why")

- When a ship moves at speed **V**, water flows past the hull with a velocity approximately equal to **(1 – w) × V**, where **w = wake fraction** (typically **0.20 to 0.40**).
- When the rudder is rotated by an angle **α (helm angle)**, water strikes the rudder at this angle and generates a **Normal Force (F_N)** perpendicular to the flow.
- This force acts at the **center of pressure (Cp)** — usually assumed at approximately **0.33 × chord length from the leading edge**.
- The **turning moment about the ship's pivot** = F_N × distance from pivot to rudder Cp.
- The **torque on the rudder stock** = F_N × moment arm from stock axis to Cp.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Every commercial ship, warship, fishing vessel, and yacht requires rudder design. Naval architects at shipyards (e.g., **Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock, Hyundai Heavy Industries**) use these exact formulas to ensure IMO-compliant steering.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following formula table — these are the highest-yield formulas for KTU exams.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rudder Area (A_R)** | $A_R = 1.75 \times L \times T$ | Single-screw ships (L = length, T = draught) — empirical |
| 2 | **Rudder Area (A_R)** | $A_R = L \times T$ | Twin-screw / multi-screw ships |
| 3 | **Rudder Thickness (t)** | $t = (0.18 \text{ to } 0.25) \times \sqrt{A_R}$ | Older empirical formula; A_R in m², t in m |
| 4 | **Rudder Aspect Ratio** | $\Lambda = b^2 / A_R$ | b = rudder span, A_R = area |
| 5 | **Normal Force (F_N)** | $F_N = C_N \times \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A_R$ | C_N = normal force coefficient ≈ 0.65 for ship rudders |
| 6 | **Rudder Torque (Q)** | $Q = C_Q \times \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A_R \times R$ | C_Q = torque coefficient; R = rudder chord to stock offset |
| 7 | **Effective Velocity** | $V_e = (1 - w) \times V$ | Where w = wake fraction |
| 8 | **Stock Dia. (Tapered, IMO A.601)** | $d_{min} = \left( M_{max} / 0.294 \right)^{1/3}$ | For mild steel (Q in N·m → d in m) |
| 9 | **Stock Dia. (IRS Rule)** | $d = \sqrt[3]{(Q \times 10^6) / (0.196 \times \sigma_y)}$ | For IRS-grade mild steel |
| 10 | **Pintle Dia. (d_p)** | $d_p = (0.25 \text{ to } 0.35) \times d_{stock}$ | Empirical proportion |
| 11 | **Bearing Pressure Check** | $p = F_{bearing} / (d \times l) \leq p_{allow}$ | p_allow ≈ 5 N/mm² for lignum vitae |
| 12 | **Centroid of Rudder** | $\bar{c} = 0.33 \times c$ from LE | For rectangular rudders |
| 13 | **Rudder Force (Manoeuvring)** | $F_{side} = 0.0648 \times A_R \times V^2$ | Empirical (Kempf) — F in N, A in m², V in m/s |
| 14 | **Equivalent Stress (Rud. Stock)** | $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$ | Von Mises criterion |
| 15 | **Slenderness Ratio (Stock)** | $\lambda = L_{eff} / d$ | For buckling check; λ ≤ 50 typical |

### 2.5 Operating Conditions Required for KTU Design

> [!NOTE]
> The rudder must withstand loads in **two design conditions:**
>
> - **Normal Operation:** Speed = **Service Speed (V_s)**, helm angle = **±35°**, sea state **3–4**.
> - **Extreme / Crash-Stop Condition:** Speed = **1.4 × V_s** (overspeed allowance) or **20 knots minimum**, helm angle = **±35°**.
>
> This **1.4× overspeed factor** is critical and frequently tested in KTU questions.

### 2.6 Rudder Stock Design — The Core Strength Check

The rudder stock carries a **combined bending + torsional + axial** load. The critical section is at the **top bearing** (above the waterline) where bending moment from hydro force is maximum.

- **Bending moment M = F_N × lever arm**
- **Torque Q = F_N × offset distance from stock axis to center of pressure**
- The equivalent **Von Mises stress** must satisfy:
$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau^2} \leq \sigma_{allow} = \sigma_y / n$$

> Where $n$ = factor of safety = **1.5 (normal)** or **1.4 (extreme)** as per IMO A.601.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Rudder Force (F_N) from First Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the normal force on a flat-plate rudder rotated by an angle α in a uniform flow of velocity V_e.

**Step 1 — Set up the flow geometry**

When a flat plate of area **A_R** is placed in a uniform flow of velocity **V_e** and inclined at angle **α** to the flow, two components of hydrodynamic force arise:
- **Lift (L)** — perpendicular to the flow direction
- **Drag (D)** — parallel to the flow direction

For a **ship rudder** (slender body, attached flow, no separation at moderate angles), we resolve the resultant force into:
- **Normal Force (F_N)** — perpendicular to the rudder chord
- **Tangential Force (F_T)** — parallel to the rudder chord

**Step 2 — Apply thin-airfoil/lifting-line theory (with empirical correction)**

By the **lifting-line theory (Prandtl)** for a finite-span flat plate, the lift per unit span is:

$$
L' = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_e^2 \, c \cdot C_{L\alpha} \cdot \alpha
$$

Multiplying by the span **b** and adding a **finite-span correction factor** (effective aspect ratio factor κ ≈ 0.85 for ship rudders), the **total normal force** becomes:

$$
F_N = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_e^2 \, A_R \, C_N
$$

Where the **normal force coefficient** C_N for a typical ship rudder at small to moderate angles is:

$$
C_N = 0.65 \cdot \sin(\alpha) + 0.13 \cdot \sin(2\alpha) \quad \text{(per Kempf / Whicker formula)}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify to the KTU working form**

For KTU board exam calculations, the simplified **"standard" form** used is:

$$
F_N = 0.0648 \times A_R \times V^2 \quad \text{(in Newtons, with } A_R \text{ in m}^2, V \text{ in m/s)}
$$

> This empirical constant **0.0648** incorporates ½ρ × C_N and a typical wake fraction.

**Step 4 — Torque derivation**

The torque on the rudder stock is the **normal force multiplied by the moment arm (R)** between the stock axis and the **center of pressure (Cp)**:

$$
Q = F_N \times R
$$

Where **R** is the horizontal distance from the stock axis to Cp. For a rectangular balanced rudder with **0.20A ahead of stock** and chord **c**:

$$
R = 0.33 \cdot c - 0.20 \cdot c = 0.13 \cdot c
$$

Thus, substituting F_N:

$$
Q = 0.0648 \times A_R \times V^2 \times R
$$

**Step 5 — Apply overspeed and sea margin**

The **design torque** is then:

$$
Q_{design} = n_{speed}^2 \times K_{sea} \times Q_{theoretical}
$$

$$
Q_{design} = (1.4)^2 \times 1.30 \times Q_{theoretical} = 2.55 \times Q_{theoretical}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Rudder Stock Diameter

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the minimum rudder stock diameter d such that the equivalent stress does not exceed the permissible value.

**Step 1 — Identify critical stresses**

The rudder stock at the top bearing experiences:
- **Bending moment M** (from F_N × distance from bearing to Cp)
- **Torque T = Q** (from the offset of Cp from stock)
- **Axial force** (from rudder weight buoyancy, usually neglected in initial design)

**Step 2 — Compute the bending stress**

For a solid circular cross-section of diameter **d**:

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^3}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the torsional shear stress**

$$
\tau = \dfrac{T}{Z_p} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Von Mises equivalent stress criterion**

$$
\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3 \tau^2} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{32M}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + 3 \left(\dfrac{16T}{\pi d^3}\right)^2}
$$

Factoring out $1/d^3$:

$$
\sigma_{eq} = \dfrac{1}{d^3} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{32M}{\pi}\right)^2 + 3 \left(\dfrac{16T}{\pi}\right)^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for d**

Setting $\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_{allow} = \sigma_y / n$:

$$
d^3 = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_{allow}} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{32M}{\pi}\right)^2 + 3 \left(\dfrac{16T}{\pi}\right)^2}
$$

$$
d = \left[ \dfrac{1}{\sigma_{allow}} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{32M}{\pi}\right)^2 + 3 \left(\dfrac{16T}{\pi}\right)^2} \right]^{1/3}
$$

**Step 6 — The IMO A.601(15) simplified form**

For practical design, IMO accepts the following **simplified formula** for mild steel rudder stock:

$$
d_{min} = \left( \dfrac{Q_{design}}{0.294} \right)^{1/3}
$$

Where Q is in **N·m** and d is in **metres**. This is the **highest-yield formula for KTU**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical check:** If Q_design = 250 kN·m = 250,000 N·m,
> $d = (250000 / 0.294)^{1/3} = (850340)^{1/3} \approx 94.8 \text{ mm}$
> — a typical value for a medium-sized vessel's rudder stock.

### 3.3 KTU Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A single-screw cargo ship has the following particulars:
> - Length (L) = **150 m**, Draught (T) = **8.0 m**
> - Service Speed V_s = **18 knots** = **9.26 m/s**
> - Rudder type: **Semi-balanced**, balanced fraction = **0.20**
> - Material: Mild Steel, σ_y = **235 N/mm²**, factor of safety = **1.5**
>
> **Required:** (a) Rudder area, (b) Rudder thickness, (c) Maximum torque, (d) Rudder stock diameter.

**Solution:**

**(a) Rudder Area:**

$$
A_R = 1.75 \times L \times T = 1.75 \times 150 \times 8.0 = 2100 \text{ m}^2
$$

**(b) Rudder Thickness (empirical):**

$$
t = 0.22 \times \sqrt{A_R} = 0.22 \times \sqrt{2100} = 0.22 \times 45.83 = 10.08 \text{ m}
$$

> Wait — that value is unrealistic! This shows the **empirical formula is only valid for t in *inches*** or for small rudders. We use the **classification society minimum** instead:

$$
t_{min,IRS} = 0.008 \times A_R^{0.5} + 2 \text{ mm} = 8.6 \text{ mm} + 2 = 10.6 \text{ mm} \approx 11 \text{ mm}
$$

**(c) Maximum Torque:**

Assume chord c = 4.0 m, R = 0.13 × c = 0.52 m

$$
F_N = 0.0648 \times 2100 \times (9.26)^2 = 0.0648 \times 2100 \times 85.75 = 11,672 \text{ N}
$$

$$
Q_{theoretical} = F_N \times R = 11672 \times 0.52 = 6,069 \text{ N·m} \approx 6.07 \text{ kN·m}
$$

$$
Q_{design} = 2.55 \times Q_{theoretical} = 2.55 \times 6.07 = 15.47 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**(d) Rudder Stock Diameter (IMO A.601 form):**

$$
d = \left( \dfrac{Q_{design}}{0.294} \right)^{1/3} = \left( \dfrac{15470}{0.294} \right)^{1/3} = (52,619)^{1/3}
$$

$$
d \approx 37.5 \text{ cm} = 375 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Add corrosion + 5 mm** → Adopt **d = 380 mm**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Rudder Functional Architecture — Force & Moment Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Service Speed V_s] --> B[Effective Velocity V_e = 1-w times V]
    C[Helm Angle alpha deg] --> D[Rudder Inclined in Flow]
    B --> D
    D --> E[Hydrodynamic Force Generation]
    E --> F[Normal Force F_N perpendicular to chord]
    E --> G[Tangential Force F_T along chord]
    F --> H[Center of Pressure Cp at 0.33c from LE]
    H --> I[Turning Moment on Hull M_turn = F_N x L_arm]
    H --> J[Rudder Stock Torque Q = F_N x R]
    J --> K[Design Torque Q_des = 2.55 x Q]
    K --> L[Rudder Stock Diameter d from Von Mises]
    L --> M[Steering Gear Selection]
    F --> N[Bending Moment on Stock M_bend]
    N --> L
    L --> O[Pintle Bearing Check p = F_bear per d x l]
    L --> P[Check Von Mises Stress sigma_eq]
    P --> Q{Acceptable?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Design OK]
    Q -->|No| S[Increase d or Material Grade]
```

### 4.2 Rudder Type Classification Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Rudder Types] --> B[Unbalanced]
    A --> C[Balanced]
    A --> D[Spade]
    A --> E[Twin]
    A --> F[High Performance]
    B --> B1[Single Plate]
    B --> B2[Double Plate]
    C --> C1[Semi Balanced]
    C --> C2[Fully Balanced]
    D --> D1[Mariner Type]
    D --> D2[Twin Spade]
    E --> E1[Common in Twin Screw Tankers]
    F --> F1[Schilling Rudder]
    F --> F2[IACS Type]
```

### 4.3 Rudder Stock Load Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RudderBody[Rudder Body]
        R1[Hydro Force F_N at Cp]
        R2[Center of Pressure at 0.33c]
        R3[Stock Axis at 0.2c]
    end
    subgraph StockLoad[Rudder Stock Loads]
        S1[Bending Moment M at Top Bearing]
        S2[Torsion T = F_N x R]
        S3[Equivalent Stress sigma_eq]
    end
    R1 --> S1
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> S2
    S1 --> S3
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4{Sigma_eq less than sigma_allow}
    S4 -->|Yes| S5[Stock Safe]
    S4 -->|No| S6[Increase d]
```

### 4.4 KTU Design Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Find L, T, V_s, type] --> P2[Step 2: Compute A_R = 1.75 x L x T]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Compute t from IRS Rules]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Compute chord c and span b]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Find Cp at 0.33c and R]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Compute F_N from empirical formula]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Compute Q_theoretical = F_N x R]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Apply overspeed 1.4 and sea margin 1.30]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Compute Q_design]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Compute stock dia d]
    P10 --> P11[Step 11: Check bearing pressure]
    P11 --> P12[Step 12: Check Von Mises stress]
    P12 --> P13{Design OK?}
    P13 -->|Yes| P14[Drawing and Final Scantlings]
    P13 -->|No| P15[Revise and Iterate]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**
>
> **Q1: Define "Rudder Area" and state the empirical formula used for estimating it in a single-screw ship.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Rudder Area (A_R):** The projected lateral area of the rudder (m²) — the area of the side view of the rudder when seen from the ship's broadside.

**Empirical formula (single-screw):**

$$
A_R = 1.75 \times L \times T
$$

Where **L** = Length Between Perpendiculars (m), **T** = Design Draught (m).

For **twin-screw ships:**

$$
A_R = L \times T
$$

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
>
> **Q2: What is meant by "Balanced Rudder"? State its advantage over an unbalanced rudder.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **balanced rudder** is one in which a small portion (typically **15%–30%**) of the rudder area is located **forward of the rudder stock axis** (the turning axis). This forward portion produces a moment opposite to that produced by the main portion behind the stock, thereby **reducing the net torque** on the rudder stock.

**Advantages over unbalanced rudder:**
1. **Lower torque** on the stock → smaller, lighter steering gear.
2. **Faster rudder response** → better maneuverability.
3. **Lower steering gear power** → reduced fuel/electrical consumption.
4. **Reduced stock diameter** → less structural weight.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> Students commonly lose marks in the following areas — avoid these mistakes:
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.4× overspeed factor** when computing design torque — KTU specifically tests this. (Lose 2–3 marks)
> 2. **Mixing units** — V in knots vs V in m/s. The empirical constant 0.0648 only works with **m/s**. (Lose 2 marks)
> 3. **Using Cp at 0.5c (mid-chord)** instead of **0.33c** — wrong moment arm, wrong torque. (Lose 3 marks)
> 4. **Skipping the Von Mises equivalent stress** check — only doing bending stress gives partial marks. (Lose 2–3 marks)
> 5. **Forgetting the sea margin multiplier (1.25–1.50)** on torque. (Lose 2 marks)
> 6. **Not stating the factor of safety** explicitly. (Lose 1 mark)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**A single-screw bulk carrier has the following particulars:**

- Length between perpendiculars, **L = 180 m**
- Design draught, **T = 9.5 m**
- Service speed, **V_s = 20 knots = 10.29 m/s**
- Rudder type: **Semi-balanced**, balanced fraction = 0.20
- Material: Mild Steel, σ_y = **235 N/mm²**, factor of safety = 1.5

**Calculate:**

**(a)** Rudder area, rudder thickness, and a suitable chord and span. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Maximum hydrodynamic torque on the stock, the design torque, and the minimum rudder stock diameter. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Rudder Geometry (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Rudder area (1 mark):**

$$
A_R = 1.75 \times L \times T = 1.75 \times 180 \times 9.5 = 2992.5 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Rudder thickness (2 marks):**

Per IRS Rules, $t_{min} = 0.008 \sqrt{A_R} + 2 \text{ mm}$:

$$
t_{min} = 0.008 \times \sqrt{2992.5} + 2 = 0.008 \times 54.70 + 2 = 0.438 + 2 = 2.44 \text{ mm}
$$

But practically $t_{min} \geq 12$ mm for ships of this size. **Adopt t = 14 mm + 2 mm corrosion = 16 mm.** (1 mark for justification)

**Step 3 — Chord and Span (4 marks):**

Assume aspect ratio Λ = 1.2, then b² = Λ × A_R = 1.2 × 2992.5 = 3591 m², so b = 59.92 m. This is impractical.

> In reality, span ≈ 0.5 × T = 4.75 m. Re-compute chord:

$$
c = A_R / b = 2992.5 / 4.75 = 630 \text{ m}
$$

That is also unrealistic. Hence for this problem, we adopt typical proportions: **span b = 4.5 m**, **chord c = 6.0 m**, A_R = 27 m² (a realistic value scaled to KTU board). The given A_R is a textbook initial estimate; in actual design, A_R is **selected** based on the empirical formula as a **starting point**, then refined by maneuvering simulation.

> **Adopt: A_R = 27 m², b = 4.5 m, c = 6.0 m** (3 marks for the choice + 1 mark for justification)

---

**(b) Torque and Stock Diameter (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Center of pressure (1 mark):**

Cp at 0.33 × c = 0.33 × 6.0 = 1.98 m from leading edge (LE).

**Step 2 — Moment arm to stock axis (1 mark):**

Stock is at 0.20 × c = 0.20 × 6.0 = 1.2 m from LE.
Distance from stock to Cp: R = 1.98 − 1.2 = 0.78 m.

**Step 3 — Normal force (2 marks):**

$$
F_N = 0.0648 \times A_R \times V^2 = 0.0648 \times 27 \times (10.29)^2
$$

$$
F_N = 0.0648 \times 27 \times 105.88 = 185.2 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical torque (1 mark):**

$$
Q_{th} = F_N \times R = 185.2 \times 0.78 = 144.5 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 5 — Design torque (1 mark):**

$$
Q_{design} = 1.4^2 \times 1.30 \times Q_{th} = 1.96 \times 1.30 \times 144.5 = 368.3 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 6 — Stock diameter (1 mark):**

$$
d_{min} = \left( \dfrac{Q_{design}}{0.294} \right)^{1/3} = \left( \dfrac{368,300}{0.294} \right)^{1/3} = (1,252,721)^{1/3}
$$

$$
d_{min} \approx 107.6 \text{ cm} = 1076 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Adopt d = 1100 mm** (1 mark for final adopted value with rounding)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the classification of ship rudders based on **support configuration**. Discuss the relative advantages and disadvantages of **balanced** and **spade** rudders. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to **IMO Resolution A.601(15)**, describe the design conditions and load combinations that must be considered for rudder stock strength. State the simplified stock diameter formula and explain the safety factor basis. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Rudder Classification (7 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **(1 mark each type, 2 marks for sketches, 2 marks for advantages/disadvantages)**

**Classification based on support configuration:**

1. **Unbalanced Rudder:** The entire rudder area is behind the stock axis. The stock carries the full hydrodynamic torque. Sketch shows stock axis at the leading edge of the rudder.

2. **Semi-Balanced Rudder:** About **15%–30%** of the area is ahead of the stock. A small balance moment is created, reducing stock torque. Sketch shows a balanced portion forward of the stock axis.

3. **Fully Balanced Rudder:** About **40%–50%** of area is forward of the stock. Maximum reduction in torque, but the forward area may "grab" water and reduce steering stability.

4. **Spade Rudder:** No hull support; the rudder is supported only by the **top stock bearing** and the **pintle** at the bottom. Sketch shows cantilevered rudder with two bearings only.

5. **Twin Rudder:** Two rudders, one behind each propeller. Common in **VLCCs** and product tankers.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Feature | Balanced Rudder | Spade Rudder |
|---|---|---|
| Torque | Reduced | Lowest |
| Hull modification | Required (cut-out) | Minimal (hull notched) |
| Efficiency | High | Highest |
| Cost | Moderate | Higher (precision bearings) |
| Maintenance | Easier (more supports) | Harder (only 2 bearings) |
| Risk of vibration | Lower | Higher if aspect ratio is low |

---

**(b) IMO A.601(15) Design Conditions (7 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **(2 marks for design conditions, 2 marks for load combinations, 2 marks for formula + safety factor, 1 mark for concluding remark)**

**Design conditions (per IMO A.601(15)):**

The rudder stock must be designed to withstand loads under the following conditions:

1. **Maximum Torque Condition:** Ship at **maximum service speed** (or **maximum astern speed**, whichever is higher), rudder angle **±35°**, sea state moderate.

2. **Crash-Stop Condition:** Ship moving at **maximum ahead speed**, with a **crash-stop astern** command — the rudder is deflected to ±35° to assist the ship in stopping.

3. **Overspeed Condition:** Rudder is designed for **1.4 × maximum service speed** to account for **surf-riding, broaching, or rogue wave** scenarios.

4. **Shock / Slamming:** In heavy seas, the rudder may experience dynamic pressure from wave slam.

**Load combinations considered:**

- **Bending moment** from F_N (transverse force) acting at distance from the top bearing.
- **Torsional moment** from offset of center of pressure from the stock axis.
- **Axial force** from rudder weight + dynamic lift (often small, sometimes neglected).
- **Whipping stress** at high RPM ship motions (typically in cyclically loaded conditions).

**Simplified Stock Diameter Formula (per IMO):**

For mild steel rudder stock:

$$
d_{min} = \left( \dfrac{Q_{design}}{0.294} \right)^{1/3} \quad \text{(Q in N·m, d in m)}
$$

**Safety factor basis:**

The factor of safety is taken as **1.5** on yield stress, meaning the design stress $\sigma_{design} = \sigma_y / 1.5$. The empirical constant **0.294** is derived from:

$$
0.294 = \left( \dfrac{16}{\pi} \times \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n} \times \dfrac{1}{C_{factor}} \right)
$$

where $C_{factor}$ accounts for the bending-to-torsion ratio and the empirical fit to classification society data.

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** The IMO A.601(15) approach is a **simplified, conservative** method based on torsional capacity alone — it implicitly includes a margin for bending, making it suitable for **initial design** of merchant ship rudders.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU PENST415 — Module 4: Rudder Design — RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST**

- ✅ **Rudder area** is empirical: $A_R = 1.75 L T$ (single screw) or $A_R = L T$ (twin screw).
- ✅ **Rudder thickness** per IRS: $t = 0.008 \sqrt{A_R} + 2$ mm (corrosion).
- ✅ **Center of pressure** for a flat plate: at **0.33 × chord** from leading edge.
- ✅ **Normal force:** $F_N = 0.0648 \times A_R \times V^2$ (V in m/s, F in N).
- ✅ **Torque:** $Q = F_N \times R$ where R = distance from stock to Cp.
- ✅ **Design torque** = $1.4^2 \times 1.30 \times Q_{theoretical} = 2.55 Q_{theoretical}$.
- ✅ **Stock diameter (IMO A.601):** $d = (Q_{design}/0.294)^{1/3}$.
- ✅ **Pintle diameter** = (0.25 to 0.35) × stock diameter.
- ✅ **Factor of safety** on yield = **1.5** for normal, **1.4** for extreme.
- ✅ **Aspect ratio** of rudder Λ = b²/A_R typically **1.0–2.0**.
- ✅ **Bearing pressure** for lignum vitae ≤ 5 N/mm²; for bronze ≤ 10 N/mm².
- ✅ **Balanced rudder** saves 30–40% torque over unbalanced.
- ✅ **Spade rudder** has highest efficiency but requires precise bearing alignment.
- ✅ **Maximum helm angle** = **±35°** (used in KTU problems).
- ✅ **Overspeed factor 1.4** is **squared** when applied to dynamic pressure.
- ✅ **Sea margin** of 1.30 accounts for wave action on the rudder.
- ✅ **ASTM A36 / IRS Grade A** mild steel used for rudder stock (σ_y = 235 MPa).
- ✅ **Von Mises stress** check: $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2} \leq \sigma_{allow}$.
- ✅ **Stock slenderness ratio** λ = L_eff / d ≤ 50 to avoid buckling.
- ✅ **Twin rudders** are used in twin-screw ships for redundancy.
- ✅ **Kempf formula** $F_N = 0.0648 A_R V^2$ is **Kempf's empirical result** — remember to cite it in answers.

> **KTU Golden Rule:** *Always convert knots to m/s (multiply by 0.5144) before using $F_N = 0.0648 A_R V^2$.*
